Security/reliability base rates: no corpus card documents real-world harm rates from UB-backed contracts (CVE data, miscompilation incident studies) or defect density inside audited unsafe blocks — the key inputs for judging whether confinement actually contains the blast radius.

Debug-mode checkability: no card addresses whether asserted contracts can be dynamically checked in development builds (UBSan/Miri-style interpreters for noalias/inbounds/nsw assertions). If contracts are checkable-in-dev/UB-in-release, my position's debuggability risk shrinks substantially; this needs sourcing.

Purity specifically: no card gives IR-level violation semantics for purity/side-effect contracts analogous to noalias/nsw; SPARK (F006) restricts at the source level but the IR channel and its violation semantics are uncarded.

Concurrency memory model: the threat-model file marks data races as only partially verifiable, and the corpus (caveats.md) has no confirmed memory-model coverage; whether races are UB (C++/Rust tradition) or defined interacts directly with every non-interference contract and is unresolved.

End-to-end contract survival: F003 and F009 negative scopes both call for evidence that source-asserted facts survive lowering and measurably fire optimizations in a real pipeline; a prototype lowering with Alive2-style validation (N006) is the promotion requirement per decision-gates.md before this position could win.

result.position: Static proof required: every optimizer-facing contract (noalias, bounds, overflow-freedom, purity) must be compiler-verified before it may be emitted to the optimizer. No source-level UB exists. Any program whose optimizer-facing claims cannot be proven is rejected (or compiled without the contract, i.e., the contract is simply not emitted — but never emitted unproven). The question "what happens when the contract is false at runtime" must be made unaskable: a false contract is a compile error, not a runtime event.
result.coreArgument:
The debate question presupposes a design in which optimizer-facing contracts can be false at runtime. My position is that this presupposition is the defect, and the corpus supports eliminating it rather than mitigating it.

First, the corpus establishes what an optimizer-facing contract IS at the LLVM layer: not a hint but a UB-bearing promise. Violating `noalias`/`alias.scope` facts is undefined behavior (F003). `nuw`/`nsw` overflow produces poison (N002). Values outside declared `range` metadata become poison (N004). `inbounds` GEP outside the object is poison, and cross-object derivation invalidates dereference (N005). Integer division precondition failure is immediate UB (N003). And N001 gives the master rule: LLVM's value lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) licenses every transformation that moves toward more-defined semantics — meaning a single false contract does not produce a locally wrong value; it licenses globally arbitrary miscompilation through refinement. There is no such thing as a "small" contract violation once poison enters the dataflow. So the blast radius of a false contract is unbounded by construction of the target IR.

Second, given that blast radius, there are exactly three coherent source policies, and two fail the debate's own non-negotiable constraint (soundness may not be sacrificed; ergonomics may). Policy (a), trust the programmer (C `restrict`-style source UB): the corpus itself flags this as the weak link — F001's negative scope states restrict-like contracts "depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions," F003's negative scope states LLVM metadata "is only sound if frontend obligations are true," and the corpus's misleading-comparisons discipline explicitly forbids pretending IR mechanisms solve source design ("IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts"). Decisively, N006 shows that even LLVM's own developers are expected to back flag-sensitive InstCombine transforms with machine-checked Alive2 refinement proofs and negative tests, because handling nuw/nsw/fast-math contracts correctly by hand is too error-prone even for compiler engineers. If the professionals who define these contracts need mechanized proof to manipulate them, a language design that asks application programmers to uphold them on trust is not a soundness policy at all. Policy (b), runtime checking: this preserves soundness but structurally forfeits the payoff. The optimizations these contracts buy are register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-write elimination, and reordering (F002) — exactly the transformations that inserted guards obstruct: N003 documents that LLVM will not hoist a `udiv` above its guarding branch precisely because a runtime guard pins the operation behind the check. Worse, several contract classes are not runtime-checkable at meaningful cost or at all: scoped no-alias disjointness (F003) is a property of the whole execution's access sets, and purity/side-effect-freedom (F006) would require instrumenting all effects. Runtime checking degenerates into "don't emit the contract," which is my position's fallback anyway — minus the static-proof cases where the contract survives. Policy (c), static proof, is the only policy under which the false-contract state is unrepresentable and the full contract strength still reaches the optimizer.

Third, the statically-verified route is not speculative; it is the best-attested territory in this corpus. Safe Rust's `&mut` exclusivity makes the caching/simplifying optimization sound "in Rust but not generally in languages without comparable no-alias guarantees" (F001) — a compiler-enforced ownership discipline, not a programmer promise. SPARK demonstrates the full generalization my position requires: compiler/prover-enforced anti-aliasing, ownership with transfer on assignment, and side-effect-free expressions, explicitly to support mathematical treatment of the program (F006). Fortran's `TARGET` attribute shows static declaration-site narrowing of alias sets (F005). F004 makes the same point from the IR side: strong contracts like return `noalias` may only be emitted once the source language has lifetime/provenance/escape RULES — i.e., emission must be gated on statically established facts. F009 (Swift SIL) shows the natural architecture for this position: carry the proven high-level facts in a semantic IR so optimizer passes consume verified facts rather than reconstructed guesses. And F007 is the cautionary contrast: when guarantees are NOT source-level, performance recovery becomes conditional, implementation-dependent, and profile-fragile (HotSpot escape analysis) — "not source-level guarantees" is the corpus's own phrasing. An optimizer-first language should be on the guaranteed side of that line.

Finally, the project's own threat model (implementation/unsound-optimization-threat-model.md) lists the constructs where the compiler "sometimes/often cannot" verify facts — raw pointer casts, int-ptr casts, FFI callbacks — and its own remedy column already contains my position's answer: prohibit, or conservative fallback with no optimizer-facing contract crossing the boundary. Since this language is explicitly allowed to be ugly and verbose but not unsound, rejection of the unprovable is the unique policy consistent with the stated constraint ordering.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes the refinement lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze > concrete) and that valid transforms move toward more-defined semantics; this is the formal reason a false contract has unbounded blast radius — the core premise for making false contracts unrepresentable rather than survivable.

Violating noalias/alias.scope facts is UB at the IR level, and its negative scope states metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true — i.e., the soundness burden sits entirely on the source language, which is exactly where static proof discharges it.

nuw/nsw make overflow produce poison: overflow-freedom is a contract, not an annotation. A language emitting these flags without proof is emitting deferred UB; proof-gated emission is the only sound emission rule.

Range metadata turns out-of-range values into poison — range/bounds facts are UB-bearing promises, so range types must be compiler-verified (or the metadata withheld), matching the static-proof policy.
inbounds GEP and provenance rules make bounds and object-derivation facts poison/UB-bearing; supports requiring proven bounds (or omitting inbounds) rather than trusting or spot-checking them.

Two-way support: precondition failure as immediate UB is maximally dangerous if unproven, AND the documented refusal to hoist udiv above its guard shows runtime guards pin code and block speculation — statically proven preconditions enable strictly more motion than dynamic checks.

LLVM's own contributors are expected to use Alive2 machine-checked proofs and negative tests for flag-sensitive transforms. If contract manipulation requires mechanized proof even inside the compiler, trusting application programmers to uphold unproven contracts is untenable as a soundness policy. (Medium confidence card, one 2-1 vote; weighted accordingly.)

Safe Rust's compiler-enforced &mut exclusivity makes optimizations sound that are unsound elsewhere — an existence proof that static enforcement of no-alias contracts works; its negative scope (restrict depends on programmer truthfulness) is the direct indictment of the trust-based alternative.

Enumerates the payoff at stake (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering) — these are exactly the transformations that inserted runtime checks obstruct, so static proof is the only policy that keeps the full payoff.

SPARK is the workflow-confirmed existence proof for my full position: prover-enforced anti-aliasing, ownership with transfer, and side-effect-free expressions — statically verified purity and non-interference in a real deployed toolchain.
Fortran's TARGET attribute shows static, declaration-site narrowing of pointer alias sets — verified-by-construction aliasing facts without runtime machinery.

Return noalias is allocation-strength but 'source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting it' — the corpus itself says strong contract emission must be gated on statically established source rules.

Contrast case: without source-level guarantees, performance recovery (HotSpot escape analysis) is conditional, inlining- and profile-dependent — showing that the alternative to static source guarantees is fragile, best-effort optimization, unacceptable for an optimizer-first language.

Swift SIL shows the architecture static proof needs: preserve high-level verified semantic facts into a semantic IR so optimizers consume proven facts; supports feasibility of carrying source proofs to the optimizer without loss.

concessions: 'No source-level UB' cannot mean zero trust anywhere: every static-proof language has a trusted computing base. F001's negative scope excludes unsafe Rust/UnsafeCell/raw pointers from the exclusivity guarantee, and the threat-model table marks FFI callbacks as unverifiable — so my position really means UB is confined to an audited vendor/boundary layer that emits no optimizer-facing contracts outward, not that UB is abolished from the universe the program touches.

Static source proof does not protect against compiler-introduced unsoundness: N006 exists precisely because InstCombine transforms can be wrong. My position eliminates programmer-falsified contracts, not optimizer bugs; translation-validation (Alive2-style) would be a separate, additional discipline.

The usability cost is real and acknowledged by the corpus: F006's negative scope says SPARK restrictions 'may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs.' Rejecting unprovable programs will exclude or degrade legitimate code (lock-free structures, graph algorithms with genuine dynamic aliasing, FFI-heavy code), and the fallback (compile without the contract) sacrifices exactly the performance an optimizer-first language exists for in those regions.

I cannot claim the runtime-check alternative is quantitatively worse: no card in my assigned files measures the cost of bounds/overflow checks after optimization, and caveats.md states documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude.' My argument that checks forfeit the payoff is structural (F002 + N003), not benchmarked. A hybrid (prove where possible, check where not) is not refuted by anything I read.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by explicit selection bias (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons #1). My evidence for statically proving noalias-class contracts is strong; my evidence for statically proving bounds, overflow-freedom, and purity at acceptable burden rests almost entirely on the single SPARK card (F006) plus IR-side cards, which is thin for a position that says 'every' contract must be proven.

Concurrency is only 'partially' compiler-verifiable per the threat-model table, and the corpus contains no confirmed memory-model evidence; my position's story for data-race-dependent contracts (value stability, non-interference across threads) is a plan, not an evidenced result.
risks: Prover incompleteness drives escape-hatch pressure: if too much real code is rejected, users route hot code through FFI or an unsafe dialect, reintroducing unverified optimizer-facing assumptions at the worst possible boundary (threat-model rows: FFI 'compiler can verify? no') — potentially at higher volume than a disciplined runtime-check design would produce.

Performance of the rejected/unproven region: where proofs fail and contracts are withheld, code compiles conservatively; F007-style conditional recovery is unavailable by design. The net program-level speed of a static-proof language versus a check-where-unproven language is unmeasured and could favor the opponent.

Separate compilation and whole-program contracts: alias.scope-style disjointness (F003) and purity across module boundaries may be unprovable modularly, forcing either whole-program compilation (compile-time and toolchain risk) or weaker per-module contracts that shrink the promised optimization payoff.

Verification burden (annotation overhead, prover time, proof instability across compiler versions) is completely unquantified in this corpus and has historically been the adoption killer for SPARK-class systems; an optimizer-first language that nobody can get past the prover optimizes nothing.

Ecosystem fragility: proofs are brittle under refactoring; a one-line change that breaks a distant proof turns maintenance cost into a step function, and this failure mode has no evidence card either way.

Per decision-gates.md, none of this can become a language default on optimizer-attractiveness alone; my position currently sits at roughly 'workflow_confirmed evidence + implementation_mapping_needed' and must not be treated as having passed the design_candidate gate.
evidenceGaps: Quantified three-way benchmark: statically-proven contracts vs runtime-checked contracts vs trusted-UB contracts on representative kernels (aliasing-sensitive loops, bounds-heavy array code, overflow-flagged arithmetic). No card quantifies any performance magnitude (caveats.md states this explicitly); this is the single gap most capable of deciding the debate.

Proof-burden field data: SPARK/Rust-borrow-check-style success rates, annotation-to-code ratios, prover time, and proportion of idiomatic performance code rejected. F006 flags usability cost qualitatively; no card measures it.

Coverage beyond aliasing: adversarially verified evidence on static verification of bounds, overflow-freedom, and purity specifically (the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection; N-cards cover the IR consequences, not the source-side provability).
Runtime-check cost and check-elimination evidence: whether modern check-hoisting/elimination recovers enough of F002's payoff to make the hybrid position dominant; nothing in my files addresses this.

Contract survival through lowering: F003's negative scope warns metadata must survive lowering to matter; no card measures how much frontend-proven fact actually reaches and pays off in LLVM optimization pipelines.

FFI/boundary design under 'reject the unprovable': whether a conservative no-contract boundary is performance-viable for real programs (threat-model remedy column asserts it as an option; no evidence evaluates it).

The A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 card series referenced in the debate ground rules were not present in any of the seven files I was assigned; if they contain Ada/SPARK toolchain practice, CompCert-style verified-compilation, or JIT/dynamic-language evidence, they could materially strengthen or weaken this position and must be read before a decision.

Concurrency/memory-model evidence: the corpus contains none (caveats.md), and data races can silently falsify every non-interference contract; static race-freedom's feasibility and cost for this language is entirely open.

result.position: Graduated obligations (Ada/Zig-flavored middle road): optimizer-facing contracts (noalias, bounds, overflow-freedom, purity) are runtime-checked in debug builds, trapped or conservatively compiled when cheap, and assumed (with UB consequences) only inside explicitly-marked release contexts. Assumption is never the unmarked default; the marking is per-fact and auditable.
result.coreArgument: The question is what a false contract costs and who pays, and the corpus rules out both poles before it says anything about the middle. Pole 1, always-assume: every IR assumption mechanism in the corpus is unconditionally dangerous when the underlying fact is false — violating noalias is UB (F003), out-of-range loads become poison (N004), inbounds violations poison (N005), nsw/nuw overflow poisons (N002) — and the corpus's own discipline notes insist these are 'conditional carriers of source facts,' sound only if frontend obligations are true (F003 negative scope; misleading-comparisons entry 5). Crucially, several contract classes cannot be compiler-verified at all: the unsound-optimization threat model marks raw pointer casts 'sometimes no,' int-ptr casts 'often no,' and FFI callbacks flatly 'no,' and F001's negative scope says restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness.' So assume-by-default is unsound by construction whenever a programmer-asserted fact is false, and this debate's ground rules forbid trading soundness. The decision-gates standing rule independently blocks it: 'a feature cannot become a language default merely because it is optimizer-attractive.' Pole 2, always-check: this abandons exactly the enablers the corpus documents — register promotion, redundant-load/dead-store elimination, and reordering all hinge on non-interference being assumable (F002), and every strongly-attested performance language in the corpus (Rust, Fortran, SPARK: F001, F005, F006) wins by making facts assumable somewhere. An optimizer-first language that never assumes has no reason to exist. The decisive positive evidence for graduation is that the target substrate is already graduated, so my position costs no new semantics. LLVM's undefinedness model is an explicit lattice — immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete — with transforms only allowed to move toward more-defined (N001). Its integer semantics separate default defined arithmetic (unflagged add is modulo 2^n) from opt-in promises (nsw/nuw make overflow poison) (N002): my 'explicitly-marked release context' lowers to flagged ops; unmarked code lowers to unflagged ops; a debug build lowers to check-and-trap — all three are native lowerings of the same source contract. LLVM itself practices conservative compilation as a working strategy: it refuses to hoist udiv above a guarding branch when the divisor might be zero (N003), which means an inserted runtime check is a guard the optimizer already respects, not an alien construct. For every contract class in the corpus there is both an 'assume' encoding (noalias metadata F003, nsw/nuw N002, range metadata N004, inbounds N005) and a sound omission fallback (no metadata = conservative may-alias; unflagged = defined wraparound; non-inbounds GEP = defined address computation), with freeze (N001) as a documented containment middle-point. N006 shows the optimizer ecosystem is engineered for both states: InstCombine folds sensitive to nuw/nsw flags are expected to carry negative tests for the flag-absent case, i.e., presence and absence of a promise are both first-class, tested configurations. Graduated obligations is thus the source-level projection of the IR's own architecture, and the only one of the three positions with per-fact, per-site, per-build granularity that the backend can actually represent. Finally, graduation composes with trust provenance, which the corpus already distinguishes: facts true by construction (safe Rust &mut exclusivity, F001) or by static verification (SPARK anti-aliasing and ownership, F006) can be assumed in every build mode because they cannot be false without a compiler/verifier bug and cost nothing at runtime; programmer-asserted, runtime-checkable facts get debug checks and marked-release assumption; uncheckable boundary facts get exactly what the threat model prescribes — 'unsafe boundary / weaker metadata / conservative fallback.' The corpus's own remedy column is a graduated-obligations table. Note on rival framing: this is not a compromise that forfeits the optimizer-first premise — it preserves the full assumption ceiling (identical IR to always-assume inside marked contexts) while keeping unmarked code sound, and it converts the always-check position's cost into a per-context choice instead of a global tax.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: LLVM's own undefinedness model is a graduated lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) with transforms required to move toward more-defined semantics; freeze is a documented containment point. This shows graduated severity of contract violation is the substrate's native architecture, not an invention of my position.

Unflagged integer add has fully defined modulo semantics while nsw/nuw are opt-in promises whose violation yields poison. This is the exact shape of graduated obligations for overflow-freedom: conservative-defined by default, assumed only where explicitly flagged — and all lowerings (checked, wrapped, flagged) are natively expressible.

LLVM treats div-by-zero as immediate UB and consequently refuses to hoist udiv above a guarding branch. This proves conservative compilation around guards is a real, working optimizer strategy with bounded cost (missed speculation, not unsoundness), and that inserted runtime checks act as guards the optimizer already respects.

Range metadata makes out-of-range loaded/returned values poison — an assumption channel for bounds-like facts. It gives marked release contexts a direct encoding for bounds contracts, and its omission is always a sound fallback, enabling per-site graduation.

inbounds GEP poisons out-of-object results while non-inbounds GEP is unrestricted for address computation: the corpus's clearest example that the same operation exists in an 'assume' form and a sound conservative form, which is precisely what graduated emission needs.

InstCombine requires flag-interaction tests and negative tests when a poison-generating flag is required for correctness: the optimizer ecosystem is engineered so that promise-present and promise-absent are both legal, tested states — graduated frontends emit into an infrastructure built for exactly that. (2-1 vote on one constituent claim; medium confidence, weighed accordingly.)

Two-edged and I use both edges: positively, language-level non-interference is a central optimizer enabler (why assumption contexts must exist at all); negatively, its scope note says restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions' — the corpus itself distinguishes compiler-proven facts (assumable everywhere) from asserted facts (which need checking or marking).

Documents the concrete optimization classes (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering) that non-interference assumptions buy. This is the case against always-check: forfeiting all assumption contexts forfeits the documented enablers an optimizer-first language exists for.

noalias attributes/metadata express disjointness with UB on violation, and the negative scope states metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true. Direct evidence that unconditional assumption of unverifiable contracts is unsound by construction — the core defect of the always-assume pole.

SPARK shows the static-verification tier of the gradient is real and deployed: ownership transfer, anti-aliasing, and side-effect-free expressions verified before the optimizer ever sees them. Statically verified contracts can be assumed in all build modes at zero runtime cost, anchoring the top rung of the graduated ladder.

Fortran's model shows a language can grant assumption by default in a narrow, rule-scoped domain (dummy arguments, non-TARGET variables) while staying conservative elsewhere (pointers assumed to alias unless proven otherwise) — historical precedent for scoped, non-uniform assumption rather than a global policy.

noalias returns are an allocation-like contract the frontend can emit only where lifetime/provenance/escape rules justify it (per its negative scope) — another example where the corpus conditions assumption-emission on established obligations, i.e., graduation by fact provenance.

concessions: The corpus quantifies neither side of my central tradeoff: F002's negative scope explicitly says the evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude,' and no card quantifies runtime-check overhead. My 'trapped or conservatively compiled when cheap' clause is therefore currently unoperationalizable from evidence — 'cheap' is a placeholder awaiting benchmarks.

The strongest attested optimizer wins in the corpus (F001 safe-Rust exclusivity, F005 Fortran dummy-argument rules, F006 SPARK) come from facts that are true by construction or by static verification, not from runtime-checked assertions. An honest reading gives some support to a rival position of 'verify statically or don't claim it' for aliasing specifically; my runtime-checked middle tier is the least-evidenced tier of my own ladder.

Graduated obligations does not eliminate unsoundness; it fences it. A false contract inside an explicitly-marked release context is still full UB with silent poison propagation (N001, N002, F003), and the fence is only as strong as the audit culture around marked regions.

The 'Ada/Zig-flavored' framing of my assigned position is unevidenced in this corpus: caveats.md states there is no verified coverage of Zig, and no card documents Ada's suppressible runtime checks. I argue from LLVM-substrate evidence instead; the named precedents are branding, not citations.

Direct-extraction cards (A/D/C/J) referenced in the debate brief were not present in the files I was directed to read, so my case rests entirely on F/N cards and the two implementation docs; if those cards exist and cover Ada/Zig/checked-build practice, they could strengthen or undercut me.
risks: Debug/release behavioral divergence: you test the checked build and ship the assuming build, so violations that only manifest on release-mode inputs or timings escape the debug checker entirely. The corpus contains no empirical data on how often this bites, so the risk is unsized.

Mode-matrix complexity: every contract times {checked, trapped, conservative, assumed} multiplies frontend lowering paths and testing burden. N006 shows even LLVM's own devs need disciplined flag-interaction and negative testing to keep flag-sensitive transforms sound; a graduated frontend inherits that burden across its whole contract surface.

Marking metastasis: if the measured performance delta of marked contexts is large, users will mark everything hot, and the design degenerates in practice toward always-assume with extra steps. Nothing in the corpus speaks to whether explicit-marking discipline holds up under performance pressure.

Check-induced pessimization: inserted trap checks are control flow and (per the N003 mechanism, read in reverse) act as guards that legitimately block speculation and code motion around them; debug and 'cheap-trap' builds may be slow in ways that distort profiling and push users toward marked contexts prematurely. Unquantified either way.

Lowering-order hazards: because nsw/nuw/range violations are deferred poison rather than immediate UB (N001, N002, N004), a build that emits both checks and flags must ensure checks dominate flagged uses, or wrong values can be observed before a trap fires; getting this wrong is a frontend soundness bug of exactly the kind F003's negative scope warns about (metadata sound only if obligations hold and survive lowering).

Performance transparency is weakened in one respect: the same source program has build-mode-dependent failure semantics (trap vs wrap vs UB), which complicates the language's promise that the programmer can read performance and behavior off the source.
evidenceGaps: Quantified runtime overhead of bounds/overflow/aliasing checks in optimized builds (and how much the optimizer can elide via check-derived facts). Without this, 'trapped or conservatively compiled when cheap' cannot be given a threshold, and the always-check rival cannot be fairly costed.

Quantified performance value of noalias/nsw/range/inbounds assumptions on representative workloads (F002 explicitly does not quantify). If the delta is small, marked-assume contexts may not be worth their soundness fence; if large, marking-metastasis risk grows. Either result reshapes the debate.

Verified cards on the named precedents: Ada's suppressible checks (pragma Suppress, Constraint_Error semantics) and Zig's Debug/ReleaseSafe/ReleaseFast build modes — deployment outcomes, failure taxonomies, and whether users actually keep checks on. caveats.md confirms Zig coverage is absent.

Empirical data on debug/release divergence defects and on real-world frontend miscompiles from wrongly emitted assumption metadata (e.g., the history of Rust toggling noalias emission is commonly cited but is NOT in this corpus and must not be assumed).

LLVM trap/check mechanics: cost and optimization impact of trap intrinsics, whether llvm.assume-style encodings of checked facts recover optimization after a check, and whether checks can be safely hoisted/merged. N006 mentions llvm.assume only as an Alive2 precondition device.

Whether purity contracts (one of the four named in the debate topic) are runtime-checkable at all at acceptable cost — the corpus's purity evidence (F006 SPARK) is purely static; the graduated middle tier may simply not exist for purity, forcing that contract class to verified-or-conservative only.

The A/D/C/J direct-extraction cards referenced in the debate brief were not in the seven listed files; locating and reading them (with their provisional, non-adversarially-verified status) is required before any final decision leans on this debate.

Cross-mode semantic-equivalence requirement: no evidence addresses whether violation-free executions are guaranteed identical across debug/release modes under graduated lowering (e.g., wrap-in-unmarked vs poison-in-marked interacting with the same source expression), which the decision gates would classify as blocked_by_semantics.

result.position: General memory plus checkable contracts: retain pointer/slice flexibility as the universal substrate, surface layout, alignment, contiguity, and disjointness as first-class checkable annotations, and lower them onto LLVM's existing legality machinery (the C+restrict+pragmas direction, hardened so every annotation is verified, runtime-checked, or an explicit unsafe promise).
result.coreArgument: The optimizer does not consume array abstractions; it consumes legality facts. The best-attested evidence in this corpus says the load-bearing input to optimization is proven non-interference around writes (F002) plus concrete per-loop facts — alias/dependence information, reductions, inductions, trip counts, FP flags, alignment — which LLVM's vectorizers and passes already accept via noalias attributes, alias.scope metadata, and runtime pointer checks (F003, F004, A005). A contracts-on-general-memory design feeds exactly these consumers directly, with no new mid-level IR to build, validate, and keep sound (F009's lesson about preserving semantics long enough applies, but noalias/align/dereferenceable attributes already survive to LLVM today). Second, every restricted-structure alternative in the corpus carries an applicability cliff stated in its own evidence card: affine forms only help where code is affine (A003 negative scope), Halide's schedule split assumes DSL purity constraints (A006 negative scope), Futhark demands regular arrays and still cannot guarantee parallel execution (A008 negative scope), and DO CONCURRENT's restrictions are necessary but not sufficient (A010). A general-purpose optimizer-first language must still compile irregular, pointer-heavy code — the dossier itself poses this as an open question — and only the general-memory-plus-contracts model degrades gracefully: unannotated code is merely slower, never inexpressible or pushed through a second, weaker language mode. Third, and most telling: MLIR MemRef, the flagship structured-memory IR, still had to bolt on explicit distinct_objects no-alias contracts and in-bounds assumptions, because its views can alias the underlying storage (A001). Structure does not eliminate the need for disjointness contracts; it re-derives them. Contracts are therefore the primitive; structured arrays are sugar that a library or later dialect can add on top. Fourth, soundness is achievable without abandoning generality: F001 shows contract-style non-aliasing (restrict, Fortran dummy arguments, SPARK) is a proven optimizer enabler across four language families; F006 shows SPARK's verification route makes such restrictions checkable rather than trusted; and A005 shows LLVM already supports the sound fallback for unprovable contracts — runtime pointer checks with a conservative version. So the hardened design is: annotations are either statically discharged (SPARK-style), discharged by an emitted runtime check (LLVM-style multiversioning), or marked as explicit unsafe promises — never silently trusted as C's restrict is. Since this language accepts ugliness and verbosity, the historical ergonomic failure of restrict/pragmas is not disqualifying; the concept's evidence base is the strongest in the corpus, while the structured alternatives rest entirely on provisional, unverified extraction cards.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes that the optimization value of alias information is specifically proving non-interference around writes — a property expressible as disjointness contracts on general memory, not one requiring restricted array types. This is the semantic core my position surfaces directly.

Shows LLVM IR already has optimizer-facing carriers for exactly my annotations (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata for disjoint access sets), so the contract route needs no new IR infrastructure; its caveat (metadata sound only if frontend obligations hold) is what my 'checkable' requirement answers.

noalias-return being stronger than C99 restrict shows the contract vocabulary is not stuck at 1999 C — LLVM's contract family is richer than source restrict, and a language designed for it can emit the stronger forms.

Workflow-confirmed, high-source-accuracy finding that language-level non-aliasing rules — including restrict-like contracts — are central optimizer enablers across Rust, Fortran, SPARK, and C/C++. This is the strongest evidence tier in the corpus and it directly names my mechanism family.

SPARK demonstrates the checkability half of my position: mutable-aliasing restrictions and ownership policies can be statically verified rather than trusted, giving a soundness-preserving template for making restrict-style contracts safe.

(Provisional) Lists the concrete facts LLVM's loop/SLP vectorizers consume — alias/dependence facts, reductions, trip counts, FP flags, runtime pointer checks, cost models — showing my annotations map one-to-one onto existing backend consumers, and that runtime pointer checks provide a sound fallback when a contract cannot be statically proven.

(Provisional) MemRef, the structured-memory counterexample, itself requires explicit distinct_objects no-alias contracts and in-bounds assumptions, and its views can alias underlying storage — evidence that disjointness contracts are the irreducible primitive even inside structured designs.

(Provisional) Its own negative scope concedes affine restriction 'only applies where programs fit affine restrictions' — the applicability cliff that makes restricted structure unsuitable as the sole memory model of a general-purpose language.

(Provisional) Halide's negative scope concedes it is a DSL with purity/update constraints whose benefits are workload-dependent and 'not automatically transferable to a general language' — directly undercutting schedule-DSL-as-core-language proposals.

(Provisional) Futhark's regularity/uniqueness gains come with negative scope that parallel execution is not guaranteed and layout/codegen claims need further evidence — the restricted-array route's benefits are less verified than the contract route's.

(Provisional) DO CONCURRENT shows that even a dedicated restricted loop form's restrictions are 'necessary rather than sufficient' — compilers still need dependence analysis or runtime checks, i.e., the same legality machinery my position relies on anyway.

Supports the design constraint that facts must survive lowering to be useful; contract attributes (noalias, align) already have a preserved path into LLVM, whereas a new structured IR would need to build and validate that preservation from scratch.

concessions: Pointwise disjointness contracts cannot express what structured representations express: Linalg-style indexing maps and iterator kinds (A002) and affine forms (A003) enable dependence analysis and loop-nest transformations (tiling, fusion, interchange) that are hard or impossible to recover from general pointer code even with perfect noalias facts. My position likely forfeits some of that transformation space unless a structured layer is added above the contract substrate.

The C restrict lineage's historical soundness record is bad: F001's own scope limit says restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions,' and F003 says violating the metadata contract is UB. My position is only admissible under this project's soundness rule in its hardened (checked/runtime-verified/explicit-unsafe) form, and the corpus contains no confirmed evidence that such checking scales to real general-purpose code.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, not by established importance (caveats.md; misleading-comparisons entry 1). The fact that my position aligns with the best-verified cards partly reflects what was researched, not a demonstrated superiority over array/layout/loop-structure approaches, whose evidence (A002, A003, A006, A008, A009) is provisional rather than weak on the merits.

Per misleading-comparisons entry 5 and F003's caveat, IR metadata is only a conditional carrier: emitting noalias/align does not solve the source-design problem; the entire soundness burden lands on my frontend checking story.

No card anywhere in the corpus quantifies performance magnitude, so I cannot claim the contract route captures 'most' of the achievable speedup; documentation evidence shows mechanisms exist, not how much they buy (caveats.md).
risks: Checkability may force convergence anyway: making disjointness statically verifiable over general pointers plausibly requires an ownership/region/exclusivity system (the F001/F006 pattern), at which point 'general memory + contracts' collapses into an ownership-typed design and my position loses its distinctness.

Annotation combinatorics: disjointness among N pointers is pairwise (O(N^2) facts), where structured arrays get whole-object disjointness for free (A001's distinct_objects is per-allocation). Even in an ugliness-tolerant language, this could make hot code unmaintainable and error-prone at the unsafe-promise escape hatch.

Runtime-check fallback costs: A005 notes vectorization may need runtime pointer checks and fallback versions; heavy reliance on multiversioning risks code-size blowup and check overhead that the corpus does not quantify.

Ceiling risk on loop-nest optimization: if tiling/fusion/interchange-class transforms genuinely require preserved structure (A002, A003 suggest but do not prove this), a contracts-only language may hit a permanent performance ceiling on dense-array workloads relative to a structured-IR competitor — precisely the workloads an optimizer-first language is judged on.

Pragma/hint pollution: A005 characterizes pragmas as hints; if the design leans on unverified hints for profitability decisions, stale or wrong hints degrade silently, and the corpus offers no evidence on detecting or containing that.
evidenceGaps: No quantitative evidence in the corpus of the speedup attributable to restrict/noalias-style contracts on realistic workloads, versus structured-IR (Linalg/affine) compilation of the same kernels; this head-to-head is the single most decision-relevant missing measurement.

No evidence on what fraction of contract annotations in real general-purpose code can be statically discharged versus needing runtime checks versus unsafe promises — the viability of the 'checkable' half of my position is unmeasured.

No confirmed evidence on whether LLVM's mainline legality analysis suffices for loop-nest transformations (interchange, tiling, fusion) or whether those genuinely require MLIR-style preserved structure; A002/A003 are design-rationale docs, not comparative results.
Runtime-check and multiversioning costs (code size, check overhead, compile time) are unquantified; A005 mentions the mechanisms but no measurements.

All A-cards are direct-extraction and not adversarially verified; both my MemRef-needs-contracts argument (A001) and my applicability-cliff arguments (A003/A006/A008/A010 negative scopes) rest partly on provisional cards and need verification before this debate is decided.

Cards N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 were referenced in the debate ground rules but do not appear in the five files I was assigned to read; whatever they contain (plausibly numerics, dispatch/data-layout, concurrency, and JIT/runtime evidence) is entirely unweighed in this case and could strengthen or undermine it.

No evidence on annotation ergonomics at scale even under an 'ugly is acceptable' regime — e.g., whether pairwise disjointness annotation burden causes wrong promises in practice (the soundness-relevant failure mode), as opposed to mere verbosity.

result.position: Hybrid generics representation for the performance core: value-type instantiations are fully specialized (monomorphized), reference-type instantiations share one generic code body over a uniform pointer representation (.NET direction). Dispatch follows the same line: specialized/direct on value-type hot paths, shared/indirect (with devirtualization opportunities) on reference-type paths. Crucially for an optimizer-first language, the specialize/share boundary is made explicit and predictable in the source, not a compiler heuristic.
result.coreArgument: The hybrid model is the only position in this debate that is directly attested as a shipped, coherent design point rather than assembled from parts: D005 documents that .NET reifies generics, specializes value-type instantiations, and shares a single reference-type body — proving the hybrid is implementable and that specialization and sharing can coexist in one type system. The argument for it in an optimizer-first language has three legs. (1) It keeps the specialization win exactly where specialization matters most: value types have layout- and register-level representation differences, so sharing them would force boxing/indirection — the same class of cost D002 attributes to dynamic dispatch (lookup cost, inlining blocked). Specializing them yields concrete-code performance per D001. Reference-type instantiations, by contrast, already manipulate uniform pointers, so one shared body loses layout-specialization almost nothing; what it loses is direct-call/inlining opportunity, and D007 documents that compilers can claw much of that back via devirtualization, speculative guarded devirtualization, indirect inlining, and IPA/LTO — with D006-style source finality (sealed) as an additional, language-designable input to that reasoning. (2) Full monomorphization's costs are real and structural, not incidental: D001's own source flags compile-time/code-size tradeoffs; D009 shows a specialization-heavy system (Julia) explicitly warning that over-specialization increases compile-time and cache pressure; and D004/D008 show monomorphization's benefits depend on compilation-unit visibility and are hostile to dynamic-linking/stable-ABI boundaries, whereas a shared reference-type body is a stable, separately-compilable artifact — decisive for a language that must interoperate across ABI boundaries (the dossier's central concern). (3) Performance transparency, this language's prime directive, is best served by a hard, source-visible rule — 'value type ⇒ specialized code, reference type ⇒ shared code' — rather than by heuristic compiler specialization (the D009/D010 Julia model, where performance depends on inference and type stability the programmer must divine) or by unbounded monomorphization whose code-size cliff is invisible at the use site. D003 additionally shows a language can restrict which interfaces are legal on the shared/dispatched path, so the shared side can be kept sound and representationally coherent by construction. Since the language is optimizer-first and may be ugly, the hybrid line can be made syntactically explicit (distinct value-generic and ref-generic forms), converting .NET's runtime policy into a static, LLVM-AOT-compatible cost model.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The anchor card: documents the exact hybrid design shipped in .NET — value-type instantiations specialized, reference-type instantiations sharing one generic body over object references, with generic metadata preserved. Proves feasibility and coherence of the assigned position; it is not a hypothetical synthesis.

Establishes both halves of the tradeoff the hybrid balances: monomorphization gives runtime behavior like concrete code (why the hybrid keeps it for value types) while the same source flags compile-time/code-size tradeoffs (why the hybrid does not apply it unboundedly to reference types).

Documents that indirection-based dispatch has real optimizer-visible costs (lookup, blocked inlining). This is the cost profile boxing value types into a shared body would inherit — the strongest evidence that the specialization half of the hybrid must cover value types specifically.

Shows the shared reference-type half is not doomed to permanent indirect-call cost: GCC documents devirtualization, speculative guarded devirtualization, indirect inlining, and IPA/LTO that recover direct-call performance when targets become known — the recovery mechanism the hybrid relies on for its shared side.

Sealed/finality is a source-level construct a language can provide to feed devirtualization of the shared path — supporting the claim that the shared side's costs are design-addressable. Cited within its limits: the source itself does not prove devirtualization benefit.

Rust's dyn-compatibility rules show a language can statically restrict which interfaces are legal for shared/indirect dispatch, which is how the hybrid's shared half stays sound and representationally uniform without runtime surprises.

Codegen-unit/LTO evidence shows monomorphization's optimization benefit is contingent on cross-unit visibility and trades against compile time — weakening 'monomorphize everything' as a default and favoring a model with a bounded, separately-compilable shared artifact.

Semantic interposition shows dynamic-linking/ABI semantics can block optimizer assumptions regardless of source-level knowledge; a single stable shared body for reference instantiations is the representation that survives such boundaries, which pure monomorphization handles poorly.

Julia's own performance guidance concedes over-specialization increases compile-time and cache pressure — independent, second-system evidence that unlimited specialization has real costs, motivating a bounded specialization policy.

Shows the alternative to a static specialize/share rule — dispatch-driven specialization with world-age and unbounded-case complications — carries optimizer-assumption hazards the hybrid's static rule avoids entirely.

Supports the implementation posture the hybrid needs: an IR that preserves source-level facts (here, genericity/instantiation structure and the specialize-vs-share decision) long enough to optimize them before lowering to LLVM, per the Swift SIL precedent.

Used defensively for soundness: no-alias metadata is only sound if frontend obligations hold, so the shared generic body must only be annotated with alias facts provable for every reference instantiation — a design constraint I accept, not evidence of superiority.

concessions: D005 is a direct-extraction, provisionally verified card and its negative scope is explicit: it does not prove specific JIT optimizations, and reflection metadata may constrain closed-world assumptions. My anchor evidence establishes feasibility, not measured superiority.

The .NET evidence describes a JIT runtime with lazy instantiation and runtime dictionaries; our target is AOT LLVM. The corpus contains zero evidence on AOT realizations of the hybrid (e.g., .NET NativeAOT), so the transfer of the model to our compilation strategy is asserted, not evidenced.

The code-size motivation for the hybrid is itself unquantified: D001's negative scope says code bloat and compile time are not quantified, and no card measures monomorphization bloat. I am arguing from documented direction of tradeoff, not magnitude.

The shared reference-type path pays real costs the corpus documents (D002: lookup cost, blocked inlining), and the recovery mechanisms in D007 are compiler- and option-dependent; speculative devirtualization may be reverted. On workloads dominated by reference-type generic hot loops, full monomorphization would likely win, and I cannot cite a card refuting that.

Per caveats.md, the whole dispatch/generics card set (D001–D010) is direct-extraction and not adversarially verified, and the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection — no relative-importance conclusion about dispatch design is licensed by this corpus.

If the performance core ends up with no reference types or no uniform-pointer representation (a GC/ownership decision not yet made), the hybrid degenerates to full monomorphization and my position collapses into advocate 1's (or whoever holds the monomorphization brief).
risks: Two performance classes in one generics feature: identical-looking generic code is fast for value types and slower for reference types. In a performance-transparent language this is only acceptable if the split is syntactically explicit; if it stays implicit (as in C#), the hybrid actively violates the language's prime directive.

Implementation complexity concentrates in the shared path: uniform representation, runtime type/dictionary passing, and per-instantiation metadata are machinery LLVM does not provide; the corpus offers no evidence on its cost or on how well LLVM optimizes dictionary-passing code (D005 negative scope, F009 only shows the IR-preservation pattern).

Soundness pressure on alias facts: the corpus's strongest confirmed findings (F001–F003) are about non-interference guarantees; a shared generic body must emit only alias metadata valid for all instantiations, and F003 warns unsound metadata is UB at the IR level. The shared path thus risks either losing optimizer-enabling alias precision or emitting unsound annotations — the one failure mode the ground rules forbid.

Reified generic metadata (part of what D005 documents) can constrain closed-world assumptions the AOT optimizer wants (D005 negative scope), and interacts badly with D008-style interposition questions unless the language deliberately drops reflection — a departure from the evidence card's actual system.
The hybrid couples the generics design to an unresolved upstream decision (value vs reference type dichotomy, GC or not); arguing for it now risks pre-deciding that dichotomy through the back door.
evidenceGaps: No AOT evidence for the hybrid: nothing on .NET NativeAOT/ILC, Mono full-AOT, or any ahead-of-time realization of shared generic bodies. This is the single most important gap — without it, 'the .NET direction works under LLVM-style AOT' is unproven.

No quantified code-size or compile-time measurements for monomorphization (Rust/C++ binary bloat, build-time data), so the hybrid's core motivation — bounded code size — has documented direction but no magnitude.

No Swift evidence in the corpus for witness tables + @_specialize, which is the closest existing 'shared-by-default, specialize-on-demand' AOT system; the dossier explicitly lists Swift and HotSpot dispatch evidence as open.
No empirical head-to-head benchmarks: hybrid vs full monomorphization vs full dictionary-passing on representative workloads; the corpus contains no benchmark cards at all for dispatch/generics.

No evidence on how shared generic bodies interact with LLVM alias metadata precision (the F002/F003 machinery) — whether the shared path forfeits the corpus's best-attested optimizer enablers in practice.

The N001–N006, A001–A010, C001–C006, and J001–J005 card ranges named in the debate ground rules do not appear in the five files I was assigned; if they exist elsewhere in the corpus they must be read before a decision, since they may contain directly relevant dispatch/ABI evidence.
No evidence on devirtualization effectiveness specifically for shared generic code (D007 covers C++-style virtual calls under GCC; transfer to dictionary/vtable-per-instantiation designs is assumed).
No cost data for runtime generic metadata/dictionaries in a non-JIT setting (memory, startup, dynamic-linking implications), which the hybrid requires and pure monomorphization does not.

result.position: Monomorphization-only core: every generic instantiation specializes statically to concrete code; dynamic dispatch exists only as an explicit, restricted, object-safe (dyn-compatible) construct whose indirect-call cost is syntactically visible — Rust's model with the implicit escape hatches removed.
result.coreArgument: An optimizer-first, performance-transparent language has one prime directive: the cost model must be readable from source and must not depend on whether a downstream optimizer got lucky. Judged by that directive, monomorphization is the only dispatch/generics model in this corpus whose fast path is UNCONDITIONAL. D001 documents that Rust generics compile by monomorphization into concrete type-specific code with runtime behavior equivalent to hand-written concrete code — no heuristic, no profile, no whole-program pass required for the common case to be fast. Every alternative model in the corpus recovers performance only CONDITIONALLY: .NET shares reference-type generic bodies behind object references and preserves reflection metadata that constrains closed-world assumptions (D005); Julia recovers specialization only when code is type-stable, with function barriers, world-age constraints, and workload-dependent heuristics (D009, D010); and backend devirtualization (D007) is compiler- and option-dependent, speculative and revertible, and is silently defeated by compilation-unit boundaries (D004) and ELF semantic interposition (D008). In a performance-transparent language, 'the optimizer will probably devirtualize it' is not a feature — it is a hidden dependency on invisible toolchain state, exactly the class of unpredictability this language exists to eliminate. Meanwhile the cost of dynamic dispatch is real and documented: D002 states that dyn-Trait calls add lookup cost and prevent inlining of the method body. Inlining is the gateway through which the corpus's best-attested optimizer enablers flow — the F-series shows the optimizer's leverage comes from statically proven non-interference facts (F001, F002), and an opaque indirect call is a wall behind which the compiler must conservatively assume interference (this last linkage is my inference from F002, flagged as such below). So the design should make the cheap thing the default and the expensive thing syntactically loud. Is a restricted explicit-dyn construct even workable? Yes — D003 shows Rust already enforces a coherent dyn-compatible subset (no Self:Sized methods, no generic dispatchable methods, no associated consts, restricted receivers), proving that a type system can carve dynamic dispatch down to a representation-coherent core. Going stricter than Rust (dyn only via an explicit construct, never via silent coercion; cost visible at the call site) merely completes the transparency contract Rust started. Finally, F009 supports settling dispatch in the frontend: the corpus's IR-design finding is that facts must be preserved long enough for the optimizer to use them, and monomorphization is the degenerate best case — callee identity is resolved before the backend is even consulted, so nothing fragile needs to survive lowering. The honest form of my claim is not 'dynamic dispatch is always slow' (a pattern the corpus's own misleading-comparisons discipline warns against, cf. the refuted 'exceptions are always slow' shape) — it is that mono-only makes cost unconditional and legible, while every rival model makes cost conditional on recovery machinery this corpus documents as fragile.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Core positive case: Rust monomorphization compiles generics to concrete type-specific code with runtime behavior like non-generic code — the unconditional fast path my position rests on. Its negative scope (code bloat/compile time unquantified) is conceded below.

Documents the cost of the alternative: dyn-Trait calls use runtime vtable dispatch, add lookup cost, and prevent inlining — justifying making dynamic dispatch explicit and visibly priced rather than an ambient mechanism.

Feasibility proof for the escape hatch: Rust's dyn-compatibility rules show a type system can restrict dynamic dispatch to an object-safe subset with a coherent runtime representation, which is exactly the 'restricted, explicit dyn construct' my position requires.

Characterizes the main hybrid rival: .NET specializes value types but shares reference-type generic bodies via object references, and preserved reflection metadata constrains closed-world assumptions — i.e., the hybrid buys code-size savings at the price of representation uniformity and open-world hazards an optimizer-first core should not default into.

Shows devirtualization exists but is speculative, guarded, revertible, and option/LTO-dependent — so a design that leans on devirtualization to recover from pervasive dynamic dispatch has a non-transparent cost model; mono-only needs no such heroic recovery.

Codegen units and LTO boundaries can inhibit optimization even in Rust — evidence that recovery-by-optimizer is fragile across compilation boundaries, strengthening the case for resolving dispatch statically in the frontend where boundaries cannot break it.

ELF semantic interposition can block interprocedural assumptions for exported symbols even when code looks statically known — a second documented way conditional recovery fails silently, and a reason the language must not rely on the backend to erase dispatch costs.

Julia's model recovers performance only under type stability, concrete containers, and function barriers — heuristic and workload-dependent, the opposite of performance transparency. Its negative scope (over-specialization raises compile-time/cache pressure) also honestly cuts against me and is listed as a risk.

Julia's multiple dispatch coexists with specialization but is constrained by world age, method redefinition, and unlimited-case blowup — documenting the optimizer-assumption instability that open dynamic dispatch imports, which a mono-only core avoids by construction.

Establishes that the corpus's best-attested optimizer enablers are language-level statically-proven facts (exclusivity/non-interference) — supporting the general design principle that the frontend should hand the optimizer resolved facts, of which monomorphized callee identity is an instance.

Alias information's value is proving non-interference around writes; I use it for the inference that an opaque indirect call forces conservative interference assumptions and that inlining (which D002 says dyn blocks) is what lets these facts propagate. The card does not itself discuss call boundaries — this linkage is partially inferential and flagged as such.

IR should preserve source facts long enough to optimize them; monomorphization is the limiting best case — dispatch targets are resolved before lowering, so no fragile metadata must survive the pipeline for the common case to be fast.

concessions: Every dispatch-specific card I cite (D001-D010) is direct-extraction from documentation, provisional and not adversarially verified; the F-series is workflow-confirmed but is about aliasing/IR, not dispatch. My position's evidentiary base is therefore weaker-grade than the corpus's aliasing findings.

The corpus contains zero quantitative benchmarks (caveats.md: documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude, code-size impact, compile-time cost'). I cannot prove monomorphization's runtime win outweighs its code-size and compile-time costs — D001 explicitly flags those tradeoffs as uncovered.

D005 shows a hybrid specialize-value-types/share-reference-types model working in a mature production runtime (.NET). The evidence does not force monomorphization-only; a hybrid is a live, evidenced alternative, and my rejection of it rests on the transparency principle, not on demonstrated performance superiority.

D007 shows backends can genuinely reduce dynamic-dispatch cost (devirtualization, indirect inlining). My argument is about predictability and unconditionality of cost, not that dynamic dispatch is always slow — the corpus's misleading-comparisons file warns against exactly that overreach pattern.

D002's cost claims come from the Rust Book, a pedagogical source; it does not specify ABI or devirtualization rules, and LLVM does devirtualize some dyn calls in practice — a nuance my corpus does not cover.
The link from dyn dispatch to lost aliasing/non-interference optimization (F002 + D002) is my inference; no card directly states that indirect calls pessimize alias analysis.
'Stricter than Rust' is beyond the evidence: D003 evidences Rust's exact dyn-compatibility line as workable; no card evidences that tightening further is beneficial or even acceptable.
risks: Code-size and compile-time blowup: monomorphization duplicates code per instantiation (D001 negative scope, unquantified), and D009's negative scope shows over-specialization raising compile-time and cache pressure in Julia — an analogous i-cache/binary-bloat hazard for a mono-only core, potentially severe for large generic libraries.

Separate compilation and stable ABI: monomorphization requires generic bodies available (source or IR) at every instantiation site. D004 shows compilation-unit boundaries already inhibit optimization, and D008 shows dynamic-linking semantics constrain cross-boundary assumptions; the corpus offers no evidenced model (e.g., Swift-style resilience/dictionary passing) for shipping binary-only generic libraries under mono-only. This may be the position's fatal flaw for an ecosystem with dynamic libraries.

Expressiveness cliffs: D003-style object-safety restrictions exclude useful patterns (generic methods in dyn interfaces, etc.); a mono-only core also rules out polymorphic recursion and truly heterogeneous abstraction unless the restricted dyn construct is good enough — if it is too restricted, users will build worse ad-hoc dispatch (function-pointer tables) with no compiler support.

Interop pressure: the dossier's interop targets (JVM/.NET, Python extension APIs, GPU runtimes) are dispatch-dynamic or metadata-reified worlds; a mono-only core pushes all boundary complexity into the single dyn construct, and there is no corpus evidence it can carry that load.

Overcorrection risk: by making dynamic dispatch syntactically painful, the language may push programmers to monomorphize even where a vtable would be globally cheaper (icache, build time), and the corpus gives no data to calibrate where that crossover sits.
evidenceGaps: Quantitative benchmarks are entirely absent: runtime speedup of monomorphized vs vtable/dictionary dispatch, code-size multipliers, i-cache effects, and compile-time cost per instantiation. Without at least representative measurements, this debate cannot be settled — only default-risk-allocated.

Swift's witness-table/dictionary-passing model with ABI resilience is the strongest counter-model to mono-only (generic code across stable binary boundaries) and is explicitly marked 'Open' in the runtime/ABI dossier; no cards cover it. Closing this gap is mandatory before mono-only can win, because the separate-compilation risk above is exactly what Swift's design addresses.

Devirtualization effectiveness in practice: D007 documents GCC mechanisms but nothing measures how often devirtualization succeeds/reverts on real workloads; if success rates were shown to be high and stable, my 'conditional recovery is fragile' argument weakens materially.
HotSpot inline caches / guarded devirtualization for a JIT-based rival model are marked open in the dossier; F007 covers escape analysis only.
C++ template instantiation build-time/code-bloat evidence (the closest large-scale mono-only precedent) is absent from the corpus.

No evidenced design for how a mono-only language ships pre-compiled generic libraries or handles plugin/dynamic-loading scenarios (D008 poses the boundary problem; nothing in the corpus solves it for monomorphization).

Card series N001-N006, A001-A010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 named in the debate ground rules were not present in the five files I was directed to read (only F001-F009 and D001-D010 appear there). If those cards exist elsewhere in the corpus, they must be surfaced to all debaters before a decision; if they do not exist, the ground rules overstate the available evidence.
No evidence on the ergonomic/soundness consequences of going stricter than Rust's dyn-compatibility line — the 'stricter' half of my assigned position is currently pure design conjecture.

result.position: Advocate 2: The performance core should adopt closed-world whole-program compilation — sealed-by-default type hierarchies, mandatory LTO-style whole-program visibility, monomorphized generics, and a hard ban on symbol interposition, reflection over core types, and dynamic loading inside the core (openness relegated to an explicit, marked boundary layer).
result.coreArgument: An optimizer-first language must make dispatch a compile-time-provable fact by default, and the corpus shows the only sound way to do that is to close the world. The chain: (1) Dynamic dispatch has a documented optimizer cost precisely because it blocks inlining and downstream optimization (D002) — this is the problem to eliminate, not merely mitigate. (2) Compilers demonstrably eliminate it — devirtualization to direct calls, indirect inlining after targets become known — but only under cross-module visibility: GCC ties these to LTO/IPA (D007), and Rust documents that codegen-unit separation inhibits optimization while LTO enables whole-program analysis (D004). Mandatory LTO-style visibility simply bakes in the optimization-favorable side of a tradeoff the corpus already documents. (3) The documented blockers of that proof are exactly the features my position bans: ELF semantic interposition restricts IPA, inlining, and propagation even for statically-known exported code (D008); .NET's preserved reflection metadata "may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted" — the card's own negative scope argues for restricting it (D005); Julia's method redefinition and world age "complicate optimizer assumptions" (D010). (4) Soundness is the decisive point, since this debate forbids sacrificing it: -fno-semantic-interposition is documented as an *assumption* that interposed definitions preserve semantics (D008) — i.e., open-world devirtualization is either conservative or trust-based. Banning interposition/loading/reflection from the core converts an unsound assumption into an enforced invariant; free devirtualization becomes legal by construction rather than by trust or by revertible speculation (D007's speculative devirtualization "may be reverted"). (5) Sealed-by-default supplies the source-level finality facts the devirtualizer consumes (D006 for the semantic construct, D007 for a compiler consuming finality/whole-program facts), and monomorphization extends the same guarantee to generics: concrete-code runtime behavior (D001), with D004 showing why its cross-unit limits vanish under mandatory whole-program visibility. (6) The rival model — open dispatch with heuristic recovery — is documented as conditional: Julia's performance depends on type stability, function barriers, and specialization heuristics that are workload-dependent (D009, D010), the same "conditional recovery vs source-level guarantee" pattern the corpus flags for HotSpot escape analysis (F007) and that the misleading-comparisons discipline tells us to keep distinct. A performance-transparent language should choose guarantees: under my model every call site is either provably direct or explicitly written as dynamic, so the cost model is readable from source — ugly, verbose, and honest, exactly as the mandate demands.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes the problem: dyn-Trait dispatch carries vtable lookup cost and prevents inlining of the method body, blocking further optimization — the cost closed-world compilation exists to eliminate by default.

Shows the mechanism my position maximizes: GCC documents devirtualization to direct calls, indirect inlining once targets are known, and LTO/IPA as the visibility enablers. Its negative scope (speculative devirtualization may be reverted) also supports preferring provable devirtualization over open-world speculation for a performance-transparent language.

Direct documentation that open-world linking semantics (ELF semantic interposition) block IPA/inlining/propagation even for statically-known code, and that opting out is an unproven semantic assumption — banning interposition from the core removes this blocker soundly rather than by trust.

Documents that compilation-unit separation inhibits optimization while LTO enables whole-program analysis — evidence that dispatch/generics optimization depends on visibility boundaries, which mandatory LTO-style compilation fixes by design (at documented link-time cost).

C# sealed shows a language can carry semantic finality at the source level — the fact a devirtualizer needs. Cited strictly for the construct's existence; the optimizer benefit is inferred only via D007's separate compiler evidence.

.NET's preserved generic/reflection metadata 'may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted' — the card's own negative scope is evidence for excluding reflection from a core that wants closed-world reasoning.

Rust monomorphization gives generics runtime behavior like concrete code — the static-specialization half of my model, with tradeoffs (bloat, cross-crate limits) that whole-program visibility per D004 is positioned to reduce, though bloat remains unquantified.

The strongest rival (dynamic/multiple dispatch with JIT-style recovery) is documented as conditional: Julia performance requires type stability, concrete containers, and function-barrier discipline, and is heuristic and workload-dependent — failing the performance-transparency criterion.

Julia's world age and method redefinition — open-world mutability of the dispatch table — are documented as complicating optimizer assumptions, direct evidence that runtime openness taxes the optimizer.

Pattern evidence from HotSpot: runtime/heuristic performance recovery is conditional on inlining, profiles, and implementation details, not a source-level guarantee — supporting the principle that an optimizer-first core should encode guarantees in semantics rather than hope for recovery.

Supporting principle: optimization benefits from preserving high-level source facts down to the optimizer (Swift SIL). Sealed/finality/closed-world facts are exactly such facts, and mandatory whole-program visibility is what keeps them consumable.

concessions: The sealed-to-devirtualization link is an inference across two cards from different toolchains: D006's fetched Microsoft source explicitly does NOT claim devirtualization benefit, and D007 is GCC-specific and option-dependent. No single card shows finality annotations producing measured devirtualization wins.

All D-cards are direct-extraction and provisional, not adversarially verified; my entire dispatch-specific case rests on that weaker layer, while the workflow-confirmed F-cards mostly concern aliasing, not dispatch (caveats.md notes the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection).

D007 shows open-world compilers already recover much dispatch cost via speculative guarded devirtualization plus profiles; the corpus contains no evidence quantifying the marginal runtime benefit of a total closed-world ban over speculation+PGO. My position's headline gain has no measured magnitude anywhere in the corpus (caveats.md: documentation evidence does not quantify performance).

Whole-program compilation has documented costs I inherit: longer link times and loss of the compile-speed side of the codegen-unit tradeoff (D004), plus LTO/IPA compile-time/memory/code-size costs (D007 negative scope). Monomorphization bloat is likewise real and unquantified (D001 negative scope).

D005 shows a credible middle path exists: .NET specializes value types while sharing reference-type bodies with runtime metadata — evidence that closed-world maximalism is not the only coherent design, and my position must beat that hybrid on grounds the corpus cannot yet measure.

Banning dynamic loading/reflection from the core does not make those needs vanish; the interop dossier lists dynamic linking, JVM/.NET, and Python extension APIs as real targets, and my position is only viable with a well-specified boundary layer the corpus does not yet describe.
risks: Soundness inversion at the boundary: closed-world devirtualization is sound only if the world is actually closed. Any escape hatch (FFI callback tables, deserialization of core-type vtables, later-added plugin support) that leaks openness into the core silently converts an optimization into miscompilation — the exact failure mode D008 warns about with -fno-semantic-interposition, and the one outcome this debate's rules forbid.

Build scalability: mandatory LTO-style visibility may make edit-compile cycles and large-program builds prohibitive (D004 documents the direction of the cost; the corpus gives no scale data), which could push users to split programs across the open boundary and forfeit the model's benefits in practice.

Code-size and i-cache blowup from mandatory monomorphization plus aggressive inlining (D001 negative scope; D009's over-specialization warning is the same failure in a dynamic setting) — could regress real workloads despite better per-call-site codegen.

Ecosystem rigidity: sealed-by-default plus no dynamic loading means no plugins, hot-patching, or independently-shipped extensions in the core; every extension forces whole-program recompilation. If the boundary layer is awkward, the 'core' shrinks to a niche.

Evidence-base drift: D004/D007/D008 are compiler-option documentation, flagged as version-sensitive (caveats.md); the interposition and LTO behaviors my argument leans on could change across toolchain versions.
evidenceGaps: No quantitative evidence anywhere in the corpus for devirtualization speedups, LTO/IPA compile-time and memory costs at scale, or monomorphization code-size bloat — caveats.md states documentation evidence does not quantify magnitude. Closing this requires benchmarks before my position can win.

Head-to-head comparison missing: guaranteed closed-world devirtualization vs open-world speculative devirtualization + PGO (D007) vs JIT class-hierarchy-analysis-with-deoptimization (HotSpot) — the dossier explicitly lists HotSpot, Swift, and modern C++ evidence as open. Swift witness tables and HotSpot CHA+deopt are the strongest known counter-models (open world with invalidation) and are unexamined here.

The debate brief references card series N001-N006, A001-A010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but none of those cards appear in the five files I was directed to read; any supporting or contradicting evidence in those series is unexamined in this case.

No corpus evidence on boundary design: how a closed core soundly coexists with the dossier's interop targets (C/C++, Python extension APIs, dynamic system libraries) without leaking openness inward — this is the load-bearing precondition for my soundness claim and is currently pure design speculation.

No direct evidence that source-level finality (sealed) is consumed by LLVM specifically (e.g., whole-program devirtualization / type metadata); D006+D007 span C# semantics and GCC mechanisms, and the target backend is LLVM — flagged as an inference gap.

No evidence on developer-workflow cost of losing incremental/separate compilation in practice (build-farm requirements, debug-cycle latency), which is the most likely practical veto of mandatory whole-program compilation.

result.position: Fully static AOT: every optimizer-relevant fact must be a compiler-checked source contract. No guards, no deoptimization, no warmup, no profile-derived facts. Predictability is itself a performance feature, and for an optimizer-first, LLVM-targeted language it is the only channel that is simultaneously sound, unconditional, and transparent.
result.coreArgument: The debate is not about which system knows more facts; it is about the channel through which the optimizer learns them, because the information demand is identical. Dynamic runtimes converge on recovering, via profiling and guards, exactly what static languages state in types and contracts (J001) — the fact set is the same, only the delivery differs. Given that, compare the two channels on the three axes this language's charter fixes: soundness (non-negotiable), performance, and transparency. (1) Soundness: LLVM IR's optimizer-facing fact vocabulary — `noalias` attributes, `alias.scope`/`noalias` metadata, allocation-like `noalias` returns — makes violation undefined behavior (F003, F004).
