The rival ownership position has equally strong corpus support (F001, C004): Rust's &mut exclusivity provably delivers non-interference in safe code with far less annotation. The corpus does not adjudicate explicit-regions vs ownership-inference; my case rests partly on the charter's tolerance for verbosity and on separate-compilation/lowering-fidelity arguments the corpus does not quantify.

No card quantifies performance benefit anywhere (F002 negative scope; caveats.md: documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude'). I cannot claim explicit annotations yield X% over inferred facts.

For pointer-rich dynamic structures, declared region disjointness likely becomes undecidable to verify directly; SPARK handles exactly this frontier by falling back to ownership transfer on access types (F006). At that frontier my position partially collapses into the ownership rival — honest reading: explicit regions and ownership are complements, and my strongest form embeds an ownership sub-discipline as one verification tactic.
risks: Soundness cliff: every emitted noalias/alias.scope fact is UB if false (F003). If the verifier has any hole — unsafe escape hatches, FFI, callbacks, signals, finalizers (all open questions in implementation/memory-object-model.md) — first-class annotations become a miscompilation amplifier, worse than a conservative inference scheme that simply loses optimizations.

Granularity ceiling: named regions at function-signature granularity may be too coarse (whole-array vs element-level disjointness, loop-carried non-interference). Escaping the ceiling means dependent/indexed regions, for which the corpus offers zero precedent and verification cost is unknown.

Verifier burden becomes the real language: checking declared disjointness may require SPARK-scale proof machinery; F006's negative scope warns of usability costs, and even for generated code the generator (human or LLM) must be able to discharge obligations reliably.

Lowering fragility: F003's negative scope notes metadata must survive lowering and passes must actually consume it; a syntactically perfect region system is worthless if scoped-noalias metadata is dropped or ignored mid-pipeline. This is an implementation risk the corpus flags but does not measure.

Annotation drift and virality: effect signatures propagate through call graphs; any change to a callee's write set ripples upward. In a generated language this is regeneration cost rather than human pain, but it can still make separate compilation brittle — the very property I claim as a strength.
evidenceGaps: Card ranges N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and J001-J005 named as citable in the debate brief were not present in the six files I was permitted to read; if they exist elsewhere in the corpus they may materially change any position and must be surfaced before a decision.

No evidence cards on real region/effect-system languages or IRs: DPJ, Cyclone, Koka, Halide, ISPC, Chapel, Mojo, MLIR (caveats.md explicitly lists most of these as uncovered). My position cannot win without at least one adversarially-verified card showing a declared-region system delivering optimizer value in practice.
No quantified comparison of optimization outcomes: explicit annotations vs ownership-derived noalias vs pure analysis, on any benchmark. F002/F001 establish direction only.
No evidence on how reliably LLVM passes preserve and consume alias.scope/noalias metadata through full -O2/-O3 pipelines (F003 flags survival as a condition; nothing measures it).

No evidence on verifier feasibility/cost for declared disjointness at scale (SPARK proof effort, proportion of contracts dischargeable automatically), which is the load-bearing soundness mechanism of my position.

Memory object model prerequisites are unanswered (implementation/memory-object-model.md): object identity, provenance, interior-pointer stability, uninitialized bytes. Region semantics cannot be defined, let alone verified, before these are settled.
Corpus has no coverage of how region/effect annotations interact with exceptions/unwinding (F008 is about EH representation only) or with FFI boundaries, both of which puncture declared write sets.

result.position: General memory plus checkable contracts: keep pointer/slice flexibility as the base memory model, and surface disjointness, layout, alignment, and contiguity as first-class, checkable annotations that lower to LLVM's existing legality machinery (the C+restrict+pragmas direction, hardened so every contract is either statically verified, runtime-checked, or explicitly unsafe — never a silent trust-me pragma, because soundness may not be sacrificed).
result.coreArgument: The corpus's strongest, adversarially-verified evidence is precisely about the facts my position surfaces, and precisely about the consumption path my position lowers to. First, what the optimizer actually needs is non-interference around writes, not array-ness per se: F002 confirms the optimization value of alias information is specifically proving a location is not modified, and F001 confirms that across Rust, Fortran, and SPARK the central optimizer enablers are language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules — which are contracts, however each language spells them. Second, the target IR natively consumes contract-shaped facts: F003 (noalias attributes, alias.scope metadata expressing disjoint access sets) and F004 (allocation-like noalias returns, strictly stronger than C99 restrict) show LLVM already has optimizer-facing encodings for exactly the annotations I propose, so a contracts language has the shortest and best-attested lowering path of any position in this debate. Third, the production consumer works this way today: A005 documents that LLVM's loop and SLP vectorizers are driven by legality facts — alias/dependence information, reductions, trip counts, FP flags — with runtime pointer checks as fallback; that runtime-check-and-version mechanism is the existing, deployed template for making my 'checkable annotation' semantics sound rather than UB-on-lie. Fourth, generality: the restrictive alternatives concede their own limits in their negative scopes — A003 (affine forms apply only where programs fit affine restrictions), A006 (Halide is a purity-constrained DSL 'not automatically transferable to a general language'), A008 (Futhark requires regular arrays). An optimizer-first general-purpose language must still express irregular, pointer-heavy structures (the dossier's own open question), and general-memory-plus-contracts degrades gracefully there: absent contracts you get conservative correct code, not a rejected program, and dense-array kernels can still be fully annotated. Fifth, even the structured-IR camp bottoms out in my model: A001 shows MLIR's MemRef — the memory layer under Linalg/Affine — is itself general memory annotated with strides, offsets, memory spaces, and explicit distinct_objects no-alias contracts, with the same verifier/runtime obligations and aliasing views I must handle; structure does not eliminate the contract problem, it merely relocates it. Sixth, soundness is achievable, not hypothetical: F006 confirms SPARK demonstrates a verification-oriented route to restricting mutable aliasing via ownership and checkable policy, and F001/F005 show Fortran and Rust each made contract-like non-interference sound at the language level; my position adopts that discipline (static discharge where possible, A005-style runtime checks otherwise, unsafe-marked promises as a last resort with F003's UB consequences confined to an explicit opt-in). Finally, the design brief tolerates ugliness: verbose per-parameter disjointness/alignment/contiguity annotations are an ergonomic cost, not a soundness cost, and they make performance transparent at exactly the site where the optimization is decided — which is the language's stated first principle. Honest framing per the corpus's own misleading-comparisons discipline: I claim non-interference contracts are the best-attested enabler in this corpus, not the proven-strongest enabler overall, and I claim the shortest lowering path, not a quantified performance win.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Workflow-confirmed that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing, C restrict) are central optimizer enablers — these are all contract-style facts, which is exactly what my position surfaces as annotations. Its caveat (restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions) is why I require checkability.

Confirms the optimization value of alias information is specifically proving non-interference around writes. This means the payload the optimizer needs is a disjointness/non-modification fact — expressible as an annotation on general memory — not intrinsically an array or loop structure.

Confirms LLVM IR already provides optimizer-facing encodings (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) for disjoint access sets. My annotations have a direct, existing lowering target; competing positions must build or bypass this layer anyway. Its caveat (soundness conditional on true frontend obligations, metadata surviving lowering) defines my checking obligation.

Confirms LLVM noalias-return is stronger than C99 restrict — evidence that the IR contract vocabulary already exceeds C's source vocabulary, so a contracts-first source language can expose strictly more than restrict without inventing new backend machinery.

Confirms Fortran's dummy-argument no-conflicting-reference rule is an optimization-friendly language-level contract on otherwise fairly general memory — a long-lived existence proof that contract semantics (not just structured arrays) deliver the aliasing guarantees, with POINTER/TARGET as the documented escape-hatch cost.

Confirms SPARK's verification-oriented route: restricted mutable aliasing, ownership policy on access types. This is my steelman against the 'pragmas are lies' objection — contracts can be statically discharged, so 'checkable annotations' is an attested design point, not wishful thinking.

(Provisional) LLVM's vectorizers consume exactly my proposed fact set — alias/dependence facts, reductions, trip counts, FP flags — and already implement runtime pointer checks with fallback, the deployed mechanism for making unproven disjointness annotations sound via check-and-version rather than UB.

(Provisional) MLIR MemRef shows the structured-IR ecosystem's own memory layer is general memory annotated with shape/stride/offset/memory-space metadata plus explicit distinct_objects no-alias contracts and verifier/runtime obligations — i.e., 'general memory plus contracts' is the substrate even under Linalg/Affine, so my position is the load-bearing layer, not the discarded one.

(Provisional, cited for its negative scope) Affine's tractability benefits apply only where programs fit affine restrictions — direct evidence that a restrictive-arrays-only language forfeits coverage of semi-affine and irregular code that a general-memory-plus-contracts language retains.

(Provisional, cited for its negative scope) Halide's schedule-layer benefits come with purity/update constraints and are 'not automatically transferable to a general language' — limits the strongest competing evidence to the DSL setting, supporting general memory as the base with structured/scheduled subsets layered on top.

(Provisional, cited for its negative scope) Futhark requires regular arrays and its layout/codegen claims need further evidence — the regular-array position's coverage and payoff are both narrower and less verified than the aliasing-contract evidence backing my position.

(Provisional) Fortran DO CONCURRENT shows independence-intent contracts on loops are 'necessary rather than sufficient' — compilers still run dependence analysis and may add runtime checks. This supports my architecture (annotations feeding legality analysis with conservative fallback) and honestly bounds what annotations alone deliver.

concessions: The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, not by established importance (caveats.md; misleading-comparisons entry 1). My position looks strongest partly because the verified corpus over-covers exactly the facts contracts express; array/layout/loop-structure evidence (A-cards) is provisional and thinner.

Restriction demonstrably buys analysis tractability I cannot fully match: A003 says affine-form restriction makes dependence analysis efficient and reliable, and A002 says preserved structured ops with indexing maps and iterator kinds are what tiling/fusion/promotion are built on. Recovering that structure from annotated general pointer code is harder, and the corpus contains no card showing contracts on general memory reach parity for loop-nest transformations (tiling, interchange, fusion).

The historical C+restrict+pragma practice my position descends from is unsound as practiced: F001's caveat (programmer truthfulness) and F003's caveat (UB on violated metadata) mean I must pay a real, unquantified cost — static verification (F006's SPARK route, which F006's own caveat says may carry usability costs) or runtime checks (A005) — to satisfy this debate's soundness rule.

Vectorization and parallelization from annotations are conditional, never guaranteed: A005 (cost models, hints, fallbacks) and A010 (restrictions necessary but not sufficient) both say the optimizer may still decline or serialize; performance transparency of outcomes is weaker under my position than under designs where the transformation is explicit program structure (A006).

Some optimizations attested for restricted designs have no contract-based counterpart in this corpus: Futhark's uniqueness/consumption copy-avoidance (A008) and Halide's algorithm/schedule separation (A006) are capabilities I cannot currently cite an equivalent for.

No card quantifies any performance magnitude for any position (caveats.md: documentation evidence does not quantify performance, code size, or compile-time cost); my 'shortest lowering path' claim is architectural, not benchmarked.
risks: Contract-lie risk: if any annotation path remains uncheckable and un-gated, violated noalias-style facts are UB at the IR level (F003); a single unsound escape hatch reintroduces the classic restrict failure mode. Mitigation (verify or runtime-check everything) may be expensive or, per F006's caveat, hurt usability enough that programmers omit annotations and performance silently degrades.

Runtime-check cost risk: A005's runtime pointer checks and multi-versioning have unquantified overhead and code-size cost; kernels with many annotated pointers may need combinatorial checks, eroding the wins the annotations were meant to buy.

Fact-survival risk: F003's caveat and the dossier's open question ('how to validate that MLIR/LLVM actually consume the preserved facts') apply most sharply to my position, since it relies on metadata/attributes surviving lowering and actually being consumed rather than on structure the pipeline cannot drop.

Ceiling risk: by committing to general memory, the language may permanently forgo polyhedral/schedule-level transformations that A002/A003/A006 suggest need preserved structure — the optimizer-first goal could be undermined at exactly the dense-loop workloads where the biggest wins live.

Annotation-burden risk: performance becomes contingent on programmers writing verbose contracts everywhere; unannotated code is conservatively compiled, so real-world performance may bimodally split between expert-annotated and default code, which is hard to detect without tooling.

Corpus-bias risk: the debate could be won on selection-biased evidence (misleading-comparisons entry 1); if later phases verify array/layout/schedule evidence to the same standard as the aliasing F-cards, the balance could shift toward structured designs.
evidenceGaps: Cards N001–N006, D001–D010, C001–C006, and J001–J005 do not appear in the five files I was assigned to read; whatever they cover (plausibly numerics, dispatch/dynamic runtimes, concurrency, JIT/managed evidence) is invisible to this argument and could strengthen or weaken it.

No benchmark-grade evidence comparing restrict/noalias-annotated general code against structured-array or scheduled-DSL implementations of the same kernels; caveats.md explicitly notes documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude. This is the single most decision-relevant gap.

No card measures the runtime and code-size overhead of check-and-version fallbacks (A005) or of dynamically checking disjointness/contiguity/alignment contracts at call boundaries — the cost of making my position sound is unpriced.

No verified evidence on whether LLVM-level legality analysis over annotated general pointer code can drive loop-nest transformations (tiling, interchange, fusion, promotion) at parity with affine/structured IR; A002/A003 are provisional and one-sided, and the dossier flags 'validate that MLIR/LLVM actually consume the preserved facts' as open.

No evidence that layout facts beyond aliasing — alignment, contiguity, stride regularity — have sound, consumed, checkable encodings on the LLVM path (e.g., align attributes, assumptions); the F-cards cover disjointness only, and A001's layout metadata is MLIR-side and provisional.

No evidence on static dischargeability of disjointness/layout contracts in a general-purpose (non-SPARK-subset) language: F006 shows the route exists for a restricted verification language, but its transfer cost to a performance language is explicitly flagged as unknown in F006's own scope limits.

Slices/views aliasing semantics are an open dossier question with no confirmed card: how to keep view aliasing explicit while preserving vectorization legality is unresolved for every position, including mine.

result.position: Ownership and borrowing should be baked into the type system, with exclusive mutable references (&mut-style) as the default for all safe references. Every safe reference carries a statically proven aliasing fact; shared references are immutable by default; mutable aliasing requires an explicit, typed opt-out. This is the Rust/SPARK direction, chosen because it is the only surveyed mechanism where the non-interference facts the optimizer consumes are compiler-verified rather than programmer-promised.
result.coreArgument: The argument has four steps, each grounded in the corpus. (1) Non-interference around writes is the well-attested optimizer enabler in this corpus: when the compiler can prove a location is not modified through another path, it can promote to registers, eliminate redundant loads and dead stores, and reorder memory operations (F002), and language-level non-aliasing rules are exactly what make those transformations sound without global analysis (F001). (2) Among the mechanisms the corpus surveys — Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK ownership, and C/C++ restrict-style contracts — only type-system ownership makes the fact statically checked per reference. F001's own scope note records that restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions,' and Fortran's model is a set of rules the compiler 'may assume' the program obeys, riddled with POINTER/TARGET/coarray exceptions (F005). For a language whose ground rules forbid sacrificing soundness, unchecked promises that become UB when false (F003) are disqualified as the *default* mechanism; a borrow checker discharges the obligation at compile time instead. That two independent designs — Rust for systems programming and SPARK for verification (F006) — converged on ownership-with-transfer and exclusive mutation suggests this is a design attractor, not a single-language accident. (3) The LLVM target makes this concrete: LLVM already provides the consumer-side encodings — noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias metadata (F003), and the especially strong allocation-like noalias return contract (F004) — but the corpus is emphatic that these are sound only if the frontend's obligations are actually true (F003 negative scope; misleading-comparisons #5: 'IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts'; memory-object-model.md: do not emit noalias 'without a matching source contract and verifier obligation'). A type system that proves exclusivity IS the matching source contract plus verifier obligation, applied pervasively rather than at hand-annotated sites. (4) Ownership pays a second, independent dividend in concurrency: memory models forbid a concrete list of classic optimizations on possibly-shared memory — no introduced writes, no re-loading a read, no caching atomics in registers, fences as motion barriers (C003). Rust demonstrates that Send/Sync auto-traits plus exclusive &mut make sharing a type-level fact, so the optimizer can treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless the types say otherwise (C004), and data-race freedom — the precondition of the cross-language DRF-SC baseline (C001) — holds by construction in the safe subset, sidestepping the UB-vs-bounded-races dilemma (C002) for safe code. No competing mechanism in the corpus (Fortran rules, restrict, escape analysis) buys back both the sequential noalias facts and the concurrency freedoms with one construct. Finally, F009 (Swift SIL) shows how to cash this in: keep ownership visible in a high-level IR so our own passes exploit exclusivity before lowering conservatively-correct noalias facts to LLVM. The classic costs of this position are ergonomic — borrow-checker friction, verbose lifetime annotation — and this language's charter explicitly permits paying ergonomic costs; what it forbids is unsoundness, which is precisely the failure mode of every unchecked alternative.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules are central optimizer enablers, names Rust safe &mut exclusivity as making overlapping compute(&x, &mut x) impossible in safe code, and — crucially for my position — its negative scope disqualifies the main rival (restrict-like contracts) as 'depend[ing] on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions.'

Specifies the payoff mechanism: proving non-modification through other paths enables register promotion, read/write elimination, and reordering. Exclusive-by-default references maximize the program surface over which this proof exists, because every safe reference carries the fact rather than only annotated or procedure-boundary sites.

Shows LLVM has ready consumers for exclusivity facts (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) and that violating them is UB — which is the soundness argument for a checked frontend: only a static proof discipline makes pervasive emission of this metadata safe.

noalias returns are an especially strong, allocation-like contract that C99 restrict cannot even express; an ownership type system with move semantics naturally proves the 'fresh, disjoint from all caller-accessible storage' property for owned returns, unlocking a contract rival designs leave on the table.

Characterizes the main structured alternative — Fortran's dummy-argument and TARGET rules — as compiler-assumed rather than compiler-verified, and as complicated by POINTER/TARGET/coarray/host-association exceptions. This is my foil: procedure-boundary, unchecked rules deliver facts only where the programmer happens to be right.

SPARK independently converged on ownership-with-transfer-on-assignment, single read-write access at a time, and anti-aliasing of mutable parameters — evidence that exclusive mutation is a repeated design attractor for machine-checkable non-interference, not a Rust idiosyncrasy.

Weighs against the 'let the compiler infer it' alternative: HotSpot escape analysis yields only conditional, inlining- and profile-dependent elimination, explicitly not source-level guarantees. An optimizer-first language should prefer guaranteed, type-carried facts over best-effort recovery.

Swift SIL shows the implementation route: ownership and reference semantics kept visible in a high-level SSA IR enable optimizations LLVM alone would not see. Supports designing our pipeline so type-system exclusivity facts survive into our own optimizer before conservative lowering to LLVM noalias.

DRF-SC is the shared baseline contract across major languages; a type system that statically prevents data races in safe code gives us DRF — and therefore SC reasoning — by construction rather than by programmer discipline.

Languages must choose between UB races (maximal optimizer freedom, maximal blast radius) and bounded races (constrained optimization). Static race prevention in the safe subset largely dissolves this dilemma: the optimizer gets UB-grade freedom on safe code without exposing users to UB from ordinary code.

Enumerates the concrete optimizer freedoms lost whenever memory may be shared (no introduced writes, no re-loading, no register-caching across iterations, fence barriers). This quantifies-in-kind what ownership buys back: proving non-sharing re-legalizes these transformations.

Direct evidence that the ownership/borrowing mechanism, extended with Send/Sync-style auto traits, makes sharing a type-level fact so the optimizer can treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless types say otherwise — the double dividend unique to my position.

LLVM's graded atomic orderings define pass-by-pass freedoms; a type system that statically separates unshared memory (NotAtomic, fully optimizable) from shared memory (explicitly ordered atomics) maps cleanly onto this IR vocabulary at lowering time.

concessions: The framing 'strongest optimizer enabler' overstates the corpus: the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, not because relative importance was established across layout, dispatch, numerics, arrays, or DSL approaches (caveats.md; misleading-comparisons #1). Honest wording: non-interference is well-attested in the selected sources.

No card quantifies performance. F002's negative scope is explicit that the corpus establishes classes of enabled optimizations, not magnitudes; documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude, code-size impact, compile-time cost' (caveats.md). I cannot prove ownership-in-types outperforms Fortran-style rules or verified restrict on delivered speed.

The Rust-to-noalias lowering is not clean: unsafe code, interior mutability/UnsafeCell, raw pointers, shared references, and callbacks complicate it, and Rust's aliasing model is itself explanatory (Nomicon) and unfinalized, with future changes possible (misleading-comparisons #2; caveats.md; F001 negative scope). My position inherits the obligation to fully specify an aliasing model Rust itself has not finished specifying.

Any real language needs unsafe/FFI escape hatches, and inside them the statically-proven facts vanish; soundness of emitted metadata then rests on unchecked code and on unresolved questions about what unsafe code, callbacks, signals, finalizers, and FFI may invalidate (memory-object-model.md; F001 negative scope; C004 negative scope on incorrect manual Send/Sync impls being UB).

SPARK's restrictions are verification-oriented and 'may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs' (F006 negative scope). Our charter discounts ergonomics, but expressiveness cliffs that push whole data-structure classes into unsafe are not merely ergonomic — they erode the very fact-coverage my argument depends on.

Managed-runtime escape analysis (F007) shows some non-interference value is recoverable without any source-level mechanism at all — conditional and non-guaranteed, but nonzero, so the counterfactual baseline for my position is not zero optimization.
risks: Expressiveness cliff risk: doubly-linked structures, graphs, intrusive data structures, and callback-heavy code are the classic cases that force unsafe/raw-pointer escapes; if a large fraction of performance-critical code lives past the cliff, the pervasive-facts premise collapses and we paid the complexity cost for coverage we do not get. The corpus contains no measurement of this fraction.

Foundation-in-motion risk: emitting noalias soundly requires answers to object identity, provenance, interior-pointer stability across moves, and partial initialization (memory-object-model.md), on top of an aliasing model Rust has not finalized (caveats.md). Getting these wrong converts our strongest optimizer enabler into a miscompilation generator, and soundness is non-negotiable per the ground rules.

Metadata-survival risk: F003's negative scope notes LLVM metadata must both be true and survive lowering; the corpus says nothing about how reliably LLVM preserves and exploits scoped noalias metadata across its pass pipeline in practice.

One-way-door risk: baking exclusivity into every reference type is architecturally load-bearing; if later evidence favors region/effect systems, Fortran-style procedure contracts plus verification, or pure-value semantics (all currently unassessed in the corpus per caveats.md), retrofitting would be near-total redesign.

Complexity and compile-time risk: a borrow checker plus auto-trait machinery (C004) is a large, subtle static analysis; the corpus has zero cards on its implementation cost, compile-time impact, or defect history.

Concurrency residue: even with static race freedom in the safe subset, we still need a full atomics and memory model for the shared/unsafe boundary (C004 negative scope, C005) — ownership reduces but does not eliminate that design burden, and unsafe-code races would still need a UB-vs-bounded decision (C002).
evidenceGaps: Quantitative benefit: no card measures how much LLVM noalias/alias.scope facts improve realistic workloads, so the payoff that justifies the type-system cost is asserted in kind, not in magnitude (F002 negative scope). Closing this needs controlled benchmarks (e.g., noalias-on vs noalias-off builds).

Comparative evaluation: no card compares ownership-in-types head-to-head against alternatives — Fortran-style procedure-boundary rules, SPARK-style external verification of a simpler language, region/effect systems, or opt-in checked restrict — on optimization coverage, soundness burden, and expressiveness. The debate cannot be settled without at least a structured comparison.

Escape-hatch prevalence: no data on what fraction of performance-critical code in ownership languages requires unsafe/UnsafeCell/raw pointers, i.e., how much of the program actually carries the static facts.

LLVM exploitation reliability: the corpus confirms the encodings exist (F003, F004) but not that LLVM's pipeline preserves and profitably uses scoped noalias metadata at scale; the history of Rust toggling noalias emission due to LLVM bugs is not covered by any card and is flagged here as an unverified background claim needing sourcing.

Memory object model prerequisites: the open questions in memory-object-model.md (provenance, interior pointers across moves, uninitialized bytes, FFI invalidation) must be answered before per-reference exclusivity can be soundly lowered; no card yet addresses them.

Uncovered design space: the corpus has no verified coverage of Halide, ISPC, Futhark, Chapel, Mojo, MLIR, or pure/functional routes to non-interference (caveats.md), so a value-semantics or DSL alternative to ownership is entirely unassessed.

Missing card ranges: the debate brief references N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and J001-J005, but none of these appeared in the six files I was directed to read; only F001-F009 and C001-C006 were available. If those cards exist elsewhere in the corpus, they were not weighed in this argument.
Compile-time and implementation cost of a borrow checker: no evidence cards exist on checker complexity, compile-time overhead, or soundness-bug history in Rust/SPARK implementations.

result.position: Non-interference should be expressed as opt-in function-boundary contracts in the Fortran dummy-argument / C restrict direction: noalias and effect declarations attach to signatures, the type system stays simple, and the compiler assumes (or, where decidable, checks) conformance. The strongest form of this position is 'Fortran-grade': non-interference of writable parameters is the default rule at every procedure boundary, aliasing is the thing you opt INTO (a TARGET-like attribute), and a SPARK-style checked mode discharges the contracts where analysis or cheap runtime overlap checks can reach.
result.coreArgument:
The optimizer consumes non-interference facts at exactly one granularity: the function signature. LLVM's entire no-alias vocabulary is signature-attached — `noalias` parameter and return attributes, plus scoped metadata (F003, F004) — so a source language whose contract unit IS the signature has a lossless, one-to-one lowering path: source contract -> IR attribute, with a verifier obligation that is trivially matched per memory-object-model.md's rule ('do not emit noalias without a matching source contract and verifier obligation'). A pervasive ownership type system must instead reconstruct signature-level facts from a whole-program type discipline, and the corpus itself flags that this reconstruction is lossy and complicated in practice: 'Rust ownership automatically maps to LLVM noalias everywhere' is listed as a misleading claim, with unsafe code, interior mutability, raw pointers, shared references, and callbacks named as the complications (misleading-comparisons.jsonl line 2; F001 negative scope; caveats.md).

Second, the payoff we are chasing is specifically about writes through unknown paths at opaque boundaries. F002 enumerates the enabled optimizations — register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-write elimination, reordering — and locates the hazard precisely at 'modified through another path'. Intraprocedurally, LLVM/GCC alias and mod-ref analysis already recover much of this (F002 cites both). Where local analysis structurally fails is pointer parameters of unknown provenance and calls to opaque functions — which is exactly where signature contracts sit. The mechanism is aimed at the residual problem, not at re-proving what dataflow already proves.

Third, the corpus's strongest empirical exemplar of a whole-language optimization win from non-interference is Fortran, and Fortran is my position: dummy-argument rules are function-boundary contracts imposed implicitly at every call, and Flang documents that the compiler may optimize assuming conformance (F001, F005). F005 shows the design scales in a second dimension too: ordinary variables are outside pointer alias sets unless declared TARGET — aliasing as the marked case, non-interference as the default. This is decades of production evidence that a simple type system plus boundary contracts is sufficient for optimizer-enabling non-interference; no ownership types required.

Fourth, the soundness objection ('assumed conformance is a UB machine') is answerable within my position's own family. SPARK's anti-aliasing rule — two mutable parameters must never be aliased, checked, not assumed — is a function-boundary contract with verification (F001, F006). So the design space of signature contracts spans assumed (Fortran/restrict), statically checked where decidable (SPARK), and dynamically checked at function entry for checkable shapes like slices/arrays in checked builds. An optimizer-first language can default to checked-at-boundary and offer assumed-only as the explicit unsafe tier. Meanwhile, the supposedly sound alternative bottoms out in exactly the same trust move: Rust's Send/Sync and unsafe blocks are unverified programmer assertions whose violation is UB (C004 negative scope; F001 negative scope). Both designs have a trust boundary; mine puts it at the signature where it is auditable, greppable, and matches the IR contract one-to-one.

Fifth, decision-theoretic economy under the corpus's own discipline: no card quantifies the performance delta of any non-interference design (F002 negative scope; caveats.md — documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude'), and the corpus is admittedly selection-biased toward aliasing (misleading-comparisons.jsonl line 1). Given unquantified benefits, the reversible mechanism should win the opening position. Signature contracts are forward-compatible: a Rust-style checked ownership layer can be added later as one discharge mechanism for the same contracts (this is the SPARK ownership story — F006 — layered onto Ada, an existing simple-typed language). A pervasive exclusivity type system is a one-way door that reshapes every API and datatype before we have a single benchmark. The same argument extends to concurrency: C003 lists the optimizer freedoms lost when memory may be shared; signature-level effect/non-shared declarations are the same mechanism family and can carry those facts per-boundary without committing the whole type system, and F009's lesson — keep performance-critical semantics visible to the optimizer — is satisfied by contracts that ARE the visible IR-level facts.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Places Fortran dummy-argument restrictions and G++ __restrict__ alongside Rust exclusivity and SPARK anti-aliasing as co-equal, verified (3-0) optimizer enablers — establishing that function-boundary contracts are an attested route to the same class of non-interference facts, not a degraded substitute. Its negative scope ('restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions') is my central concession, but the same negative scope also excludes unsafe/raw-pointer/UnsafeCell from Rust's guarantee, showing the rival design carries an unverified trust boundary too.

The strongest single card for my position: Flang states compilers may optimize assuming conformance with dummy-argument aliasing rules (implicit boundary contracts), and TARGET makes aliasing opt-in — ordinary variables are excluded from pointer alias sets by default. This is production evidence that default-no-alias-at-boundaries plus a simple type system delivers optimizer-usable non-interference.

LLVM's optimizer-facing no-alias encodings are signature-attached attributes and scoped metadata. My source contracts are structurally isomorphic to the IR contract, giving the shortest sound lowering path; a type-system design must derive these same per-signature facts indirectly.

The `noalias` return attribute — stronger than C99 restrict, allocation-like disjointness — is a fact that naturally lives on a signature and has no obvious expression as a borrow/ownership type on the value alone. Signature contracts natively cover the strongest known IR-level no-alias fact; its negative scope (needs lifetime/provenance rules first) is shared by both positions via memory-object-model.md.

Defines the actual payoff (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering) as proving no writes through another path, and shows via LLVM/GCC docs that intraprocedural analysis already recovers much of this — so the marginal value of language design concentrates at opaque call boundaries, exactly where signature contracts attach. Its negative scope (no quantified magnitude) grounds my economy argument: choose the reversible mechanism when benefits are unquantified.

SPARK's anti-aliasing ('two mutable parameters should never be aliased') is a checked function-boundary contract, proving my position has a sound, verified variant — the choice between assumed and checked conformance is internal to the contract approach, so 'soundness may not be sacrificed' does not by itself force a pervasive ownership type system. Also shows ownership can arrive later as a layered discharge mechanism on a simple-typed base language. Its negative scope (usability costs) is conceded.

Cuts both ways and I claim the favorable half: Rust's data-race freedom rests on Send/Sync auto traits whose incorrect manual impls, plus unsafe code, are UB — i.e., the type-system route also bottoms out in unverified signature-adjacent assertions the compiler assumes. The difference from my position is placement of the trust boundary, not its existence.

Enumerates optimizer freedoms lost when memory is possibly shared (no introduced writes, no re-loading, no register caching across iterations). These are per-location, per-boundary facts; signature-level effect/non-shared declarations can restore them at boundaries without a whole-program sharing type discipline — extending my mechanism to the concurrency findings.

The design lesson is that performance-critical semantics must be visible to optimizer passes rather than buried. Signature contracts satisfy this maximally: the contract is literally the optimizer-visible fact, preserved by construction rather than re-derived through lowering from a type system.

concessions: Programmer truthfulness is the load-bearing wall of the assumed-conformance variant: F001's negative scope says verbatim that restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions', and F003 confirms violation is UB at the IR level. Under the debate's soundness constraint, the pure Fortran/restrict form is not defensible alone; my position survives only in its checked-or-tiered form (SPARK-style static checks plus runtime overlap checks where decidable, with assumed-only as an explicitly unsafe tier).

Rust's safe &mut exclusivity is the best-attested machine-checked non-interference guarantee in the corpus (F001, C004), and machine-checked beats audited-by-humans for soundness. If the future evidence shows annotation error rates are high in practice, the type-system position wins on the corpus's own soundness rule.

Fortran's clean story is not clean: POINTER/TARGET/coarray/host-association exceptions (F005 negative scope, caveats.md) show that opt-in contract regimes accrete escape hatches and permitted-aliasing carve-outs over time, eroding the 'simple type system' selling point.

Type-level sharing facts compose transitively for free (C004): a Send/Sync-style discipline propagates through generic code automatically, whereas signature effect declarations must be maintained transitively by hand or by inference machinery the corpus says nothing about.

SPARK's own caveat (F006 negative scope) — verification-oriented restrictions 'may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs' — applies to my checked variant, and the debate's ergonomics-may-be-sacrificed rule only partially absorbs this because annotation burden also affects annotation correctness.

The framing 'strongest optimizer enabler in the seed corpus' is itself flagged as selection-biased wording (misleading-comparisons.jsonl line 1; caveats.md): non-interference is well-attested in selected sources, not proven dominant across layout, dispatch, numerics, or concurrency.
risks: Silent miscompilation surface: every wrong or stale annotation in assumed mode is UB (F003), and unlike a type-system violation it produces no diagnostic — the failure mode is wrong answers in optimized builds. Runtime overlap checks only cover checkable shapes (contiguous ranges/slices); arbitrary pointer graphs at boundaries remain assumed.

Default-direction risk: if contracts are opt-in per annotation (restrict-style) rather than default-on at boundaries (Fortran-style), unannotated code is pessimized everywhere, inverting the optimizer-first goal; if they are default-on, all existing intuitions from C-family languages become hazards and every FFI boundary needs explicit aliasing opt-outs.

Annotation rot at scale: signature contracts are maintained facts, not derived facts; refactors that introduce aliasing do not fail to compile in assumed mode. No corpus evidence exists on real-world error rates for restrict-like annotations, so this risk is unbounded by current evidence.

Checked-variant coverage cliff: SPARK-style static anti-aliasing checking works in SPARK partly because the language also bans side-effecting expressions and restricts access types (F006) — porting only the check without those restrictions may leave large undecidable regions where the language must fall back to assumed mode, quietly reintroducing the soundness hole.

Concurrency extension is under-evidenced: extending signature contracts to non-shared/effect declarations (my C003 argument) has no direct corpus exemplar of a contract-based (non-type-based) language doing this soundly; C004's exemplar is type-based.
Layering debt: if benchmarks later show pervasive checked exclusivity is needed anyway, a language that standardized signature contracts first will carry two overlapping non-interference systems.
evidenceGaps: Card series N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and J001-J005 do not appear in any of the six files I was directed to read; only F001-F009 and C001-C006 were available. My case must be re-weighed once those cards are on the table.

No quantified performance comparison anywhere in the corpus between restrict/Fortran-style boundary contracts and ownership-derived noalias — or of non-interference optimizations at all (F002 negative scope; caveats.md). A benchmark suite isolating boundary-noalias wins vs whole-program-exclusivity wins is the single most decision-relevant missing artifact.

No corpus evidence on annotation error rates: how often do programmers write false restrict-like contracts in practice, and at what defect severity? This directly determines whether assumed-conformance tiers are tolerable.

Unverified background knowledge, flagged not asserted: I believe Rust repeatedly toggled emission of mutable-reference noalias due to LLVM miscompilation bugs, which would show the IR channel itself was historically unreliable for BOTH positions — but no card covers this and it needs adversarial verification before use.

Cost of my soundness mitigation is unmeasured: runtime disjointness checks at function entry (slice overlap tests) have unknown overhead, and the static-checkability coverage of SPARK-style anti-aliasing outside SPARK's other restrictions is unknown.

memory-object-model.md's prerequisite questions (object identity, provenance, interior-pointer stability, lifetime boundaries, FFI invalidation) are unanswered; per its own rule, neither position can soundly emit noalias/lifetime metadata until they are, so this debate cannot be finally settled yet.

No evidence on inference: whether effect/noalias signature contracts can be reliably compiler-inferred for internal (non-API) functions, which would largely neutralize the annotation-burden and annotation-rot objections.

result.position: Adopt algorithm/schedule separation as the primary array/loop surface: pure computation over index domains is specified once in an algorithm layer that is affine-analyzable and non-interfering by construction; loop order, tiling, fusion, vectorization, unrolling, parallelization, and storage layout are chosen in a distinct schedule layer (Halide direction), auto-derivable with mandatory human override, lowering through structured IR (Linalg/Affine/Vector-like) to LLVM.
result.coreArgument:
The debate is about how much array/layout/loop structure to SURFACE to the optimizer. Schedule separation is the only design on the table where the optimizer-relevant facts are not surfaced as annotations to be trusted or re-derived, but are true by construction — and where the optimization decisions themselves become first-class, manipulable program structure.

(1) The facts the optimizer needs become free, not annotated. The corpus's best-verified finding is that the optimization value of alias information is proving non-interference around writes (F002), and that language-level non-interference rules are the central optimizer enabler (F001). A pure algorithm layer is the maximal form of this: there are no source-level writes to disprove interference around. Crucially, F001 and F003 both warn that restrict/noalias-style contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness' and that IR metadata 'is only sound if frontend obligations are true' — the annotation route (the main rival) inherits an unsound-if-lied-to contract. In schedule separation, the compiler itself generates every loop nest and every buffer, so it discharges its own noalias/alias.scope obligations (F003) rather than trusting the programmer. This is the only position where 'soundness may not be sacrificed' comes structurally cheap: a bad schedule yields slow code, not wrong code, because the schedule manipulates loop structure without changing pure algorithm semantics (A006) — with the FP-reordering caveat conceded below.

(2) The compiler infrastructure we would target has already converged on this factoring. MLIR Linalg keeps structured array ops whose operands define iteration spaces, whose indexing maps relate loops to data, and whose iterator kinds expose parallel/reduction roles, explicitly to support tiling, fusion, promotion, vectorization, and lowering (A002) — that is algorithm/schedule separation rebuilt inside the IR because scalar loops had already destroyed the structure. A source language with schedule separation feeds this machinery its native food. Affine (A003) shows that restricting index expressions makes dependence analysis 'efficient and reliable'; a pure algorithm layer can be designed to sit inside the affine fragment by construction rather than by luck. Vector (A004) gives the staged lowering path so a vectorize() schedule directive becomes structured n-D vector ops instead of a prayer to the auto-vectorizer. MemRef (A001) shows layout — shape, strides, offsets, memory spaces, views — is already an optimizer-visible IR concept with explicit no-alias contracts (distinct_objects), i.e., the schedule layer's layout decisions have a sound carrier all the way down.

(3) The rival designs' own evidence shows their failure modes. LLVM's vectorizers are gated on legality facts they must prove — alias/dependence facts, reductions/inductions, trip counts, FP flags, control-flow shape — and vectorization is conditional, with pragmas being hints and runtime pointer checks or fallbacks often required (A005). Writing explicit loops and hoping means the programmer knew the facts, threw them away into scalar form, and the backend must re-derive them or insert runtime checks. The annotate-the-loops route is directly evidenced as insufficient: Fortran DO CONCURRENT's restrictions are 'necessary rather than sufficient' for parallelization — compilers may still need dependence analysis, runtime checks, or serial lowering (A010). Annotated intent on imperative loops does not guarantee the optimizer can act; schedule separation makes parallel/reduction roles semantic properties of the representation (A002's iterator kinds), not hints.

(4) Purity does not doom performance, and the domain concept generalizes. Futhark shows pure array semantics with regular arrays and size-tracking types can avoid copies via uniqueness/consumption rules (A008), so the algorithm layer's purity need not mean value-semantics copying everywhere. Chapel shows first-class index domains shared across arrays are themselves credited (in documentation) with enabling optimization (A009) — independent support for separating iteration domains from loop bodies. Even ISPC, the strongest card for the explicit-control rival (A007), is target-parameterized (gang size, masks vary by target), conceding that binding execution strategy into the algorithm is fragile across hardware — exactly what a schedule layer factors out; SPMD-style execution can itself be a schedule directive.

(5) The debate's premise removes the classic objection. The historical case against Halide-style scheduling is ergonomic: two sublanguages, arcane schedule syntax, expert-only tuning. This language is explicitly optimizer-first and allowed to be ugly/verbose. What may NOT be sacrificed is soundness — and schedule separation is the position whose soundness story is architectural (schedules can't change meaning) rather than contractual (annotations are UB if false, per F003/F001 caveats and the corpus's own 'IR metadata solves source design' misleading-comparison entry).

Scope honesty: this argument is strong for dense, regular, affine-friendly array computation — the fragment A002/A003/A006 actually cover — and I propose schedule separation as the primary surface for that fragment with an explicit imperative escape hatch, not as the sole mechanism for all code.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The existence proof for the position: Halide demonstrates loop order, split/fuse, tiling, vectorization, unrolling, and parallelization separated from a pure algorithm, 'exposing optimization choices as manipulable program structure' without changing algorithm semantics. This is the position stated as an implemented system. (Provisional direct-extraction card.)

MLIR Linalg shows the modern optimizing-IR stack independently converged on the same factoring: structured ops carrying iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds specifically to enable tiling/fusion/promotion/vectorization. A schedule-separated source language preserves exactly the structure this infrastructure consumes, instead of destroying it into scalar loops and asking passes to reconstruct it. (Provisional.)

Affine restrictions make dependence analysis 'efficient and reliable.' A pure algorithm layer over index domains can be designed to be affine by construction, so the language lands its programs in the analyzable fragment deliberately rather than accidentally. Also defines the position's honest scope limit: outside affine indexing the machinery weakens. (Provisional.)

Cuts against the write-explicit-loops rival: LLVM vectorization is conditional on legality/profitability facts (alias/dependence, reductions, trip counts, FP flags, control-flow shape), pragmas are only hints, and runtime checks/fallbacks may be needed. Scalar-loop source forces the backend to re-derive facts the algorithm layer would have preserved; the schedule layer turns 'hope legality is provable' into 'legality by construction, profitability by choice.' (Provisional.)

Cuts against the annotate-imperative-loops rival: Fortran DO CONCURRENT's independence restrictions are 'necessary rather than sufficient' — compilers may still need dependence analysis, runtime checks, or serial lowering. Direct evidence that intent annotations on explicit loops do not reliably surface actionable structure, which is the schedule layer's core differentiator. (Provisional.)

Workflow-confirmed: the optimization value of alias information is proving non-interference around writes. A pure algorithm layer delivers non-interference wholesale — the strongest-verified optimizer enabler in the corpus is obtained structurally rather than via analysis or annotation.

Workflow-confirmed: language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules are central optimizer enablers, with the explicit caveat that restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness.' Supports both the value of non-interference and the specific weakness of the annotation-based rival that schedule separation avoids.

Workflow-confirmed: LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata can carry disjointness facts but is 'only sound if frontend obligations are true.' In schedule separation the frontend generates the loops and buffers itself, so it can emit this metadata with self-discharged obligations — the sound way to use the carrier F003 describes.

MemRef shows layout (shape, strides, offsets, memory spaces, views) plus explicit no-alias contracts (distinct_objects) exist as optimizer-visible IR, i.e., the schedule layer's layout decisions have a concrete, preservable carrier from source decision to LLVM. (Provisional.)

The Vector dialect's n-D virtual vectors with progressive lowering give schedule directives like vectorize() a structured target above scalar load/store form, so scheduling decisions lower deterministically rather than depending on backend auto-vectorization. (Provisional.)

Futhark's uniqueness/consumption rules show pure array semantics can avoid copying for in-place-style updates, blunting the 'purity means copies' objection to the algorithm layer. (Provisional; card itself notes codegen/fusion behavior needs more evidence.)

Chapel's first-class domains — index sets separated from loop bodies and shared across arrays, documented as helping optimization — are independent corroboration that separating iteration structure from computation is optimizer-relevant. (Provisional; primer-level evidence only.)

Steelman-and-turn of the rival: ISPC shows explicit SPMD/lane control works, but gang size and masks are target-dependent and performance hinges on layout/coherence — evidence that baking execution strategy into the algorithm is hardware-fragile, which is precisely what a retargetable schedule layer factors out. (Provisional.)

concessions: Nearly my entire case rests on provisional direct-extraction cards (A001-A010) that have not been adversarially verified; the only workflow-confirmed cards I can invoke (F001-F003) support the non-interference premise, not schedule separation itself. Per caveats.md, the corpus has no verified coverage of Halide specifically.

A006's own negative scope: Halide is a constrained DSL (purity, restricted update semantics); its benefits are workload-dependent and 'not automatically transferable to a general language.' I cannot claim the corpus shows schedule separation working as the core of a general-purpose language — no such system is evidenced.

The evidenced sweet spot is dense, regular, affine-friendly array computation (A003's negative scope is explicit: semi-affine or arbitrary indexing weakens applicability). For pointer-heavy, irregular, data-dependent code the position has no corpus support and must delegate to an escape hatch, where F001/F002-style aliasing questions return in full.

The 'schedules cannot change semantics' soundness claim is not unconditional: reordering or parallelizing reductions changes floating-point results (non-associativity — the same FP-math-flags issue A005 surfaces for vectorization legality). The schedule language must be explicitly restricted or the language must define numeric semantics that license reordering; no corpus card settles this.

Per caveats.md, documentation-grade evidence does not quantify performance magnitude, compile-time cost, or code size; I can argue structure-preservation is what the infrastructure wants (A002), not that it wins by X% over hand-scheduled loops.
The 'auto-derived with overrides' half of my assigned position has zero corpus evidence — no card covers Halide autoschedulers, TVM/Ansor-style search, or their result quality.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md); the apparent alignment between my position and the best-verified findings partly reflects what was researched, not established relative importance across arrays/layout/scheduling topics.
risks: Two-sublanguage complexity: algorithm layer plus schedule layer means two grammars, two semantic definitions, and a defined interaction — more specification surface where soundness bugs can hide, in a language whose one hard constraint is soundness.

Expressiveness cliff at the fragment boundary: workloads that almost fit the pure/affine fragment get pushed into the escape hatch, and the boundary between scheduled pure regions and imperative regions reintroduces exactly the aliasing/interference problems (F001/F002) the design was meant to dissolve; boundary semantics are unevidenced.

Semantics-preservation of schedule primitives must be proven, not assumed: parallel/reorder/rfactor-style directives interact with FP non-associativity and reduction ordering; a schedule layer that can silently change numeric results violates the soundness constraint.

Dependence on structured-IR consumption that is documented rationale, not proof: A002 explicitly notes Linalg docs are design rationale, 'not proof of profitability,' and the dossier's open question — how to validate that MLIR/LLVM actually consume the preserved facts — applies with full force to this position's lowering path.

If auto-scheduling turns out weak for our workloads, the language degenerates into mandatory expert hand-scheduling for all performance-critical code; acceptable under optimizer-first ergonomics rules, but it raises the effective cost of every kernel and is unquantified.
Compile-time and search-space cost of schedule exploration is completely unmeasured in the corpus.
evidenceGaps: Corpus-access gap: the ground rules authorize citing N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but none of these card series appear in the five files I was directed to read; my case could strengthen or weaken once those cards are visible to advocates.
Adversarial verification of A001-A010, especially A006 (Halide) and A002 (Linalg), which are load-bearing for this position.
Auto-scheduler quality evidence: Halide autoscheduler papers, TVM/Ansor or equivalent — needed before 'auto-derived with overrides' can be more than aspiration.
Quantified head-to-head performance: scheduled-pure-algorithm code vs hand-written imperative loops vs annotation-assisted loops on representative workloads, including compile-time cost.

Workload coverage study: what fraction of the language's intended programs fit the pure/affine-schedulable fragment (directly answers the dossier's open question about privileging affine/regular arrays vs irregular structures).
Formal or empirical evidence that a defined set of schedule primitives is semantics-preserving, including an explicit floating-point reordering/reduction policy.

Evidence on general-purpose embedding: any system where Halide-style separation coexists with imperative host code in one language, and how the boundary handles aliasing, views/slices, and effects (dossier open questions on views and fact validation).

End-to-end validation that facts preserved by an algorithm/schedule frontend actually survive lowering and are consumed by MLIR/LLVM passes (A005's legality inputs; F003's metadata-preservation caveat).

result.position: Structured-arrays-first: the language's privileged core should be regular/rectangular arrays with shapes carried in types, index expressions restricted to affine-style forms, and first-class parallel/reduction loop constructs (Fortran DO CONCURRENT / Futhark / MLIR-affine direction), with anything outside that subset explicitly marked and conservatively treated.
result.coreArgument: An optimizer-first, LLVM-targeted language should make structured arrays and loops the default because it is the only design direction in this corpus where the facts the optimizer demonstrably consumes are (a) present by construction, (b) checkable rather than promised, and (c) already representable at every layer of the target compilation stack. First, the consumer side is documented: LLVM's loop/SLP vectorizers gate on legality and profitability facts — alias/dependence facts, reduction and induction recognition, trip counts, control-flow shape, runtime pointer checks (A005). Every one of those facts is a direct corollary of the proposed constructs: shapes in types yield known bounds and trip counts; affine-restricted indexing is precisely the restriction MLIR Affine imposes so that dependence analysis and loop transformation become efficient and reliable (A003); first-class reduction/parallel loop kinds replace fragile pattern-matching of scalar code with declared semantics (A002, A010). Second, the carrier side is documented: the MLIR stack exists to transport exactly these facts — MemRef carries shape/stride/offset/memory-space metadata plus explicit noalias contracts like distinct_objects (A001); Linalg keeps iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds alive to drive tiling, fusion, promotion, and vectorization (A002); the Vector dialect stages lowering so structure survives until target-specific choices (A004). A structured-arrays-first surface is a nearly lossless frontend to this pipeline, and the workflow-confirmed principle that an IR should preserve source semantics long enough to optimize them (F009) says exactly this: do not force the compiler to re-derive what the programmer already knew. Third — and this is the strongest convergence in the corpus — the best-verified findings are about non-interference: language-level non-aliasing rules are central optimizer enablers (F001), and their value is specifically proving non-interference around writes (F002). Structured parallel loops and whole-array operations are the forms in which per-iteration non-interference can be stated as a checkable language rule — Fortran DO CONCURRENT's cross-iteration restrictions (A010), Futhark's uniqueness/consumption discipline that lets pure-looking updates avoid copies (A008) — rather than an unverifiable restrict-style promise (F001's caveat about programmer truthfulness). LLVM already has the encodings to receive these facts (F003), and Fortran demonstrates a whole language whose first-class-array, no-alias-by-default model is optimization-friendly at the language level (F005). Fourth, the design fits this project's stated asymmetry: ergonomics may be sacrificed, soundness may not. Declared shapes, affine index restrictions, and explicit loop kinds are verbose, but they enable static rejection or checked obligations instead of trust-the-programmer UB; precedents span the spectrum — Futhark size types (A008), Chapel first-class domains shared across arrays (A009), Halide's demonstration that loop/layout choices can be surfaced separately from algorithm semantics (A006), ISPC's uniform/varying showing language-level structure exposing vectorization and divergence cost (A007). The decisive engineering claim is directionality: lowering structure is easy and staged lowering paths exist (A002, A004); raising unstructured pointer-and-while-loop code back into vectorizable/parallelizable form is the conditional, failure-prone direction (A005 documents vectorization as contingent on facts the optimizer may fail to establish). Therefore make the default source form the one from which all required facts follow by construction, and make escape into irregular forms explicit rather than ambient.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Direct precedent for the core proposal: MLIR Affine deliberately restricts loop bounds and memory accesses to affine forms so dependence analysis and loop transformations are efficient and reliable, and it encodes parallel bands and reductions explicitly (affine.parallel). This is the restricted-affine-index-forms plank stated as an existing, working IR design rationale.

Identifies the concrete downstream consumer: LLVM's vectorizers need alias/dependence facts, reduction/induction recognition, trip counts, FP flags, and structured control flow. Shapes-in-types, affine indexing, and declared reduction loops supply exactly this legality information by construction, instead of hoping analysis recovers it from scalar code.

MLIR Linalg shows that keeping array computation as structured ops — operands defining iteration spaces, indexing maps relating loops to data, iterator kinds marking parallel/reduction roles — is what enables tiling, fusion, promotion, and vectorization. A structured-arrays-first source language maps near-losslessly onto this representation.

MLIR MemRef proves the target stack can carry shape, stride, offset, and memory-space metadata plus explicit noalias contracts (distinct_objects) in optimizer-visible IR — so the layout and shape facts the source language surfaces have a place to live beyond parsing.

Futhark is the direct language precedent for shapes-in-types: regular arrays with sizes tracked in types, plus uniqueness/consumption rules that let semantically pure in-place updates avoid copying — evidence that shape regularity and checked ownership over arrays support optimization without unsound promises.

Fortran DO CONCURRENT is the precedent for first-class independence-declaring loops: arbitrary-order iteration with language-imposed restrictions on cross-iteration dependences and locality specifiers — a loop form that encodes optimizer-relevant intent in source semantics.

Workflow-confirmed: language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules are central optimizer enablers across Rust, Fortran, and SPARK. Structured parallel loops and whole-array ops are the constructs that make such rules statable and checkable per-iteration, tying my position to the corpus's best-verified finding.

Workflow-confirmed: alias information's optimization value is specifically proving non-interference around writes. Parallel/reduction loop constructs with restricted effects are precisely mechanisms for proving iterations do not interfere at writes, giving structured loops a principled (not merely aesthetic) justification.

Workflow-confirmed: LLVM IR has receiving encodings (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) for the disjointness facts structured loops establish — the pipeline from checked source fact to consumed IR fact is representable end-to-end.

Workflow-confirmed: Fortran, the archetypal first-class-array language, has a language-level aliasing model that is optimization-friendly — evidence that array-first language design and no-alias-by-default semantics compose well in practice.

Workflow-confirmed general principle: optimization-first design benefits from preserving source-language semantics long enough to optimize them (Swift SIL precedent). Structured arrays/loops are the array-domain instantiation: preserve shape, iteration-space, and iterator-kind facts instead of lowering to pointer arithmetic at the frontier.

MLIR Vector shows staged, progressive lowering of n-D vector structure exists below the loop level, so surfacing structure in source does not strand the compiler — there is a continuous structured path down to target SIMD.

Chapel domains show index sets can be first-class and shared across arrays, with documentation claiming compiler-optimization benefit — supporting shapes/index-domains as reified language objects rather than implicit conventions.

Halide demonstrates that loop order, tiling, vectorization, unrolling, and parallelization can be surfaced as explicit, manipulable program structure without changing algorithm semantics — supporting the broader thesis that optimization-relevant loop/layout structure belongs in the language surface, ergonomics notwithstanding.

ISPC shows language-level lane/uniformity distinctions (uniform/varying, foreach, masks) make vectorization structure and divergence costs visible — corroborating that surfacing execution structure at the language level, not just in the optimizer, is a viable and performance-transparent design.

concessions: All A-series cards (A001-A010) are direct-extraction and provisional — not adversarially verified like the F-series — so the load-bearing MLIR/Futhark/Halide/ISPC/Chapel claims carry lower evidentiary weight until a verification pass runs (per ground rule 3 and /Users/bytedance/Dev/xlang/optimizer-language-research/implementation/array-layout-iteration-dossier.md).

The entire corpus is documentation/specification evidence: it shows what these systems claim and how they are structured, but quantifies nothing — no performance magnitude, compile-time cost, or code-size evidence supports structured-first over alternatives (caveats.md).

Affine restrictions only pay off where programs fit them; A003's own negative scope says semi-affine or arbitrary indexing weakens applicability, and the corpus has no confirmed coverage of sparse, graph, or pointer-heavy irregular workloads at all.

First-class parallel constructs are necessary-not-sufficient: A010 explicitly warns DO CONCURRENT restrictions do not guarantee concurrent execution, and A005 frames vectorization as conditional on cost models and runtime checks — so 'structured' does not mean 'guaranteed fast', which is in tension with a performance-transparency goal.

Halide, Futhark, and ISPC are DSLs or restricted models; their negative scopes (A006, A007, A008) warn benefits are workload-dependent and not automatically transferable to a general-purpose language.
Fortran's optimization-friendly aliasing story carries exceptions (POINTER/TARGET/coarrays, F005 and caveats.md), so 'array language implies clean aliasing' cannot be claimed unconditionally.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons.jsonl line 1), so I cannot claim array/loop structure is the single most important thing to surface — only that it is well-precedented and converges with the confirmed non-interference findings.

The N-, D-, C-, and J-series cards named as citable in the debate brief do not appear in the five files I was assigned to read, so my case is built on F and A cards only; counter-evidence may exist in the unread series.
risks: Two-tier cliff: privileging a regular/affine core risks severe pessimization of code outside the subset (sparse, ragged, pointer-chasing), and the corpus contains zero evidence on what fraction of target workloads fits the affine subset — if that fraction is low, the design optimizes the wrong center.

Soundness-of-emission risk: shape types, distinct_objects, and noalias metadata are UB-bearing contracts (A001, F003); if the frontend emits them from unchecked source claims, or facts are mangled during lowering, the language violates its own soundness red line. Every surfaced fact needs a static check, runtime check, or verified obligation — none of which the corpus costs out.

Shapes-in-types drags in dependent-type-like machinery (sizes as type-level data, Futhark-style size inference); frontend complexity, error-message quality, and typechecking cost are unquantified and could dominate the project.

Guarantee-vs-hint mismatch: an optimizer-first language arguably owes users transformation guarantees, but A005/A010 show vectorization and parallelization remain cost-model- and legality-conditional even with perfect facts; promising transparency the backend cannot honor damages the language's core value proposition.

Fact-consumption risk: preserved metadata is only useful if MLIR/LLVM passes actually consume it end-to-end; the dossier itself lists 'how to validate that MLIR/LLVM actually consume the preserved facts' as open, so the whole pipeline argument rests on an unvalidated link.

Reduction semantics risk: first-class reduction loops force the language to define FP reduction ordering/reassociation semantics up front; A005 shows FP math flags gate vectorization, and a wrong default is either slow (strict order) or silently non-deterministic (relaxed order).
evidenceGaps: No quantified benchmark evidence that structured-first source designs outperform pointer-first designs plus analysis on representative workloads; all current support is documentation-level (caveats.md standing rule requires flagging this).

Adversarial verification of the A-series cards, especially A003 (affine tractability), A008 (Futhark uniqueness/copy-avoidance), and A002 (Linalg transformation claims), before any decision cites them as confirmed.

A workload census: what fraction of the language's intended programs fits regular/rectangular arrays with affine indexing, and what the fallback story costs for the remainder (sparse/irregular coverage is entirely absent from the corpus).

Dynamic and symbolic shapes: how much optimization survives when sizes are runtime values rather than static type-level constants — Futhark size types (A008) hint at an answer but the corpus does not cover degradation behavior.

End-to-end fact-consumption validation: build a spike proving shape/affine/parallel facts emitted by a frontend survive MLIR-to-LLVM lowering and measurably change vectorizer/parallelizer decisions (open question in /Users/bytedance/Dev/xlang/optimizer-language-research/implementation/array-layout-iteration-dossier.md).
Views/slices aliasing design: A001 warns view operations can alias original storage; a sound story for slices that preserves noalias facts is required and uncovered.

Defined failure modes for violated facts: static rejection vs runtime check vs trap vs conservative fallback vs UB — the dossier poses this but no card answers it; my position can only win with a soundness-preserving answer here.
FP reduction ordering semantics for first-class reduction constructs: no card addresses determinism/reassociation contracts beyond A005's note that math flags influence vectorization.
Contents of the N/D/C/J card series, which were outside my assigned read set and may contain supporting or opposing evidence for structured-arrays-first.

result.position: Advocate 2: The performance core should adopt closed-world whole-program compilation — sealed-by-default hierarchies, mandatory LTO-style visibility, and a core in which symbol interposition, reflection, and dynamic loading are banned — so the compiler can devirtualize and inline freely, soundly, and without guards.
result.coreArgument:
The debate is about what the optimizer is ALLOWED to assume, and the corpus shows a consistent pattern: every documented obstacle to eliminating dispatch overhead is an open-world escape hatch, and every documented mechanism for eliminating it works better — or only works at all — when the world is closed.

(1) Dynamic dispatch has a real, optimizer-visible cost, and the cost is not the indirect call itself but the inlining it blocks. D002 documents that Rust dyn-Trait calls add lookup cost and prevent inlining of the method body, blocking downstream optimizations. Inlining is the gateway optimization; a dispatch model that cannot be statically resolved forfeits the whole optimization pipeline behind each call site, not one indirect jump.

(2) Compilers already know how to erase that cost — when they are given visibility and finality. D007 documents that GCC performs devirtualization to direct calls, indirect inlining once targets become known, and LTO/IPA mechanisms that increase cross-file visibility. D004 documents the same lever in Rust: more separate codegen units inhibit optimization, while LTO enables whole-program analysis. D006 documents that source-level finality (sealed) gives the compiler semantic finality information. The closed-world position simply makes these the DEFAULT and GUARANTEED state: sealed-by-default makes finality facts total rather than opt-in, and mandatory LTO-style visibility makes 'targets become known' an invariant rather than a lucky configuration.

(3) The open-world alternative pays for its flexibility in exactly the currencies an optimizer-first language cannot spend: soundness or guarantees. D008 documents that ELF semantic interposition restricts interprocedural analysis, inlining, and propagation even for code that appears statically known — and that the escape flag (-fno-semantic-interposition) is sound only under an unverifiable ASSUMPTION that interposed definitions preserve semantics. Banning interposition from the core replaces an assumption with a fact. Likewise D007's speculative guarded devirtualization 'may be reverted' — it is a heuristic with a guard and a slow path, i.e., performance opacity, the exact thing this language exists to eliminate. Closed-world devirtualization needs no guard because the set of overriders is statically complete; it is sound by construction, satisfying the ground rule that ergonomics may be sacrificed but soundness may not.

(4) Reflection and runtime metadata are documented closed-world spoilers, not neutral features.
