D005's evidence on .NET explicitly notes that preserved reflection metadata 'may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted.' Banning reflection from the core is therefore not gratuitous asceticism — it removes a documented constraint on the strongest class of dispatch optimization.

(5) The managed/dynamic alternatives recover performance only CONDITIONALLY, which violates performance transparency. F007 shows HotSpot escape analysis is inlining-, profile-, and implementation-dependent — 'not source-level guarantees.' D009/D010 show Julia recovering performance through type stability and specialization, but heuristically, workload-dependently, and with world-age/redefinition complications precisely because the world can change at runtime. The corpus's own misleading-comparisons discipline says managed runtimes recover performance 'conditionally, unlike source-level guarantees.' An optimizer-first language should choose the branch of that comparison that yields guarantees.

(6) Closed-world composes with the rest of the evidence-backed design. D001 (monomorphization gives concrete-code runtime behavior) is the natural generics companion: static specialization for generics, exhaustive devirtualization for the residual dynamic dispatch, with D003 showing that dispatchable interfaces can be legally restricted by type-system rules when dynamism is genuinely needed. F009 supports carrying high-level facts in the IR long enough to exploit them — and closed-world is what makes frontend-emitted facts (exact hierarchies, unique callees) globally TRUE rather than conditionally true, addressing the corpus caveat that IR metadata is only sound if frontend obligations hold.

The honest form of the claim: the corpus does not prove closed-world is fastest — nothing here is benchmarked. What it does show is that (a) the costs of dynamic dispatch are documented, (b) the machinery to remove them exists and is gated on visibility and finality, (c) every open-world feature in evidence (interposition, reflection, redefinition) is documented as blocking or complicating that machinery, and (d) all alternatives that keep the world open buy performance back only via speculation or profiles, i.e., without guarantees. For a language whose entire premise is performance transparency, the closed world is the only position in this corpus whose performance story consists of invariants rather than hopes.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes the problem my position solves: Rust dyn-Trait dynamic dispatch adds lookup cost and prevents inlining of method bodies, blocking downstream optimizations — so unresolved dispatch has real, documented optimizer cost, and a model that statically resolves all dispatch removes it.

Establishes the mechanism: GCC documents devirtualization to direct calls, indirect inlining once targets are known, and LTO/IPA for cross-file visibility — exactly the machinery a mandatory-whole-program core hands to the optimizer unconditionally. Its negative scope (speculative devirt may be reverted) also shows why the open-world variant of the same optimization lacks guarantees.

Establishes the cost of NOT closing the world: ELF semantic interposition restricts IPA, inlining, and propagation even for statically-visible code, and the opt-out flag rests on an unverifiable semantic assumption. Banning interposition at the language level converts an unsound assumption into a sound invariant.

Shows precedent for source-level finality (C# sealed) as optimizer-relevant semantic information; sealed-by-default generalizes this from per-class opt-in to a global invariant. (Honestly weighted: the source itself does not claim devirtualization benefit — the benefit link goes through D007.)

Documents that compilation-unit boundaries inhibit optimization and LTO enables whole-program analysis at link-time cost — direct evidence that visibility, not just language semantics, gates dispatch/inlining optimization, motivating making LTO-style visibility mandatory rather than optional.

Its negative scope explicitly states reflection metadata 'may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted' — the corpus's most direct support for banning reflection from the performance core.

Monomorphization gives generics concrete-code runtime behavior, providing the static-specialization half of the model that pairs with closed-world devirtualization for the dynamic residue; the generics story does not need runtime type metadata.

Shows a language can legally restrict which interfaces are dynamically dispatchable (Rust dyn-compatibility), supporting the design pattern of a constrained, explicit dynamic-dispatch escape hatch inside an otherwise closed core.

Counter-evidence to the open-world alternative: HotSpot's performance recovery via escape analysis is conditional on inlining, profiles, and implementation details — 'not source-level guarantees' — so the JIT route fails the performance-transparency mandate that closed-world AOT satisfies.

Julia recovers performance only when types are stable and concrete, via heuristic, workload-dependent specialization — showing that keeping dispatch dynamic makes performance a property of program style plus compiler heuristics rather than a guarantee.

Julia's world-age and method-redefinition machinery documents the ongoing semantic complexity a compiler must carry when the method world can change at runtime — complexity the closed-world core eliminates by construction.

Supports the implementation strategy: an IR preserving source-level facts long enough to optimize them (Swift SIL precedent) is where closed-world hierarchy/finality facts would live; closed-world is what makes those frontend-emitted facts globally sound, per the corpus caveat that IR metadata is only as sound as frontend obligations.

concessions: Nothing in this corpus quantifies the runtime benefit of guaranteed devirtualization over speculative/profile-guided devirtualization; D007 shows speculative devirt plus PGO-style mechanisms exist and might capture much of the benefit without closing the world. My case rests on guarantees and transparency, not on a measured speed gap.

D006's own source does not claim sealed yields devirtualization benefit; the finality-to-performance link is inferential (via D007), and D cards generally are direct-extraction and not adversarially verified — per ground rule 3 they carry less weight than F cards.

Mandatory LTO-style whole-program compilation has documented costs: D007's negative scope notes LTO/IPA costs compile time, memory, and code size, and D004 shows the same tradeoff in Rust (fewer units / LTO = slower builds). The developer iteration loop pays for the optimizer's certainty, and the corpus does not show this cost is acceptable at scale.

Monomorphization's code-bloat and compile-time costs are explicitly unquantified (D001 negative scope); a closed world with aggressive inlining plus monomorphization could compound code-size problems.

Banning dynamic loading excludes real deployment models (plugins, hot-reload, dlopen-based ecosystems). Ground rules permit sacrificing ergonomics, but dynamic loading is arguably a capability, not an ergonomic nicety; my position depends on a well-drawn core/non-core boundary that the corpus does not design.

D005 and D009/D010 show that hybrid and dynamic systems (.NET value-type specialization, Julia specialization under type stability) do recover substantial specialization without a closed world — the corpus supports closed-world as sufficient for guaranteed devirtualization, not as necessary for good performance.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md); dispatch evidence is thinner, documentation-level, and lacks HotSpot, Swift, and modern C++/LLVM whole-program-devirtualization coverage, per the dossier's own 'Open' list.
risks: Scalability risk: mandatory whole-program compilation may be impractical for large programs or large dependency graphs (compile time/memory per D007 negative scope, D004); if teams respond by splitting into dynamically-linked components, the closed-world guarantee collapses exactly where programs get big.

Boundary-leak risk: every real program links C libraries and runs under OS dynamic loaders; D008 shows ABI/dynamic-linking semantics can silently invalidate optimizer assumptions. If closed-world facts leak across the FFI boundary, the design becomes unsound (forbidden) or the compiler must pessimize around every foreign call (eroding the claimed benefit).

Workaround risk: if sealed-by-default and no-dynamic-loading are too restrictive, users will reintroduce dynamism via function-pointer tables and data-driven dispatch that the optimizer cannot devirtualize at all — potentially worse than a disciplined open-world dispatch the compiler understands (analogous to D009's warning that hidden call targets in Function-typed fields defeat Julia's optimizer).

Code-size risk: exhaustive devirtualization + indirect inlining + monomorphization (D001, D007) may inflate binaries and hurt instruction-cache behavior; the corpus contains no code-size measurements to bound this.

Ecosystem/versioning risk: banning interposition and dynamic loading interacts badly with shared-library security patching and OS packaging conventions; the dossier lists package/versioning as a constraint area but provides no evidence, so this risk is unassessed.

Overcommitment risk: if future benchmarking shows profile-guided speculative devirtualization matches guaranteed devirtualization in practice, the language will have paid permanent expressiveness and build-model costs for a benefit obtainable more cheaply.
evidenceGaps: No quantitative benchmarks anywhere in the corpus comparing guaranteed (closed-world) devirtualization vs speculative/PGO devirtualization vs raw indirect calls — the central performance claim of my position is unmeasured (caveats.md notes documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude).

No cards on LLVM's actual whole-program devirtualization machinery (e.g., !type metadata, -fwhole-program-vtables/WPD, virtual-function elimination) — for an LLVM-targeted language this is the single most load-bearing implementation question and it is uncovered; the dossier's 'Open' list confirms C++/Swift/HotSpot dispatch evidence is missing.
No evidence on compile-time/memory scalability of mandatory LTO on large codebases, needed to show the build model is viable rather than merely principled.
No code-size measurements for monomorphization plus aggressive devirtualization/inlining (D001 and D007 negative scopes both flag this).

No evidence on how a closed-world core soundly coexists with an open FFI/dynamic-linking periphery (the runtime-abi-interop dossier is mostly a topic list); the core/boundary design determines whether my position is even implementable without soundness holes.

The N001–N006, A001–A010, C001–C006, and J001–J005 card series named in the debate ground rules do not appear in any of the five files I was assigned to read; whatever they contain (plausibly numerics, arrays/aliasing, C++, and JVM/Julia evidence) is unexamined by this advocate and could strengthen or undercut this case.

No evidence on human-factors/ecosystem response to sealed-by-default (do users defeat it with dynamic tables?), which determines whether the theoretical optimizer freedom survives contact with real codebases.

result.position: The performance core should adopt a hybrid generics representation in the .NET direction (D005): every value-type instantiation is statically specialized into concrete, layout-aware code, while reference-type instantiations share a single compiled generic body over a uniform pointer-shaped representation. The specialize/share boundary is a static, type-system-visible property, and the shared path is restricted (dyn-compatibility-style rules) and backstopped by devirtualization under closed-world/no-interposition module semantics.
result.coreArgument: An optimizer-first language must guarantee two things simultaneously, and the hybrid model is the only design in this corpus attested to deliver both. First, hot data-plane code over value types must compile to fully specialized machine code, because specialization is where representation actually differs per instantiation: D001 attests that monomorphization yields 'runtime behavior like concrete code', and D002 attests the cost of the alternative — dynamic dispatch adds lookup cost and blocks inlining, the single most important enabler of downstream optimization. A pure shared/dictionary model would pay D002's costs (plus boxing) precisely where layout matters most. Second, code growth must stay bounded and predictable. Full monomorphization's own primary documentation concedes compile-time/code-size tradeoffs (D001), monomorphized code interacts with compilation-unit and LTO boundaries in ways that force a compile-time-vs-optimization tradeoff (D004), and Julia's experience shows over-specialization carries compile-time and cache-pressure costs even in a system built around specialization (D009). The hybrid resolves this asymmetrically and rationally: specialize exactly where specialization changes representation (value types — different sizes, layouts, register classes), share exactly where representation is provably uniform (reference types — all pointer-shaped), so sharing is a pure code-size win with no representational compromise. D005 attests this is a shipped, load-bearing design in .NET, not a paper proposal. The residual cost — indirect dispatch on the shared reference path — is exactly the cost class backend compilers document machinery to recover: GCC documents devirtualization, speculative guarded devirtualization, indirect inlining, and IPA/LTO (D007); source-level finality annotations feed that machinery (D006, with its stated limits); and the language can maximize recovery by restricting dispatchable interfaces the way Rust restricts dyn-compatible traits (D003) and by defining module semantics that forbid ELF-style semantic interposition, which D008 shows can otherwise block these optimizations. Finally, the hybrid is performance-transparent in precisely the sense this language prioritizes: whether a call site is specialized or shared is decidable from the static value/reference distinction in the source, unlike JIT-heuristic specialization whose cliffs depend on type-stability inference and profile state (D009, D010). An optimizer-first language can even surface the boundary explicitly and verbosely (distinct specialized vs shared instantiation forms), sacrificing ergonomics for a legible cost model — which pure monomorphization cannot do for its code-size costs, since bloat is an emergent whole-program property, not a per-site-visible one.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The load-bearing card: attests that .NET ships exactly this hybrid — the runtime specializes value-type instantiations while sharing one generic body for reference types over object references — proving the model is implementable and deployed at scale, not hypothetical. Its negative scope (reflection metadata may constrain closed-world assumptions) directly motivates my design refinement: keep the representation strategy, drop unrestricted reflection.

Attests both halves of my tradeoff: monomorphization gives concrete-code runtime behavior (why value types must specialize) AND carries acknowledged compile-time/code-size tradeoffs (why specializing everything, including uniformly-represented reference types, buys nothing representational at real cost).

Attests the cost of the pure-sharing alternative: dyn-style dispatch adds lookup cost and prevents inlining. This is why the hybrid refuses to share value-type instantiations — it confines D002's cost class to the reference path, where devirtualization can attack it.

Attests that a language can restrict which interfaces are usable for runtime-dispatched/shared code (Rust dyn-compatibility) to keep the runtime representation coherent — the mechanism my shared reference-type path needs to remain sound and optimizer-legible.

Attests the backstop for the shared path's residual indirect calls: documented devirtualization, speculative guarded devirtualization, indirect inlining, and IPA/LTO can convert virtual calls to direct calls when finality/whole-program facts are available — meaning the hybrid's worst case is recoverable, not permanent.

Attests source-level finality (sealed) as an existing input for dispatch reasoning that my model would feed to D007-style machinery; I cite it within its stated limit — the fetched source does not itself claim devirtualization benefit.

Attests that dynamic-linking semantic interposition can block interprocedural optimization even for statically-known code — supplying the design obligation my position carries: the shared generic path must live under no-interposition module semantics or the D007 backstop is forfeit.

Attests that monomorphization's optimization quality depends on codegen-unit and LTO boundaries, weakening the rival claim that full monomorphization straightforwardly delivers its benefits at scale — its cross-unit costs are real and option-dependent.

Attests from Julia that over-specialization increases compile-time/cache pressure even in a specialization-centric system — independent evidence that unbounded specialization has real costs, supporting bounded specialization as a principle.

Attests that dynamic-dispatch systems recovering performance via runtime specialization face world-age/unbounded-case complications — supporting my transparency argument that the specialize/share boundary should be a static type property, not a runtime heuristic.

Attests that optimization-first design benefits from IR preserving source-level semantics long enough to optimize (Swift SIL): the hybrid needs generic-instantiation and finality facts carried into the optimization pipeline rather than erased at lowering — evidence this IR strategy is viable.

concessions: No card in this corpus quantifies anything: there are zero measurements comparing code size, compile time, or runtime performance of hybrid vs full monomorphization vs dictionary passing. caveats.md is explicit that documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude, code-size impact, compile-time cost.' My core tradeoff claim (bounded code size at acceptable hot-path cost) is architecturally argued, not measured.

Every D-card I rely on — including D005, my load-bearing card — is direct-extraction and provisional, not adversarially verified. The workflow-confirmed F-cards barely touch dispatch (the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, per caveats.md), so my position rests on the weaker evidence tier.

D005 describes a JIT runtime with live type metadata that instantiates value-type specializations lazily on demand. An LLVM-targeted AOT language cannot copy this directly; it must compute the instantiation closure at compile/link time or emit a fallback shared form. The corpus contains no evidence on .NET NativeAOT or any AOT realization of the hybrid.

D005's negative scope: the card 'does not prove specific JIT optimizations' — I cannot claim from it that .NET's shared reference-type code performs well, only that the representation exists and bounds code size by construction.

D006 explicitly does not establish that sealed/finality yields devirtualization in practice; my finality-feeds-devirtualization chain splices D006 (semantic finality exists) with D007 (compilers can consume such facts) — the composed claim is mine, not any single card's.

The 'reference type' concept the hybrid presupposes (uniform pointer-shaped values, likely with headers, possibly GC/RC-managed) is itself an undecided design question for this language; if the performance core has no uniform reference representation, the sharing half of my position needs redefinition.

Full monomorphization is arguably MORE performance-transparent per call site (everything specialized, no bimodal cost model); my transparency claim is specifically about making the code-size/compile-time dimension legible, and about the boundary being statically decidable — not about the hybrid being uniformly simpler to reason about.
risks: Hot paths that traverse reference-type instantiations inherit D002's cost class (indirect calls, blocked inlining), and the D007 backstop is compiler- and option-dependent — speculative devirtualization can be reverted, and IPA/LTO costs compile time, memory, and code size (D007's own negative scope). If a workload's hot loop is reference-generic, the hybrid loses to monomorphization with no source-level recourse unless the language adds an explicit force-specialize escape hatch.

D008 shows dynamic-linking interposition semantics can silently block the devirtualization my shared path depends on; if the language's module/ABI design (undecided) permits interposition or late binding across shared-library boundaries, the shared path degrades from 'recoverable indirect calls' to 'permanent indirect calls.'

Soundness obligation: shared code must be observably identical in behavior to what specialization would produce for every reference instantiation (layout, dispatch, variance, exception behavior). The corpus contains no evidence about the soundness pitfalls of shared generic code, and the ground rules forbid trading soundness away — this is an unbounded design-verification cost I am accepting on faith.

AOT instantiation closure for value types may be undecidable or explosive in the presence of generic recursion or open-world linking (e.g., polymorphic recursion instantiating unboundedly many value-type specializations); .NET handles this with runtime JIT support the LLVM target will not have. The hybrid could thus need a boxed/universal fallback for value types in corner cases, partially reintroducing the cost it exists to avoid.

Complexity risk: the hybrid requires maintaining two codegen strategies, two ABIs for generic code, and rules for their interaction (e.g., a value-type instantiation calling through a shared reference-type path). Corpus evidence says nothing about this engineering cost; .NET is the only attested implementation and is backed by a very large engineering investment.

Interop risk: the runtime-abi-interop dossier lists generics/monomorphization-vs-dictionaries and vtable/witness-table ABI questions as open constraints; committing to a hybrid before deciding C/C++/Rust/Swift interop ABIs may force uniform representations or metadata the interop story cannot carry.
evidenceGaps: Quantified three-way comparison — full monomorphization vs hybrid vs dictionary-passing/witness-tables — on code size, compile time, and runtime performance for representative workloads. This is the decisive gap: without it my central 'bounded code size at acceptable cost' claim cannot beat the full-monomorphization position on evidence.

AOT realization of the hybrid: how .NET NativeAOT (and Mono full-AOT) compute value-type instantiation closure, when they fall back to universal shared code, and what performance/size results. No corpus card covers this, and it determines whether the .NET direction transfers to an LLVM AOT target at all.

Swift's witness-table + @_specialize model — the main rival hybrid-ish design and directly relevant since Swift is AOT on LLVM. The dossier explicitly lists Swift dispatch evidence as open; F009 covers SIL's IR philosophy but not its generics ABI or specialization effectiveness.

Empirical devirtualization effectiveness on shared/virtual call sites (HotSpot, GCC/LLVM PGO data): D007 documents mechanisms only; the dossier lists HotSpot as open. My backstop argument needs measured recovery rates, not documented capability.

Adversarial verification of D005 itself and of the shared-generics soundness story (variance, layout, exact semantic-equivalence guarantees of shared vs specialized bodies) — all my dispatch cards are provisional direct-extractions.

Cross-boundary behavior of shared generic code under this language's (undecided) module/linking model: whether no-interposition, closed-world semantics (needed per D008) are compatible with the planned interop targets.

The debate brief references card series N001-N006, A001-A010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but none of these cards appear in the five files I was directed to read; if they contain dispatch-relevant evidence (e.g., C++ templates, JIT data), my case is argued without it and should be re-weighed once those cards are on the table.

result.position: Static correctness plus profile-guided cost decisions: every correctness-critical optimizer fact (aliasing, non-interference, ownership, layout invariants, effect-freedom) must be a statically checked source contract lowered to IR; offline profile data may steer only cost decisions — inlining, code/data layout, hot/cold splitting, and speculative devirtualization behind explicit guards — under a hard firewall rule that no profile-derived fact may ever change program meaning or introduce UB-grade assumptions without a total runtime guard whose fallback is the statically correct path.
result.coreArgument: The debate is not "static vs dynamic" — it is about which channel each kind of fact belongs in, and the corpus supports a clean partition. (1) Correctness-grade facts must be contracts because their IR encodings are UB on violation. The highest-leverage optimizations the corpus attests — register promotion, redundant-read/dead-write elimination, reordering (F002) — rest on non-interference facts whose LLVM carriers (`noalias` attributes, `alias.scope`/`noalias` metadata) make violation undefined behavior (F003, F004). A fact whose violation is UB cannot be sourced from a profile: a profile is a measurement of past runs, and a workload it did not see would turn a stale measurement into a miscompile, not a slowdown. Only language-level rules of the Rust/Fortran/SPARK family (F001, F005, F006) discharge the frontend obligation that the corpus's own misleading-comparisons note insists on: IR metadata is sound only if the frontend semantics behind it are sound. For an optimizer-first language that may sacrifice ergonomics but never soundness, this half is forced. (2) But frequency facts are not contracts and cannot be made into them. Hotness, branch bias, and actual receiver-type distributions are measurements, not invariants — there is nothing for a static checker to verify. The corpus's own JIT evidence concedes the key point against pure-static: J002's negative scope states that speculation enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles. My position captures exactly that benefit in AOT form: offline PGO steers inlining/layout, and speculative devirtualization compiles a guarded fast path (cheap identity check → direct/inlined call) with the statically correct indirect call as fallback. Wrong profile ⇒ slower, never wrong. (3) Full runtime speculative machinery is the wrong price for an AOT, performance-transparent language. The JIT cards document its inherent costs: invalidation/deoptimization machinery and one-directional de-specialization (J002), warmup and code-cache memory traded for profiling time (J005), and runtime analyses that yield conditional rather than guaranteed results (J004, F007) — the misleading-comparisons file itself frames managed-runtime recovery as conditional, "unlike source-level guarantees." Runtime speculation also destroys performance transparency: what the optimizer did depends on unobservable runtime history, whereas an offline profile is an inspectable build artifact and the guarded fast path is visible in the emitted code — consistent with F009's lesson that performance-critical facts should be visible in the IR pipeline, not buried in opaque runtime effects. (4) J001 shows the information demand is constant across designs — what dynamic runtimes reconstruct via profiling+guards, static languages express in types. The rational split is therefore: everything type-expressible goes in contracts (getting J003's shape-stability benefit by construction rather than by reconstruction); everything frequency-shaped goes in profiles behind the firewall. Forcing frequencies into source (mandatory `likely`/inline annotations) would pollute a contract language with unverifiable claims that rot silently — the one kind of "contract" the compiler can never check.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes that the core payoff optimizations (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering) hinge on proving non-interference around writes — these are the correctness-grade facts my position insists must come from source contracts, not profiles.

LLVM `noalias`/`alias.scope` facts are UB on violation; this is the load-bearing reason profile data must never carry them — a stale profile feeding a UB-grade IR promise is a miscompile, whereas a stale profile feeding a branch weight is a slowdown. It also grounds the firewall's soundness condition: metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are true.

Shows four independent language traditions (Rust, Fortran, SPARK, restrict) achieve optimizer-enabling non-interference through language-level rules — proof that the static-contract channel is viable and well-attested for the correctness half of my split.

Return-value `noalias` is an especially strong contract that needs source-level lifetime/provenance rules to emit; no profile could ever justify it, reinforcing that the strongest optimization facts are inherently contract-shaped.

SPARK demonstrates statically checked (not merely programmer-asserted) anti-aliasing and effect-freedom — the verification-oriented model my position requires so contracts do not degenerate into unverified programmer promises.

Double duty: documents the invalidation/deopt machinery cost of runtime speculation (against the full-JIT alternative), while its negative scope honestly concedes profiles enable optimizations static compilers cannot legally do — the exact benefit my guarded-PGO middle position retains without runtime deopt.
HotSpot tiering shows runtime profiling has inherent warmup and code-cache memory costs; offline PGO moves those costs to build time, which is the correct trade for an AOT LLVM-targeted language.

Vendor-confirmed evidence that runtime escape analysis yields conditional, not guaranteed, allocation optimization — supporting my rule that allocation/aliasing facts belong in guaranteed source contracts rather than being left for runtime machinery to maybe recover.

Same conditionality lesson with explicit low design-generalization confidence: escape-analysis wins depend on inlining, tier, and implementation — precisely the non-transparent, non-guaranteed behavior an optimizer-first language should not depend on for correctness-relevant facts.

The information demand is identical across static and dynamic designs; only the channel differs. This justifies the partition principle: use the type/contract channel for everything it can express, and reserve the profile channel for frequency facts that types cannot express.

Stable object layout is the optimizer-relevant property; a static language guarantees it by construction (contract side), while the remaining layout choices (field ordering, hot/cold splitting) are pure cost decisions safely steerable by PGO — a concrete instance of the firewall working.

Swift SIL shows performance-critical semantics should be visible to optimizer passes in the IR pipeline; offline PGO plus contracts keeps all optimization inputs inspectable (source + profile artifact + IR), whereas runtime speculation hides them in runtime state — an argument from the language's performance-transparency goal.

concessions: Guarded AOT speculation is strictly weaker than JIT deopt-based speculation in some cases: a JIT can eliminate guards entirely (invalidate-on-write, patch running code) and re-specialize as the workload shifts, while my position pays the guard branch on every execution and is frozen at build time. J002's negative scope cuts against me here too, not just against pure-static.

The corpus does not quantify the residual cost of guards versus true static knowledge (explicitly flagged in J001's negative scope), so I cannot claim guarded devirt approaches contract-devirt performance — only that it cannot be wrong.

Per the caveats file, documentation-grade evidence shows what systems claim, not performance magnitude; I have no card quantifying how much PGO-steered inlining/layout actually buys in an AOT setting, so the 'PGO is worth its complexity' half of my position rests on weaker ground than the 'contracts for correctness' half.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons #1); my confidence in the static-contract half partly reflects what was researched, not a demonstrated ranking against layout, dispatch, numerics, or concurrency evidence.

Static contracts of the strength I require carry real usability costs (F006 negative scope: SPARK-style restrictions may not map to a general-purpose performance language), and restrict-style contracts that are asserted rather than verified depend on programmer truthfulness (F001 negative scope) — the contract channel is only as sound as its checker.

PGO compromises a strict reading of performance transparency: performance now depends on source plus a profile artifact, so two builds of identical source can differ. My mitigation (profile as a versioned, inspectable build input) is a design proposal, not an evidenced result.
risks: Firewall erosion: the correctness/cost boundary is a policy, not a natural law. Incremental pressure ('profile says this index never overflows, emit nowrap') can leak profile facts into UB-grade assumptions; without a spec-level hard rule and compiler enforcement, my position decays into unsound profile-trusting.

Stale-profile pessimization and workload drift: PGO decisions tuned to the profiled workload can actively pessimize others (guard-miss storms on the speculative devirt fast path, wrong hot/cold splits). No corpus card bounds this downside.

Guard-cost blowup for alias-style speculation: runtime alias checks (loop versioning) can have code-size and check costs that swamp the win; if designers respond by dropping guards, they violate the firewall; if they forbid such speculation, they leave the F002-class optimizations entirely dependent on contract coverage being complete.

Build-system and determinism burden: profiles as build inputs complicate reproducible builds, cross-compilation, and CI, and create a two-artifact provenance problem for a language whose selling point is auditability.

If the contract checker cannot verify enough non-interference facts in practice (SPARK-style restrictiveness rejected by users, Rust-style unsafe escape hatches proliferating), the static half under-delivers and pressure mounts to let profiles carry facts they must not carry.
evidenceGaps: No cards at all on offline/AOT PGO systems (LLVM instrumentation PGO, AutoFDO/sample PGO, BOLT/Propeller layout): magnitude of inlining/layout/devirt wins, profile-staleness sensitivity, and regression rates are unevidenced — this is the single biggest gap, since my position's PGO half currently rests on inference from JIT cards (J001-J005) rather than direct AOT evidence.

No quantification of guard overhead versus static knowledge (J001 negative scope names this explicitly); closing it decides whether guarded speculative devirt is worth having or whether contracts-plus-nothing suffices.

No evidence on whether LLVM's profile inputs (branch weights, value profiles) are actually correctness-isolated in the implementation — i.e., that no LLVM pass turns profile metadata into semantic assumptions; my firewall assumes this is enforceable but no card verifies it.

No cards on speculative alias versioning (runtime alias-check loop versioning), the hardest test case for the firewall: it is a correctness-grade fact made safe by a guard, and evidence on its cost/benefit would define exactly where the guarded-speculation boundary sits.

No cards on devirtualization specifics, class-hierarchy analysis, or dispatch cost — plausibly covered by the N/A/D/C card series referenced in the debate brief but absent from my assigned reading; I cannot cite them and my speculative-devirt example is therefore mechanism-plausible but corpus-unevidenced.

No evidence on annotation rot or the maintenance cost of source-level frequency hints (likely/inline pragmas), which is my main argument against forcing frequency facts into source; it is currently reasoning, not evidence.

Per caveats.md the corpus lacks verified coverage of numerics, concurrency, memory models, and data layout; whether the static-contract channel can even express the correctness facts those domains need is open.

result.position: Fully static AOT: every fact the optimizer needs must be a source contract, enforced by the type system or verified statically; no runtime guards, no deoptimization, no warmup. Predictability — source text determining machine code — is itself a first-class performance feature of an optimizer-first language.
result.coreArgument: The corpus's own JIT evidence establishes that the information demand is identical across strategies: what static languages express in types and contracts, dynamic runtimes must reconstruct via profiling and guard it with checks (J001). So the debate is not "do we need the facts?" but "which delivery channel is better for an optimizer-first language?" — and the corpus documents the runtime channel's costs from primary vendor sources while confirming the static channel is sufficient for the best-attested optimizer enablers. The runtime channel's documented price: (1) speculation requires permanent invalidation machinery — de-specialization counters, deopt marking, one-directional element-kind generalization (J002); (2) tiered compilation buys startup speed with code-cache memory and still has warmup (J005); (3) the recovered facts are conditional, not guaranteed — Oracle explicitly states HotSpot escape analysis eliminates scalar-replaceable allocations but does not do stack allocation, and results depend on inlining, profiles, and implementation (F007, J004); (4) optimization quality silently degrades with shape churn and polymorphism, creating performance cliffs invisible in source text (J003). Every one of these violates performance transparency: the same source can run fast or slow depending on runtime history. The static channel, by contrast, is the corpus's strongest-attested success story: Rust `&mut` exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, and SPARK anti-aliasing/ownership are all source-level contracts that let AOT compilers assume non-interference (F001), which is precisely what unlocks register promotion, redundant-load/dead-store elimination, and reordering (F002). These contracts have direct, documented LLVM encodings (`noalias` attributes, `alias.scope`/`noalias` metadata — F003), and the contract channel can even be stronger than what C programmers get: LLVM's return `noalias` expresses allocation-like disjointness that C99 `restrict` cannot (F004). Fortran proves decades of production viability for rule-assumed non-aliasing under pure AOT (F005), and SPARK proves contracts need not rest on programmer honesty — a verification discipline can discharge them statically, keeping the whole scheme sound with zero runtime checks (F006), which matters because emitting `noalias` on an unverified promise is UB (F003). Finally, F009's design lesson — carry high-level source semantics into the IR so the optimizer sees them (Swift SIL) — presupposes the facts exist as source-level semantics in the first place; a contract-first language maximizes exactly what that pipeline can preserve. For a language whose stated charter sacrifices ergonomics for optimizer power, the verbose-contract cost is the cost we already agreed to pay; guards, deopt, and warmup are costs paid at runtime, by every execution, forever, and they buy back only conditional versions of facts we could have guaranteed.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes the framing that wins the debate for static contracts: dynamic runtimes (CPython PEP 659, V8, PyPy) converge on recovering via profiling+guards the same type/shape information static languages state in source. The optimizer's information demand is channel-independent, so the choice reduces to comparing channel costs — and only the runtime channel carries guards, deopt, and warmup.

Documents the concrete price of the speculative channel: de-specialization counters, code marked for deoptimization when assumptions break, one-directional element-kind generalization. Its positive scope explicitly says these are mechanisms 'a source-level-contract language could avoid by making facts static.'

Shows that the optimizer-relevant property (stable object layout) is something 'dynamic languages reconstruct, static languages could guarantee,' and that shape churn/polymorphism silently degrade optimization — the performance-opacity failure mode a performance-transparent language exists to eliminate.

Primary-vendor (Oracle) confirmation that runtime analysis yields conditional, not guaranteed, allocation optimization — HotSpot escape analysis explicitly does not do stack allocation. Conditional recovery is categorically weaker than a source contract the compiler may always assume.

Documents that profile-driven optimization has inherent warmup and code-cache memory costs (HotSpot tiering trades memory for startup). Fully static AOT pays neither, supporting 'predictability is a performance feature' on the startup/footprint axes.

The corpus's central confirmed finding: source-level non-interference contracts (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing, restrict) are proven optimizer enablers in shipping AOT languages — direct existence proof that the static-contract channel works for the best-attested class of optimizer facts.

Specifies what the contracts buy: proving non-interference around writes enables register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-write elimination, and reordering. The enabler is the fact itself, not any runtime machinery — so a contract that guarantees the fact captures the full class of enabled optimizations.

Shows the AOT pipeline is complete end-to-end: LLVM provides optimizer-facing encodings (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) for source-proven no-alias facts. Its caveat — violation is UB, soundness requires the frontend obligation to be true — is why my position demands enforced/verified contracts rather than programmer promises.

Demonstrates the static channel can exceed common practice: LLVM's return noalias expresses an allocation-like disjointness contract that C99 restrict cannot even state. A contract-first source language can be designed to mint such strong facts deliberately.

Fortran is a decades-long production existence proof that rule-assumed non-aliasing (dummy-argument restrictions, TARGET-based pointer narrowing) enables aggressive optimization under pure AOT with no guards or deopt.

SPARK proves the soundness leg: mutable-aliasing restrictions, ownership transfer for access types, and side-effect-free expressions can be statically verified, so contracts need not rest on programmer truthfulness. This answers the strongest soundness objection to 'no runtime checks' without conceding any runtime machinery.

Workflow-confirmed counterpart to J004: managed-runtime recovery of allocation/lock performance is implementation-, inlining-, and profile-dependent — 'not source-level guarantees.' The corpus's own misleading-comparisons file endorses the framing 'managed runtimes can recover some performance conditionally, unlike source-level guarantees.'

The Swift SIL lesson — keep high-level semantics visible to the optimizer rather than burying them in opaque runtime effects — presupposes those semantics exist as source-level facts. A fully-static contract language maximizes what such a semantics-preserving IR can carry; speculative machinery instead materializes facts only at runtime, after the AOT pipeline can no longer use them.

concessions: The corpus itself records that deoptimization is not purely a cost: J002's negative scope states deopt 'enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles.' My position forfeits that class of optimization wherever a fact is genuinely input-dependent and cannot be stated as a contract (e.g., value distributions, workload-dependent branch behavior).

No card quantifies the residual cost of guards versus static knowledge (J001 negative scope) or the magnitude of any static-contract win (caveats.md: documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude'). My case is qualitative and mechanism-based, not benchmark-based; the runtime side's cost could be small in practice (J003's negative scope notes real-world shape differences 'are often small').

The soundness requirement cuts hard against unverified contracts: restrict-like promises depend on programmer truthfulness (F001 negative scope) and false noalias is UB (F003). So my position is only tenable with SPARK-style verification or Rust-style type enforcement of every contract — bare 'trust me' annotations would sacrifice soundness, which the ground rules forbid. Guarded speculation, by contrast, is sound by construction.

SPARK's own caveat (F006 negative scope) is that verification-oriented restrictions 'may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs.' The language brief permits sacrificing ergonomics, but there is a point where contract-authoring burden reduces the code people actually write, and no card measures where that point is.

Some optimizer-relevant facts may be statically unknowable in principle for open-world programs (dynamic loading, plugin dispatch, data-dependent hot paths). For those, my position's honest answer is 'the optimizer does not get that fact,' which is a real, unquantified performance sacrifice — not a solved problem.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md; misleading-comparisons entry 1), so my strongest confirmed evidence covers non-interference specifically; extrapolating 'contracts suffice' to dispatch, layout, numerics, and concurrency outruns the cards I was assigned.
risks: Unsoundness via false contracts: every static fact lowered to LLVM noalias/metadata is UB if wrong (F003). The design must carry a full verification or type-enforcement story for every contract kind, or the position collapses into 'fast because we lied to the optimizer.'

Performance ceiling on input-dependent behavior: forgoing profile-driven and speculative optimization (J002 negative scope) may leave measurable throughput on the table for workloads where hot facts are only observable at runtime; competitors with JIT or guarded specialization could win those benchmarks.

Contract-authoring and verification cost at scale: SPARK-style discipline may make large programs slow to write and slow to verify (F006 negative scope), and an optimizer-first-but-unused language delivers zero real-world performance.

Overreliance on a selection-biased corpus: the confirmed static-channel successes are almost entirely aliasing/non-interference findings (caveats.md); if dispatch or numeric evidence (unread D/N cards) shows strong profile-only wins, my absolutist 'no runtime machinery ever' framing weakens.

The 'predictability is a performance feature' pillar is currently argued from mechanism (warmup, deopt cliffs, conditionality — J002/J003/J005/F007) rather than from any card measuring variance, tail latency, or real-time value; if challenged, that pillar has no quantified backing in the files I read.
evidenceGaps: No quantitative comparison of steady-state residual guard cost versus static knowledge (explicitly flagged in J001's negative scope). Closing this — e.g., benchmarks of guarded-specialized versus contract-compiled code — is necessary before 'static wins on throughput' can be claimed rather than 'static wins on predictability.'

No corpus coverage of build-time profile-guided AOT (LLVM PGO/BOLT-style), the strongest rival to my pure position: it uses profiles without guards, deopt, or warmup. Whether PGO's wins are large, and whether they can instead be expressed as source contracts (branch-weight/hotness annotations), is entirely unevidenced in my assigned files.

The N-series (numerics), A-series (arrays), D-series (dispatch), and C-series (concurrency) cards referenced in the debate brief live in phase2 files outside my assigned reading list, so my case is built only on F and J cards; dispatch in particular is where speculative devirtualization is traditionally strongest, and my position cannot claim to cover it until those cards are weighed.

No evidence quantifying the cost of writing and statically verifying contracts at scale (verification time, annotation density, developer throughput) — needed to show the fully-static position is viable for a general-purpose performance language, not just SPARK-sized verified cores.

No card measures predictability's value directly (startup-sensitive, memory-constrained, real-time, or tail-latency-sensitive deployments); J005 documents warmup/memory costs qualitatively for one JVM implementation only.

No evidence on whether every optimizer-relevant fact class even has a sound source-contract encoding: the corpus confirms encodings for non-interference (F003/F004) and visibility for ownership/ARC (F009), but is silent on contracts for value ranges, hot-path likelihood, shape monomorphism at open-world call sites, and data-dependent loop trip counts.

result.position: Selective speculation runtime: the language should permit guarded specialization (shape/type/value) only at explicitly designated module boundaries where static contracts are impractical, with deoptimization as the sound escape hatch. Static source contracts remain the default and preferred channel for every optimizer-relevant fact that can practically be expressed and checked; speculation is a marked, source-visible, opt-in mechanism for the residual set — not an ambient JIT.
result.coreArgument: The debate is not "contracts vs. speculation" — it is what to do about facts that contracts cannot practically carry. Three lines of evidence make selective speculation the right answer for that residual set. (1) The information demand is invariant; only the channel differs. CPython, V8, and PyPy independently converged on the same architecture — observe concrete types/shapes/values, emit specialized fast paths, guard them cheaply (J001) — because the optimizer needs the same facts static languages express in types, and guards are a working second channel when the type system cannot carry them. A guard dominating a region gives the optimizer locally the same shape-stability facts a contract would (J003: stable monomorphic shape/layout is the optimizer-relevant property; V8 merely reconstructs what a static language would guarantee). (2) Soundness — the one thing this language may not sacrifice — actually favors guards over asserted contracts at unprovable boundaries. The corpus shows that every contract system studied has escape hatches whose misuse is undefined behavior: violating LLVM noalias promises is UB (F003), restrict-like contracts "depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions" (F001 negative scope), Fortran's clean story carries POINTER/TARGET/coarray exceptions (F005), and SPARK's fully-verified route has acknowledged usability costs that "may not map directly to a general-purpose performance language" (F006 negative scope). Where a fact is true-in-practice but statically unprovable, the design space is: (a) forgo the optimization, (b) let the programmer assert it and eat UB on error, or (c) guard it and deopt on error. Only (c) is simultaneously fast and sound. A checked guard converts a dynamic fact into a compiler-trustable fact with a defined failure mode; an unchecked assertion converts it into a landmine. (3) Speculation is not merely a recovery mechanism for dynamic-language sins — deoptimization support enables optimizations a static compiler cannot legally perform without profiles (J002 negative scope states this explicitly). Even a contract-maximal language leaves profile-dependent facts (hot value ranges, dominant callee at an open boundary, dominant data shape from deserialized input) on the table; guarded specialization is the only sound way to monetize them. The selectivity and source-visibility of my proposal answer the standard objections: warmup/memory costs (J005) and conditional, non-guaranteed results (F007, J004) are arguments against whole-program profile-driven optimization as the primary channel — which I do not propose — not against narrow, designated, source-marked speculation regions. Marking speculation boundaries in source keeps the optimizer-first ethos: guards and deopt points become visible semantic objects the optimizer and the programmer can both see, exactly the lesson Swift SIL teaches about keeping performance-critical semantics visible to passes rather than buried in opaque runtime effects (F009).

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Three independent runtimes (CPython PEP 659, V8, PyPy) converged on observe-specialize-guard, evidencing that guards are a viable second channel for the same optimizer facts static types carry; where contracts are impractical, the information can still be recovered soundly at runtime.

Documents the concrete, shippable invalidation machinery (counters, deopt marking) my position requires, and its negative scope explicitly concedes my strongest point: deoptimization enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles — speculation adds optimization power, it is not purely a cost.

Shows shape stability is the optimizer-relevant property and that guarded monomorphic regions optimize best; a guard at a designated boundary therefore delivers contract-equivalent layout/shape facts to the region it dominates.

Its negative scope — restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness, and safe-Rust exclusivity excludes unsafe/raw-pointer cases — shows every studied contract system has an unverified escape hatch; my guarded escape hatch is the sound replacement for exactly those spots.

Violating noalias attributes/metadata is undefined behavior; since asserted-but-unchecked contracts at unprovable boundaries are unsound, and the ground rules forbid sacrificing soundness, guard-plus-deopt is the only admissible way to exploit facts that cannot be statically verified.

Even Fortran's optimization-friendly static aliasing model carries POINTER/TARGET/coarray exceptions, corroborating that purely static fact coverage is incomplete in practice and residual boundary cases are normal, not exotic.

SPARK proves the fully-static-verification route exists, but its negative scope concedes usability costs that may not transfer to a general-purpose performance language — direct support for 'static contracts are impractical at some boundaries', the precondition of my position.

Used as a self-limiting principle: HotSpot escape analysis yields conditional elimination, not guarantees, so runtime recovery must never be the default channel for facts contracts can carry — this is why my position is selective and boundary-designated rather than JIT-everything.

Primary-vendor confirmation that runtime analysis gives conditional, not guaranteed, allocation optimizations — again motivating the 'contracts by default, speculation only at designated boundaries' split rather than undermining it.

HotSpot tiering's warmup and code-cache memory costs quantify why speculation must be confined to narrow designated boundaries; whole-program profiling costs are evidence for my selectivity constraint, not against guarded specialization per se.

Swift SIL shows performance-critical semantics should be visible to optimizer passes rather than hidden as runtime effects; source-designated speculation regions make guards and deopt points first-class, visible IR objects, keeping speculation compatible with performance transparency.

concessions: All J-cards (J001-J005) are provisional direct-extraction cards, not adversarially verified like the F-cards; the strongest evidence for my position is the weakest-vetted evidence in the corpus.

Every runtime cited (CPython, V8, PyPy, HotSpot) is a whole-program JIT for a dynamic or managed language; the corpus contains zero evidence about selective speculation grafted onto an AOT, LLVM-targeted, contract-first language. My position is an extrapolation and must be flagged as such.

The residual cost of guards versus genuine static knowledge is unquantified (J001 negative scope says exactly this); if guard overhead in hot loops turns out large, the value of speculation at boundaries shrinks materially.

Runtime-recovered optimizations are conditional, not guaranteed (F007 with design-generalization confidence rated low; J004), so speculation can never substitute for contracts where contracts are practical — my position concedes the entire non-residual territory to the static-contract advocates.

Per misleading-comparisons.jsonl, I must not claim managed runtimes 'recover performance' generally; recovery is conditional on inlining, profiles, and implementation details, and I have not shown the conditional wins are large.

The size of the target set — how often static contracts are genuinely impractical in an optimizer-first codebase (FFI, plugins, deserialized data shapes, open-world dispatch) — is unmeasured; if that set is tiny, the machinery may not pay for itself.

Deopt machinery imposes language-wide implementation costs (frame reconstruction metadata, invalidation bookkeeping, restrictions on optimizations across deopt points) even when speculation is used rarely; the corpus provides no evidence on how heavy that tax is for an AOT design.
risks: Performance cliffs and deopt churn: J002 documents one-directional generalization (e.g., V8 element kinds never re-specialize), so a few bad inputs can permanently degrade a speculation site — directly at odds with the performance-transparency goal unless cliffs are surfaced as diagnosable events.

Warmup and memory costs: J005 shows profile-driven tiers trade code-cache memory and startup time for peak performance; even confined to boundaries, duplicate specialized code bodies and profiling counters cost space and predictability.

Ecosystem erosion: if annotating a boundary as speculative is easier than writing and proving a contract, programmers will overuse it, and the language quietly degenerates from contract-first to profile-first; 'designated' must be enforced with real friction, which is a social/design mechanism the corpus says nothing about.

Deopt soundness burden: the escape hatch is only sound if the deoptimization machinery itself is correct (state reconstruction at guard failure, invalidation races); the corpus contains no evidence on deopt bug rates or verified-deopt techniques, and a soundness bug here violates the one non-negotiable rule.

Cross-region contamination: specialized facts must not leak past guards into contract-verified regions (e.g., inlining a guarded specialization into a caller whose alias facts came from contracts); preventing this may force pessimization barriers at every speculation boundary, eating the gains.

Opportunity cost versus offline PGO: profile-guided AOT optimization with no runtime deopt might capture much of the same benefit with none of the runtime machinery; the corpus has no cards on PGO, so I cannot show my mechanism dominates that cheaper rival.
evidenceGaps: No corpus evidence on AOT-plus-deopt hybrids (e.g., LLVM deoptimization operand bundles/statepoints, Graal native-image with deopt, .NET tiered compilation with dynamic PGO, speculative devirtualization with fallback); this is the single most load-bearing gap — my position cannot win without at least one verified card showing selective speculation working in an ahead-of-time, LLVM-family setting.

No quantification of residual guard cost versus static knowledge (explicitly flagged in J001 negative scope); need microbenchmark or literature evidence on guard overhead in hot paths and on how well guards hoist/combine under LLVM.

No measurement of how large the 'contracts impractical' boundary set actually is in optimizer-first codebases (FFI, plugin dispatch, deserialized shapes, data-dependent values); if near-empty, the machinery is unjustified.

No evidence on offline PGO as the cheaper rival: whether profile-guided static compilation without deopt captures most of the win; the corpus cannot currently adjudicate speculation-with-deopt versus PGO-only versus contracts-only.
No evidence on deoptimization correctness engineering: bug history of V8/HotSpot deopt, formal treatments of deopt soundness, or the cost of maintaining deopt metadata through an optimizing pipeline.

No evidence on the interaction semantics between guarded regions and contract-verified regions (inlining across guards, alias metadata validity across deopt points); needed to show the escape hatch cannot silently weaken contract-derived optimization elsewhere.
J-cards are provisional (per ground rule 3) and would need adversarial verification before any decision leans on them.

Cards N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and C001-C006 were referenced in the debate brief but their files were not in my assigned reading list, so my case could not draw on or be checked against them; the debate moderator should confirm none of them contradicts the J-card extrapolations.

result.position: Adopt fully explicit numeric semantics: wrapping, saturating, checked, and unchecked-assume-no-overflow are distinct types/operators in the source language, and floating-point strictness / fast-math relaxations are scoped, lexically visible annotations. The compiler never implicitly strengthens or weakens a numeric contract; every optimizer-facing flag emitted at the IR level corresponds to a promise the programmer actually wrote.
result.coreArgument: An optimizer-first language's numeric semantics must be a visible, auditable contract between programmer and optimizer, because that is literally how the target IR works. LLVM has no single 'integer addition': unflagged `add` wraps modulo 2^n, while `add nsw`/`nuw` makes overflow poison (N002) — the corpus card itself concludes 'overflow facts are contracts, not mere annotations.' Legality of every rewrite is decided against a definedness lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) in which transforms must move toward more-defined semantics, and per-use nondeterminism can break rewrites as basic as x*2 -> x+x (N001). The failure modes are not interchangeable: poison defers UB and permits speculation, while immediate UB (integer div-by-zero) blocks hoisting above a guard (N003). Given this target, a language with one implicit overflow semantics faces a forced dilemma: (a) lower every operation to the weakest contract (wrapping), forfeiting the entire nsw/nuw class of optimization facts; or (b) silently emit promises the programmer never made, which the corpus's own implementation warning forbids — 'LLVM numeric attributes/metadata are not comments. They are contracts... emit them only when the source semantics... make the promises true' (implementation/numeric-semantics-and-poison.md), and which violates this project's rule that soundness may not be sacrificed. Explicit per-operation modes dissolve the dilemma: each of wrapping, checked, and unchecked-assume-no-overflow lowers to exactly one point in LLVM's existing contract vocabulary (unflagged add; overflow-check-then-branch; add nsw/nuw), so the emitted contract set equals the stated contract set by construction, and performance transparency — this language's stated purpose — is achieved because the cost and the optimizer permission of every operation are readable at the operation itself. The validation ecosystem confirms per-operation flags are the native unit of optimizer correctness: InstCombine requires flag-interaction tests and Alive2 @src/@tgt proofs for flag-sensitive folds, and applies 'analogous care to fast-math flags' (N006) — which is also the strongest available LLVM-side precedent that FP relaxation is naturally a per-operation/scoped permission, not a global mode. `range` metadata shows the same pattern for value facts: an explicitly declared promise with poison consequences, not an inferred hint (N004). Finally, the corpus's best-validated architecture points the same way from a different domain: explicit language-level semantic commitments (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules) are what centrally enable optimization (F001), the payoff is consumed locally around specific program points (F002), and IR encodings are sound only if frontend obligations are true (F003) — with notes/misleading-comparisons.jsonl explicitly warning that 'IR metadata solves source design' is a misleading framing and metadata is only a 'conditional carrier of source facts.' Nothing-implicit is the only policy under which soundness, performance transparency, and per-operation Alive2-style auditability all hold simultaneously. One honest refinement: 'nothing implicit' should be read as 'no implicit semantic strengthening or blending' — the language may still let a bare `+` exist, but only as a defined abbreviation for exactly one named mode, never as an ambient mode the optimizer reinterprets.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Directly establishes that LLVM already distinguishes wrapping (unflagged add = modulo 2^n) from assume-no-overflow (nsw/nuw = poison on overflow) per instruction, and states that overflow facts are contracts, not annotations. My four source modes are near-1:1 spellings of the target's existing contract vocabulary, so the design has zero lowering-invention risk for its core.

The definedness lattice and the rule that transforms must move toward more-defined semantics show optimizer legality is decided by operation-level semantic precision (including the x*2 -> x+x counterexample under per-use nondeterminism). An implicit, blended source semantics cannot be soundly mapped to the strongest legal flags; explicit modes can.

Integer div-by-zero is immediate UB and cannot be hoisted above a guard, while poison-based overflow can be speculated. This proves the failure modes are semantically non-interchangeable with materially different optimization consequences — so the choice among them must be a deliberate, visible per-operation decision, not an ambient global policy.

`range` metadata is an explicitly declared numeric promise with poison consequences for out-of-range values — a verified precedent that LLVM treats numeric facts as stated contracts with UB teeth rather than inferred hints, supporting a source design where facts are declared, not implied.

InstCombine requires flag-interaction tests and Alive2 proofs for nuw/nsw-sensitive folds and applies analogous care to fast-math flags. Per-operation flags are thus the native granularity of optimizer validation; explicit source modes make each operation independently provable, and the fast-math parallel is the corpus's only direct hook supporting scoped FP permissions. (Noting the card's own medium confidence and one 2-1 constituent vote.)

Analogical but corpus-central: explicit language-level semantic rules (Rust exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument restrictions, SPARK anti-aliasing) are what centrally enable optimization. The same architecture — source-level explicit commitment lowered to IR facts — is what my numeric proposal instantiates.

Analogical: the optimization value of semantic facts is realized locally, in proving non-interference around specific writes/program points. Supports per-operation/per-scope granularity of numeric contracts over whole-program implicit modes, since that is the granularity at which optimizers consume facts.

LLVM no-alias encodings are only sound if frontend obligations are true and survive lowering. This is the soundness core of 'nothing implicit': the unchecked-assume-no-overflow obligation must be lexically visible in source so that it can be audited as true, rather than manufactured by an implicit default the programmer never affirmed.

concessions: No card quantifies the performance benefit of nsw/nuw-class facts or the cost of checked arithmetic; caveats.md states documentation/spec evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude.' My performance case is structural (what rewrites become legal), not measured.

Every N-card carries design_generalization_confidence 'low' and a negative scope explicitly saying it supports optimizer-facing LLVM contracts, not a final source-language policy; the corpus's 'Still undecided' list names exactly this question (checked vs wrapping vs trapping vs poison-like defaults). I am arguing an inference from IR structure, which the corpus deliberately declines to settle.

Saturating arithmetic has zero direct evidence in the corpus (no card covers LLVM's saturating intrinsics). I include it for completeness of the mode lattice; it is the mode most easily dropped or deferred without weakening the architecture.

The FP half of my position rests on much thinner evidence than the integer half: only N006's fast-math-flag mention plus every N-card's disclaimer that strict-vs-fast FP, FMA/contraction, rounding, and reproducibility 'need follow-up evidence.' Scoped FP annotations are the natural extension of the per-operation architecture, but they are not independently evidenced here.

'Nothing implicit' has an internal tension: bare `+` must either be forbidden (severe verbosity, even for an ergonomics-sacrificing language) or defined as an abbreviation for one named mode. The defensible version of my position is 'no implicit semantic strengthening/blending,' not 'no default spelling.'

The phase-2 numeric workflow reported many stalled subagents; its coverage is strong only for the surviving LLVM-focused findings, so absence of counter-evidence in this corpus is weak evidence of absence.

The F-card support (F001/F002/F003) is analogical — it validates the explicit-contract architecture in the aliasing domain, not numerics specifically; per caveats.md the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection, not established relative importance.
The A/D/C/J direct-extraction cards named in the ground rules were not present in the five files I was directed to read, so I could not weigh them for or against this position.
risks: Unchecked-assume-no-overflow imports UB/poison into the source language; the corpus explicitly lists 'whether source-level UB is acceptable or should be replaced with proof, debug checks, traps, or unsafe-only assumptions' as undecided. If the project later bans source UB outside unsafe regions, this mode must be confined there — and programmer misuse of it is a soundness hazard the type system cannot catch (the F003 lesson: the flag is only sound if the obligation is true).

Mode proliferation in generic code: four modes crossed with integer types forces either mode-polymorphism machinery or code duplication, and mixed-mode expressions need conversion rules. If source modes do not collapse cleanly onto LLVM's small flag vocabulary, canonicalization quality (N006's target-independent canonical forms) could suffer. Mitigation: all core modes do lower to existing LLVM constructs, but the mixing rules are unvalidated design work.

Annotation fatigue: developers may apply blanket wide fast-math scopes or reach reflexively for unchecked operators to silence verbosity, recreating implicit-mode hazards under nominally explicit syntax. The corpus contains no human-factors evidence either way.

Scoped FP annotation semantics across inlining, call boundaries, and library calls are entirely unvalidated in this corpus — no card covers how LLVM fast-math flags or constrained-FP intrinsics behave at those boundaries, and scoping is exactly where a 'scoped annotation' design can go unsound.

Checked mode's trap/branch semantics may inhibit vectorization, reordering, and speculation (plausibly analogous to N003's immediate-UB hoisting restriction, since traps are observable); if checked is the recommended everyday mode, the language could be systematically slower than wrapping-default competitors on hot loops. Unevidenced in either direction.
evidenceGaps: Quantified benchmarks: measured optimization benefit of nsw/nuw-class facts on real workloads, and measured cost of checked overflow (e.g., -ftrapv / UBSan-style overflow checks) and saturating intrinsics. Without these, the case for offering unchecked at all, and for how much checked costs, is structural rather than empirical.

Primary-source cards on LLVM floating point: fast-math flags in LangRef (per-flag semantics, flag dropping), constrained FP intrinsics / strictfp, FMA contraction rules, rounding modes, FP exceptions, NaN/signed-zero/infinity handling, and cross-platform reproducibility. Every N-card's negative scope declares this uncovered; the FP half of my position cannot win without it.

Precedent cards on existing source-level explicit-mode designs: Zig's +% / +| operators, Rust's wrapping_add/checked_add/saturating_add and Wrapping<T>, Swift's &+ and checked-by-default traps, C# checked/unchecked blocks. caveats.md states the corpus lacks verified Zig/Go/C#/Julia/Swift-numerics coverage; these are the closest real-world tests of this exact design.

Behavior of fast-math and nsw/nuw contracts across inlining and call boundaries, and whether scoped annotations can be preserved soundly through LTO-style pipelines (the F003/F009-adjacent 'survives lowering' question, unexamined for numerics).

Whether per-operation explicit modes measurably affect compile time, IR canonicalization quality, or optimizer pattern coverage relative to a single-default design (N006 establishes the canonicalization framework but says nothing about mode-diverse input).

The A/D/C/J direct-extraction cards (A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, J001-J005) were not in the files provided to me; they must be read and weighed — especially any covering language numeric defaults or FP semantics — before this debate is decided.

Human-factors/misuse evidence for explicit unchecked operators: since a false no-overflow promise is a soundness failure, the rate at which programmers misuse such operators is soundness-relevant, not merely ergonomic, and is entirely unevidenced in this corpus.

Interaction of checked semantics with the poison lattice: whether a checked operation's guard can be optimized using nsw-style facts without creating time-travel UB, and the cost of freeze insertion (N001 defines freeze semantics but not its optimization cost).

result.position: Default integer types carry a no-overflow contract: overflow is a contract violation, mandatorily checked (trap) in every debug build, and assumed absent in release by lowering to LLVM nsw/nuw-flagged arithmetic, making violation deferred UB (poison) that the optimizer may exploit fully. Explicit wrapping/checked/saturating types are provided as opt-outs, and a freeze-on-violation hardened-release mode is the soundness fallback. For floating point: strict IEEE-conforming semantics by default with per-operation/per-region opt-in fast-math contract flags, mirroring the same contract discipline — but this FP half is proposed on consistency grounds, not corpus evidence.
result.coreArgument:
For an optimizer-first, LLVM-targeted language, the question is not "what overflow semantics are nicest?" but "which source semantics turn into optimizer fuel through mechanisms LLVM actually has, with a soundness story we can audit?" The corpus gives exactly one verified answer for integers, and it is the nsw/poison contract.

(1) The mechanism exists and is precisely specified. N002 (workflow-confirmed, 3-0 votes) establishes that LLVM separates default arithmetic results from optimization promises: unflagged add wraps modulo 2^n, while nsw/nuw make overflow produce poison. The card's own positive scope states the design lesson directly: "overflow facts are contracts, not mere annotations." A language that defaults to wrapping integers emits unflagged arithmetic and permanently forfeits this channel on the majority of its arithmetic — the default type is where almost all code lives, so the default determines what the optimizer sees.

(2) Deferred UB (poison) is the optimization-maximal point that is still lattice-disciplined, not lawless. N001 confirms LLVM's explicit refinement order — immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete — where valid transforms move only toward more-defined semantics, and freeze provides a specified containment operator. Poison is not "anything can happen at any distance"; it is a defined value-level taint with rules the optimizer and any future language verifier can reason about.

(3) The main rival with teeth — trap-on-overflow in release — pays a speculation tax that poison was designed to avoid. N003 shows LLVM treats division-by-zero as immediate UB precisely because that classification "prevents otherwise tempting speculation before guards": operations whose misbehavior must happen at a program point cannot be hoisted past guards or reordered. A mandatory release-mode trap makes every add such an operation-with-a-side-effect. Poison-style overflow is deferred exactly so arithmetic stays freely speculatable. (I flag honestly: the analogy from N003's division example to trap-semantics cost on ordinary adds is my inference; the corpus contains no card quantifying trap overhead.) My position buys the checking anyway — in debug, where it is mandatory, not optional — so the contract is enforced where programs are tested and exploited where they ship.

(4) The contract composes into a uniform discipline. N004 (range metadata: out-of-range values become poison) and N005 (inbounds GEP: out-of-object results become poison) show that contract-violation-becomes-poison is LLVM's standard pattern for numeric facts, value ranges, and index arithmetic alike. An overflow-free default integer slots into the same story: one violation semantics, one propagation model, one set of verification obligations for the frontend, instead of a bespoke overflow regime bolted onto a poison-based everything-else.

(5) The soundness-audit tooling for exactly this contract already exists in the ecosystem. N006 confirms that InstCombine's flag-sensitive folds (nuw/nsw, and analogously fast-math flags) are expected to be tested with negative tests and backed by Alive2 refinement proofs. An optimizer-first language that emits nsw is leaning on the most heavily machine-verified corner of LLVM's transformation suite — not on folklore.

(6) The frontend obligation is dischargeable. The corpus's implementation warning says LLVM numeric flags "are contracts... emit them only when the source semantics, verifier, runtime checks, or unsafe boundary make the promises true." My position is the design that takes this warning seriously at the source level: the language semantics *define* default-integer overflow as a contract violation (so nsw is a truthful lowering of the source contract, per the misleading-comparisons rule that IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts), and mandatory debug trapping is the enforcement mechanism that makes the contract socially real rather than a comment.
