F009 supports the general principle that optimizer-first design should carry source-level facts into the IR rather than erase them; nsw is precisely such a carrier for "this arithmetic cannot overflow."

(7) Soundness ground rule, addressed head-on. "Soundness may not be sacrificed" is satisfied under the contract reading: the compiler is sound with respect to the language definition — every program that honors the documented, debug-enforced contract gets refinement-correct optimized code (N001's lattice, N006's Alive2 discipline). What release builds do not give is defined behavior for contract-violating programs. If the project adopts the stricter reading (every executable must have defined behavior), my position has a specified retreat that preserves most of its structure: a freeze-on-violation release mode, where overflow yields an arbitrary-but-fixed value (N001 confirms freeze's semantics), converting UB into bounded nondeterminism at some optimization cost. Poison-with-freeze-fallback dominates wrapping-by-default because wrapping silently blesses the bug as defined behavior and destroys the fact channel; it dominates trap-by-default in release because it keeps arithmetic speculatable.

For floating point, the corpus is nearly silent (every N card's negative scope excludes strict-vs-fast FP, FMA/contraction, reproducibility). The only hook is N006: fast-math flags receive the same test-and-prove care as nsw/nuw in InstCombine. I therefore propose only the philosophically consistent default — strict semantics, with fast-math as explicit, scoped, contract-style opt-in flags rather than a global compiler switch — and I mark the entire FP question as requiring a dedicated evidence phase before any decision.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Core mechanism: verified 3-0 that LLVM's nsw/nuw flags turn signed/unsigned overflow into poison while unflagged add wraps. Shows my release semantics have a first-class, precisely specified IR target, and the card's own scope note ('overflow facts are contracts, not mere annotations') is the contract framing my position adopts at source level.

Establishes that poison is deferred UB inside an explicit refinement lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze(poison) > concrete) with freeze as a specified containment operator. This makes 'poison-style release semantics' a disciplined, analyzable contract rather than unconstrained UB, and supplies the freeze-on-violation fallback mode.

Shows the cost model of the alternative classification: immediate-UB-like semantics (division by zero) block speculation before guards. Supports the claim that must-fire semantics (traps, immediate UB) constrain hoisting/reordering in ways deferred poison does not — the central optimizer-first reason to prefer poison over release-mode trapping. (Extension from division to trap-on-add cost is inference, flagged as such.)

Range metadata makes out-of-range loaded/returned integers poison — evidence that violation-becomes-poison is LLVM's standard channel for numeric value facts, so an overflow-free default integer composes with range contracts under one uniform semantics my language can verify once.

inbounds GEP makes out-of-object results poison — the same contract pattern for index/address arithmetic, where no-overflow facts about indices feed. Supports architectural uniformity: overflow, range, and indexing contracts all discharge into one poison discipline.

InstCombine treats nsw/nuw-sensitive folds as canonicalization constraints requiring flag-interaction tests, negative tests, and Alive2 refinement proofs. Supports feasibility and auditability: the optimization surface my design leans on is the most machine-verified part of LLVM. Also the corpus's only FP hook (fast-math flags get analogous care). Note: medium confidence, one constituent claim voted 2-1.

Secondary support: optimizer-first design benefits from IRs that preserve source-level semantic facts long enough to optimize them; nsw flags are exactly such a preserved source fact ('this arithmetic cannot overflow'), whereas wrapping-by-default erases the fact at the source boundary.

concessions: The corpus explicitly lists 'checked vs wrapping vs trapping vs poison-like integer overflow defaults' and 'whether source-level UB is acceptable' as still undecided (implementation/numeric-semantics-and-poison.md); my case is that poison-style has the strongest verified IR-level support in this corpus, not that the evidence settles the default.

All N cards carry design_generalization_confidence: low and their negative scopes state they support optimizer-facing LLVM contracts, not a final source-language policy. Per the misleading-comparisons rule, the existence of nsw/poison machinery does not by itself justify the source design — it only makes the design implementable and auditable.

No card quantifies the performance benefit of nsw over wrapping or the runtime cost of checked/trapping arithmetic; the caveats file warns documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude.' If benchmarking later shows the nsw win is small on real workloads, the safety-oriented rivals (trap-always, or checked-by-default) become strictly more attractive.

Trap-in-release (Swift-style) is a serious rival with a genuine advantage my position lacks: contract violations are caught in production, not just in debug. My debug-only enforcement catches only violations exercised by tested inputs; adversarial or rare inputs hit UB in release.

Under the strict reading of the ground rule that soundness may not be sacrificed (every executable program has defined behavior), pure poison-in-release fails and I must retreat to the freeze-on-violation hardened mode, whose optimization cost is unmeasured.

The debug/release semantic divergence my position institutionalizes (trap in debug, UB in release) is a known source of works-in-debug/miscompiles-in-release bugs; the corpus contains nothing measuring how bad this is in practice.

My floating-point proposal (strict default, scoped fast-math opt-in) rests on consistency with the integer contract philosophy plus one sentence in N006, not on FP-specific evidence — the corpus has essentially none.

The N-card corpus itself is flagged as incomplete: the phase-2 workflow reported many stalled subagents, so absence of evidence for rival designs (e.g., trap-based designs' real costs, wrapping designs' real losses) is partly a coverage artifact, not proof of their weakness.
risks: Security exposure in release: contract-violating arithmetic on untested paths becomes exploitable UB in shipped binaries — the C/C++ signed-overflow failure mode. Mitigations (freeze mode, optional release checks, sanitizer builds) exist but their costs are unmeasured in this corpus. No card covers overflow-UB exploitation studies; flagged as an evidence gap, but the risk is real regardless.

Frontend soundness burden: per the implementation warning, nsw is a contract the frontend must never emit falsely. Any language feature that legitimately wraps (hash mixing, crypto, ring arithmetic) must be routed to explicit wrapping types; a single leak of nsw onto genuinely-wrapping source arithmetic is a miscompile-by-design. This demands a verifier/test discipline (Alive2-style, per N006) from day one.

Debug/release divergence weakens the testing story my position depends on: mandatory debug checks only enforce the contract on inputs actually run in debug. Coverage-dependent enforcement may be far weaker than it sounds; no corpus evidence quantifies detection rates.

Poison propagation subtleties (e.g., per-use nondeterminism issues that deprecated raw undef, branch-on-poison rules, freeze insertion blocking later flag-based folds — N001) mean the language's own middle-end and any source-level reasoning tools must implement the lattice correctly; getting this wrong reintroduces the very miscompile class the lattice exists to prevent.

If the project later mandates deterministic cross-platform reproducibility (listed as undecided for FP and plausibly extended to integers), the freeze fallback's arbitrary-but-fixed values conflict with reproducibility goals.

Ecosystem drift: N-cards carry implementation_stability_confidence: medium and the caveats file warns LLVM semantics can change across versions; the design couples the language's core integer semantics to LLVM's poison model as of the checked docs (LLVM 23.0.0git era).
evidenceGaps: Quantified performance data: measured benefit of nsw-flagged vs wrapping arithmetic on representative workloads (loop induction-variable widening on 64-bit targets is the folklore payoff but has no card), and measured overhead of always-on overflow checks/traps in release (Rust-style and Swift-style designs). Without this, the central optimizer-first tradeoff is unpriced.

Comparative language-policy evidence: no cards on Rust's actual debug-panic/release-wrap policy and its field experience, Swift's trap-always policy, Zig's split types, or C/C++ signed-overflow bug/exploit studies. These are the direct precedents for my exact proposal and for its strongest rivals.

Detection-efficacy evidence: how much real overflow does mandatory debug checking plus fuzzing/UBSan-style tooling actually catch before release? My position's safety story rests on this unmeasured claim.

Freeze-mode cost: how much optimization is lost when contract violations lower to freeze(nondeterministic value) instead of poison? This determines whether the soundness-strict fallback is viable or a fig leaf.

Floating-point evidence is almost entirely absent: strict-vs-fast defaults, FMA/contraction, reassociation, NaN/signed-zero/infinity handling, rounding modes, reproducibility, GPU math are all named as needing follow-up in every N card's negative scope and in the still-undecided list. A dedicated FP evidence phase must precede any FP decision.

Trap-semantics optimization cost: my use of N003 to argue traps inhibit speculation extrapolates from integer division (immediate UB) to checked adds (defined trap); a card directly addressing how precise-trap semantics constrain LLVM reordering/speculation is needed.

Card-set coverage: the debate brief references A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, J001-J005 direct-extraction cards, but none appeared in the five files I was directed to read; my case rests only on F/N cards, and the A/D/C/J material may strengthen or undercut it.
The phase-2 numeric workflow itself reported many stalled subagents; the numeric corpus should be re-run or extended before treating its coverage as representative of the design space.

result.position: Type-enforced data-race freedom: Send/Sync-like sharing capabilities plus exclusive borrowing in the type system make data races unrepresentable in safe code, so non-atomic memory is provably unshared and retains full single-thread optimizer freedom; sharing and atomicity become explicit, type-level facts lowered to LLVM's graded atomics only where the types demand it.
result.coreArgument: For an optimizer-first language, the decisive concurrency question is: over how much of the program's memory may the compiler apply unrestricted single-thread reasoning? C003 enumerates exactly what is lost when memory *may* be shared: no introducing writes on paths that would not store, no re-loading a value a single read must observe once, no caching possibly-shared values in registers across loop iterations, and acquire/release fences as code-motion barriers. Any design that leaves "possibly shared" as the default state of memory pays these taxes pervasively. The three candidate designs partition as follows. (1) Bounded-race models (Java/Go per C002) keep soundness but must apply C003's restrictions to all memory the analysis cannot prove thread-local — and F007 shows that recovering these costs via runtime analysis (HotSpot escape analysis, lock elision) is conditional, inlining- and profile-dependent, and never a source-level guarantee. (2) UB-race models (C/C++/Rust-unsafe per C002) grant the optimizer maximal freedom but make the DRF precondition of C001's DRF-SC contract a programmer obligation; when programmers err, behavior is unbounded — a soundness sacrifice the ground rules forbid. (3) Type-enforced DRF is the only point in the space that gets both: the optimizer freedom of the UB column with the safety of the bounded column, because the "data-race-free" hypothesis of DRF-SC (C001) becomes a theorem about safe code rather than a hope about programmers (C004). Crucially, this is not a new mechanism bolted onto the language — it is the concurrency face of the aliasing story this corpus has already workflow-confirmed as the central optimizer enabler. F001 confirms that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules (Rust's safe &mut exclusivity foremost) are what enable optimization; F002 confirms the value is specifically proving non-interference around writes; exclusive borrowing extended across threads via Send/Sync capabilities is the same proof extended to the concurrent case (C004 states this directly: the optimizer can treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless types say otherwise). F003 confirms LLVM has the consuming vocabulary (noalias attributes, alias.scope/noalias metadata) — but also that violating these encodings is UB, which means the frontend's non-sharing claims must be *sound*, not merely conventional. Only a type system discharges that obligation mechanically; programmer-asserted restrict-style contracts "depend on programmer truthfulness" (F001 scope note). C005 completes the lowering picture: LLVM grades orderings from NotAtomic to SequentiallyConsistent with pass-specific legality at each level, so a type-enforced language lowers provably-unshared memory as NotAtomic with zero legality anxiety and lowers the explicitly-shared remainder at exactly the ordering its type declares — the C003 taxes are confined to memory the programmer explicitly marked as shared, which in an optimizer-first, ergonomics-be-damned language is precisely the honest, performance-transparent surface we want: sharing is visible, ugly, and paid for only where written. Finally, the standard objection to this model — borrow-checking ergonomics — is explicitly out of scope for this language; the objections that remain in scope (unsafe-boundary soundness, lowering fidelity) are real and I concede them below, but they are obligations every competitor also carries in worse form: the UB model makes the whole program the unsafe boundary, and the bounded model pays optimizer tax forever to avoid defining one.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Provides the concrete list of optimizer freedoms lost on possibly-shared memory (no introduced writes, no re-loading, no register caching across iterations, fences as motion barriers). This is the cost function my position minimizes: type-enforced non-sharing shrinks the 'possibly shared' set to exactly the explicitly-typed shared locations, so these restrictions apply nowhere else.

Direct evidence that a type-system route to data-race freedom exists and works as designed in Rust: Send/Sync auto traits plus exclusive &mut borrowing prevent data races in safe code, letting the optimizer treat non-atomic memory as unshared unless types say otherwise. This is the existence proof for my assigned position. (Provisional card; Nomicon-based, explanatory not formal.)

DRF-SC is the convergent baseline contract across C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Rust, Swift, Go. My position does not fight this consensus — it strengthens it by making the DRF precondition a compiler-checked theorem for safe code instead of a programmer obligation, which is the strongest way to inherit the SC guarantee.

Documents the UB-vs-bounded split and its tradeoff: UB races give maximal optimizer freedom; bounded races constrain optimization to limit blast radius. Type-enforced DRF dissolves the dilemma for safe code — we keep UB-column optimizer freedom because the racy programs the UB column fears cannot be written in the safe fragment.

Workflow-confirmed (not merely provisional) that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules — Rust's safe &mut exclusivity chief among them — are central optimizer enablers. Exclusive borrowing across threads is the same mechanism; my position is continuous with the corpus's strongest-attested finding rather than a separate bet. Its scope note (restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness) is the argument for type enforcement over assertion.

Workflow-confirmed that alias information's optimization value is specifically proving non-interference around writes. A cross-thread data race is exactly an unproven interference around a write; type-enforced DRF is the theorem that extends F002's single-thread value to the concurrent program.

Workflow-confirmed that LLVM provides optimizer-facing no-alias encodings (noalias, alias.scope metadata) and that violating them is UB. This cuts two ways for me: the lowering target exists, and the UB-on-violation contract means only mechanically sound non-sharing facts — i.e., type-derived ones — may be emitted. Programmer-asserted sharing annotations would be a miscompile generator.

LLVM's graded atomic orderings (NotAtomic through SequentiallyConsistent, each with specific transformation legality) give the lowering vocabulary: provably-unshared memory lowers as NotAtomic with full pass freedom; typed-shared memory lowers at exactly its declared ordering. Also notes LLVM treats racing non-atomic loads as undef, relevant to defining the unsafe fragment's semantics.

Workflow-confirmed that the managed-runtime alternative — recovering allocation/synchronization costs via escape analysis and lock elision — is conditional, inlining- and profile-dependent, and not a source-level guarantee. This is direct evidence against the bounded-race competitor's claim that runtime analysis can recover what static non-sharing proofs give for free.

Java final-field initialization safety (readers see final values without synchronization only if the reference does not escape construction) shows that even bounded-race languages must carve narrow, escape-conditioned publication guarantees to regain optimizer-compatible behavior — evidence that publication safety wants to be a static non-escape fact, which is what a capability/borrowing type system provides uniformly rather than as a special case.

concessions: The guarantee covers only safe code. C004's negative scope is explicit: unsafe code and incorrect manual Send/Sync-like implementations are UB, and F001's scope note excludes unsafe/raw-pointer/UnsafeCell details from the exclusivity evidence. My position relocates and shrinks the trusted boundary; it does not eliminate it. The language will still need a defined story (UB vs LLVM-undef containment per C005) for races originating in the unsafe fragment, FFI, foreign threads, and signal handlers — all listed as open in the implementation file.

The performance advantage is directionally supported but nowhere quantified. C002's negative scope says there is no performance quantification of the UB-vs-bounded difference, and caveats.md states documentation evidence 'usually does not quantify performance magnitude.' I cannot honestly claim a measured speedup over a Go/Java-style bounded model — only that the corpus confirms which optimizations become legal.

The lowering path from type facts to LLVM metadata is nontrivial. misleading-comparisons.jsonl explicitly flags 'Rust ownership automatically maps to LLVM noalias everywhere' as misleading: interior mutability, callbacks, shared references, and compiler model details complicate it, and F003 warns metadata must survive lowering with true frontend obligations. Rust's own history of toggling mutable-noalias emission is the cautionary tale (not in corpus — flagged as gap).

An interior-mutability escape hatch (UnsafeCell-equivalent) is unavoidable for locks and atomics themselves, and caveats.md notes Rust's formal aliasing model for exactly this fragment is incomplete ('future aliasing-model changes remain important caveats'). The hardest soundness work of my position concentrates where the corpus evidence is thinnest.

All C-cards are direct-extraction and provisional, not adversarially verified; C004 in particular rests on the Nomicon, which caveats.md describes as explanatory rather than a complete formal aliasing model. The workflow-confirmed F-cards support the single-thread aliasing half of my argument more strongly than the cross-thread half.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons line 1), which structurally favors my position; I cannot rule out that an unexamined model (e.g., Pony's capabilities, actor isolation) dominates mine, because those models are explicitly absent from the corpus.
risks: Soundness single-point-of-failure: because emitted noalias/NotAtomic facts are UB to violate (F003, C005), any hole in the Send/Sync-like trait system or borrow rules becomes a silent miscompile engine rather than a localized bug. The type system's soundness proof is load-bearing for every optimization it enables, and the ground rules forbid sacrificing soundness — this position bets the language on getting a hard formal artifact right (Rust's is still unfinished per caveats.md).

Unsafe-code concentration: patterns that genuinely need shared mutation (lock-free structures, epoch reclamation, custom synchronization primitives) all route through the unsafe fragment, whose race semantics the corpus leaves undefined (C002 open question, implementation-file open items). If real optimizer-first workloads are unsafe-heavy, the safe-code guarantee protects less of the program than the argument assumes, and there is no corpus evidence on that ratio.

Blast-radius asymmetry: when unsafe code does race, we are in C002's UB column with none of Java/Go's bounded-outcome or race-detection-halt protections. A hybrid (typed DRF plus bounded semantics for the unsafe fragment) might hedge this, but would surrender some of the optimizer freedom I am claiming, and no corpus card evaluates that hybrid.

Lowering fidelity risk: if the frontend cannot reliably translate type-level non-sharing into metadata that survives LLVM's pipeline (F003 scope note; misleading-comparisons line 2), the position's optimizer benefits exist on paper but not in emitted code — the design would pay full type-system cost for partial optimizer payoff.

Design coupling: this choice entangles the concurrency model with ownership/borrowing/aliasing across the entire language. Retrofitting a different sharing model later (e.g., if actor-style isolation proves superior) would be near-impossible; the bounded-race competitor keeps more design freedom in reserve.
evidenceGaps: No empirical cost data for bounded-race semantics versus UB/type-enforced semantics — explicitly listed as open in implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md. Closing this is the single most decisive gap: if the bounded-race optimizer tax is measured at ~0 on representative workloads, my position's main advantage evaporates.

No quantified benchmark of what unshared-memory/noalias assumptions are worth end-to-end (caveats.md: documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude, code-size, or compile-time cost). Need measurements, e.g., LLVM with and without cross-thread non-sharing facts on optimizer-relevant kernels.
Primary sources unfetched: C++ standard memory-model text and JLS chapter 17 (implementation file notes fetches were blocked); C001-C003 rest partly on a secondary survey (research.swtch.com).

No formal or verified account of the interior-mutability/unsafe fragment's aliasing model — the exact place my position's soundness burden lives (caveats.md flags Rust's model as subject to future change; Stacked/Tree Borrows-style formalizations are not in the corpus).

Alternative race-free models unexamined: Swift/Ada/Pony/Erlang and actor/message-passing isolation are explicitly open in the implementation file. Pony's reference capabilities are a direct competitor realization of 'type-enforced DRF' with different tradeoffs; the debate cannot fairly conclude without at least one card on them.
No evidence on how often realistic optimizer-first workloads must drop into unsafe/shared-mutation code, i.e., what fraction of memory the safe-fragment guarantee actually covers in practice.
Rust's production history of enabling/disabling mutable-noalias in LLVM (miscompile-driven toggles) would be the best real-world evidence on lowering-fidelity risk; no card covers it.

GPU/heterogeneous memory models (open in implementation file) — unknown whether type-enforced sharing capabilities extend soundly to non-CPU sync scopes (C005's sync-scope vocabulary is CPU-oriented).

Card families N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, J001-J005 were referenced in the debate ground rules but are not present in the five files I was directed to read; if they contain relevant concurrency or numeric-semantics evidence, this case should be re-checked against them.

challenges.challenge: The step-1 trichotomy ('any defined violation semantics forfeits free assumption') is refuted by the substrate the position claims to inherit. LLVM itself handles one broken non-interference contract — racing non-atomic loads — with undef, not UB (C005), and deployed languages split between UB races and BOUNDED racy outcomes (Java/JS/Go, C002, including Go's race-detection halts). N001's own lattice contains freeze(poison) as a defined-but-arbitrary middle point. So there is a fourth semantics class — bounded nondeterminism — that is sound, requires no runtime check, and still permits substantial optimization. The claim that non-UB semantics 'forfeits the entire category' of asserted facts is contradicted by the corpus the advocate cites.

'Safe code has zero UB from false contracts' is unproven without a memory-model commitment the position never makes. The threat model marks data races only 'partially' compiler-verifiable, and C004 shows Rust's guarantee depends on an additional Send/Sync-style type discipline. A data race can falsify a compiler-PROVEN noalias fact at runtime, injecting UB into code with no unsafe block anywhere. The position's central theorem quantifies only over single-threaded executions.

N006 is misused twice. First, Alive2/InstCombine discipline validates IR-to-IR transforms; it is structurally blind to the frontend-emission truth obligation that F003's negative scope identifies as the actual soundness condition — the validation ecosystem does not check whether an asserted contract was true. Second, the claimed differential advantage ('alternatives must rebuild this ecosystem') is false: all three rival policies emit the same LLVM contract channels and inherit the same tooling; the debate is only about when emission is permitted. Also note N006 is medium confidence with a 2-1 vote on the flag-testing claim.

The 'prefer deferred UB (poison)' refinement is generalized beyond its evidence. N003's scope limit is integer division specifically, and every N-card carries design_generalization_confidence 'low.' Moreover the preference is not a dominance: N001's lattice places immediate UB as least-defined, i.e., maximal optimizer freedom for reachability/assume-driven reasoning (branch-on-poison detonation is narrower than assume-false). Poison-vs-immediate-UB is an unmeasured tradeoff, not a corpus-supported design rule.

The assume-or-forfeit dichotomy ignores A005: LLVM's loop vectorizers already deploy runtime pointer checks with multi-versioning as a standard legality strategy — recovering assume-grade optimization inside checked regions. A sound, precedented middle path exists in the exact backend this position claims zero impedance mismatch with, and the position never prices it against 'unremovable runtime checks.'

'Runtime checking structurally forfeits the payoff' overreads N003 and is contradicted by A005. A passed check establishes the fact on all defined paths downstream, so the SAME contract (inbounds, range, nsw) can be legitimately emitted after the guard; the true cost is check execution plus motion barriers across the guard — which no card quantifies — not loss of the contract. A005 shows runtime alias checks plus loop versioning are how LLVM's own vectorizers buy noalias facts today. The claim that checking 'degenerates into don't emit the contract' is false as stated.

F007 is a category error: HotSpot escape analysis is profile-driven runtime RE-DERIVATION of facts, not runtime CHECKING of declared source contracts. Its fragility (inlining/profile dependence) says nothing about the reliability of a trap-on-overflow or bounds-check semantics, and the card's design_generalization_confidence is 'low.' Using it to indict the check-based rival stretches the card past its scope.

The position conflates two static regimes with opposite failure modes: by-construction type discipline (Rust &mut exclusivity, F001; Send/Sync, C004) versus prover-discharged obligations (SPARK, F006). The first restricts the language so contracts are theorems of typing — cheap, compositional, but excludes whole idioms; the second admits more programs but inherits F006's warned usability costs, annotation burden, and proof brittleness. 'Static proof required' never says which regime it is, and the rejection/fallback story, ecosystem risk, and expressiveness ceiling differ radically between them.

'The false-contract state is unrepresentable' fails under concurrency, so the debate question the position claims to make unaskable gets asked again one layer down. Proofs quantify over single-threaded executions unless race-freedom is also established; the threat model marks races only partially verifiable, and C002 shows race semantics (UB vs bounded) is itself an open, prior design choice. A data race can falsify a proven noalias or purity fact at runtime — 'what happens then?' has no answer in this position.

'Exactly three coherent source policies' is false: speculate-and-deoptimize (J001, J002) is a fourth — assume the contract, guard cheaply, invalidate on violation — sound, with no source UB, and J002's negative scope explicitly notes deoptimization 'enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles.' It may lose on AOT grounds (J005 warmup/memory costs), but a position built on exhaustive case analysis must enumerate and reject it, not omit it.

The ladder's middle rung is missing exactly where the payoff is highest. Noalias-class disjointness is a whole-execution property with no carded runtime check (Position 2's uncontested point), and purity likewise (conceded). So for the contract class the corpus attests most strongly (F001, F002), 'graduated obligations' degenerates to proven-or-marked-assume — i.e., Position 1 plus mode-matrix complexity — and the position's distinctive tier contributes nothing on the highest-value facts.

N003 is inverted: the card documents guard-pinning as a LIMITATION (immediate UB 'prevents otherwise tempting speculation'), not an endorsement that inserted checks are optimizer-friendly. 'A guard the optimizer already respects' means 'a guard the optimizer is pessimized by' — the very cost the position holds against the always-check pole. The advocate self-flags this in risks yet keeps it load-bearing in the core argument.

'All three are native lowerings of the same source contract' is uncarded for the checked tier: N002 covers only unflagged (wrapping) versus flagged (poison) arithmetic; no card in the corpus covers trap intrinsics, check lowering cost, or the soundness of combined check+flag emission — and the position's own risks list (lowering-order hazards) admits the checked lowering is where frontend soundness bugs of the F003-negative-scope kind live. The claim of zero new semantics is therefore unsupported at the exact tier that differentiates the position.

'For every contract class in the corpus there is both an assume encoding and a sound omission fallback' overclaims from an admittedly incomplete evidence phase: the N-cards' notes state 'many stalled subagents... treat coverage as incomplete overall,' purity has no IR violation-semantics card at all, and pointer provenance (int-ptr casts, threat model 'often no') has no carded defined fallback. The exhaustiveness claim is exactly the kind the caveats file exists to block.

Every advocate's affirmative case derives source-language policy from N-cards whose negative scope uniformly disclaims 'not a final source-language policy' and whose design_generalization_confidence is 'low' — the precise move misleading-comparisons.jsonl entry 5 forbids ('IR metadata solves source design'). Since all three positions lower to the identical IR channels (noalias, nsw/nuw, range, inbounds), N001-N005 are policy-neutral among them and cannot discriminate; each advocate is citing the substrate as if it endorsed their source policy.

Two advocates (Positions 2 and 3) cite the threat-model remedy column as corroboration ('already contains my position's answer'; 'is a graduated-obligations table'), and Position 1 leans on it for confinement. That file is scaffolding with an unfilled 'Evidence needed' column, and caveats.md line 19 states implementation files 'should constrain later debates by asking what can be represented, verified, preserved, and tested, not decide source syntax or semantics.' Citing it as endorsement violates the corpus's own evidence discipline.

All three advocates repeated a stale caveat and drew a false conclusion from it: they cited caveats.md (dated Jun 30) for 'no confirmed memory-model coverage' and declared the A/D/C/J card series absent from the corpus. Those cards exist — notes/phase2-concurrency-findings.jsonl (C001-C006, dated Jul 1, after caveats.md), phase2-jit-findings.jsonl (J001-J005), phase2-arrays-findings.jsonl (A001-A010), phase2-dispatch-findings.jsonl (D001-D010). The C-cards in particular bear directly on this debate's topic and reverse several shared assumptions; the debate ran on a stale evidence view. (Process defect to log: the debate brief's card ranges and its file list are inconsistent — the files containing A/D/C/J were omitted from the assigned reading.)

C003 caps everyone's claimed payoff and nobody priced it: memory models forbid invented writes, re-loading a value into multiple observations, and caching atomics/volatiles across iterations regardless of contract policy. The F002 optimization classes all advocates treat as the prize are partially unavailable on possibly-shared memory in ANY concurrent language, so the performance stakes of the violation-semantics choice are overstated by an unquantified amount until the sharing story (e.g., C004-style type-level non-sharing) is fixed.

unsupportedClaims: Position 1: 'Since this language is LLVM-targeted...' — the target backend is an unstated design assumption, not a decided fact; the capability-map files are scaffolding and MLIR (A001-A004) is an equally corpus-supported substrate with different contract channels (e.g., distinct_objects in A001).

Position 1: 'If violation has ANY defined semantics — trap, wrap, log, diagnostic — ... the assumption itself is never free' — no card supports this trichotomy, and C005 (racing loads are undef, not UB) plus N001's freeze(poison) show the substrate itself uses defined-but-nondeterministic violation semantics that still permit optimization without checks.

Position 1: 'Alternatives must either rebuild this validation ecosystem for novel semantics or pay unremovable runtime checks' — unsupported; rival policies emit the same IR contract channels and inherit the same Alive2/InstCombine tooling (N006 validates IR transforms, not source policies).

Position 1: Fortran framing that 'every program is one aliasing mistake from UB' — F005 says compilers may optimize assuming conformance with dummy-argument rules; the card never characterizes violation consequences as UB. The inference is plausible but uncarded.

Position 1: F009 'is how the frontend discharges the F003 obligation that metadata be true and survive lowering' — F009 evidences semantic VISIBILITY in SIL (ARC ops, high-level info), not a truth-preservation or verification discipline; it does not discharge any soundness obligation.

Position 2: 'Runtime checking degenerates into don't-emit-the-contract' — contradicted by check-dominated regions (a passed guard makes the fact true on all defined paths) and by A005's documented runtime-pointer-check-plus-versioning strategy; no card supports the degeneration claim.

Position 2: 'Several contract classes are not runtime-checkable at meaningful cost or at all' — plausible for scoped noalias and purity, but no card addresses dynamic checkers (Miri/TSan-class tools are uncarded in the read set); stated as fact, is actually an evidence gap.

Position 2: 'If the professionals who define these contracts need mechanized proof, trusting application programmers is untenable' — an analogy, not evidence: N006 (medium confidence, one 2-1 vote) concerns optimizer-transform correctness, not the empirical reliability of programmer assertions; no card measures assertion-defect rates.

Position 3: 'A debug build lowers to check-and-trap — all three are native lowerings of the same source contract' — trap/check lowering mechanics are uncarded (the advocate's own gaps list admits this), so 'my position costs no new semantics' is unsupported at its differentiating tier.

Position 3: 'An inserted runtime check is a guard the optimizer already respects, not an alien construct' — a reverse reading of N003 beyond its stated scope (integer division, generalization confidence low); the card documents cost, not compatibility.

Position 3: 'An optimizer-first language that never assumes has no reason to exist' — rhetorical and aimed at a strawman; no advocated rival proposes never-assume (Position 2 assumes all proven facts), and F002 explicitly does not quantify what non-assumption forfeits.

All positions: variants of 'this policy keeps/forfeits the full payoff' — F002's negative scope and caveats.md state documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude; every comparative performance claim in this debate is structural conjecture, as each advocate partially concedes but continues to treat as load-bearing.
missingConsiderations: The memory model is a prior decision, not a parallel one: C002 shows deployed languages already split on exactly this debate's question at the concurrency level (UB races in C/C++/Rust/Swift vs bounded outcomes in Java/JS/Go, including Go's race-detection halts), C004 shows type-level race freedom is the prerequisite for ANY 'proven facts stay true at runtime' story, and C005 shows LLVM already assigns a sub-UB violation semantics (undef) to racy loads. No advocate treated race semantics as a dependency that must be fixed before contract-violation semantics can even be defined.

Speculate-and-deoptimize as a fourth policy (J001, J002, J005): assume the contract, guard cheaply, invalidate/recompile on violation — sound, no source UB, and per J002 enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally perform. Likely rejected for an AOT optimizer-first language on warmup/memory grounds (J005), but it must be enumerated and rejected with reasons, not omitted; hybrid forms (link-time or install-time specialization with guard-based fallback bodies) were never discussed.

Runtime alias checks with code versioning as existing precedent (A005): LLVM's vectorizers already buy noalias facts via runtime pointer checks plus fallback loops, trading code size for assume-grade inner loops. This is direct corpus evidence for costing the checked tier that none of the three advocates cited, and it partially collapses the assume-vs-check dichotomy structuring the whole debate.

ABI and linking boundaries can falsify contracts independently of programmer honesty: D008 documents ELF semantic interposition restricting interprocedural assumptions for exported symbols, and D004 shows optimization already depends on codegen-unit/LTO boundaries. A purity or noalias fact 'proven' intra-module can be false after dynamic linking; no advocate addressed contract emission across linkage boundaries, which the threat model does not even list as a row.

Violation observability and forensics as a design axis: the debate topic is literally 'what should happen,' yet no advocate designed the diagnosis story beyond debug builds — e.g., trap-with-report semantics as precedented by Go's race-detector halts (C002), or shipping check-enabled canary builds. If violations are poison-silent in release (Positions 1 and 3), the language needs a carded story for how a false contract is ever discovered post-deployment; none exists.

MLIR as an alternative or complementary substrate (A001-A004): distinct_objects and in-bounds contracts at the MemRef level, plus Linalg/Affine structured semantics, offer contract channels above LLVM's — relevant to whether 'the substrate forces UB semantics' (Positions 1 and 2 both argue from LLVM specifics) and to F009-style semantic-IR strategies. Uncited by all.

Purity may need decomposition before any policy applies: the corpus's only purity evidence (F006) is restriction-based, there is no IR violation-semantics card, and C003/C006 suggest effect and publication facts interact with the memory model differently than aliasing facts. Treating 'noalias, bounds, overflow-freedom, purity' as one policy question is itself an unexamined assumption; the right answer may be per-contract-class, which only Position 3 gestures at without evidence.

Process: the debate brief's evidence-card ranges (A/D/C/J) and its assigned file list are inconsistent, and all three advocates concluded the cards do not exist when they exist at notes/phase2-{arrays,dispatch,concurrency,jit}-findings.jsonl. Per decision-gates.md, this debate's output should be marked as run on incomplete evidence and re-opened after the C- and J-cards are formally admitted.
result.sharpestTension: The debate's sharpest unresolved tension is that all three positions fail simultaneously, in different ways, on the same point: the contract class with the highest attested value is the one none of them can handle at their own stated standard. Noalias-class non-interference has the strongest optimization payoff in the corpus (F001, F002), unbounded blast radius when falsely assumed (F003, N001), no runtime-check story because disjointness is a whole-execution property (no card exists; Position 3's middle rung is empty exactly here), only partial provability in the dynamic-aliasing, FFI, and concurrency cases where it would pay most (threat-model rows: 'sometimes no', 'often no', 'no'; Position 2's rejection bites hardest exactly here), and — per the C-cards the debate never read — falsifiability by data races even when compiler-proven (C002, C004, C005; Position 1's 'safe code has zero UB' theorem quantifies only over single-threaded executions). So on the highest-value contract, Position 1 collapses to trust-inside-unsafe with no diagnosis story, Position 2 collapses to forfeit-the-contract, and Position 3 collapses to Position 1 plus mode complexity. Worse, this cannot be adjudicated in the debate's current frame: whether 'the contract is false at runtime' is even a well-defined event depends on the memory model (UB races make the question moot by fiat; bounded races force an answer), and C002 shows that choice is open and prior. The debate is running one decision too early, on the premise — disclaimed by every N-card's negative scope — that IR-level violation semantics can settle source-level policy.

result.position: Adopt DRF-SC with bounded racy behavior: data-race-free programs are sequentially consistent; data races are program errors but produce bounded outcomes (no undefined behavior), and implementations may detect a race and halt (Go/Java direction). Sharing proofs in the type system are an optimization gradient, not a soundness precondition; the optimizer accepts Go-style implementation constraints on memory it cannot prove unshared.
result.coreArgument:
The debate is not about DRF-SC itself — every major language (C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Rust, Swift, Go) converges on DRF-SC as the baseline contract (C001). The only live question is what racy programs mean, and the documented split is UB (C/C++/Rust/Swift) versus bounded outcomes (Java/JavaScript/Go) (C002). For a language whose one inviolable rule is soundness ('ergonomics may be sacrificed, soundness may not'), bounded races are the option that keeps semantics compositional: under UB races, a single race anywhere retroactively voids the meaning of the entire execution, so every property the optimizer or type system establishes is conditional on a global no-race proof — a proof my assigned 'simpler types' design deliberately does not perform, and which even Rust only delivers inside the safe subset (C004's negative scope: unsafe code and wrong Send/Sync impls are UB anyway). Bounded semantics cap the blast radius (C002's own framing of the tradeoff) and make detect-and-halt a legal implementation behavior, turning races from semantic annihilation into diagnosable defects.

The performance objection is weaker than its reputation, on three evidence-grounded legs. (a) The optimizer freedoms lost are a finite, concrete, implementable list — no invented writes on paths that would not store, no letting one read observe two values, no register-caching of synchronization variables across iterations, fences as motion barriers (C003) — not an open-ended prohibition; and the actual performance delta between bounded and UB races is explicitly unquantified in the corpus in either direction (C002 negative scope), so the rival position's headline benefit is itself an evidence gap. (b) The tax applies only to memory the compiler cannot prove unshared. The corpus's strongest confirmed theme is that proven non-interference — not race UB — is the central optimizer enabler (F001, F002): where the frontend proves non-sharing via type-level facts (Rust-style Send/Sync and exclusivity, C004), it can emit full-strength noalias facts (F003) and reclaim the F002 class of optimizations (redundant-load elimination, store forwarding, code motion around calls). Bounded race semantics then serve as the sound fallback for the unproven residue — which is exactly optimizer-first philosophy: performance flows from facts the compiler can verify, not from programmer promises that races never happen. A missing or wrong sharing annotation degrades performance; under the UB position the same missing proof is a soundness hole. (c) The LLVM target already speaks this dialect: LLVM grades atomic orderings from NotAtomic to SequentiallyConsistent with per-level transformation legality, and treats racing non-atomic loads as undef rather than instant UB (C005) — the IR's own model sits closer to bounded than to C++-style UB, so a lowering path exists (with real constraints I own in the concessions). Supporting evidence: managed-runtime experience shows synchronization costs can be conditionally recovered by analysis such as lock elimination (F007, weighed as low-generalization per its scope limits), and Java final fields show a bounded-model language can still carve narrow, optimizer-compatible publication guarantees like initialization safety (C006), so cheap immutable publication is not forfeited.

Net: bounded races buy unconditional soundness and debuggability at a cost that is (i) confined to non-proven-unshared memory, (ii) enumerable as concrete pass restrictions (C003), and (iii) empirically unquantified — while the alternative buys an unmeasured speedup by making the whole language's soundness contingent on race-freedom the design cannot check. Under this project's stated priority ordering, that is the wrong trade to gamble on; the burden of proof sits on UB advocates to produce the missing cost data, not on us to accept unsoundness by default.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes DRF-SC as the cross-language consensus baseline (C, C++, Java, JavaScript, Rust, Swift, Go), so my position's DRF-SC core is uncontroversial and the debate reduces to racy-program semantics only.

Documents the exact split I advocate one side of — UB races vs bounded races — including that Go explicitly constrains implementations and allows race-detection halts (my detect-and-halt clause), and its negative scope concedes no performance quantification of the difference exists, which neutralizes the rival position's headline claim.

Provides the concrete, finite list of optimizer freedoms lost on possibly-shared memory (no invented writes, no double-observed reads, no register-caching across iterations, fence motion barriers) — showing the cost of my position is enumerable pass restrictions, not open-ended optimizer paralysis; its negative scope also grounds my concession that LLVM undef-racing-load semantics differ from source-level bounded models.

Shows sharing can be a type-level fact (Send/Sync + &mut exclusivity), which is my mechanism for recovering full optimization on proven-unshared memory; its negative scope (unsafe code and wrong Send/Sync impls are UB) also shows the rival type-system-only route does not deliver unconditional soundness either.

LLVM's graded atomic-ordering ladder with per-level transformation legality, plus racing non-atomic loads being undef rather than instant UB, shows the LLVM target has native vocabulary for bounded-race lowering and per-access strength selection.
Java final-field initialization safety shows a bounded-race language can still carve narrow, optimizer-compatible publication guarantees, so my position does not forfeit cheap immutable publication.

Workflow-confirmed finding that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules are the central optimizer enablers — supporting my claim that optimization value comes from proven non-interference, not from declaring races UB.

Identifies the specific optimization classes unlocked by proving a location is not modified/shared — these are exactly what my design reclaims on memory the type system proves unshared, confining the bounded-race tax to the residue.

LLVM noalias attributes/metadata can carry frontend-proven disjointness to the optimizer — the concrete lowering channel for my fast path — with the caveat that metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are true, which my slow-but-sound fallback respects.

HotSpot escape analysis and conditional lock elimination show implementations in the managed/bounded tradition can recover some synchronization and allocation costs via analysis — weighed cautiously per its 'low' design-generalization rating and implementation-dependence caveats.

concessions: The single most load-bearing empirical question — the real performance delta between bounded and UB race semantics — is explicitly unquantified (C002 negative scope; listed as open in implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md). I cannot prove the tax is small; I can only show opponents cannot prove it is large.

C003's constraints are real and spread wide in a language without strong sharing types: if most heap memory is 'possibly shared', Go-style constraints tax ordinary code. My mitigation (type-level non-sharing facts per C004/F001) reintroduces type machinery and partially erodes the 'simpler types' selling point of my assigned position.

LLVM lowering is not free: LLVM's undef-on-racing-load semantics are documented as not identical to source-level bounded models (C003 negative scope), and faithful lowering of ordinary potentially-shared accesses likely requires stronger-than-NotAtomic orderings, which C005 confirms forbid specific transformations (splitting, narrowing, rematerialization, hoisting, DSE variants).

All C-series cards are provisional direct-extraction, not adversarially verified, and rest largely on secondary sources (research.swtch.com survey, JSR-133 FAQ); primary texts (C++ standard, JLS chapter 17) were not fetched (implementation file 'Open' note). The corpus caveats also warn it is aliasing-heavy by selection, so concurrency coverage is thin relative to the aliasing findings I lean on.

Only F- and C-series cards were present in the five files I was directed to read; the N/A/D/J card ranges referenced in the ground rules were not in those files, so this case is argued from a partial deck.

Detect-and-halt is permission, not obligation: an always-on race detector has runtime cost the corpus does not measure, and without it 'bounded' outcomes for races may still be arbitrary-but-memory-safe wrong answers, which is better than UB but not correctness.
risks: If future benchmarks show a large bounded-race tax on the non-proven residue, the optimizer-first mandate points the other way, and the type system would have to grow to Rust strength anyway — at which point bounded semantics for safe code becomes belt-and-suspenders we paid optimizer freedom for (mitigated only at unsafe/FFI boundaries).

Specification risk: precisely bounding racy outcomes without admitting out-of-thin-air values is historically hard (Java's memory-model revisions); the corpus contains no card on this, so my position risks committing to a semantics that is expensive or subtly unsound to specify — flagged as a gap, not evidence.

Boundary punctures: foreign threads, callbacks, signal handlers, finalizers, and unsafe/FFI code (open research questions in implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md) execute outside our codegen control and could violate the bounded guarantee, forcing either conservative codegen at boundaries or documented exceptions that weaken the headline promise.

The recovery mechanism I rely on (type-proven non-sharing eliminating the bounded-race tax) is a composition claim no single card states directly; if C003-style constraints cannot actually be dropped on proven-unshared memory in the chosen IR pipeline, my performance story weakens substantially.
Secondary-source dependency: if primary sources (JLS 17, C++ standard, Go spec details) contradict the survey-derived C-cards on any load-bearing detail, parts of this case need re-arguing.
evidenceGaps: Empirical cost data for bounded-race semantics vs UB-race semantics on realistic optimized workloads — explicitly listed as open in implementation/concurrency-atomics-memory-model.md and in C002's negative scope. This is the decisive gap for the whole debate, for both sides.

Pass-by-pass legality and measured cost of lowering ordinary potentially-shared accesses at LLVM's weaker atomic orderings (e.g., Unordered-class) versus NotAtomic: C005 confirms the gradation exists and that pass-specific legality varies, but nothing quantifies the delta.
Primary-source verification of the Java and Go model details currently sourced from a secondary survey and the JSR-133 FAQ (JLS chapter 17 and C++ standard fetches were blocked).

Formal specifiability: whether a bounded racy-outcome semantics can be stated soundly and tractably (out-of-thin-air exclusion, coherence for mixed-size accesses) — no card in the corpus addresses this at all.

Direct evidence that bounded-race optimizer constraints (C003) can be fully lifted on memory proven unshared by the type system — the F002/C004 composition my performance argument assumes; plausible from the cards but not stated by any single card.

Runtime overhead of detect-and-halt implementations (Go-race-detector-style) if offered as a supported production mode, and GPU/heterogeneous memory-model interaction — both open in the implementation file.

result.position: Trap-by-default: integer overflow traps deterministically in all build modes (Ada direction); floating point is strict IEEE-754 by default; every relaxation (wrapping, unchecked/assume-no-overflow, fast-math, contraction) is an explicit opt-in with lexically visible scope, and unchecked opt-ins must sit behind a proof or unsafe boundary.
result.coreArgument:
The debate frame fixes two constraints: the language is optimizer-first, and soundness may not be sacrificed. Trap-by-default is the only integer-overflow default in the candidate set that satisfies both simultaneously, and the corpus's own LLVM evidence shows why.

(1) Overflow facts are contracts, and contracts need truth-makers. N002 establishes that LLVM separates default arithmetic (unflagged `add` wraps modulo 2^n) from optimization promises (`nuw`/`nsw` make overflow poison): 'overflow facts are contracts, not mere annotations.' The corpus's implementation warning (implementation/numeric-semantics-and-poison.md) states the frontend may emit these contracts only when 'source semantics, verifier, runtime checks, or unsafe boundary make the promises true.' Now compare the defaults. Wrap-by-default makes source semantics genuinely modular, which *forbids* emitting nsw/nuw and permanently destroys value-range reasoning in default code — the optimizer must preserve wrapping. C-style UB-by-default licenses nsw everywhere but only by injecting source-level UB, which the corpus explicitly lists as an unresolved soundness question and which the debate's ground rules prohibit resolving by fiat. Trap-by-default is unique: after every checked operation, non-overflow is true *by construction* on the continuing path, so the frontend can truthfully emit nsw/nuw on dominated recomputations and `range` facts (N004: range metadata is 'an optimizer-facing semantic promise', valid only if actually true — and a checked default is a generator of true range facts). Runtime checks are one of only four sanctioned truth-makers named in the corpus, and the only one available by default before proof tooling exists.

(2) The 'most-defined default' is the safe end of LLVM's own lattice. N001 confirms LLVM orders semantics from immediate UB through poison, undef, freeze to concrete values, with valid transforms moving only toward more-defined semantics, and documents that raw undef is deprecated because per-use nondeterminism invalidates ordinary rewrites (x*2 → x+x). A source language that defaults to poison-like or UB-like overflow puts every user program at the dangerous end of that lattice; a trapping default keeps all default-mode programs at the fully-defined end, where every LLVM transform is unconditionally valid. Relaxation then moves specific, marked regions toward less-defined semantics deliberately — which is exactly the direction of reasoning LLVM's model is built to audit.

(3) Relaxations are precisely where the bugs live, so they must be explicit and scoped. N006 confirms that InstCombine requires flag-sensitive transforms (nuw/nsw and, analogously, fast-math flags) to carry dedicated tests, negative tests, and usually Alive2 proofs. The compiler community's own discipline treats these flags as the highest-risk semantic surface in the pipeline. A language whose *default* semantics silently grants those flags (UB overflow, fast-math FP) maximizes exposure to exactly the transform class LLVM itself handles with the most ceremony. Explicit, visible opt-in scopes align source accountability with the IR's risk model, and they satisfy the corpus's standing rule (notes/caveats.md) that relaxed claims carry the burden of proof.

(4) Strict IEEE FP default follows from evidence discipline, not from FP evidence. The corpus is explicit (numeric-semantics-and-poison.md 'Still undecided'; every N-card's negative_scope) that strict-vs-fast FP, contraction, reassociation, and reproducibility have no verified coverage. With zero verified evidence quantifying fast-math benefit and N006-grade evidence that FP flags are proof-demanding hazards, the sound null hypothesis is: emit no fast-math flags the programmer did not write. Strict-by-default is the only FP default that cannot be wrong about semantics; it can only be slow, and an optimizer-first, performance-transparent language can surface that cost honestly through visible opt-ins (e.g., scoped fma/reassoc blocks).

(5) Deterministic in all builds avoids a semantic fork. A debug/release split (Rust-style) means the shipped artifact runs semantics that were never tested; it also means the optimizer works on different definedness in different modes. One deterministic semantics keeps 'tested program' and 'optimized program' the same program. (I flag this as principle plus lattice-spirit reasoning — the corpus has no card directly on build-mode divergence; see gaps.)

(6) The honest cost, stated up front: N003 shows the mechanism by which traps cost performance — immediate-UB-like operations (div-by-zero) cannot be speculated above guards, and trapping checks are side effects constraining code motion and vectorization. I do not deny this. My claim is narrower and defensible: the corpus contains no quantification of that cost (caveats.md: documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude'), soundness is non-negotiable in this debate, and the trap default uniquely converts its runtime cost into true optimizer-consumable facts plus a lawful pathway (per F006's SPARK analogy, proof-discharged check elimination; per the unsafe-boundary rule, scoped unchecked regions) to buy the performance back where it matters — without ever making default-mode programs unsound.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Core of the argument: LLVM separates wrapping default arithmetic from nuw/nsw poison contracts, and the card states overflow facts are 'contracts, not mere annotations.' Trap-by-default is the only source default that makes those contracts true by construction (post-check paths provably did not overflow), whereas wrap-by-default forbids emitting them and UB-by-default makes them true only by sacrificing source soundness.

Range metadata is a semantic promise with poison consequences if false. A trapping default is a systematic generator of *true* range facts on every post-check path, giving the frontend a lawful, high-volume channel for feeding the optimizer — something wrap-by-default cannot provide and UB-by-default provides only unsoundly.

LLVM's definedness lattice (immediate UB > poison > undef > freeze > concrete) with transforms valid only toward more-defined semantics, plus the deprecation of raw undef for breaking rewrites like x*2 -> x+x, supports keeping default source semantics at the fully-defined end (trap = defined behavior) and treating movement toward poison/UB as explicit, audited opt-in.

InstCombine requires flag-sensitive folds (nuw/nsw and fast-math) to carry negative tests and Alive2 proofs — i.e., LLVM's own contributors treat exactly these relaxation flags as the riskiest semantic surface. This supports making all such relaxations explicit, scoped, and verified at the source level rather than silently granted by defaults; it is my primary card for strict-IEEE-by-default. (Note: medium confidence, one constituent claim was 2-1.)

Cited honestly for both sides: it proves LLVM can correctly represent and preserve guard-dependent, non-speculatable numeric operations (so trapping semantics are implementable without heroics), while also being the corpus's best evidence for the *cost* of my position — guards block hoisting/speculation. I cite it to show the cost model is understood, representable, and not soundness-threatening.

Analogy, weighted accordingly: SPARK shows a verification-oriented route where restricted semantics plus proof yields optimizer-friendly facts. It supports the escape-path story of trap-by-default — checks eliminated by proof rather than by weakening the default — but it is aliasing evidence, not overflow evidence, and 'may not map directly... without usability costs.'

LLVM no-alias encodings are sound only if frontend obligations are true and 'UB consequences belong to IR contract, not necessarily desired source semantics.' Same-principle support from the aliasing domain: IR-level UB machinery is a tool for the frontend, not a template for source defaults — which is exactly the trap-by-default stance on overflow.

Used for a risk, not the case: exception/unwind representation measurably affects optimization, so if traps are made catchable or precise-ordered, EH representation costs become a real design coupling that must be settled deliberately.

The implementation warning enumerates the only sanctioned truth-makers for emitting numeric contracts: 'source semantics, verifier, runtime checks, or unsafe boundary.' Runtime checks — i.e., trap-by-default — are the only truth-maker available by default before a verifier exists, which is the load-bearing step in argument (1).

citedEvidence.cardId: notes/caveats.md standing rule + misleading-comparisons.jsonl entry 5 (file-level, not numbered cards)
citedEvidence.howItSupports: The corpus's evidence discipline places the burden of proof on relaxed claims and warns that IR metadata is only a conditional carrier of source truths. This grounds the meta-argument that unproven relaxations (fast-math, unchecked overflow) must be opt-in, making strict/trapping the default posture.

concessions: The corpus contains zero quantified performance data on checked-overflow overhead. Claims like 'checks cost only a few percent' or 'checks destroy vectorization' are equally unevidenced here; caveats.md explicitly warns documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude. My position may be paying an unmeasured price.

N003 genuinely cuts against me mechanically: trap-guarded operations resist speculation, hoisting, and code motion, and a trapping default multiplies such guards across all default-mode arithmetic. Wrap-by-default (LLVM's own unflagged add semantics per N002) is the zero-runtime-cost sound alternative, and I cannot cite corpus evidence that trap's optimizer-fact benefits outweigh its code-motion costs.

'Deterministic traps in all builds', if read as strict program-order trap precision, is even more constraining than plain trapping; a defensible design likely needs a carefully specified weaker trap-ordering semantics (e.g., traps may fire early/coalesce within a region), and the corpus has nothing on how to specify that soundly.

My strict-IEEE FP default rests on evidence discipline and the N006 hazard analogy, not on verified FP evidence — the corpus says outright that strict-vs-fast defaults, FMA/contraction, reassociation, and reproducibility are undecided. An opponent's fast-math-scoped-default position is exactly as unproven as mine.

The Ada/SPARK lineage I invoke is supported in the corpus only for aliasing/verification style (F006), not for numeric semantics; 'Ada direction' as applied to overflow is currently an appeal to a tradition the corpus has not verified.

A/D/C/J cards were referenced in the debate framing but were not present in the five files I was permitted to read (they exist in other corpus files per directory listing); if those cards contain numeric-relevant evidence, my case is argued without it.

Per ground rule 3, direct-extraction cards are provisional; more importantly, all N-cards carry design_generalization_confidence: low and explicitly state they support optimizer-facing LLVM contracts, 'not a final source-language policy' — so even my strongest cards constrain rather than select the source default.
risks: Opt-out creep: if checked arithmetic is slow in hot loops and the proof/verifier path is immature at launch, users will blanket-wrap code in unchecked scopes, socially recreating C-style UB while the language nominally claims trap-by-default. Mitigation (scoped, auditable opt-ins plus proof-discharged check elimination per the F006 analogy) is unproven.

Vectorization and code-motion regression: per the N003 mechanism, per-operation guards constrain speculation; whether LLVM's overflow intrinsics and check-combining recover enough performance in loops is unmeasured in the corpus. An optimizer-first language that loses standard numeric benchmarks by default risks its entire positioning.

Trap semantics couple into exception/unwind design: F008 confirms EH representation affects optimization quality. If traps are observable/handleable, the trap default imports EH-representation costs into every arithmetic operation; if traps only abort, the language needs a separate recoverable-check story. Either branch is an unsettled design dependency.

Strict IEEE default may forfeit FMA contraction and reassociation-driven vectorization by default, potentially large on FP-heavy targets (unquantified in corpus), and makes GPU/approximate-math targets (explicitly listed as undecided) awkward without a well-designed scoped-relaxation system from day one.

Deterministic-in-all-builds removes the escape valve other languages use (release-mode wrap/UB) — if the overhead is high, there is no cheap 'ship fast' mode, and pressure lands entirely on opt-out ergonomics, which conflicts with the language's willingness to be ugly/verbose.
evidenceGaps: Quantified runtime and code-size overhead of trap-on-overflow defaults across scalar and vectorized workloads on current LLVM (e.g., via sadd/ssub/smul.with.overflow lowering), including how much overhead check-combining, hoisting-of-guards, and post-check range facts recover. No corpus card touches this.

Evidence on whether LLVM actually exploits post-check facts well in practice: does explicit-check code regain nsw-grade optimization via dominated range/known-bits reasoning, or is the theoretical truth-maker argument (N002/N004-based) hollow in the current optimizer?

Primary-source cards on Ada/GNAT overflow-check semantics and cost, and on SPARK's static discharge rate for overflow proofs — needed to convert my 'Ada direction' framing and F006 analogy into actual numeric-domain evidence.

Field data on debug/release overflow-semantics divergence (e.g., Rust wrap-in-release, Zig modes): bug-escape rates and optimizer implications — needed to support or refute 'deterministic in all builds' beyond principle.

A sound specification pattern for relaxed trap ordering (imprecise/coalescible traps) and its interaction with LLVM's side-effect and speculation model — the key to reducing the N003-style code-motion cost without giving up trapping.

Any verified evidence on strict-IEEE vs fast-math performance deltas, FMA contraction defaults, reproducibility requirements, and GPU/approximate math — all explicitly listed as undecided in the corpus; both my FP default and its rivals are currently argued on discipline, not data.

The A/D/C/J card sets (arrays, dispatch, concurrency, JIT phase-2 findings) exist in the corpus but were outside my permitted read set; concurrency cards in particular could bear on trap semantics under multithreading (trap ordering vs memory model) and should be read before a decision.

Comparative evidence for the strongest rival: how much optimization is actually lost by wrap-by-default (which forbids nsw/nuw per N002) in real programs — if that loss is small, the case for paying trap overhead to obtain contracts weakens materially.

result.position: MLIR-first: define a custom high-level MLIR dialect that carries ownership, effects, shape/layout, and schedule facts as first-class ops/types/attributes with verifier obligations; reuse affine/linalg/vector/memref infrastructure where the program fits; progressively lower to the LLVM dialect, emitting LLVM's noalias/alias.scope/attribute encodings as the final, derived form of already-verified facts (the Mojo/Flang/Swift-SIL direction).
result.coreArgument: The corpus converges on three propositions that, taken together, make MLIR-first the strategy that best preserves source-level facts. (1) The facts worth preserving are known and language-level: non-interference around writes, ownership/exclusivity, effect purity, and structured loop/array shape are the attested optimizer enablers (F001, F002, F005, F006). (2) LLVM IR's native carriers for these facts are point encodings that arrive after structure is destroyed and are fragile by the corpus's own accounting: noalias/alias.scope metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are true and only useful if it survives lowering (F003 negative scope), and our own pass-consumer matrix lists 'lowering strips metadata' and 'false noalias causes miscompile' as the standing hazards for ScopedNoAliasAA, with LoopVectorize needing dependence/alignment/bounds/FP facts that plain LLVM IR must reconstruct from scalar soup (llvm-pass-consumer-matrix.csv). The corpus's misleading-comparisons file warns directly that 'IR metadata solves source design' is a trap: metadata is a conditional carrier, not a preservation strategy. (3) The one workflow-confirmed precedent on IR strategy, Swift SIL (F009), says exactly what MLIR-first operationalizes: keep source-language semantics in an SSA IR long enough to optimize them, run language-level passes before LLVM IR generation so passes see information LLVM never would, and make performance-critical semantics (ARC there; ownership/effects/shape here) explicit instructions rather than implicit runtime effects. MLIR-first is SIL's confirmed design lesson plus infrastructure reuse instead of a bespoke IR: memref already reifies shape/strides/offset/memory-space and no-alias contracts like distinct_objects as typed IR with verifier obligations (A001); linalg preserves iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds expressly to support tiling, fusion, promotion, and vectorization (A002); affine makes dependence analysis tractable by construction (A003); vector provides the staged n-D abstraction that LLVM's 1-D vectors lack (A004). And the diversity of optimization-relevant facts the broader corpus surfaces — Halide's separable schedule layer (A006), ISPC's uniform/varying and mask semantics (A007), Futhark's uniqueness/consumption (A008), Chapel's first-class domains (A009), Fortran's DO CONCURRENT independence intent (A010) — is exactly the kind of extensible fact vocabulary that a fixed IR like LLVM cannot host natively but a custom dialect can, as ops and types the verifier checks rather than droppable metadata a pass may ignore. Crucially, MLIR-first is not an alternative to LLVM's encodings but their generator: progressive lowering ends in the LLVM dialect, where noalias parameter/return attributes and scoped-alias metadata (F003, F004) are emitted as conclusions derived from verified high-level facts (ownership types, distinct_objects, affine dependence proofs), which is the only route the corpus offers to make those UB-bearing promises sound rather than programmer-trusted. For an optimizer-first language where soundness is non-negotiable, verifier-checked structural facts that lower into LLVM's contracts dominate a strategy of hand-emitting those contracts directly from an AST.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The only workflow-confirmed (3-0 adversarially verified) finding on IR strategy: Swift SIL shows that an SSA IR preserving source-level semantics, optimized before LLVM IR generation, lets passes use information LLVM would not otherwise see, and that performance-critical semantics (ARC) should be explicit IR operations. This is the direct precedent for a high-level dialect above LLVM; MLIR-first is this lesson with shared infrastructure.

Establishes what must be preserved: language-level non-interference rules (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing/ownership) are the central optimizer enablers. These are source-level, structural facts — the kind a custom dialect can carry as types/ops, not facts recoverable from lowered LLVM IR by analysis.

Specifies the payoff mechanism — proving non-interference around writes enables register promotion, read/write elimination, and reordering — which defines the fact-preservation requirement any IR strategy is judged against.

Cuts both ways, in my favor: LLVM does offer noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias metadata (so progressive lowering has a real target), but its own negative scope says metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are true and only useful if it survives lowering. That fragility is the core argument for deriving these encodings from a verified higher-level IR instead of emitting them directly from the frontend.

LLVM noalias return is an allocation-like, stronger-than-restrict contract — but its negative scope says source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting it. An ownership-carrying dialect is precisely the layer where those rules live and are checked before the attribute is emitted.

SPARK shows ownership-with-transfer and side-effect-free expressions as a verification-oriented route to optimizer-friendly semantics; a custom dialect with verifier obligations is the IR-level analogue — facts as checkable invariants, matching the debate's soundness constraint.

(Provisional, direct-extraction.) MemRef already reifies shape, strides, offsets, memory spaces, and explicit no-alias/UB-bearing contracts (distinct_objects) as optimizer-visible IR — first-class typed structure rather than stripped-prone metadata; concrete reuse rather than greenfield engineering.

(Provisional.) Linalg preserves iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds expressly to support tiling, fusion, promotion, vectorization, and lowering — high-level loop/array facts that are destroyed before LLVM IR ever sees them under a direct-to-LLVM strategy.

(Provisional.) Affine shows that restricting index forms in the IR makes dependence analysis efficient and reliable, and encodes parallel bands/reductions explicitly — supplying by construction the 'no loop-carried deps' facts the pass-consumer matrix says LoopVectorize requires.

(Provisional.) The vector dialect's n-D virtual vectors with progressive lowering give a staged abstraction between loops and target SIMD that LLVM's 1-D vector model cannot express, demonstrating the value of the intermediate level MLIR-first adds.

(Provisional.) Enumerates what LLVM's Loop/SLP vectorizers actually need — alias/dependence facts, reductions/inductions, trip counts, FP flags, control-flow shape — i.e., the consumer side of the contract; a high-level dialect can carry these facts explicitly and lower them, rather than hoping LLVM analyses reconstruct them.

(Provisional.) Halide's explicit schedule layer shows schedule facts (tiling, loop order, vectorize/unroll/parallelize) can be program structure separate from algorithm semantics — a fact category with no LLVM IR home at all, but a natural custom-dialect attribute/op, supporting the 'schedule facts' component of my assigned position.

(Provisional.) ISPC's uniform/varying and execution-mask semantics are another optimization-relevant fact vocabulary that exists above LLVM IR; an extensible dialect can host such lane-uniformity facts, a fixed IR cannot without bolted-on conventions.

(Provisional.) Futhark's size-typed regular arrays and uniqueness/consumption rules show ownership-like facts enabling copy-avoidance in pure array updates — the kind of ownership/shape fact my dialect is specified to carry.

(Provisional.) DO CONCURRENT shows independence-intent loop forms whose restrictions are necessary but not sufficient — implying the IR must retain the intent and its obligations for later checking/lowering (Flang's MLIR-based direction), rather than discarding it at parse time.

concessions: The corpus's own caveats state it has NO workflow-verified coverage of MLIR, Mojo, Halide, ISPC, Chapel, or Futhark; every MLIR-specific card I cite (A001-A005) is a provisional direct extraction from a single documentation source, not adversarially verified.
