My position's infrastructure-reuse premise rests on the weakest evidence tier in the corpus, while the direct-to-LLVM story can cite mostly workflow-confirmed F-cards.

F009 (Swift SIL), my strongest confirmed card, supports 'a semantics-preserving IR above LLVM' generically — it validates a bespoke SIL-style IR at least as much as MLIR specifically. The choice of MLIR over a purpose-built IR is not settled by any confirmed card.

The A-cards' negative scopes are real: linalg docs are design rationale, not proof of profitability (A002); affine applies only where code fits affine restrictions (A003); vector faces target-legality and LLVM 1-D lowering constraints (A004); memref views can alias underlying storage and need verifier/runtime obligations (A001). The reuse payoff concentrates in array/loop-structured code; general pointer-heavy code gains much less from these dialects.

Our own fit-gap document (implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md) is essentially empty — every cell for alias, layout, and effects facts is TBD or 'missing primary sources'. Whether ownership/effects facts have a sound home in MLIR, and which MLIR passes preserve them, is currently unmapped, not merely unverified.

The metadata-fragility argument I use against direct-to-LLVM applies recursively: custom dialect attributes can be dropped or invalidated by MLIR's own passes and dialect-boundary lowerings; MLIR-first relocates the preservation problem, and only wins if verifier obligations actually close it — which no card in this corpus demonstrates.

Documentation evidence throughout the corpus shows what systems claim, not measured performance; nothing here quantifies that MLIR-first produces faster code than a careful direct-to-LLVM frontend (F002 negative scope; caveats.md).
risks: Engineering and maintenance cost: a custom dialect plus progressive lowering means designing, verifying, and maintaining two IR levels and every lowering between them; each dialect boundary is a new place to lose facts or introduce miscompiles — the pass-consumer matrix's 'lowering strips metadata' and 'false noalias causes miscompile' hazards now exist at more layers, not fewer.

Abstraction mismatch for the language's core facts: ownership and effects — the best-evidenced fact categories (F001, F006) — have no established MLIR dialect home (fit-gap rows: 'missing primary sources'); we would be pioneering novel dialect design in exactly the areas where reuse was the selling point, without Mojo/Modular-scale resources.

Uneven payoff profile: affine/linalg/vector reuse pays mainly for regular array/loop code; if the language's real workloads are pointer- and control-flow-heavy, we pay full MLIR complexity for facts that mostly lower to the same LLVM noalias/effect encodings a direct frontend could emit, and the opponent's simpler strategy wins on cost.

Version and ecosystem drift: MLIR dialect semantics and upstream pass behavior evolve; the corpus flags version-sensitivity even for stabler LLVM docs (caveats.md), and a strategy coupled to many upstream dialects inherits more drift surface than one coupled to LLVM IR alone.

Soundness burden under the debate's non-negotiable constraint: UB-bearing contracts (distinct_objects, noalias) appear at both MLIR and LLVM levels; a verifier gap at either level, or an unsound lowering between them, silently converts a preservation mechanism into a miscompile vector.
evidenceGaps: Adversarial verification of the MLIR cards: A001-A005 are single-source doc extractions; before MLIR-first can win, the memref/linalg/affine/vector claims need the same 3-0 adversarial treatment the F-cards received, plus version pinning.

Fill the fit-gap matrix: for each fact class (alias/noalias, ownership, effects, shape/layout, schedule), establish where it lives in MLIR, which upstream passes preserve vs. drop it, and what verifier obligations attach — every cell in implementation/mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md is currently TBD.

End-to-end preservation experiments: demonstrate (FileCheck-style, per the matrix's validation-tests column) that facts carried in a custom dialect survive progressive lowering into LLVM noalias/alias.scope/attributes and are actually consumed by ScopedNoAliasAA, LICM/GVN/DSE, SROA, and LoopVectorize — and measure how often they are stripped.

Case-study evidence on Mojo and Flang: the corpus contains zero verified coverage of the two flagship MLIR-first languages; their experience with fact preservation, dialect maintenance cost, and compile time is the most decision-relevant missing evidence.

Comparative baseline: a matched direct-to-LLVM prototype (same source facts, emitted as attributes/metadata from the frontend) versus the dialect route, with measured optimization outcomes, compile-time cost, and fact-survival rates — nothing in the corpus quantifies the delta my position depends on.

Ownership/effects dialect feasibility: primary sources or a prototype showing borrow/ownership and effect facts can be soundly represented and verified in MLIR (Swift SIL's OSSA is the analogue, F009, but it is not MLIR).

Missing card sets: the debate charter references N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but none of these appear in the seven files I was permitted to read; if they exist (e.g., a D-card on Mojo or C-card on compile-time costs), this argument must be re-checked against them before any decision.

result.position: Message-passing core: shared mutable memory does not exist in the core language; ownership of data transfers by move across channels/actors; atomics and any shared-memory constructs live only in an explicitly-marked systems module exposing LLVM-graded orderings.
result.coreArgument: An optimizer-first language should make the optimizer's single most valuable assumption — "this memory is not concurrently accessed by anyone else" — a structural invariant of the core language, not a per-program analysis result or a per-type proof obligation. The corpus's best-confirmed theme is that language-level non-interference rules are the central optimizer enabler (F001) and that their payoff is specifically proving non-modification around writes (F002). C003 gives the concrete bill for failing to prove it: wherever memory may be shared, the compiler loses the right to introduce writes, to re-load values, to cache values in registers across iterations, and to move code across fences. A message-passing core with move-only ownership transfer is the maximal non-interference rule: at every program point each datum has exactly one owning task, so every core-language access lowers as NotAtomic with the full transformation freedom LLVM grants at the bottom of its ordering ladder (C005), and the frontend obligations that make LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata sound (F003) are discharged by construction — the sender provably loses access at the send — rather than by programmer promise (Fortran/restrict-style, F001's caveat) or by a subtle borrow-checker boundary. Received messages are allocation-like fresh values disjoint from all other reachable storage, structurally analogous to LLVM's strong noalias-return fact (F004; flagged as extrapolation, since F004 is about return attributes). This position also dissolves the field's hardest open tradeoff instead of picking a side of it: C001 shows every major language converges on DRF-SC, and C002 shows they then split painfully between races-as-UB (maximal optimizer freedom, unbounded blast radius on bugs) and bounded races (soundness under bugs, but paying C003's optimization tax on all possibly-shared memory). In a core where shared mutable memory is unrepresentable, racy core programs cannot be written: DRF-SC holds trivially, soundness is preserved without bounding anything, and the UB-vs-bounded decision is confined to a small, explicitly-marked systems module whose operations map one-to-one onto LLVM's graded orderings (C005) — exactly the vocabulary the backend already understands. Feasibility is not speculative: SPARK demonstrates a verification-oriented ownership-transfer policy for pointer-like types (F006), and Rust's Send discipline shows cross-thread ownership transfer can be a checked, type-level fact (C004) — a channel send is precisely a checked Send. Immutable-after-construction publication (C006) gives an optimizer-compatible carve-out for the main workload message passing handles badly (shared read-mostly data) without reintroducing shared mutation. Finally, the debate's own charter neutralizes the traditional counterargument: the classic objections to actor/channel style are substantially ergonomic (verbosity, restructuring), and this language explicitly sacrifices ergonomics for optimizer transparency — but it may not sacrifice soundness, which is exactly what races-as-UB in a shared-memory core does the moment a bug exists.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Workflow-confirmed that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules are the central optimizer enablers across Rust, Fortran, SPARK; a single-owner message-passing core is the strongest possible such rule — non-interference for all core memory, everywhere, by construction.
Confirms the optimization value of alias information is proving non-interference around writes; exclusive per-task ownership proves exactly this globally, without per-location alias analysis.

Enumerates the concrete optimizer freedoms lost wherever memory may be shared (no introduced writes, no re-loading, no register caching across iterations, fence motion barriers); making non-sharing structural recovers all of these for the entire core language, which is the direct performance payoff of my position.

LLVM's NotAtomic-to-SequentiallyConsistent ordering ladder is exactly the target vocabulary my position prescribes: core memory lowers as NotAtomic (maximal transformation freedom), and the explicit systems module exposes the graded orderings verbatim rather than inventing a new model.

DRF-SC is the converged baseline contract; a core with no shared mutable memory satisfies DRF-SC trivially because data-race-free is the only expressible state, with channel operations supplying the happens-before edges.

Documents the UB-vs-bounded racy-semantics split with no performance quantification either way; my position sidesteps the dilemma for the core (racy core programs are unrepresentable) and confines it to the small systems module, shrinking the surface where the unresolved question matters.

Rust Send/Sync shows cross-thread ownership transfer can be a checked type-level fact and that optimizers may treat non-atomic memory as unshared when types prove it; a channel send is a checked Send, so the mechanism my core requires already exists in a shipped language.

SPARK's ownership policy with transfer for access types is workflow-confirmed evidence that ownership-transfer semantics are statically verifiable, supporting the feasibility of checked move-on-send without a full Rust borrow system.

LLVM noalias/alias.scope metadata is only sound if frontend obligations are actually true; structural non-sharing discharges those obligations by construction, whereas restrict-style contracts depend on programmer truthfulness (F001 caveat) — my position turns unsound-if-lied-about metadata into always-emittable metadata.

LLVM's noalias return models an allocation-like value disjoint from all caller-accessible storage; a received message under move-only transfer has the same disjointness shape, suggesting a strong existing IR encoding for channel-receive results (flagged: extrapolation from return attributes to receive semantics, not a confirmed mapping).

Java final-field initialization safety shows a narrow, optimizer-compatible publication guarantee for immutable-after-construction data; this is the principled carve-out letting a message-passing core share read-only data cheaply without reintroducing shared mutation.

concessions: All C-series cards are provisional direct extractions from secondary sources (research.swtch.com survey, JSR-133 FAQ, Nomicon), not adversarially verified like the F-series; my concurrency-specific claims rest on the weaker evidence tier.

Message-passing-only is sufficient but not necessary for data-race freedom: C004 shows Rust achieves DRF with shared memory via Send/Sync plus &mut exclusivity, so my position must win on invariant strength and optimizer/checker simplicity, not on necessity — and that simplicity advantage is qualitative, not evidenced.

The corpus contains zero performance quantification (caveats.md: documentation evidence 'does not quantify performance magnitude'); I cannot show that channel/actor transfer overhead is cheaper than shared-memory synchronization for any workload, which is a serious admission for an optimizer-first language.

Checked move-on-send without residual aliases still requires an ownership/linearity discipline that resembles a subset of a borrow checker (F006, C004); 'no borrow checker needed' would overstate my case.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection bias (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons entry 1); the strong F-card support for non-interference does not establish that concurrency-model choice is the highest-leverage design axis.

The flagship real-world exemplars of my position (Erlang, Pony, Swift actors) have no evidence cards at all — the implementation file lists them as explicitly open — so I argue from adjacent evidence (Rust Send, SPARK transfer), not from direct actor-language evidence.

The N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, and J001-J005 card series were not present in the five files I was directed to read; I could neither use nor rebut them, so opposing advocates citing them face no answer from me here.
risks: Systems-module leakage: if real performance code routinely needs the atomics/shared-memory module, optimizer conservatism (C003's full restriction list) spreads to every function touching module types, and the core guarantee becomes nominal — potentially worst-of-both-worlds. The core/module interaction semantics are load-bearing and currently unspecified.

Copy-instead-of-move fallback: if the transfer-checking discipline proves too restrictive and the implementation or programmers fall back to deep copies at channel boundaries, the design destroys the very performance it exists to enable; nothing in the corpus bounds this cost.

Miscompilation at channel boundaries: channel happens-before must be lowered to LLVM acquire/release atomics or fences (C005), and the strong disjointness metadata I want to emit is UB-if-false (F003); any soundness hole in the transfer checker converts directly into silent miscompiles rather than mere lost optimization.

FFI, foreign threads, callbacks, and signal handlers (the implementation file's open research questions) can mutate memory outside the core's model; without a containment story, 'unshared by construction' is false at exactly the boundaries where systems code lives.

Workloads built on shared mutable structures (concurrent hash maps, work-stealing deques, read-mostly caches with in-place update) may be inexpressible efficiently in the core; the C006 immutable-publication carve-out covers only frozen data, so such programs migrate wholesale to the systems module (see leakage risk).
The unresolved UB-vs-bounded question (C002) is confined, not solved: it reappears inside the systems module, and my position gives no guidance on which side that module should take.
evidenceGaps: Empirical cost data comparing channel/actor ownership transfer against shared-memory synchronization (locks, atomics, lock-free structures) on realistic workloads — no cards exist; the implementation file lists even bounded-race cost data as open.
Actor/message-passing language evidence: Erlang, Pony, Swift actors, Ada tasking memory models are explicitly open in the implementation file; my position's closest real exemplars are uncarded.

Lowering study: how channel send/receive happens-before maps to LLVM fences/atomic orderings, and which noalias/alias.scope facts provably survive that lowering (the F004 receive-disjointness analogy needs confirmation or refutation).

FFI and foreign-thread containment: what a message-passing core can soundly guarantee when linked against C libraries, signal handlers, and callback-driven runtimes — a listed research question with zero evidence.

Marginal optimizer benefit of structural non-sharing over Rust-style type-proven non-sharing (C004): if a Send/Sync-plus-exclusivity core recovers the same C003 freedoms at equal soundness, my position's added restriction buys nothing measurable — this comparison is the decisive unbenchmarked question of the debate.
Primary-source memory model texts (C++ standard, JLS chapter 17) were fetch-blocked during research; C001/C002/C003 rest on secondary surveys and FAQs.
The N, A, D, and J evidence-card series were absent from the files provided to me and need to be surfaced and weighed before any final decision.

result.position: Direct-to-LLVM with maximal metadata: skip MLIR; the frontend lowers straight to LLVM IR and encodes source-level facts immediately as noalias/alias.scope attributes and scoped metadata, range/nsw-style value facts, and fast-math flags (the Rust/Clang direction), with the source language designed so those facts are declared explicitly and map near-1:1 onto LLVM's contracts.
result.coreArgument: The verified core of this corpus is a non-interference corpus, and non-interference already has a documented, adversarially-checked, production-proven encoding in LLVM IR — while the MLIR alternative has, in this corpus, essentially zero verified evidence. F001 and F002 establish that the central optimizer enablers we know about are language-level non-aliasing/non-interference facts, and that their payoff is register promotion, redundant-read elimination, dead-store elimination, and reordering — all of which are performed by LLVM's own scalar passes (the consumer matrix rows for ScopedNoAliasAA, SROA/mem2reg, and LICM/GVN/DSE list exactly these consumers and the LLVM-level facts they require). F003 confirms LLVM provides first-class encodings for precisely these facts: noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias scoped metadata expressing disjoint access sets, with a documented subset/superset domain rule implemented in ScopedNoAliasAA. F004 shows LLVM's contract vocabulary is in places *stronger* than any source-language precedent (noalias returns model allocation-like disjointness that C99 restrict cannot even express) — meaning LLVM IR is not merely a lossy target but in this dimension a richer fact language than C's. Since every optimization the corpus verifies is ultimately executed by an LLVM pass consuming LLVM-level facts, any mid-level IR must eventually re-materialize its facts as these same attributes and metadata; direct emission removes one lossy, bug-prone translation layer and one unverified set of conversion passes. Meanwhile the corpus's own caveats state MLIR is among the topics with no verified coverage, the MLIR fit/gap scaffold is all TBD with 'missing primary sources' for layout and effects, and the MLIR cards (A001–A004) are provisional direct extractions. An evidence-first process cannot prefer the architecture whose load-bearing claims are the unverified ones. Finally, because this language is optimizer-first and may be ugly/verbose, we can invert the hard part of the Rust/Clang precedent: instead of inferring attributes from rich implicit semantics (the fragile step flagged in misleading-comparison #2), the source language can make the LLVM contract *be* the declared semantics — parameters declared disjoint, ranges declared, fast-math scoped explicitly — shrinking the frontend soundness obligation that F003's negative scope warns about. (This inversion is a design argument, not card-backed; noted under gaps.)

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Establishes that language-level non-aliasing/non-interference rules (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing, __restrict__) are the central verified optimizer enablers — i.e., the fact category my strategy optimizes for carrying is the one the corpus verifies as most valuable.

Names the concrete payoff optimizations (register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering) and grounds them in LLVM and GCC alias/mod-ref documentation — these are LLVM-pass-level optimizations, so the facts must reach LLVM IR in LLVM's vocabulary regardless of what sits above it; direct emission is the shortest sound path.

Directly verifies that LLVM IR has optimizer-facing encodings for exactly these facts: noalias attributes and alias.scope/noalias scoped metadata with a documented subset/superset semantics corroborated in the ScopedNoAliasAA implementation. This is the load-bearing card: the target encoding my position depends on is workflow-confirmed at high source-accuracy confidence.

Shows LLVM's contract vocabulary exceeds source-language precedent (noalias return is stronger than C99 restrict, allocation-like disjointness), with wording stable across current and historical docs — evidence that LLVM IR is a rich and stable fact language, not merely a low-level sink.

Fortran's optimization-friendly aliasing model is consumed via Flang, an LLVM frontend: a second production precedent (besides Rust/Clang) of source-level non-interference rules being exploited through the LLVM pipeline rather than requiring a preserved high-level optimizer IR for the aliasing payoff. (Caveat: Flang's internal use of MLIR/FIR is not covered by this corpus either way.)

SPARK shows a source-language route to making non-interference facts *true by construction* (ownership transfer, no non-benign aliasing, side-effect-free expressions) — exactly the property my strategy needs so the frontend can emit noalias/effect attributes without a mid-level verifier: the source rules discharge the F003 frontend obligations.

(Provisional) LLVM's Loop/SLP vectorizers consume alias/dependence facts, reductions/inductions, FP math flags, and runtime pointer checks — showing even the loop-level consumers of source facts are LLVM passes with LLVM-level inputs (including fast-math flags, part of my assigned encoding set), reachable without an MLIR layer.

Cited against interest and answered: Swift SIL verifies that a semantics-preserving pre-LLVM IR lets language-specific passes (ARC) see what LLVM would not. I concede this is the strongest verified counter-card; my rebuttal is that its verified content is about *language-specific* optimizations (reference counting) that our optimizer-first language may deliberately not have, and it evidences a custom SSA IR (SIL), not MLIR adoption — it does not show that alias/range/FP facts need a mid-level home.

concessions: F009 is genuine verified evidence that preserving source semantics in a pre-LLVM SSA IR enables optimizations LLVM cannot see (Swift ARC). If our language ends up with high-level semantics needing their own passes (ownership moves, array shape reasoning, iteration-structure rewrites), pure direct-to-LLVM either loses them or forces us to quietly reinvent a mid-level IR on the AST — at which point part of my position's simplicity claim evaporates.

LLVM metadata is a best-effort carrier: the corpus's own consumer matrix lists 'lowering strips metadata' and 'metadata dropped' as failure cases, and misleading-comparison #5 warns that IR metadata is only a conditional carrier of source facts. First-class operations in a structured IR are structurally preserved; metadata is legally discardable by passes that do not understand it. My position bets that the facts we care most about (per F001/F002) are ones LLVM's own passes already understand and preserve — a bet, not a proof.

The provisional A-cards (A001–A004, A008–A010) collectively suggest a real expressiveness ceiling: array shape/strides/memory-space (A001), iteration-space and parallel/reduction structure (A002/A003), staged n-D vectors (A004), and independence intent (A010) have documented homes in MLIR dialects and no first-class LLVM IR encoding in this corpus. If array/loop restructuring (tiling, fusion) turns out to matter as much as aliasing, direct-to-LLVM is fighting uphill. These cards are provisional, but I cannot claim the opposite is verified either.

Misleading-comparison #2 concedes the Rust precedent is harder than it looks: ownership does not map to noalias everywhere (unsafe, interior mutability, callbacks). My 'make the attribute the semantics' inversion mitigates but does not eliminate the lowering-soundness burden, and that inversion itself is uncarded design reasoning.

Per the caveats file, none of this evidence quantifies performance magnitude; I cannot claim direct-to-LLVM produces faster code than an MLIR pipeline, only that its required encodings are the verified ones.
risks: Soundness blast radius: F003's negative scope and the consumer-matrix row for ScopedNoAliasAA ('false noalias causes miscompile') mean a single incorrect frontend-emitted attribute is silent UB and miscompilation. Direct emission concentrates all soundness obligations in one unverified lowering step with no mid-level verifier between source rules and UB-bearing contracts — in tension with the ground rule that soundness may not be sacrificed. A mitigation (source rules that make facts true by construction, per the SPARK model in F006, plus differential testing) is design work not yet evidenced.

Fact attrition: metadata that LLVM passes do not actively maintain can be stripped mid-pipeline (consumer matrix), so facts may not survive to their intended consumers (e.g., LoopVectorize). If key facts silently vanish, the language's core promise — performance transparency — fails in a hard-to-diagnose way.

Expressiveness lock-in: committing to LLVM's fact vocabulary caps what the optimizer can ever be told at what attributes/metadata can express. Loop dependence-freedom, shape regularity, and schedule-like structure (A002/A003/A006/A008) may be conveyable only as droppable hints, and retrofitting a structured mid-level IR later is a major architecture reversal.

Reinvented mid-end: if language-specific optimizations prove necessary (the F009 pattern), we will build ad-hoc AST-level passes without SSA, verifiers, or testing infrastructure — potentially worse than either clean alternative.

LLVM version coupling: every consumer-matrix row says 'LLVM version TBD'; attribute/metadata semantics and pass behavior drift across LLVM releases (caveats note version sensitivity), and a direct-to-LLVM design has no insulation layer against that drift.
evidenceGaps: Cards N001–N006, D001–D010, C001–C006, and J001–J005 were referenced in the debate brief but are not present in any of the seven listed corpus files; my case is built only on F001–F009 and A001–A010 and could shift if those series bear on IR strategy.

No verified evidence exists on MLIR at all (caveats.md lists MLIR as uncovered; the fit/gap table is entirely TBD). This cuts both ways: my anti-MLIR argument is largely an absence-of-evidence argument, and a verification pass over MLIR dialect docs (A001–A004 sources) could erase my evidentiary advantage.

My assigned encoding set beyond noalias — TBAA, range, nsw, fast-math flags — has no dedicated evidence cards. Only noalias/alias.scope (F003/F004) is workflow-confirmed; A005 mentions FP math flags only provisionally. Need cards on TBAA soundness conditions, range/nsw consumers, and fast-math scoping before claiming 'maximal metadata' is well-supported end to end.

Metadata survival needs empirical closure: which LLVM passes preserve vs. drop each metadata kind, per LLVM version (the consumer matrix's own 'validation tests needed' column: FileCheck metadata tests, negative alias tests, vectorization remark tests). This is the single most decision-relevant experiment for my position.

The known history of noalias-related miscompiles and Rust's repeated toggling of noalias emission is not in the corpus; it directly bears on the soundness-risk assessment of my position and must be carded before a decision.

No quantified performance data anywhere in the corpus (caveats.md): the marginal speedup of scoped-noalias/range/fast-math metadata versus a structured mid-level IR doing loop transforms is unmeasured; a benchmark harness comparing the two pipelines on aliasing-heavy and loop-restructuring-heavy workloads would be decisive.

Whether loop-level facts (dependence freedom, trip counts, alignment — the LoopVectorize row's requirements, flagged 'backlog') can be reliably conveyed to LLVM via llvm.loop metadata and llvm.assume without a structured IR is unevidenced; this is the key question F009-style counterarguments hinge on.

Whether our language will need language-specific optimization passes at all (the Swift ARC analogue) is undetermined; if the answer is yes, the F009 counter-card strengthens materially and my position needs the 'thin custom pre-LLVM checker' variant rather than pure direct emission.

result.position: Custom language-specific IR first (SIL direction): design a purpose-built, verifier-checked IR that carries the language's optimizer-relevant semantics (ownership, non-interference, effects, iteration structure) as first-class structural IR — run language-specific optimization and diagnostics there — then lower once, late, through a single audited boundary into LLVM IR attributes/metadata. Maximal fact preservation at acknowledged maximal engineering cost.
result.coreArgument:
The debate topic is fact preservation, and the corpus's only direct evidence about IR-level semantic preservation is F009 — and it points squarely at my position. Swift SIL is production proof that an SSA-form, language-specific IR can keep source semantics (ownership, ARC operations) visible "long enough to optimize them," with Swift-level optimization deliberately staged before LLVM IR generation "so passes can use information LLVM would not otherwise see." That is not a hypothetical architecture; it is the one existing, corpus-attested instance of the strategy this debate is about.

The argument has three legs. (1) The facts worth preserving are language-level facts. F001 and F002 establish that the best-attested optimizer enablers in the corpus — Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK ownership/anti-aliasing (F006) — are properties of source semantics: non-interference around writes, which unlocks register promotion, load/store elimination, and reordering. These facts are born in the type system and borrow/ownership discipline. An IR whose vocabulary is the language's vocabulary can represent them structurally (the way SIL represents ownership in OSSA form), so preservation is the default and loss requires an explicit, reviewable lowering step. (2) The alternative carrier — LLVM attributes and metadata emitted directly from the frontend — is a conditional and fragile carrier, by the corpus's own account. F003's negative scope states LLVM no-alias metadata "is only sound if frontend obligations are true and metadata survives lowering," and violation is UB, i.e., silent miscompilation. The project's own pass-consumer matrix (llvm-pass-consumer-matrix.csv) lists as standing hazards: "lowering strips metadata," "metadata dropped," "false noalias causes miscompile," "escape mis-modeled." The corpus's misleading-comparisons file explicitly bans the claim "IR metadata solves source design," preferring "IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts." For a language whose ground rule is that soundness may not be sacrificed, an architecture in which every fact is a frontend-asserted UB-bearing promise scattered across the whole LLVM pipeline is the riskiest option on the table. A custom IR inverts this: facts are structural invariants checked by an IR verifier throughout the language-level pipeline, and the UB-bearing metadata translation happens exactly once, at a boundary narrow enough to test exhaustively (the matrix itself calls for FileCheck metadata-survival and negative-alias tests — far more tractable at one boundary than everywhere). F004 sharpens this: LLVM's strongest alias contract (return noalias, allocation-like disjointness) requires the source language to have "lifetime/provenance/escape rules" established before emitting it — precisely the proof work a language-level IR with ownership structure can discharge, and a metadata-spraying frontend cannot check. (3) Representation choice inside an IR is itself an optimization fact. F008 shows that even within LLVM, choosing funclets vs. landingpad for the same behavior changes what the optimizer can do (funclets pin values out of registers). If you commit to LLVM IR as your only semantic medium, you inherit its representation constraints for every language feature; a language IR lets you optimize in a representation chosen for your semantics and pick the LLVM encoding late, per target.

The provisional A-cards extend the same lesson beyond aliasing: A002 (Linalg keeps iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction roles as first-class ops to enable tiling/fusion/vectorization), A003 (affine restriction makes dependence analysis tractable), A001 (memref keeps shape/stride/memory-space facts optimizer-visible), A006 (Halide's schedule layer manipulates loop structure above the algorithm), and A008 (Futhark's size types and uniqueness/consumption avoid copies). All of these are instances of one principle: high-level structure preserved above LLVM enables transformations that are hard to rediscover below. A005 is the converse evidence: LLVM's LoopVectorize must reconstruct legality (no loop-carried deps, alignment, FP flags) from scalarized IR plus fragile hints — the exact structure a language IR could have simply kept. Note that the MLIR-dialect cards do not rescue the 'reuse generic dialects' alternative here: our own fit/gap analysis (mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md) is entirely TBD on whether existing dialects can carry our candidate facts (alias, layout, effects) — nobody has verified generic dialects fit this language, whereas a purpose-built IR fits by construction. (If MLIR is used as infrastructure to host our custom dialect, that is a variant of my position, not a rival to it.)

For an optimizer-first, performance-transparent language, the custom IR also buys the diagnostics story: explaining why an optimization did not fire in terms of source semantics requires an IR that still contains source semantics. I flag the strength of this benefit as partially an evidence gap (no card measures diagnostic quality), but its precondition — that LLVM IR no longer contains the language-level facts unless deliberately carried — is exactly what F009 and F003 document.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The corpus's only card on IR semantic preservation, and it directly instantiates my position: Swift SIL is an SSA-form, language-specific IR keeping Swift-level semantics and explicit ARC operations, with Swift-level optimization staged before LLVM IR generation so passes can use information LLVM would not otherwise see. Production existence proof of the SIL-direction architecture.

Shows the highest-value optimizer enablers (Rust &mut exclusivity, Fortran dummy-argument rules, SPARK anti-aliasing/ownership) are language-level semantic guarantees — the kind of facts a language-specific IR can represent structurally rather than as bolt-on metadata.

Identifies what fact preservation buys: proven non-interference around writes enables register promotion, read/write elimination, and reordering. This defines the payload the IR strategy must carry without loss from source to optimizer.

Documents both that LLVM can encode no-alias facts (so my late lowering target exists) and, in its negative scope, that this encoding is sound only if frontend obligations are true and metadata survives lowering, with UB on violation — the fragility and soundness hazard that a verifier-checked custom IR with a single audited lowering boundary is designed to contain.

LLVM's strongest alias contract (return noalias) requires the source language to establish lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting it. A language IR with structural ownership is the natural place to discharge that proof obligation; a frontend emitting metadata directly has nowhere to check it.

SPARK's ownership-with-transfer and side-effect-free expression rules show a second language whose optimizer-friendly facts are semantic disciplines — evidence that the fact vocabulary a custom IR must carry (ownership, effect purity) recurs across languages and is not LLVM-native.

Within LLVM itself, representation choice (landingpad vs funclets) for identical behavior changes optimizability. Committing all semantics to LLVM IR early means inheriting its representation constraints; a custom IR defers the encoding choice until per-feature, per-target lowering.

(Provisional) Linalg keeps iteration spaces, indexing maps, and parallel/reduction iterator kinds as first-class IR precisely to enable tiling, fusion, promotion, and vectorization — direct support for keeping high-level structure in IR instead of lowering immediately to scalar loops.

(Provisional) Affine shows that an IR level with restricted index forms makes dependence analysis efficient and reliable — an analysis regime available only above LLVM's general scalar IR, i.e., only if a higher-level IR exists.

(Provisional) MemRef demonstrates shape/stride/offset/memory-space facts held optimizer-visible in IR with explicit no-alias contracts — evidence that structured memory facts can live as IR structure rather than droppable metadata.

(Provisional) Converse evidence: LLVM's vectorizers must reconstruct legality (loop-carried dependences, alignment, FP flags, trip counts) from low-level IR plus hints, with runtime checks and fallbacks — the rediscovery cost my position avoids by never destroying that structure early.

(Provisional) Halide's schedule layer shows optimization-relevant loop/layout choices surfaced as manipulable program structure above the algorithm — supporting a staged, semantics-rich representation between source and LLVM.

(Provisional) Futhark's size-typed regular arrays and uniqueness/consumption rules avoiding copies show language-level facts (shape, uniqueness) driving optimization decisions that must be made above LLVM IR.

concessions: The 'maximal engineering cost' label is earned and the corpus contains zero evidence quantifying it: no card measures the person-years, compile-time cost, or maintenance burden of SIL versus alternatives. F009's negative scope warns SIL specifics are implementation-dependent (OSSA/non-OSSA phases, stale docs), so even the existence proof comes with maintenance-hazard caveats.

SIL is effectively N=1 in this corpus. No card provides a controlled comparison showing a custom IR outperforms a metadata-rich direct-to-LLVM frontend on end performance; per caveats.md, documentation evidence shows what implementations claim, not performance magnitude.

A custom IR reduces but does not eliminate the metadata-fragility problem: the final lowering to LLVM attributes/metadata still carries F003's full UB-bearing contract, and hostile facts can still be dropped or mis-emitted at that boundary. My claim is that one audited boundary is more testable than many, not that the hazard disappears.
The A-cards doing much of my arrays/loops work (A001-A008) are direct-extraction and not adversarially verified; A002/A003 are also design-rationale documents, not profitability proof.

An MLIR-hosted custom dialect could plausibly deliver most of my position's benefits at lower cost by reusing infrastructure; the corpus cannot distinguish build-from-scratch from MLIR-hosted, and I concede the MLIR variant may dominate mine on cost if the fit/gap analysis (currently all TBD) comes back favorable.

The corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection (caveats.md), so my 'the facts worth preserving are language-level' claim is well-attested only for non-interference; for layout, numerics, concurrency, and dispatch it rests on provisional or absent evidence.
risks: Engineering cost and schedule: a custom IR means designing and maintaining an IR spec, verifier, serializer, pass pipeline, and lowering — potentially duplicating LLVM passes (as SIL does for some optimizations) with a small team. If the team cannot sustain it, a half-maintained custom IR is worse than none.

Soundness risk migrates to the lowering boundary: a semantic mismatch between custom-IR invariants and the LLVM contract they are translated into (e.g., emitting noalias from an ownership fact whose exceptions were forgotten, cf. F001/F005 negative scopes on permitted-aliasing cases) produces exactly the silent miscompiles the architecture exists to prevent, now concentrated in code only we audit.

Opportunity cost against LLVM's mature optimizer: time spent on language-level passes is time not spent ensuring facts reach LLVM's existing consumers (ScopedNoAliasAA, SROA, LICM/GVN/DSE, LoopVectorize per the consumer matrix); a custom IR that optimizes above but lowers facts poorly could underperform a simpler frontend that lowers facts well.

Spec drift and stale documentation: F009's caveat about Swift's stale SIL docs and OSSA phase distinctions is a warning that custom-IR semantics rot without continuous investment, and rotted IR semantics are a soundness hazard, not just an ergonomics one.
The diagnostics/performance-transparency benefit I claim is partially unevidenced; if LLVM's optimization remarks prove adequate for source-level transparency, one of my differentiators weakens.
evidenceGaps: No quantified performance evidence: nothing measures how much end performance is gained by language-IR-level optimization (e.g., SIL ARC optimization wins) versus direct lowering with rich metadata. A benchmark-grade comparison is the single most decision-relevant missing item.

Metadata-survival data is missing: the pass-consumer matrix marks every 'metadata ignored/stripped' and version cell as TBD, and calls for FileCheck survival plus negative-alias tests. If experiments show LLVM metadata reliably survives the passes we care about, my fragility argument weakens materially; if it is dropped often, my position strengthens.

The MLIR fit/gap table (mlir-dialect-fit-gap.md) is entirely TBD for alias/noalias, array layout/shape, and effects. Closing it decides between build-from-scratch and MLIR-hosted variants of the custom-IR position — and whether generic dialects alone could suffice, which would undercut me.

No engineering-cost evidence: no card addresses implementation effort, compile-time overhead, or team-size requirements of a custom IR versus alternatives; 'maximal engineering cost' is asserted, not measured.
No evidence on diagnostics quality: the claim that a language IR yields better optimization-transparency diagnostics than LLVM remarks has no supporting card and needs targeted study.

Cards N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 referenced in the debate ground rules do not appear in any file I was assigned to read; whatever they contain (plausibly native-lowering, direct-extraction, compilation, or JIT evidence) is invisible to this argument and could shift it.

The corpus lacks verified coverage of MLIR itself, Julia, Halide internals, Mojo, and most non-aliasing optimization domains (caveats.md), so the generalization from non-interference facts to 'all optimizer-relevant facts benefit from a custom IR' is unproven.

F009 does not establish which SIL design elements (OSSA ownership, explicit ARC ops, pre-LLVM pass placement) carry the benefit; a decomposition study or comparison against Rust's MIR (absent from the corpus) would be needed before copying the architecture wholesale.

challenges.challenge: F009 is cited backwards. A1 uses F009 to argue that monomorphized instantiations 'preserve concrete types into LLVM IR' while shared bodies 'discard them before the optimizer sees them.' But F009's exemplar is Swift SIL — the IR of a witness-table, shared-generics language that preserves GENERIC (unspecialized) semantics precisely so specialization can be decided in the optimizer rather than the frontend. The corpus's only workflow-confirmed IR-design card thus evidences the viability of NOT monomorphizing in the frontend at least as strongly as it evidences A1's reading. A1's strongest card cuts against A1's architecture.

The top-line argument — shared/erased generic bodies 'cannot carry type-dependent alias facts,' so monomorphization is the gateway to the F001/F002/F003 payoffs — is A1's own inference, conceded as unverified in the fine print but presented as load-bearing point (1). It is also technically contestable: a uniform pointer-shaped shared body can still carry parameter-level noalias facts, and no card addresses whether erasure forfeits alias metadata. Misleading-comparisons note 5 ('IR metadata solves source design' — metadata is a conditional carrier) warns against exactly this move. The corpus's strongest evidence class is being conscripted for a dispatch conclusion it does not reach.

Predictability double standard: A1 indicts devirtualization as 'option-dependent' (D007) while claiming 'monomorphization's output is predictable from source.' A1's own cited card D004 contradicts this — whether monomorphized bodies are inlined and optimized depends on codegen-unit boundaries and LTO settings, i.e., is equally option-dependent. To make the predictability claim true, A1 must also mandate a build/visibility model — at which point the position quietly needs most of Advocate 2's, without arguing for it.

Composition hole between the two halves of the position: per D003, dyn-compatible interfaces exclude generic dispatchable methods. So the mono-only core plus strict-dyn escape hatch offers NO sanctioned path for heterogeneous use of generic APIs — the exact workload class (plugin registries, executors, object graphs) the escape hatch exists for. A1's own risk list predicts the outcome (hand-rolled void*/function-pointer erasure the optimizer and F003 machinery understand worse) but the position offers no mitigation and does not treat this as the design-killing interaction it may be.

The central move is the same conditional-vs-guarantee conflation A2 accuses everyone else of, with the sign flipped. D007 documents devirtualization MECHANISMS that are explicitly compiler- and option-dependent; no card in the corpus documents any compiler that GUARANTEES devirtualization given whole-program visibility — LLVM's actual WPD machinery is uncovered (A2's own gap list). 'Sound by construction,' 'guaranteed,' and 'invariant' are assertions about a hypothetical compiler, not about anything evidenced. Closed-world makes devirtualization POSSIBLE without guards; nothing in the corpus makes it PROMISED, and inliner heuristics remain heuristics even in a closed world.

The universal claim 'every documented obstacle to eliminating dispatch overhead is an open-world escape hatch' is refuted from inside the corpus: D004's codegen-unit boundaries are build-parallelism artifacts, not open-world semantics, and D009's Function-typed fields hide call targets inside a fully closed program. First-class functions and closures reintroduce optimizer-opaque indirect dispatch in a sealed, statically-linked world, and A2's model says nothing about them — sealing hierarchies does not seal function values.

Position conflation: sealed-by-default plus mandatory LTO is a visibility/build model, not a dispatch-and-generics model. A2 answers the debate topic by borrowing A1's monomorphization (D001) and gesturing at D003, leaving the actual contested question — specialize vs share vs dictionary-pass — undecided. Closed-world visibility is largely orthogonal: it could be bolted onto A1's or A3's model. Presenting it as a rival answer inflates what is really an amendment.

Boundary soundness is conceded as a risk but is actually a precondition. The interop dossier lists dynamic linking, JVM/.NET, and Python extension APIs as targets; every real deployment of this core will have a periphery. D008 shows boundary semantics silently invalidate optimizer assumptions. Until the core/periphery fact-attenuation mechanism is designed, 'sound by construction' is conditional on an undesigned component — which under the ground rules (soundness may not be sacrificed) means the position is currently unimplementable as stated, not merely risky.

'Sharing is a pure code-size win with no representational compromise' is contradicted by A3's own risk #1 and by D005's negative scope ('does not prove specific JIT optimizations'). Shared bodies pay D002-class indirect dispatch, cannot inline type-argument-dependent calls, and in .NET require runtime dictionary/type-handle lookups that no corpus card even mentions. The compromise is performance, not layout — 'representation is uniform' does not entail 'nothing representational is lost,' because what is lost is the compiler's knowledge OF the representation-dependent call targets.

The D007 backstop is a mis-transfer and possibly circular. D007 documents GCC devirtualization of C++ vtable-based virtual calls; no card shows this machinery applies to shared generic-body dispatch or dictionary lookups at all. Worse: if recovery on the shared path works by specializing hot instantiations, it regenerates exactly the code growth the hybrid exists to bound — the backstop, where it matters most, dissolves the position's core promise. 'The hybrid's worst case is recoverable, not permanent' is unsupported in both directions: recovery is unevidenced for this call shape, and where it works it un-bounds the size guarantee.

A3 never engages the corpus's strongest-attested evidence class: whether the shared reference path preserves or erodes F001-F003 non-interference facts. If shared bodies cannot discharge per-instantiation noalias obligations (F003's soundness condition), the hybrid taxes the language's flagship optimization exactly where it shares. A1 at least argued this link (as inference); A3 built a position whose hot shared path may conflict with the aliasing machinery and never priced it.

The transparency advantage — 'specialized vs shared is decidable from the static value/reference distinction' — presupposes the language HAS a uniform reference-type representation (headers, GC/RC, pointer-shaped everything). A3 concedes this is undecided, but the concession guts the core argument: a performance core might plausibly have no uniform reference types at all, in which case the sharing half has nothing to share over and the position reduces to A1 plus an unimplementable annex. The load-bearing precedent (D005) is also a JIT with lazy runtime instantiation; the AOT instantiation-closure problem A3 concedes is not a corner case, it is the whole feasibility question for an LLVM target.

challenges.targetPosition: Advocates 1 and 2 jointly (use of Julia cards)
challenges.challenge: D009/D010 are used by both A1 and A2 to tax 'dynamic dispatch' generally, but Julia's conditionality stems from runtime method redefinition, world age, and JIT type-inference cliffs — a model no advocate proposes. Using these cards against explicit AOT vtable dispatch or static reference-type sharing is a category stretch of exactly the kind the misleading-comparisons file polices (note 3's discipline about conditional recovery applies to managed/JIT runtimes, not to every design containing an indirect call).

unsupportedClaims: A1: 'type-erased shared generic bodies cannot carry type-dependent alias facts' — no card addresses alias metadata in shared generic bodies; F003 says metadata is sound if frontend obligations hold, and pointer-level noalias facts in uniform-representation bodies are not ruled out. Conceded as inference in concessions, but deployed as the load-bearing argument (1).

A1: monomorphized instantiation 'sidesteps' D008-class interposition blockage — D008 is ELF/GCC-specific, says nothing about generic instantiation, and its negative scope says the language must separately choose module/interposition rules; exported or deduplicated instantiations could themselves be interposable symbols. Evidence gap dressed as a card.

A1: 'Julia recovers performance only when programmers manually enforce type stability' — D009 says performance is recovered through type inference and specialization when types are stable; inference frequently succeeds without manual enforcement. 'Only' and 'manually' are additions.

A2: closed-world devirtualization is 'sound by construction' / 'guaranteed' / an 'invariant' once visibility is mandatory — no card documents guaranteed devirtualization under whole-program visibility; D007 documents conditional, revertible, option-dependent mechanisms, and LLVM WPD is uncovered per A2's own gap list.

A2: 'every documented obstacle to eliminating dispatch overhead is an open-world escape hatch' — refuted in-corpus by D004 (codegen-unit boundaries) and D009 (Function-typed fields hiding call targets in closed programs).

A2: D005 as 'the corpus's most direct support for banning reflection' — the card's conditional is 'may constrain closed-world assumptions unless restricted'; it supports restriction, and a ban is a stronger design claim than the card licenses.
A2: '-fno-semantic-interposition is sound only under an unverifiable assumption' — D008 says the flag 'assumes interposed definitions preserve semantics'; 'unverifiable' is A2's addition.

A3: 'sharing is a pure code-size win with no representational compromise' — contradicted by D002's cost class on the shared path, by D005's negative scope, and by A3's own risk #1; dictionary/type-handle lookup costs of .NET shared code appear in no card at all.

A3: 'the hybrid's worst case is recoverable, not permanent' via D007 — D007 covers C++ virtual-call devirtualization, not shared-generic or dictionary dispatch, and is explicitly revertible and option-dependent.

A3: the hybrid is 'the only design in this corpus attested to deliver both' guarantees — D005 attests a representation exists in a JIT; it attests neither performance delivery (negative scope) nor AOT feasibility (conceded), so 'attested to deliver' overstates what any card shows.

All three: the implicit premise that dispatch/generics choice is a first-order determinant of overall performance — caveats.md states the corpus is aliasing-heavy by selection and relative importance across dispatch, layout, numerics, and concurrency 'has not been established.' A1 concedes this; A2 and A3 note it only obliquely while arguing as if the stakes are established.
missingConsiderations: First-class functions and closures: no position covers dispatch through function values, yet D009 explicitly documents hidden call targets in Function-typed fields as an optimizer defeat. A dispatch-and-generics model that only addresses trait/interface calls is incomplete for any language with closures — which includes every candidate design here (iterator adapters, callbacks, async).

A fourth model implied by the corpus's own strongest card: F009 (Swift SIL) evidences preserving generic semantics into a high-level IR and deciding specialization there. IR-level generics with guaranteed-specialization annotations could deliver A1's guarantees on marked paths with A3's bounded sharing elsewhere — no advocate proposed or rebutted this option, even though all three cite F009.

The decisive testable question underlying the A1-vs-A3 dispute was never formulated: can a shared/erased generic body soundly discharge F003 noalias/alias.scope obligations, and at what precision loss relative to per-instantiation code? This is checkable by construction against LLVM semantics without benchmarks, and it would settle whether A1's inference or A3's sharing survives — nobody proposed verifying it.

Composition of each generics model with its own escape hatch: instantiating generics AT dyn/erased types, dyn inside generic bodies, generic methods on dyn interfaces (excluded by D003), and value-type instantiations calling through shared paths (A3) are the boundary cases where soundness bugs and cost-model surprises live; all three positions specify the two mechanisms separately and none specifies their product.

Core/periphery fact attenuation: all three positions depend on a boundary (explicit dyn, non-core dynamic loading, restricted shared path), and D008 shows boundaries silently invalidate optimizer assumptions — but no advocate designs how optimizer-visible facts are soundly weakened when crossing their own boundary. Under the 'soundness may not be sacrificed' rule this is a precondition, not a periphery concern.

A decision procedure: given that all three concede zero quantitative evidence, the rational debate output is a measurement plan (code size / i-cache / compile time across mono vs shared vs dictionary on representative workloads, plus LLVM WPD and Swift/@_specialize dossier work already listed as Open) with pre-committed decision thresholds. No advocate proposed benchmark gates or which provisional D-cards (D002, D005, D007 are load-bearing for all three) to adversarially verify first — despite caveats.md's standing rule and its warning that implementation files should constrain debates via what can be represented, verified, and tested.

Whether 'performance transparency' means a predictable cost model or a guaranteed-optimization floor when the two diverge (e.g., should the compiler be FORBIDDEN from devirtualizing an explicit dyn call it can prove unique?). A1 raises this as a policy gap; A2 and A3 silently assume opposite answers, and the charter question is antecedent to all three positions.
result.sharpestTension: All three positions campaign on 'invariants, not hopes,' but the corpus shows each achieves its invariant on one axis only by exporting conditionality to another, and the export is never priced: A1's guaranteed specialization is conditional on unmeasured code-size/i-cache effects (D001, D009 negative scopes) and on build/LTO boundaries its own card D004 shows are option-dependent; A2's guaranteed devirtualization rests on machinery the corpus documents only as conditional and revertible (D007), with the actual guaranteeing mechanism (LLVM WPD) entirely absent from evidence; A3's bounded code size is bought with a hot-path dispatch cost whose recovery backstop is mis-transferred from C++ vtables and, where it would work, regenerates the code growth the bound promised away. The concrete, unresolvable fault line is guaranteed specialization (A1) versus bounded code growth (A3) — the single trade every generics design must make — and with zero quantitative cards in the corpus (caveats.md: documentation evidence does not quantify magnitude, size, or compile time) that trade is empirically unarbitrable today. Deciding it now would be a values commitment masquerading as an evidence-driven conclusion, which ground rule 4 forbids; the only evidence-respecting output is the missing measurement program plus construction-level verification of whether shared bodies can carry F003 alias obligations, since that one checkable question determines whether A1's core inference or A3's core mechanism is even coherent.

result.question: When an optimizer-facing contract (noalias, bounds, overflow-freedom, purity) turns out to be false at runtime, what should happen?

positionAssessments.position: Position 1: UB on violation, confined to explicitly marked unsafe boundaries (poison-preferring)
positionAssessments.strengths: The confinement architecture survives the critique largely intact: safe-layer-proves / unsafe-asserts is the best-attested split in the corpus (F001 safe Rust exclusivity, F006 SPARK, with F005 Fortran as the unconfined cautionary contrast), and the poison-over-immediate-UB refinement is genuinely grounded (N001 lattice; N003 shows immediate UB blocks speculation) even though every N-card carries low design-generalization confidence. If LLVM is the substrate, its contract channels (F003 noalias, N002 nsw/nuw, N004 range, N005 inbounds) map onto this position with no impedance mismatch, and the advocate's concessions were unusually honest (unquantified payoff, misleading-comparisons #5, unfinished Rust unsafe model).
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Three attacks land hard. (1) The 'forced by logic' trichotomy is refuted by the substrate itself: C005 shows LLVM gives racing non-atomic loads undef — a defined-but-nondeterministic violation semantics requiring no runtime check — and N001's freeze(poison) is a defined middle point; UB maximizes transform freedom (N001 lattice) but is not the unique check-free option, and the freedom delta is unquantified. (2) 'Safe code has zero UB from false contracts' quantifies only over single-threaded executions: data races can falsify compiler-proven noalias facts (threat model 'partially'; C002 shows race semantics is an open prior choice; C004 shows the fix is an additional type discipline the position never commits to). (3) The claimed differential tooling advantage from N006 is void — all three positions emit the same IR channels and inherit the same Alive2 ecosystem, which validates IR transforms, not frontend truth. Additionally, A005 shows the 'unremovable runtime checks' alternative is actually a precedented payoff-recovery mechanism (runtime alias checks + loop versioning), and the position has no story for how a silently-poisoned release binary is ever diagnosed. The F005 'one mistake from UB' framing and the F009 'discharges the F003 obligation' claim were both uncarded extrapolations.

positionAssessments.position: Position 2: Static proof required; unprovable contracts are never emitted; false-contract state made unrepresentable
positionAssessments.strengths: Best alignment with the ground rule that soundness may not be sacrificed: it is the only position under which the false-contract state is unrepresentable for covered code, and its fallback (omit the contract) is always sound. Its existence proofs are real and workflow-confirmed (F001 safe Rust &mut exclusivity; F006 SPARK prover-enforced anti-aliasing, ownership, side-effect-free expressions), and it is correct that strong contract emission must be gated on established source rules (F004 negative scope). Its use of N001 to argue that a false contract has unbounded blast radius by construction of the refinement lattice is the debate's most solid semantic point and survives the critique.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Its structural indictment of the checking alternative is refuted by corpus evidence it did not read: A005 documents runtime pointer checks plus multi-versioning as LLVM vectorizers' standard legality strategy, and a passed guard legitimizes emitting the same contract on dominated paths — so 'checking degenerates into don't-emit-the-contract' is false as stated, and the true cost (check execution + motion barriers, N003) is unquantified. F007 was stretched past scope (HotSpot escape analysis is profile-driven re-derivation, not checking of declared contracts; generalization confidence low). The position conflates two static regimes with opposite failure modes (by-construction typing vs prover-discharged obligations) without choosing. Its 'exactly three coherent policies' case analysis omitted speculate-and-deoptimize (J001/J002 — sound, no source UB, and per J002 enables optimizations static compilers cannot do; plausibly rejectable for AOT on J005 warmup/memory grounds, but it had to be enumerated). And it shares Position 1's concurrency hole: proofs quantify over race-free executions, so 'unrepresentable' fails one layer down until the memory model is decided (C002, C004). Proof burden, rejection rates, and escape-hatch pressure are all unquantified and historically adoption-limiting (F006 negative scope).

positionAssessments.position: Position 3: Graduated obligations — debug-checked, trapped/conservative when cheap, assumed only in explicitly marked release contexts, per-fact and auditable
positionAssessments.strengths: Closest to the post-critique convergence point: it already contains Position 2's top tier (compiler-proven facts assumable in every build at zero runtime cost — the corpus's best-attested wins, F001/F005/F006, are all in this tier) and Position 1's marked-assumption contexts, and it is the only position with any violation-observability story (debug checks; Go's race-detection halts in C002 are now carded precedent for trap-with-report). The substrate genuinely supports graduation: every carded contract channel has an assume form and a sound omission fallback (N002 unflagged/flagged, N004, N005 inbounds/non-inbounds), freeze is a documented containment point (N001), and — decisively, supplied by the critic's own evidence — A005 gives the middle tier a carded mechanism for aliasing facts in loop-shaped code (runtime pointer checks + versioning buying assume-grade inner loops). It is also the only position structurally able to accommodate per-contract-class decomposition, which the purity evidence gap suggests is necessary.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Its distinctive middle tier is uncarded exactly where the payoff is highest: general scoped-noalias disjointness is a whole-execution property with no carded runtime check (A005 covers only bounded pointer-range checks in loops), and purity has no dynamic-check evidence at all — so on those contract classes it currently degenerates to Position 1 plus mode-matrix complexity, as the critic charged. The checked tier's lowering mechanics (trap intrinsics, soundness of combined check+flag emission, dominance ordering) have no card, so 'all three are native lowerings, no new semantics' is unsupported at the position's differentiating tier — and that tier is where F003-negative-scope-style frontend soundness bugs live. N003 was read in reverse (guards pessimize; that is the cost, not evidence of compatibility). Debug/release divergence, marking metastasis under performance pressure, and cross-mode semantic equivalence are all real, unsized risks. The Ada/Zig branding has zero corpus support (caveats.md confirms no Zig coverage).

keyTradeoffs: Assumption strength vs blast radius: UB-backed assertion (F003, N002, N004, N005) grants maximal transform freedom by the N001 lattice, but a single false contract licenses unbounded, silent, non-local miscompilation; defined violation semantics (undef for racing loads C005, bounded racy outcomes C002) shrink blast radius without runtime checks but invalidate some rewrites (N001: per-use nondeterminism breaks x*2 -> x+x). The size of the freedom delta is unquantified anywhere in the corpus.

Static proof coverage vs expressiveness and burden: proof-gated emission makes false contracts unrepresentable (F001, F006) but rejects or deoptimizes exactly the dynamic-aliasing, FFI, and concurrency-adjacent code where the threat model says verification fails ('sometimes no', 'often no', 'no') and where payoff may be highest; annotation burden, prover time, and rejection rates are unquantified and F006's negative scope flags usability costs.

Runtime checking cost vs recovered assumption plus observability: checks pin code motion (N003) and cost cycles/code size (A005 versioning trades code size for assume-grade loops), but a passed check legitimizes the contract on dominated paths and checking is the only tier with any post-violation diagnosis story (C002 Go race halts as precedent). Check overhead after modern elision is unmeasured in this corpus.

Uniform policy vs per-contract-class decomposition: noalias-class disjointness (whole-execution property, runtime-checkable only in loop-bounded form per A005), bounds/overflow (per-operation checkable, N002/N004/N005), and purity (static-only evidence, F006; no IR violation-semantics card) have different checkability, provability, and IR channels — one violation-semantics policy for all four may be a category error only Position 3 can even express.

Memory-model choice caps every position: UB races (C/C++/Rust/Swift per C002) make racy contract falsification moot by fiat but import maximal blast radius into 'safe' code; bounded races (Java/JS/Go) preserve diagnosability but forbid some F002-class optimizations on possibly-shared memory (C003) unless C004-style type-level non-sharing is adopted — which couples this debate to the type-system design.

Debug/release graduation vs shipped-binary fidelity: checked builds give the only carded diagnosability, but you then test semantics you do not ship; divergence-escape rates are completely unevidenced.
evidenceGaps: The A/D/C/J card series (phase2-arrays/dispatch/concurrency/jit findings) exists but carries no adversarial verification votes — provisional per ground rule 3 — and was absent from the debate's assigned reading; the debate brief's card ranges and file list are inconsistent (process defect, confirmed by inspection: files dated Jul 1 postdate caveats.md's Jun 30 'no concurrency coverage' statement).

No quantified performance data for any tier: caveats.md and F002's negative scope state documentation evidence does not quantify magnitude; every comparative performance claim by every advocate is structural conjecture. A three-way benchmark (UB-asserted vs runtime-checked+versioned vs proof-gated) on aliasing/bounds/overflow kernels is the single most decision-capable missing artifact.

Race semantics is an open prior decision: C002's negative scope says the UB-vs-bounded choice 'is an open debate question' with no performance quantification; every 'proven facts stay true at runtime' claim in all three positions depends on it, and C004-style type-level non-sharing is the only carded route to closing it.

Checked-tier mechanics uncarded: no card covers trap intrinsic costs, soundness of combined check+flag emission (dominance ordering), or whether llvm.assume after a passed check recovers assumption-grade optimization.

Debug-mode dynamic checkability uncarded: no evidence on Miri/UBSan/TSan-class detection of false noalias/inbounds/nsw/purity assertions or its cost — this decides whether Positions 1 and 3 have a debuggability story at all.
Purity has no violation-semantics evidence: the only purity card (F006) is restriction-based and static; no IR channel or dynamic check for purity violations is carded.

Violation observability post-deployment: no card addresses how a false contract in a poison-silent release binary is ever discovered (canary builds, trap-with-report); C002's Go race-halt is the only adjacent precedent.
Contract survival through lowering: F003 and F009 negative scopes both condition soundness/value on metadata being true and surviving lowering; no end-to-end measurement exists.

Linkage-boundary falsification: D008 (ELF semantic interposition) and D004 (codegen-unit/LTO boundaries) show contracts can be falsified by linking, not just by programmers; no position addressed emission policy for exported symbols, and the threat model has no row for it.
Substrate undecided: LLVM channels vs MLIR MemRef distinct_objects/in-bounds (A001) offer different violation-semantics vocabularies; both Positions 1 and 2 argued from LLVM specifics as if decided.

Unsafe/marked-region proliferation: no empirical evidence on whether explicit-marking or unsafe-block discipline holds under performance pressure, or on defect density inside audited assertion regions.
result.leadingCandidate: Position 3 (graduated obligations), amended post-critique: compiler-proven contracts assumable in every build mode; human-asserted contracts only in per-fact explicitly marked contexts, dynamically checkable in debug builds; A005-style runtime-check-plus-code-versioning as the carded middle mechanism where checks exist; conservative no-contract fallback at unverifiable boundaries (FFI, int-ptr casts, per the threat model); purity and general scoped-noalias restricted to proven-or-conservative until dynamic-check evidence exists. This subsumes Position 2's top tier and Position 1's confinement while being the only position with an observability story.
result.rationale: The outcome is research_needed rather than a design lean for three reasons. First, the debate demonstrably ran on a stale evidence view: I verified that the C/J/A/D card files exist (notes/phase2-{concurrency,jit,arrays,dispatch}-findings.jsonl, dated after caveats.md), all three advocates wrongly declared them absent, and those cards reverse load-bearing shared premises — C005 refutes Position 1's 'any defined semantics forfeits assumption' trichotomy, A005 refutes Position 2's 'checking forfeits the payoff' structure, and J001/J002 expose a fourth policy Position 2's exhaustive case analysis omitted. Per the corpus's own standing rules (decision-gates promotion requirements; caveats.md 'no final design decisions from this plan'), a debate whose central arguments were falsified by unadmitted cards must be re-run, and those cards must first be adversarially verified since they currently carry no votes. Second, the critic's sharpest point is confirmed by the cards: whether 'contract false at runtime' is even a complete question depends on the race-semantics choice, which C002 explicitly marks open — though I weigh this as a dependency to be decided first or jointly, not full incommensurability, because the single-threaded core of the question (what a false human assertion means) is well-posed regardless and the positions converge substantially on it: all three agree proven facts are assumable everywhere, unproven assertion is never ambient, and unverifiable boundaries get conservative fallback. Third, the residual disagreement — the asserted-but-unproven middle tier — sits exactly where the corpus is empty: zero performance quantification (caveats.md), no checked-lowering or trap-cost cards, no dynamic-checker cards, no purity violation-semantics card, no observability evidence. That is 'evidence too thin', not 'mechanism plausible but untested', so research_needed dominates prototype_needed; the prototype gate comes after the missing cards exist. Position 3 (amended) is named leading because its weaknesses after critique are evidence gaps, while Position 1's central theorem ('safe code has zero UB') is refuted as stated pending the memory-model decision and Position 2's central structural claim is refuted by A005 — but it is not 'clearly leading' in the design_candidate_later sense: its distinctive tier is uncarded on the highest-value contract class, and per decision-gates no position has met even the prototype_required bar.
followUps: Formally admit the A/D/C/J card series into the corpus and run adversarial verification on them (they currently lack verification votes, unlike F/N cards); fix the debate-brief inconsistency between cited card ranges and the assigned file list; then re-run this debate on the full corpus.

Run the memory-model / race-semantics debate (UB races vs bounded outcomes vs C004-style type-level non-sharing) as a decision prior to or joint with violation-semantics; C002 marks it open and every position's soundness story depends on it.

Produce the three-way benchmark caveats.md says is missing: UB-asserted vs runtime-checked(+A005-style versioning) vs proof-gated contracts on representative aliasing/bounds/overflow kernels, measuring speed, code size, and check-elision rates — this operationalizes Position 3's 'cheap' threshold and tests Position 2's forfeiture claim.

Card the checked tier's mechanics: LLVM trap/check lowering cost, soundness rules for co-emitting checks with nsw/range/inbounds flags (dominance ordering), and whether llvm.assume after a passed check recovers assumption-grade optimization.

Card debug-mode dynamic checkers (Miri, UBSan, TSan class): which asserted contracts (noalias, inbounds, nsw, purity) they can falsify and at what cost — this decides whether the checkable-in-dev/assumed-in-release story exists.
Decompose the topic per contract class and source purity-specific violation-semantics evidence (IR channel, dynamic checkability); F006's static-only evidence cannot carry a purity policy.

Decide or dual-track the substrate: map contract channels and violation semantics for MLIR MemRef (distinct_objects, in-bounds; A001-A004) against LLVM's, since both UB-advocating positions argued from LLVM specifics as if the backend were decided.

Design and card the post-deployment observability story: trap-with-report semantics (Go race-halt precedent, C002), check-enabled canary builds, and how a poison-silent release miscompile is ever attributed to a false contract.
Add a linkage-boundary row to the threat model and card emission policy for exported symbols under ELF interposition (D008) and codegen-unit/LTO boundaries (D004).

Per decision-gates, before any position can pass prototype_required: a lowering sketch for the marked-assume path plus an Alive2-style validation plan (N006) demonstrating asserted source contracts survive lowering and measurably fire F002-class optimizations.

challenges.challenge: Checkability bait-and-switch at the core of the case. The position's decisive differentiator is that structured loops make non-interference 'checkable rather than promised' — but the two precedents cited for this (A010 DO CONCURRENT, F005 Fortran dummy arguments) are promise-based in the corpus's own terms: A010 says restrictions are 'necessary rather than sufficient' and compilers may still need dependence analysis or runtime checks, and neither card says any compiler verifies the programmer's independence obligations. F001's caveat about programmer truthfulness is quoted against restrict but applies equally to Fortran's rules. The only genuinely checked systems in the corpus (Rust &mut, SPARK ownership, Futhark uniqueness/sizes) check aliasing and consumption — no card attests any system statically checking cross-iteration independence of arbitrary loop bodies. If the answer is 'affine restriction makes independence decidable,' the position silently narrows to 'only affine-bodied loops get parallel semantics,' whose workload coverage is an admitted unknown.

F009 is stretched and non-discriminating. F009 is a Swift SIL finding about IR design (preserve semantics in the IR long enough to optimize), rated medium generalization with implementation-specific caveats. It says nothing about restricting source forms to affine indexing or shapes-in-types — and every position in this debate can claim F009 equally (advocate 3's metadata-preservation path and advocate 2's structured lowering are also 'preserving semantics'). Using it as the principle that clinches structured-first is citation inflation.

F005 causal misattribution. The card attributes Fortran's optimization-friendliness to its aliasing model — dummy-argument contracts and default no-alias rules — not to first-class arrays. Recasting it as evidence that 'array-first language design and no-alias-by-default semantics compose well' smuggles in the array-ness conclusion; as written, F005 supports the contracts position at least as well, since the operative mechanism is an argument-level contract on fairly general storage.

The escape hatch is waved at, not designed, and it is where the soundness story breaks. 'Anything outside the subset explicitly marked and conservatively treated' means irregular code and structured arrays share memory; one escape-hatch pointer or view into a shaped array can invalidate every noalias/shape fact the structured core emitted (A001's own negative scope: views can alias original storage; F003: violated metadata is UB). The advocate lists views as a gap but never confronts that the two-tier design makes the boundary ubiquitous — the same boundary problem they would press against the other positions.
