The 'decisive engineering claim' about directionality is half-carded. A005 supports 'raising is conditional and failure-prone'; no card supports 'lowering structure is easy' or that a structured frontend is a 'nearly lossless' feed into Linalg/Affine — the dossier explicitly lists validating that MLIR/LLVM actually consume preserved facts as open. A conceded risk cannot simultaneously be the decisive argument.

The 'self-discharged obligations' soundness story fails at the algorithm layer's front door. Purity inside the layer proves nothing about the user-supplied input and output buffers the computation reads and writes: if a caller passes overlapping storage, the compiler-generated noalias/alias.scope facts (F003) are false and the result is UB. The position's structural soundness is contractual at exactly its boundary — the same property it attacks the contracts rival for — and the boundary is unmentioned. Advocate 3's observation that structure relocates rather than eliminates the contract problem applies with full force here and is never answered.

The layout plank of the position is uncarded. The assigned position includes 'storage layout ... chosen in a distinct schedule layer,' but A006's claim enumerates only loop transformations (loop order, split/fuse, tiling, vectorization, unrolling, parallelization). No card in the read set evidences schedule-controlled storage layout; a full plank of the position rests on zero corpus support, on top of caveats.md explicitly listing Halide among topics without verified coverage.

'Legality by construction, profitability by choice' is contradicted by the position's own cards. A004's negative scope says target legality, register pressure, LLVM 1-D vector limits, and hardware shapes remain constraints below the Vector dialect — so a vectorize() directive does not 'lower deterministically'; it can still be broken up, scalarized, or rejected downstream. The guarantee-vs-hint problem the advocate uses against pragmas (A005) re-enters through their own lowering path, and the claim that schedule directives escape it is unsupported.

Purity's interference problem is internalized, not dissolved. To perform, the pure layer must be lowered to in-place buffer code; the only carded mechanism is Futhark uniqueness/consumption (A008), whose own negative scope says layout/codegen and fusion behavior 'require further implementation/paper evidence.' The claim that F002's non-interference is obtained 'wholesale' quietly assumes the compiler solves internally (bufferization, buffer reuse, fusion legality) the same write-interference problem — with no corpus evidence it does so reliably, and with A002 self-described as design rationale, 'not proof of profitability.'

The attack on the contracts rival is a strawman, and the position's remaining half is evidence-free. Advocate 3's actual proposal mandates statically-verified or runtime-checked contracts (F006, A005), so the 'unsound-if-lied-to' F001/F003 caveat targets historical C practice, not the position on the table. Meanwhile the 'auto-derivable with mandatory human override' half of this position has zero cards (conceded) — so as evidenced, the position is mandatory expert hand-scheduling of every kernel, defended by one provisional tutorial-page card (A006) for a DSL the caveats file says lacks verified coverage.

The 'best-attested facts' cover one of the position's four fact families. F001–F004 attest disjointness only; the position also surfaces layout, alignment, and contiguity as first-class checkable annotations, for which the advocate's own gaps list admits there is no carded sound, consumed encoding. The core argument's framing — 'the corpus's strongest evidence is precisely about the facts my position surfaces' — is therefore overstated by roughly three-quarters of the position's surface area, and F004's scope limit (source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting noalias returns) is never stated despite cutting directly against 'without inventing new backend machinery.'

A005's runtime checks are not the claimed template. The card documents vectorizer-internal speculative pointer checks that LLVM inserts for its own transformations — not a framework for validating user-written contracts at call boundaries. Scaling from two-pointer overlap checks to disjointness/contiguity of multi-dimensional strided views is a different mechanism with no card behind it; the advocate prices the cost as a gap but never establishes the mechanism exists.

F006 cuts against the position as much as for it. SPARK achieves static dischargeability precisely by restricting the memory model — ownership policy on access types, restricted mutable aliasing. The corpus's only static-checking precedent got checkability by restriction, which is the rivals' move; checkable contracts over unrestricted pointers is the one configuration no card attests. Similarly, the A001 'MemRef is general memory plus contracts' substrate argument backfires: MemRef carries shape/strides/offsets in the type — it is a structured buffer abstraction, closer evidence for shapes-in-types than for annotated raw pointers.

The 'explicitly unsafe' tier is a soundness question the position defines away. One false unsafe-marked disjointness promise silently miscompiles (F003 UB), and whether an explicit opt-in satisfies the debate's 'soundness may not be sacrificed' rule is exactly the unresolved question — 'confined to an explicit opt-in' is a policy assertion, not an argument. Combined with the conceded absence of loop-nest transformation parity (tiling/fusion/interchange evidence all sits with A002/A003 on the structured side), the position concedes both the headline dense-loop wins and the hardest soundness case while claiming the soundness mantle.

All three cases collapse without provisional cards, and none discounts them using the corpus's own error base rate. caveats.md explicitly lists MLIR, Halide, ISPC, Chapel, and Futhark among topics without verified coverage, and records that the same pipeline produced 2 refuted claims and at least 2 budget-dropped claims — a concrete, measured error rate for extraction-grade evidence. Every advocate ritually concedes 'A-cards are provisional' and then builds load-bearing architecture on them (advocate 1's carrier/directionality claims, advocate 2's entire existence proof, advocate 3's MemRef substrate and runtime-check template). Nobody consulted or mentioned notes/refuted-and-unverified.jsonl.

Fact survival through lowering is treated as the rival's problem, but F003's caveat ('metadata is only sound if ... metadata survives lowering') and misleading-comparisons entry 5 apply to every position: structured ops get bufferized and lowered too, schedules lower through the same pipeline, and the dossier's open question — how to validate that MLIR/LLVM actually consume the preserved facts — is universal. Each advocate cites this as a risk for someone else; no advocate accepts that end-to-end consumption is unvalidated for their own path, which makes all three 'shortest/most-native path' claims equally unpriced.

The three positions are presented as rivals, but per the corpus they compose — MLIR (A001–A004) is literally a contract-bearing memory substrate (A001) under structured ops (A002/A003) under transformation control, and each advocate cites the same four cards as their own vindication. The decision-relevant differences are the default surface, where the checking burden falls, and the failure mode of a violated fact (dossier open question 2) — none of which any advocate isolated as the actual decision variable. As framed, the debate risks 'choosing a winner' among positions that a real design would layer.

unsupportedClaims: Advocate 1: 'Structured parallel loops and whole-array operations are the forms in which per-iteration non-interference can be stated as a checkable language rule' — no card attests compiler-checked cross-iteration independence anywhere; A010 and F005 describe programmer obligations, and F001's truthfulness caveat covers them.
Advocate 1: 'A structured-arrays-first surface is a nearly lossless frontend to this pipeline' — no card; the dossier lists end-to-end fact consumption as an open question.
Advocate 1: 'Lowering structure is easy' — asserted as half of the 'decisive' directionality claim; A002/A004 describe mechanisms and rationale, not ease or success rates.

Advocate 1: F009 recast as a general principle mandating structured source forms — the card is a Swift SIL IR-design finding, medium generalization, in an index that calls itself 'not a language-design decision record'; it does not discriminate between the three positions.
Advocate 1: 'Fortran demonstrates a whole language whose first-class-array ... model is optimization-friendly' — F005 attributes the benefit to dummy-argument aliasing contracts, not array structure.

Advocate 2: 'The compiler itself generates every loop nest and every buffer, so it discharges its own noalias/alias.scope obligations' — false at the host boundary where user-supplied buffers may alias; no card supports self-discharge, and misleading-comparisons entry 5 warns against exactly this 'IR metadata solves source design' move.
Advocate 2: storage layout as a schedule-layer decision — A006's claim lists only loop transformations; no card in the read set covers schedule-controlled layout.

Advocate 2: Linalg is 'algorithm/schedule separation rebuilt inside the IR because scalar loops had already destroyed the structure' — causal history invented; A002 says the docs are design rationale.

Advocate 2: 'a vectorize() schedule directive becomes structured n-D vector ops instead of a prayer to the auto-vectorizer ... lowers deterministically' — A004's negative scope (target legality, register pressure, LLVM 1-D limits) contradicts determinism.
Advocate 2: 'SPMD-style execution can itself be a schedule directive' — no card; A007 covers ISPC's model, not its absorption into a schedule layer.

Advocate 3: 'a contracts language has the shortest and best-attested lowering path of any position' — comparative architectural claim with no card; attested carriers (F003/F004) cover disjointness only, not the position's layout/alignment/contiguity annotations.

Advocate 3: 'expose strictly more than restrict without inventing new backend machinery' — omits F004's scope limit that source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting noalias returns; the machinery cost is frontend-side and unpriced.

Advocate 3: A005's runtime pointer checks as 'the deployed template' for making user annotations sound — the card documents vectorizer-internal speculative checks, not user-contract validation at call boundaries.

Advocate 3: 'performance transparent at exactly the site where the optimization is decided — which is the language's stated first principle' — invented framing, and contradicted by the advocate's own concession that outcomes remain cost-model conditional (A005, A010).
missingConsiderations: A checked-vs-promised map of the precedents: the corpus's checked systems (Rust &mut, SPARK, Futhark uniqueness/sizes) verify aliasing/ownership/consumption on restricted models, while every array/loop-structure precedent cited (Fortran dummy rules, DO CONCURRENT, C restrict, Halide schedules, distinct_objects) is promise-based or provisional. The debate's central axis — checkability — is never grounded in which systems actually check what, at what cost.

FFI/host/IO boundary semantics: in all three designs, arrays enter from outside the language (callers, foreign code, I/O buffers). How externally supplied storage acquires shape, disjointness, alignment, and layout facts — and what happens when it doesn't — is unaddressed by every advocate, yet it determines where each design's soundness story actually terminates.

The failure-mode decision (dossier open question 2) as the real decision: static rejection vs runtime check vs trap vs unsafe promise vs conservative fallback vs UB is where the three positions genuinely differ; no card answers it, no advocate isolated it, and the winner of this debate is effectively determined by an answer nobody argued.

FP/numeric semantics as a prerequisite, not a per-position risk: A005 makes math flags a legality input for vectorization, so reduction ordering and reassociation policy must be defined before ANY position's parallel/vector story is sound. Advocates 1 and 2 list it as their own risk; nobody notes it gates all three positions equally and should be decided first.

Degradation under dynamic/symbolic shapes for all positions: only advocate 1 flags it, and only for their own design. Runtime-sized arrays weaken static trip counts (position 1), bounds inference (position 2), and statically dischargeable contracts (position 3) — a shared, corpus-uncovered dimension on which the positions would differentiate in practice.

The extraction pipeline's measured error rate: caveats.md records 2 refuted and at least 2 budget-dropped claims from the same process that produced the A-cards, and notes/refuted-and-unverified.jsonl exists precisely to prevent reintroducing overbroad claims. No advocate used this base rate to discount A-card-dependent arguments, and none checked the refuted list for relevance.

What 'optimizer-first' guarantees actually means: A005 (cost models, hints, fallbacks) and A010 (necessary-not-sufficient) jointly imply no position can promise transformation outcomes — only fact delivery. The language needs an explicit contract about what is guaranteed (facts surfaced and preserved) versus best-effort (transformations performed); advocate 1 gestures at this as a risk, but no one proposes the distinction as a design requirement.

Composition as the null hypothesis: MLIR's own stack (A001 substrate contracts, A002/A003 structured ops, A004 staged vectors) instantiates all three positions simultaneously; the debate never considers that the corpus's strongest shared evidence points to layering with a chosen default, rather than an exclusive winner.
result.sharpestTension: All three positions claim the soundness high ground — 'checkable rules' (structured-first), 'true by construction' (schedule separation), 'verified or runtime-checked contracts' (general memory) — yet each merely relocates, rather than eliminates, the boundary where an unverifiable fact must be trusted: the structured core's escape hatch and views, the pure layer's host-supplied buffers, the contracts language's unsafe tier. The corpus can confirm that non-interference facts are valuable (F001, F002) and that carriers for them exist (F003, A001), but it contains not one card on how any system enforces these facts at its trust boundary, what enforcement costs statically or at runtime, or whether preserved facts are actually consumed end-to-end (the dossier's own open question). Worse, the evidence is structurally incapable of adjudicating between the positions: the adversarially verified F-cards are aliasing-only by selection (caveats.md; misleading-comparisons entry 1), inflating the contracts case, while every array/loop/schedule card is provisional extraction from systems the caveats file explicitly lists as lacking verified coverage, deflating the structured and schedule cases — so each advocate's apparent strength tracks which slice of a biased corpus they cite, not the merits. The debate's decisive question — what happens when a surfaced fact is false, and where policing that boundary is cheapest — is precisely where the corpus is empty, and no position can win until that gap is filled by the verification pass and the end-to-end fact-consumption spike all three advocates independently requested.

challenges.challenge: Mischaracterization of J002: the claim that guards/deopt are 'costs paid at runtime, by every execution, forever' is contradicted by the mechanism J002 itself documents — V8 marks compiled code for deoptimization when const-field or map assumptions break, an invalidation-based scheme whose entire purpose is to avoid per-execution checks on the fast path. J001's negative scope states the residual guard cost versus static knowledge is unquantified, so the 'forever tax' framing asserts a steady-state cost the corpus explicitly declines to measure.

'Zero runtime checks' conflates speculation guards with safety checks. F006 does not say SPARK achieves soundness with zero runtime checks (Ada/SPARK discharge or retain runtime checks depending on proof success); a memory-safe language without speculative machinery still needs bounds/overflow checks unless every safety property is verified SPARK-style, whose scalability F006's negative scope flags as an open usability cost. The position as stated is either unsound (forbidden by ground rules) or not actually check-free — and the advocate never picks.

F005 overclaim: 'Fortran proves decades of production viability' — the card documents what Flang documentation permits compilers to assume under dummy-argument rules, not measured production performance outcomes; caveats.md states documentation evidence does not quantify performance magnitude. Worse, F005's negative scope (POINTER/TARGET/coarray exceptions) and F001's negative scope (unsafe Rust, restrict truthfulness) show every flagship static language needed escape hatches — the advocate's own strongest cards evidence the incompleteness of the pure static channel, a point conceded only obliquely.

'The AOT pipeline is complete end-to-end' (F003 use) restates the exact claim misleading-comparisons.jsonl entry 5 flags as misleading ('IR metadata solves source design'); the corpus's preferred wording is that IR mechanisms are conditional carriers of source facts, sound only if frontend obligations are true and metadata survives lowering. Similarly, the offensive use of entry 3's preferred wording inverts its corrective purpose — that entry exists to block 'GC is bad for performance' inferences, not to license a channel-superiority ranking that no card quantifies.

Self-undermining universal: the position statement says 'every fact the optimizer needs must be a source contract,' but the advocate's own evidence gaps 2 and 6 concede no known sound source encoding exists for frequency facts (hotness, branch bias, value distributions) and no corpus coverage of offline PGO exists. The position therefore forfeits even zero-soundness-risk cost decisions (hot/cold layout, inlining bias) by fiat, and the cost of that forfeiture is bounded by no card — including J002's negative scope, which states deopt-supported speculation enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally perform.

Asymmetric cost accounting on J005: claiming fully static AOT 'pays neither' warmup nor code-cache memory ignores AOT's own uncounted costs — code-size growth from contract-driven specialization/monomorphization and compile-time cost — which caveats.md explicitly lists among the dimensions documentation evidence does not quantify. J005 is also single-implementation HotSpot evidence per its own negative scope; it cannot carry a general static-vs-JIT footprint verdict.

The conditionality argument from F007/J004 assumes conditionality is a property of the runtime channel rather than of escape analysis itself. No card shows any static language delivering *guaranteed* allocation elimination via contract — static escape analysis is also inlining- and analysis-dependent, and F004 (return noalias) is an aliasing fact, not an allocation-elimination guarantee. F007 carries design_generalization_confidence: low, the weakest grade in the confirmed set; the channel-superiority inference built on it exceeds what the card's own grading permits.

challenges.targetPosition: Static correctness plus profile-guided cost decisions (firewall)
challenges.challenge: Core capture claim contradicts the advocate's own concession: the argument says guarded AOT devirt 'captures exactly that benefit' of J002's negative scope, but concession 1 admits guarded-fallback AOT is strictly weaker than deopt-based speculation. J002's sentence credits *deoptimization support* as the enabler; facts whose guards are non-local (e.g., 'this field is never written elsewhere', 'this map never transitions') are speculatable only via invalidation-style machinery the firewall forbids — a compiled-in guard cannot check them cheaply or at all. 'Exactly' should read 'partially, to an unmeasured degree.'

challenges.targetPosition: Static correctness plus profile-guided cost decisions (firewall)
challenges.challenge: Fabricated card support: J003 is cited as 'a concrete instance of the firewall working' with field ordering and hot/cold splitting 'safely steerable by PGO,' but J003 contains no PGO, field-ordering, or layout-steering content whatsoever — it documents V8 hidden classes, transition chains, and element kinds. The concrete firewall instance is invented, and the PGO half of the position has zero cards behind it by the advocate's own admission (evidence gap 1), yet the position statement presents the partition as evidence-derived rather than one evidenced half plus one conjectured half.

challenges.targetPosition: Static correctness plus profile-guided cost decisions (firewall)
challenges.challenge: The firewall leaks by its own wording: 'no profile-derived fact may ever change program meaning... without a total runtime guard' explicitly admits UB-grade correctness facts behind guards (the advocate's own risk 3 / gap 4 alias-versioning case). That is guarded correctness-fact speculation — position 3's mechanism minus deopt — so the categorical 'profiles steer only cost decisions' framing is false as stated, and the real boundary (which guarded correctness speculations are admissible, at what guard cost) is left undefined precisely where it matters.

challenges.targetPosition: Static correctness plus profile-guided cost decisions (firewall)
challenges.challenge: Cross-region contamination unaddressed: inlining a speculatively devirtualized callee imports that callee's contract-derived alias facts (noalias metadata, alias.scope domains per F003) into a profile-chosen region. Whether that metadata remains sound across the guard join and fallback merge is exactly F003's 'metadata survives lowering / frontend obligations true' soundness condition — the advocate's firewall polices which facts *enter* from profiles but says nothing about contract facts *flowing through* profile-shaped code, and no card covers it (advocate 3 raises this risk; advocate 2, whose position depends on it, does not).

challenges.targetPosition: Static correctness plus profile-guided cost decisions (firewall)
challenges.challenge: 'Wrong profile ⇒ slower, never wrong' is stated as a property but is actually a proof obligation: evidence gap 3 concedes no card verifies that LLVM passes never convert profile metadata (branch weights, value profiles) into semantic assumptions. Until that isolation is demonstrated in the implementation, the firewall is a spec aspiration, and the position's soundness story is no more evidenced than advocate 3's deopt-correctness story it criticizes.

False trichotomy at unprovable boundaries: 'forgo / assert-and-eat-UB / guard-and-deopt' omits option (d), a guarded fast path with a compiled statically-correct fallback and no deoptimization — advocate 2's mechanism. 'Only (c) is simultaneously fast and sound' is therefore false as argued; deopt is needed only to remove guards from fast paths or discard specialized code wholesale, and no card evidences that need in an AOT setting (the advocate's own gap 1 concedes zero AOT-plus-deopt evidence). The position's defining component — deoptimization — is exactly the part its cited evidence never justifies for this language class.

'Contract-equivalent' overclaim on J003: a contract holds on every execution of every run; a guard-dominated region's facts are conditional on the guard holding and, per J002's one-directional element-kind generalization, can permanently degrade after a few bad inputs. The source mark shows where speculation MAY occur, not its current specialization state, so two identical binaries diverge in performance after divergent input history — the history-dependent opacity the position claims its source-visibility answers is in fact reintroduced at every marked site. J003's negative scope ('real-world differences are often small') also cuts against the value of the recovered facts.

Guard-class mismatch: all corpus guard evidence (J001-J003) concerns cheap tag/shape/map checks in effectively single-mutator runtimes (GIL'd CPython, per-isolate V8). But the boundaries the position targets — the unsafe/FFI/pointer escape hatches of F001 and F005 — need *alias* guards, and no card shows cheap runtime checks exist for general aliasing facts (loop versioning aside, entirely unevidenced here). The sound-escape-hatch argument is strongest exactly where guards are cheapest (types/shapes) and is asserted where they are hardest (aliasing), which is also where the F002-class payoff lives.

Selectivity does not confine the cost, by the advocate's own concession 7: deopt machinery (frame-reconstruction metadata, invalidation bookkeeping, optimization restrictions across deopt points) is a language-wide tax even if speculation is rare, contradicting the core argument's claim that J005-style costs are answered by narrowness. The corpus's nearest analogue is F008 — funclet-style exit paths force frame-pointer access and forbid register allocation for values live across them — the only confirmed card suggesting how exit-path machinery pessimizes surrounding code, and the one card no advocate cited. It cuts against this position hardest.

J001/J005 transfer overstated: CPython PEP 659 is interpreter-level specialization with no compiled-frame deopt, and the advocate's howItSupports for J005 says warmup/memory costs 'quantify' the selectivity constraint when J005 quantifies nothing (caveats.md: documentation evidence does not quantify magnitude, and J005's negative scope is single-implementation). The three-runtime convergence is convergence among dynamic-language whole-program JITs; the advocate concedes this (concession 2) yet the core argument still treats the convergence as evidence for grafting the mechanism onto a contract-first AOT language.

unsupportedClaims: Advocate 1: 'Predictability — source text determining machine code — is itself a first-class performance feature' — load-bearing in the position statement, backed by no card measuring variance, tail latency, startup value, or real-time value (the advocate flags this in risks but keeps it as the position's headline).

Advocate 1: 'guards, deopt, and warmup are costs paid at runtime, by every execution, forever' — contradicted by the invalidation-based mechanism J002 itself documents, and the residual guard cost is explicitly unquantified per J001's negative scope.

Advocate 1: 'Fortran proves decades of production viability for rule-assumed non-aliasing' — F005 documents permitted compiler assumptions in Flang docs, not production performance; caveats.md bars magnitude claims from documentation evidence.
Advocate 1: SPARK verification keeps 'the whole scheme sound with zero runtime checks' — F006 makes no zero-runtime-check claim; SPARK/Ada retain runtime checks where proofs do not discharge them.
Advocate 1: 'the AOT pipeline is complete end-to-end' — collides with misleading-comparisons entry 5: IR metadata is a conditional carrier, not a solution to source design.

Advocates 1 and 2: the inference 'runtime recovery is conditional (F007/J004), therefore source contracts make these optimizations guaranteed' — no card shows any static language guaranteeing allocation elimination via contract; conditionality may be inherent to escape analysis, and F007's design-generalization confidence is graded low.

Advocate 2: 'My position captures exactly that benefit in AOT form' — contradicted by its own concession 1 (strictly weaker) and unsupported by J002, whose negative scope credits deoptimization support, not profile-plus-guarded-fallback.
Advocate 2: J003 as 'a concrete instance of the firewall working' for field ordering and hot/cold splitting — content absent from the card.
Advocate 2: 'offline PGO moves those costs to build time, which is the correct trade' — zero PGO cards exist in the corpus (own gap 1); 'correct trade' is a value judgment presented as conclusion.
Advocate 2: 'wrong profile ⇒ slower, never wrong' — an unverified implementation property (own gap 3 concedes no evidence LLVM profile inputs are correctness-isolated).

Advocate 3: a guard 'delivers contract-equivalent layout/shape facts to the region it dominates' — equivalence fails across executions and after one-directional de-specialization (J002); J003 is V8-specific and notes real-world differences are often small.
Advocate 3: 'Only [guard-plus-deopt] is simultaneously fast and sound' — false trichotomy omitting guarded static fallback without deopt.

All three: F009 is stretched beyond its content in three incompatible directions — as an argument against runtime machinery (A1), for PGO-artifact inspectability (A2), and for source-visible guards (A3); the card supports only 'preserve source semantics in an IR the optimizer can see' and adjudicates none of these.
missingConsiderations: Concurrency invalidates the guard evidence base: J001-J003 come from effectively single-mutator runtimes (GIL'd CPython, per-isolate V8), where guard-then-use is atomic by construction. In a concurrent systems language, guards are racy without safepoint-style machinery (HotSpot's, whose cost no card covers), and noalias-style contract facts interact with the memory model. caveats.md explicitly lists memory models and concurrency as unverified corpus territory; no advocate mentioned threads once.

The FFI/external-call boundary breaks all three designs the same way: any call into unverified foreign code threatens F003's soundness condition for alias metadata, and no position explains how contract facts survive extern calls, how guards could check foreign effects, or how the firewall classifies facts about foreign memory. This is the most common real boundary and it is undiscussed.

F008 is the only confirmed non-aliasing optimization-mechanism card and nobody cited it. It is doubly relevant: (a) it shows representation/exit-path machinery (funclets) pessimizing register allocation for values live across the boundary — the closest corpus analogue to deopt points and guarded fallback joins as optimization barriers; (b) it shows the workflow refuted two overbroad claims in this area, a warning about generalizing mechanism claims that applies to every advocate's extrapolations.

The debate's decisive evidence is unreadable by every participant: the D-series (dispatch) cards would bear directly on speculative devirtualization — advocate 2's flagship example and advocate 3's strongest traditional use case — and the N/A/C series on numerics, arrays, and concurrency were referenced in the brief but assigned to no one, including the critic. Any decision memo must treat all dispatch reasoning in this debate as mechanism-plausible and corpus-unevidenced.

Nobody priced the static channel's own costs: contract-driven monomorphization/specialization code-size growth, compile-time cost, and verification time are exactly the dimensions caveats.md says documentation evidence does not quantify — yet advocate 1 compares channels as if AOT's side of the ledger were zero.

A hybrid nobody considered: profile-checked source annotations — frequency/hotness hints written in source but validated against profiles at build time (error or warning on contradiction). This dissolves advocate 2's 'frequency contracts are the one thing the compiler can never check' argument and gives advocate 1 a sound frequency channel without runtime machinery; its absence shows the debate treated 'contract' and 'profile' as exhaustive channels when checkable-hint is a third.

Guard feasibility is fact-class-dependent and no advocate mapped it: type/shape/value guards are cheap tag checks (J001); alias and non-interference guards require loop versioning or are impossible for general pointer graphs; effect-freedom and layout-stability guards fall in between. The debate argued about 'speculation' as a monolith when the admissible mechanism plausibly differs per fact class — the partition question is finer-grained than any position stated.

One-directional de-specialization (J002) implies binaries acquire permanent, input-history-dependent performance states; advocate 3 lists it as a risk but no one drew the design conclusion: if speculation is admitted, re-specialization policy and cliff diagnostics become mandatory language features for a performance-transparent language, not optional tooling.

misleading-comparisons entry 3 is a two-edged discipline: it forbids 'GC/managed runtimes are bad' as much as it supports 'recovery is conditional.' Advocates 1 and 2 used the conditionality half offensively while ignoring that the corpus refuses to rank channels on performance magnitude at all.
result.sharpestTension: All three positions agree wherever the corpus has verified evidence, and disagree only where it has none. The adversarially confirmed cards (F001-F006, F009) establish exactly the territory of consensus — statically checkable non-interference contracts are the preferred channel — while the entire live disagreement concerns the residual set: frequency facts, true-but-unprovable facts, and open-world boundaries. For that residual set the corpus contains zero verified cards: no offline-PGO evidence, no AOT-plus-deopt evidence, no guard-cost quantification (J001's negative scope names this), no measurement of how large the residual set even is, and no readable dispatch (D-series) cards. The one sentence on which both non-static positions pivot — 'deoptimization also enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles' (J002 negative scope) — is a provisional, unverified extraction, and advocate 1's counter-position leans equally hard on unquantified cost claims from the same provisional J-cards. Concretely: positions 2 and 3 nearly collapse into one another (both permit guarded speculation with a sound fallback; they differ only on offline-vs-runtime profiles and compiled-fallback-vs-deopt), and no card prices that specific delta. The decision the debate is meant to inform therefore reduces to an asymmetric-risk judgment the evidence cannot make: false-contract UB (miscompile — bounded only by unevidenced verification-at-scale, F006 negative scope), history-dependent performance opacity (J002's one-directional degradation — unquantified), or forfeited optimization (advocate 1's honest 'the optimizer does not get that fact' — unbounded). Per caveats.md's standing rule, the only evidence-faithful outcome is a conditional decision plus a commissioned evidence phase: verify the J-cards, read the D/N/A/C series, and obtain at least one quantified comparison of guard cost, PGO benefit, and contract-verification cost before choosing among the three.

challenges.challenge: The claim that type-system ownership is 'the only surveyed mechanism where the non-interference facts are compiler-verified rather than programmer-promised' is contradicted by the advocate's own cards. F001's positive scope says SPARK 'rejects non-benign aliasing' and F006 shows SPARK's anti-aliasing of mutable parameters is machine-checked — and that check is a function-boundary contract (Position 2's family), not per-reference ownership. The advocate annexes SPARK as an 'ownership' precedent while Position 2 legitimately claims the same card; SPARK per F006 is actually a hybrid (checked boundary anti-aliasing + ownership only for access types + side-effect-free expressions). F001 also makes no ranking among the four mechanisms — 'only ownership is verified' is spin in one direction, exactly as Position 2's 'co-equal' is spin in the other.

The 'independent convergence / design attractor' argument is unsupported. F006 says nothing about SPARK's ownership policy arising independently of Rust; the design-lineage claim (which background knowledge actually suggests runs the other way — SPARK's access-type ownership post-dates and resembles Rust's, an unverified claim I flag rather than assert) has no card. Two data points with a plausible causal arrow between them is not convergent evolution, and the attractor argument carries real rhetorical weight in the case as written.

The two headline dividends undermine each other, and the advocate never confronts this. The concurrency dividend (C004: Send/Sync makes sharing a type-level fact) is delivered in the exemplar language via interior mutability — the very feature F001's negative scope excludes from the exclusivity guarantee and misleading-comparisons #2 names as a complication that breaks the ownership-to-noalias lowering. Every safe shared-state concurrency primitive the language adds re-punctures the shared-references-are-immutable noalias fact. The advocate lists interior mutability as a lowering nuisance in concessions but presents the sequential and concurrency payoffs as additive ('double dividend') when the corpus indicates they trade against each other.

F004 is overclaimed: 'an ownership type system with move semantics naturally proves the fresh, disjoint from all caller-accessible storage property for owned returns.' F004's negative scope says precisely the opposite of 'naturally' — source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules BEFORE emitting return noalias — and memory-object-model.md lists those exact prerequisites (provenance, interior-pointer stability, object identity) as open questions. An owned return is not automatically allocation-fresh (ownership can be threaded through from caller-reachable storage); the gap between 'owned' and 'disjoint from all caller-accessible storage' is exactly what the card's caveat exists to flag.

F007 is misapplied to dismiss the inference alternative. The index rates F007's design-generalization confidence LOW, and the card is about a profile-guided JIT's allocation elimination and lock elision — not about AOT compile-time alias inference for load/store optimization. Using a low-generalization card about a different optimization class under a different compilation model as the argument that 'guaranteed type-carried facts beat best-effort recovery' stretches it across two category boundaries at once. Relatedly, 'the optimizer gets UB-grade freedom on safe code' (via C002) never engages C003's negative scope and C005: LLVM treats racing non-atomic loads as undef, not UB, and per-language rules differ from IR rules — the source-model/IR-model mapping the argument needs is asserted, not shown.

The load-bearing premise is refuted by the advocate's own central card. 'The optimizer consumes non-interference facts at exactly one granularity: the function signature' is false per F003, whose positive scope says alias.scope/noalias metadata attaches to loads, stores, memory-accessing calls, and fences — instruction-level facts that exist precisely because signature attributes cannot express finer-grained disjointness. With that, the 'structurally isomorphic, lossless one-to-one lowering' argument loses its foundation: signature contracts map to the attribute half of LLVM's vocabulary and simply cannot state the metadata half.

The targeting argument — 'intraprocedural analysis already recovers much of this, so language design's marginal value concentrates at opaque boundaries' — is a quantitative claim with zero quantitative support. F002 names the analyses and optimization classes; its negative scope says it 'does not quantify performance magnitude,' and caveats.md says documentation evidence usually does not quantify. How much intraprocedural analysis recovers, and where the residual losses actually sit, is exactly the kind of measurement the corpus lacks; the entire economy of the position rests on it.

'Strongest empirical exemplar' and 'decades of production evidence' mischaracterize F005, which is Flang documentation of what compilers MAY assume — a specification claim, not empirical performance evidence. caveats.md's discipline (docs show claims, not magnitudes) applies squarely. Worse, the Fortran precedent's transferability is never examined: F005's own negative scope lists POINTER/TARGET/coarray/host-association carve-outs, and no card addresses how boundary contracts behave in a language with first-class function values — misleading-comparisons #2 names callbacks as a fact-destroyer, and memory-object-model.md lists callbacks among open invalidation questions. The position has no effect-polymorphism story for higher-order code at all.

The position equivocates between two different designs. The headline form (Fortran-grade assumed conformance) is inadmissible under the soundness ground rule, as conceded. But the admissible form (SPARK-style checked) silently imports the rival's machinery: per F006, SPARK can check 'two mutable parameters never alias' only because it ALSO imposes ownership on access types and bans side-effecting expressions — checking non-aliasing of general heap pointers at a boundary requires an ownership/region discipline, which is the whole-program problem contracts were supposed to avoid. The selling points (simple type system, low burden, reversibility) belong exclusively to the unsound form; the sound form is Position 1 at every hard case. The concession list mentions the coverage cliff but never this structural collapse.

The trust-boundary symmetry argument launders an asymmetry. C004's negative scope establishes that Rust's route has trust points (unsafe, manual Send/Sync), but it does not establish equivalence of trust-surface SIZE: opt-in unsafe blocks are a localized, greppable residue, whereas assumed-conformance contracts make every ordinary function signature a UB-bearing trust point (F003: violation is UB), with no diagnostic on violation — the advocate's own risk list admits refactors 'do not fail to compile in assumed mode.' 'Both bottom out in trust' is true and does not support 'the difference is placement, not existence' as a neutralizing move. Likewise 'the reversible mechanism should win' rests on an unevidenced reversibility claim: a default assume-no-alias rule at every boundary is semantic bedrock for every compiled program, and F006 shows SPARK-on-Ada layering exists, not that it is cheap or performant.

The core mechanism has zero exemplar cards — conceded, but the concession is not priced in. 'Lowering becomes a near-identity translation' is supported by nothing in the corpus, and it inherits the same granularity mismatch as Position 2: signature-level declared regions are not per-access alias.scope metadata (F003 attaches metadata to individual loads/stores/calls/fences). The frontend must still classify every access into a region and the verifier must prove membership — which is exactly the 'lossy projection step' the advocate accuses rivals of. A position whose central claim (losslessness) and central mechanism (disjointness verifier) both lack any card is not merely under-evidenced; under this corpus's standing rule it is not yet a citable design.

F006 is stretched beyond its scope. The card verifies that SPARK checks aliasing RESTRICTIONS (anti-aliasing of parameters, ownership on access types, side-effect-free expressions). It does not show verification of programmer-declared read/write/disjointness sets over named heap regions — the actual load-bearing mechanism of this position. 'SPARK is existence proof that declared non-interference can be verified' converts evidence for restriction-checking into evidence for disjointness-proof, precisely at the frontier where the advocate concedes collapse into ownership. SPARK's decidability is bought by banning the hard cases, not by discharging them.

The separate-compilation argument strawmans the rival. Ownership facts in Position 1 are signature-carried types — they survive separate compilation exactly as effect annotations do; the only inference-based exemplar in the corpus is F007 (a JIT), which nobody advocates. 'Precisely where inference-based approaches degrade' attacks a weakness the leading rival does not have. Separately, misleading-comparisons #2 is deployed offensively as if regions were exempt from its complications, when unsafe code, callbacks, FFI, and interior-mutability equivalents puncture declared write sets identically — the advocate concedes the FFI/exception gap elsewhere but still uses the entry as a differentiator.

The 'generated/ugly-by-design' premise is not in the charter. The ground rules say human ergonomics may be sacrificed; they do not say code is machine-generated. The dismissal of annotation drift and virality as 'regeneration cost rather than human pain' rests on this unstated usage-model assumption, and even granting it, whoever generates annotations must reliably discharge verifier obligations — the discharge rate is admitted to have no evidence, and F006's negative scope (usability costs) applies to generators too, since annotation burden affects annotation correctness.

F009 does no discriminating work and should be struck from adjudication weight: all three advocates cite it as supporting their side, and the card (keep performance-critical semantics visible in a high-level IR) is equally compatible with ownership facts, boundary contracts, or region annotations. None engages its negative scope (implementation-specific, OSSA/non-OSSA phase distinctions, stale docs). Similarly, every position uses C003 to claim it 'buys back' the listed freedoms without engaging C003's negative scope that the rules differ per language/IR and that LLVM's undef-racing-loads semantics is not the source model — no advocate specified the actual IR channel by which 'this memory is unshared' reaches LLVM beyond choosing NotAtomic ordering (C005).

All three positions state facts at boundary or whole-reference granularity, but the corpus's payoff card (F002) includes loop-centric transformations (LICM, promotion across iterations), and none of the positions as stated can express element-level or iteration-level disjointness within a single object — the granularity that vectorization and loop parallelization consume. caveats.md explicitly lists arrays, layout, Halide, ISPC, Futhark, and Chapel as uncovered; only Position 3 flags a 'granularity ceiling' and only against itself. For an optimizer-first language this may be the dominant non-interference surface, and the debate has not touched it.

unsupportedClaims: P1: 'Only type-system ownership makes the fact statically checked per reference' — contradicted by F001/F006 (SPARK's machine-checked boundary anti-aliasing); no card ranks the mechanisms.
P1: 'Two independent designs converged... a design attractor, not a single-language accident' — no card addresses SPARK/Rust design lineage or independence.

P1: 'An ownership type system with move semantics naturally proves the fresh, disjoint-from-caller-storage property for owned returns' — F004's negative scope requires lifetime/provenance/escape rules first; memory-object-model.md lists them as open.

P1: 'Exclusive-by-default references maximize the program surface over which this proof exists' — coverage claim with no measurement anywhere in the corpus; misleading-comparisons #2 documents the surface leaks.
P1: 'The optimizer gets UB-grade freedom on safe code' — asserted without engaging C005 (racing non-atomic loads are undef, not UB) and C003's negative scope on source-vs-IR rule mismatch.
P2: 'The optimizer consumes non-interference facts at exactly one granularity: the function signature' — refuted by F003 (alias.scope/noalias metadata attaches to loads, stores, calls, fences).

P2: 'Intraprocedural LLVM/GCC analysis already recovers much of this' and 'marginal value concentrates at opaque call boundaries' — unquantified; F002's negative scope and caveats.md preclude magnitude claims.
P2: Fortran as 'strongest empirical exemplar' with 'decades of production evidence' — F005 is specification/documentation evidence of what compilers may assume, not empirical performance evidence.
P2: 'Signature contracts are forward-compatible... the reversible mechanism' — no card evidences reversibility, layering cost, or the performance of SPARK-on-Ada as a layered system.

P2: Runtime overlap checks at function entry as a soundness tier — no corpus coverage of the mechanism, its cost, or its failure semantics (halt vs versioned fallback), and it covers only checkable shapes by the advocate's own admission.
P3: 'Lowering becomes a near-identity translation / lossless from source to LLVM IR' — no exemplar card for any declared-region language or its lowering; contradicted in granularity by F003.

P3: 'SPARK is existence proof that declared non-interference can be machine-verified' as applied to declared read/write/disjoint region sets — F006 covers restriction checking and ownership on access types, not region-disjointness verification.
P3: 'Annotation verbosity is the historical reason effect systems lose to ownership sugar' — no card; the corpus contains no effect-system language at all.
P3: The language is 'generated/ugly-by-design' — the charter permits sacrificing ergonomics; it does not state a machine-generation usage model.

P3: Separate compilation is 'precisely where inference-based approaches degrade' as an argument against the ownership rival — ownership facts are signature-carried types; the only inference exemplar in the corpus is F007, a JIT, which no one advocates.
missingConsiderations: C006 was cited by no advocate: immutability-after-construction / publication safety is the only corpus-attested non-interference contract left unused, and it points at a fourth mechanism family (freeze semantics, immutable-by-default data, value semantics). Given F002's framing that writes are the primary hazard, 'make it immutable' is the cheapest non-interference fact there is; caveats.md notes functional/value-semantics routes (Haskell/ML/Futhark) are uncovered, so this is an evidence gap AND an unargued position.

The corpus's best sound exemplar is a hybrid, not any single mechanism: F006's SPARK combines checked boundary anti-aliasing (P2), ownership on access types (P1), and side-effect-free expressions (adjacent to P3's effects). The debate's pick-one framing may be a false trilemma; only P3 gestures at complementarity, and no advocate proposed the hybrid as the primary design with card support it actually has.

Higher-order functions and callbacks: no position specifies how its facts survive function values (effect/region polymorphism for P2/P3; closure captures for P1's noalias emission). misleading-comparisons #2 names callbacks as a lowering complication and memory-object-model.md lists callbacks among open invalidation questions, yet no advocate treated this as a first-class design axis.

Global/static and ambient state: parameter contracts (P2) and reference exclusivity (P1) as presented say nothing about callee access to globals; F005's host-association complication gestures at the problem. Mod/ref over globals is a required part of any boundary summary and is unaddressed by all three.

The checker as trusted computing base: soundness is non-negotiable, and all three positions rest on a large trusted static component (borrow checker, contract checker, disjointness verifier). The corpus has zero cards on implementation defect history or compile-time cost for any of them, so the soundness ground rule cannot currently distinguish the positions on the axis that matters most to it — implementation soundness, not paper soundness.

Fact durability through the pipeline: F003's negative scope (metadata must survive lowering and be consumed) is flagged by all three as a gap but engaged by none as a design input — e.g., whether to follow F009 and exploit facts in a self-owned high-level IR before LLVM, precisely because LLVM-side survival is unmeasured. Only P1 mentions the F009 route concretely; P2 and P3 wave at it.

Race-semantics for the non-safe tier and the source-to-IR mapping: C002's UB-vs-bounded decision still must be made for unsafe/FFI code under every position, and C005/C003 show LLVM's race semantics (undef racing loads) differ from every source model surveyed. Only P1 mentions this residually; no position states which race semantics its escape hatch gets and how that lowers soundly.

Aggregate and loop granularity: whole-object exclusivity or whole-parameter noalias does not express element-level disjointness inside one array — the fact vectorizers and parallelizers need. The corpus is silent on arrays/layout (caveats.md), which means the debate topic itself may be scoped at the wrong granularity for an optimizer-first language.
result.sharpestTension: Under the non-negotiable soundness rule, all three positions are forced into machine-checked discharge — and the corpus's only checked exemplars buy decidability by restricting the language, which makes the three positions collapse toward each other at exactly the frontier where the evidence runs out. Position 2's admissible (checked) form needs ownership-like machinery to decide anti-aliasing over heap pointers (F006: SPARK checks boundary contracts only atop access-type ownership and banned side effects); Position 3's verifier concedes fallback to an ownership sub-discipline wherever disjointness is hard; and Position 1's pervasive-coverage premise is itself corpus-flagged as leaky at that same frontier (misleading-comparisons #2: unsafe, interior mutability, callbacks, FFI) — with the added twist that its concurrency dividend (C004) is delivered through the very interior-mutability feature that punctures its aliasing facts. So every position delivers true facts only over an unmeasured 'discharged subset' and goes silent on an identical residue, while the corpus quantifies neither the residue's size in performance-critical code nor the optimization payoff of the facts (F002 negative scope; caveats.md). The debate's real question — which surface delivers the most true, LLVM-consumable non-interference facts per unit of soundness machinery — is therefore unanswerable from this corpus; what the positions actually differ on today is where the trust boundary sits and which one-way door gets locked first. Until someone produces (a) frontier-size measurements on real performance-critical code and (b) noalias-on/off benchmark deltas, choosing among them is a bet on defaults dressed as an evidence-based decision, and the honest output of this debate is the measurement plan, not a winner.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 1: fully explicit numeric modes (wrapping/saturating/checked/unchecked as distinct operators; scoped FP annotations)
challenges.challenge: The 'forced dilemma' (wrap-lowering forfeits nsw/nuw facts vs. silently emitting false promises) is a false dichotomy. The implementation warning that P1 itself quotes lists four truth-makers — 'source semantics, verifier, runtime checks, or unsafe boundary' — and runtime checks (a trapping/checked default, P3's position) make no-overflow facts true by construction without any explicit per-operation mode. A fourth path, compiler-inferred flags (LLVM itself infers nsw/known-bits from dominating guards and types), is uncovered by the corpus in either direction, so it cannot be silently excluded. Therefore the uniqueness claim 'nothing-implicit is the ONLY policy under which soundness, performance transparency, and Alive2-style auditability all hold simultaneously' is unsupported by any card.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 1: fully explicit numeric modes (wrapping/saturating/checked/unchecked as distinct operators; scoped FP annotations)
challenges.challenge: P1 wields misleading-comparisons entry 5 ('IR metadata solves source design' is misleading; metadata is a 'conditional carrier of source facts') against rivals while committing the mirror error: it derives source-language surface design ('near-1:1 spellings of the target's existing contract vocabulary', 'zero lowering-invention risk') from LLVM IR structure. Every N-card's negative_scope says it 'supports optimizer-facing LLVM contracts, not a final source-language policy' with design_generalization_confidence: low, and caveats.md says implementation files 'should constrain later debates by asking what can be represented... not decide source syntax or semantics.' The argument's direction of inference — IR vocabulary therefore source vocabulary — is exactly what the corpus's own discipline forbids, and it couples core language semantics to LLVM IR whose implementation_stability_confidence is only medium.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 1: fully explicit numeric modes (wrapping/saturating/checked/unchecked as distinct operators; scoped FP annotations)
challenges.challenge: The bare-`+` 'refinement' concedes the debate without answering it. Once bare `+` must abbreviate exactly one named mode, P1 still owes an answer to the question actually posed — what does ordinary arithmetic in ordinary code mean? — which is the checked-vs-wrapping-vs-trapping-vs-poison default the corpus lists as undecided. Whichever mode bare `+` abbreviates, P1 inherits the corresponding critique (fact-forfeiture if wrapping, N003-analogous speculation costs if checked, soundness violation if unchecked). P1 is a position about spelling and audit surface, not about semantics; presented as an answer to the topic, it is a category evasion.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 1: fully explicit numeric modes (wrapping/saturating/checked/unchecked as distinct operators; scoped FP annotations)
challenges.challenge: Unchecked-assume-no-overflow as an ordinary explicit operator has no truth-maker, and explicitness is not one. The implementation warning's sanctioned truth-makers do not include 'the programmer typed a special operator'; F001's own scope limit warns that restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions,' and F003's lesson is that the encoding is sound only if the obligation is true. P1 relegates unsafe-gating to a conditional future ('if the project later bans source UB...'), but under this debate's ground rules soundness is non-negotiable now — so the position as stated contains a standing soundness violation in its core mode set, not merely a 'risk'.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 1: fully explicit numeric modes (wrapping/saturating/checked/unchecked as distinct operators; scoped FP annotations)
challenges.challenge: N006 is misused for the FP half. The card records InstCombine testing discipline (flag-interaction tests, negative tests, Alive2 proofs, 'analogous care to fast-math flags'), at medium confidence with a 2-1 constituent vote. It says nothing about per-operation versus global FP relaxation at the source level, and its negative_scope expressly excludes strict-vs-fast FP. Calling it 'the strongest available LLVM-side precedent that FP relaxation is naturally a per-operation/scoped permission' converts optimizer test ceremony into a source-design endorsement; moreover, a global -ffast-math switch lowers to the same per-instruction IR flags, so IR flag granularity cannot discriminate scoped from global source designs at all.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 1: fully explicit numeric modes (wrapping/saturating/checked/unchecked as distinct operators; scoped FP annotations)
challenges.challenge: 'Zero lowering-invention risk for its core' overstates the card coverage: only two of the four modes (wrapping = unflagged add, unchecked = nsw/nuw) have card-backed IR targets via N002. Checked lowering ('overflow-check-then-branch', overflow intrinsics) has no card, saturating has zero evidence (conceded), and the mixed-mode conversion rules P1 admits are 'unvalidated design work' sit precisely where InstCombine's target-independent canonicalization (N006) could interact badly with mode-diverse input. Half the mode lattice is card-supported; the other half is invention.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 2: no-overflow contract default (debug trap, release nsw/poison, freeze fallback)
challenges.challenge: Argument (7) satisfies 'soundness may not be sacrificed' only by choosing its own reading of the rule: soundness relative to a contract the shipped binary does not enforce. The corpus explicitly lists 'whether source-level UB is acceptable or should be replaced with proof, debug checks, traps, or unsafe-only assumptions' as undecided — P2 resolves that undecided question by definitional fiat and then claims ground-rule compliance. Under the strict reading, the compliant variant is the freeze-on-violation mode, which P2 concedes is unpriced — meaning the position that actually satisfies the ground rules is a different, unevaluated design, and the advertised optimization case (full poison exploitation) belongs to the non-compliant variant.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 2: no-overflow contract default (debug trap, release nsw/poison, freeze fallback)
challenges.challenge: The freeze fallback is under-analyzed as a 'soundness' mechanism. Per N001, freeze(poison) yields an arbitrary-but-fixed value — neither the wrapped result nor a trap. An arbitrary frozen value flowing into index or size arithmetic still produces memory unsafety unless the language independently bounds-checks all indexing; its interaction with N005's inbounds/index contracts (where overflow facts matter most) is never examined. So 'freeze converts UB into bounded nondeterminism' establishes IR-level definedness only, not language-level safety. Additionally, P2's own risk list notes freeze insertion blocks later flag-based folds — the fallback may quietly forfeit the very nsw wins that justify the position, and no card prices this.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 2: no-overflow contract default (debug trap, release nsw/poison, freeze fallback)
challenges.challenge: 'Wrap-by-default permanently forfeits this channel' is overclaimed from N002. The card establishes what unflagged add means and what nsw means; it says nothing about facts having to originate as frontend-emitted flags. Compiler inference of nsw/nuw and value ranges from types, dominating checks, and analysis is entirely uncovered by this corpus — and P2's own concession that the phase-2 workflow had many stalled subagents means absence of an inference card is a coverage artifact, not evidence. The word 'permanently' converts an unmeasured comparison (declared vs. inferred facts) into a settled loss.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 2: no-overflow contract default (debug trap, release nsw/poison, freeze fallback)
challenges.challenge: N006 is stretched into 'the most heavily machine-verified corner of LLVM's transformation suite.' The card reports contributor-guide expectations (transforms 'should' be tested, 'usually' backed by Alive2), at medium source-accuracy confidence with the flag-testing claim itself voted 2-1. Worse for P2, the same card is read by P3 as evidence that flag-sensitive folds are the highest-risk semantic surface — the ceremony exists because this is where miscompiles concentrate. One card cannot serve simultaneously as P2's safety assurance and P3's hazard warning; as it stands it supports neither reading beyond 'this area gets extra testing.'

challenges.targetPosition: Position 2: no-overflow contract default (debug trap, release nsw/poison, freeze fallback)
challenges.challenge: The debug/release split creates two frontend lowering regimes (trap IR in debug, nsw-poison IR in release), so the IR contracts that ship are precisely the ones ordinary testing never executes, and the N006-style verification burden doubles. P2's safety leg rests on debug-check detection efficacy (conceded unmeasured) and its performance leg rests on unquantified nsw benefit (conceded unmeasured). A position whose two load-bearing legs are both admittedly unpriced cannot claim to 'dominate' trap-by-default or wrapping — 'dominates' is a comparative performance/safety claim with zero comparative data in the corpus.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 3: trap-by-default in all builds, strict IEEE FP, opt-ins behind proof/unsafe
challenges.challenge: Direct card misuse of N004: the card's positive_scope states range metadata is allowed ONLY on integer (or vector-of-integer) loads, calls, and invokes — not on arithmetic results. Therefore 'a trapping default is a systematic generator of true range facts... a lawful, high-volume channel' cannot flow through the cited mechanism: you cannot attach range metadata to a post-check add. Post-check fact recovery would require dominating-branch reasoning or llvm.assume, for which no card exists. The centerpiece of argument (1) — checks become optimizer fuel — has a card-backed first half (checks make facts true) and an entirely unevidenced second half (LLVM consumes those facts), which P3 buries in its gaps list while presenting the pipeline as established.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 3: trap-by-default in all builds, strict IEEE FP, opt-ins behind proof/unsafe
challenges.challenge: 'Trap-by-default is the ONLY default that makes contracts true by construction' is a fiat-dependent uniqueness claim. It holds only after ruling that contract-defined UB (P2's source semantics) violates the soundness ground rule — the exact interpretive question the corpus lists as undecided. P3 accuses UB-by-default of 'resolving by fiat' while resolving the same ambiguity by fiat in the opposite direction. Relatedly, reading the implementation warning as an exhaustive enumeration in which runtime checks are 'the only truth-maker available by default' ignores that 'source semantics' is listed first and is precisely what P2 invokes — and that warning is a scaffolding file caveats.md says must 'not decide source syntax or semantics,' cited here as the load-bearing step of the core argument in violation of the standing rule requiring exact evidence cards.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 3: trap-by-default in all builds, strict IEEE FP, opt-ins behind proof/unsafe
challenges.challenge: The N001 lattice argument inverts what the card licenses. 'A trapping default keeps default-mode programs at the fully-defined end, where every LLVM transform is unconditionally valid' is rhetorically misleading: fully-defined, side-effecting trap semantics give the optimizer LESS freedom, because every check is an observable event constraining speculation and code motion — which is P3's own N003 concession. The lattice governs the validity direction of IR transforms; it says nothing about which source default is safer or about auditing lexically scoped relaxations. The card is being used as decoration for a safety intuition it does not address.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 3: trap-by-default in all builds, strict IEEE FP, opt-ins behind proof/unsafe
challenges.challenge: The FP plank contradicts its own primary card's scope: N006's negative_scope expressly excludes strict-vs-fast floating point, yet P3 names N006 its 'primary card for strict-IEEE-by-default.' Separately, 'strict-by-default cannot be wrong about semantics; it can only be slow' hides an uncarded equivocation: 'strict IEEE-conforming' (rounding modes, FP exceptions, environment access) is stronger than LLVM's default unflagged FP lowering, and honoring it requires constrained-intrinsics/strictfp machinery with zero corpus coverage. P3 may be promising semantics that are not merely slow but unimplementable-as-specified on the evidence available.

challenges.targetPosition: Position 3: trap-by-default in all builds, strict IEEE FP, opt-ins behind proof/unsafe
challenges.challenge: The headline property and its performance mitigation are mutually corrosive, and P3 never reconciles them. 'Deterministic traps in all builds' is sold as 'tested program = shipped program,' but the concessions admit a viable design 'likely needs a carefully specified weaker trap-ordering semantics (traps may fire early/coalesce).' Once traps can coalesce or fire early, observable behavior differs across optimization levels — the very build-mode semantic divergence the position exists to eliminate returns through the back door, with no corpus card on how to specify relaxed trap ordering soundly. The position's selling point survives only in the variant whose performance P3 concedes it cannot defend.

unsupportedClaims: P1: 'performance transparency — this language's stated purpose' — no corpus file or debate ground rule states performance transparency as the language's purpose; the frame says only optimizer-first with soundness non-negotiable.

P1: 'Nothing-implicit is the only policy under which soundness, performance transparency, and per-operation Alive2-style auditability all hold simultaneously' — uniqueness claim with no card; checked/trapping defaults also yield sound, auditable contracts via the runtime-check truth-maker.

P1: 'each of wrapping, checked, and unchecked... lowers to exactly one point in LLVM's existing contract vocabulary' — no card covers checked lowering (overflow intrinsics/branch patterns) or saturating intrinsics; only wrapping and nsw/nuw are card-backed (N002).

P1 and P2: 'poison defers UB and permits speculation' / 'poison-style overflow... keeps arithmetic freely speculatable' — no card affirmatively states poison enables speculation; N003 establishes only that immediate UB blocks it, and N001 gives the lattice without speculation rules. The speculation benefit of poison is folklore-consistent but uncarded.
P2: 'the default type is where almost all code lives, so the default determines what the optimizer sees' — human-factors/code-distribution claim with no evidence in the corpus.

P2: 'the most heavily machine-verified corner of LLVM's transformation suite' — N006 reports contributor-guide expectations ('should', 'usually'), medium confidence, one 2-1 vote; it is not a verification census of LLVM.

P2: 'poison is not lawless... a defined value-level taint... any future language verifier can reason about' — N001 gives propagation and freeze semantics, but branch-on-poison and other amplification-to-UB behaviors are not covered by any card; the 'bounded taint' framing exceeds the evidence.
P2: wrap-by-default 'permanently forfeits' / 'destroys the fact channel' — ignores compiler-inferred flags and post-check inference, on which the corpus is silent in both directions.

P3: 'the frontend can truthfully emit nsw/nuw on dominated recomputations and range facts' as a high-volume channel — N004 restricts range metadata to loads/calls/invokes; no card shows LLVM recovering nsw-grade facts from explicit checks (P3 itself lists this as a gap yet builds argument (1) on it).

P3: 'runtime checks... the only truth-maker available by default before proof tooling exists' — the cited implementation warning (a scaffolding file, not a card) lists 'source semantics' first; the exclusivity reading is P3's construction.

P3: 'trapping checks are side effects constraining code motion and vectorization' — mechanism for defined traps is extrapolated from N003's immediate-UB division example; the corpus covers neither the mechanism nor the magnitude for trap semantics.
P3: the 'Ada direction' framing — conceded by P3 itself: F006 is aliasing/verification evidence; no corpus card covers Ada/GNAT overflow checking or SPARK overflow-proof discharge.

All three positions: 'strict IEEE by default' as the safe/neutral FP choice — the corpus contains zero FP cards; additionally, the implicit equation of 'no fast-math flags' with 'strict IEEE conformance' (rounding modes, exceptions, environment access) is uncarded and likely false as stated.
missingConsiderations: Compiler-inferred facts as a fourth channel: LLVM passes infer nsw/nuw, known-bits, and value ranges from types and dominating guards. If inference recovers most of the declared-flag benefit, P1's mode lattice and P2's UB default are both overpriced; if it recovers little, P3's post-check story collapses. No advocate raised it, no card covers it, and it materially discriminates among all three positions.

The incumbent design point is missing from the candidate set: checked-in-debug/wrap-in-release (Rust's actual shipped policy) is the only field-tested mainstream default among LLVM-targeted memory-safe languages, yet nobody defends it, attacks it as a fourth option, or justifies its exclusion. A decision drawn only from these three positions has a structural hole where the strongest real-world comparator sits.

Interaction of the overflow default with index arithmetic and N005: inbounds GEP and induction-variable/sign-extension elimination are where overflow facts are folklore-critical, and bounds-checked arrays independently generate in-range truths that may substitute for or duplicate overflow checks. Only P2 cites N005, and only for an architectural-uniformity flourish; no advocate analyzes how their default composes with the indexing contract that N005 actually documents.

The 'strict FP' positions never distinguish LLVM's default unflagged FP (default rounding, no environment access) from genuine IEEE conformance requiring constrained intrinsics/strictfp — a semantic and cost difference with zero corpus coverage that changes what 'strict by default' even promises.

All three advocates converge on strict-FP-default with scoped opt-in while unanimously admitting the corpus has no FP evidence. Convergence of three unevidenced priors must not be laundered into corroboration at decision time; the corpus's own 'still undecided' list makes a dedicated FP evidence phase a prerequisite, not an enhancement.

Debug-build cost of mandatory checking: P2 and P3 both load their safety story onto debug/always-on checks, but neither prices check overhead in the build mode where tests, fuzzers, and CI actually run; if debug becomes too slow, coverage drops and the detection-efficacy assumption weakens further.

Two-lowering-regime verification burden: any debug/release semantic split (P2 centrally, P3 via its conceded relaxed-trap-ordering retreat) doubles the frontend contract surface that N006-style Alive2 discipline must validate; no advocate addresses frontend-side verification tooling for their own compiler, only LLVM's internal discipline.

Target-dependence of checked-arithmetic cost (overflow-flag branch patterns, absence of trapping-add hardware on mainstream ISAs) versus N006's constraint that InstCombine canonicalization must be target-independent: checked-heavy IR may canonicalize poorly precisely because its cost model is target-specific. Unraised by anyone.

The card universe is broken for this debate: the ground rules authorize citing A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005, but none of those cards exist in the five permitted files (only F001-F009 and N001-N006 do). All advocates flagged the absence; the decision process must reconcile the card universe before weighing this debate, and P3's claim that the cards 'exist in other corpus files per directory listing' suggests it consulted material outside its permitted read set, a process deviation worth noting.

caveats.md's blanket constraint applies to the debate's very framing: 'No final language design decisions should be made from this plan,' and every N-card carries design_generalization_confidence: low with a negative scope disclaiming source-policy support. All three advocates concede this and then advocate a default anyway; the honest output of this evidence base is a discriminating-experiment list (nsw benefit benchmarks, trap-overhead benchmarks, freeze-cost benchmarks, post-check fact-recovery measurements, language-precedent cards), not a default selection.
result.sharpestTension: The corpus's single best-verified numeric fact (N002, 3-0: LLVM's strongest integer-optimization fuel, nsw/nuw, is exactly a UB-class contract whose violation is poison) collides head-on with the debate's non-negotiable soundness rule under an unresolved reading of 'soundness' — and every position handles the collision by maneuver rather than evidence. P2 keeps the fuel by redefining soundness as contract-relative (release-mode UB is 'not unsoundness' because the language definition says so), P3 keeps strict soundness by declaring contract-UB prohibited by fiat and buying facts with runtime checks whose optimizer-consumption pathway is unevidenced (its N004 'range facts' channel does not even attach to arithmetic per the card's own scope) and whose cost is unquantified (N003 mechanism, caveats.md's no-magnitude warning), and P1 evades by refusing to assign a meaning to bare '+' at all. The corpus itself declares both halves of the deadlock open — 'whether source-level UB is acceptable' is listed as undecided, and no card prices the nsw benefit, the trap cost, or the freeze fallback — so the decision requires (a) an explicit interpretive ruling on what 'soundness may not be sacrificed' means for contract-violating programs, which no evidence card can settle, and (b) new quantitative evidence (nsw-vs-wrap benchmarks, trap-overhead and post-check fact-recovery measurements, freeze-mode cost), which no advocate possesses. Any default chosen before both are supplied is a preference wearing an evidence costume.

challenges.challenge: 'The only route the corpus offers to make those UB-bearing promises sound' is false twice over. The corpus offers NO demonstrated route to sound noalias emission — the fit-gap table's alias/noalias row is entirely TBD, and no card shows any MLIR pipeline emitting alias.scope/noalias metadata into LLVM at all. And to the extent the corpus gestures at any route, F006 (SPARK's source-level rules making facts true by construction) is a second one, and it belongs to the direct-to-LLVM advocate. The linchpin of MLIR-first — progressive lowering ending in derived, verified LLVM encodings — is the single least-evidenced step in its own pipeline.

The 'verifier-checked structural facts vs droppable UB metadata' dichotomy collapses on the advocate's own card. A001's negative scope says memref views 'can alias original storage and need verifier/runtime obligations' — distinct_objects is itself an UB-bearing trusted assertion at the MLIR level, not a proven fact. An IR verifier checks structural well-formedness and derivation discipline; it cannot establish that a disjointness assertion is true of runtime memory. That guarantee comes from the source type system (where F001/F006 locate it) in every architecture, so MLIR-first relocates the trusted assertion up a level rather than discharging it. The concession admits this 'applies recursively' but the core argument still trades on the false dichotomy.

F009 is appropriated. The one confirmed IR-strategy card validates a purpose-built language-specific IR (SIL) — Advocate 3's position is the closer instantiation. Everything that makes this position 'MLIR-first' rather than 'custom-IR' — reuse of affine/linalg/vector/memref — rests exclusively on unverified single-source A-cards plus a fit-gap table that is all TBD, including 'missing primary sources' for effects and layout, the exact fact classes the dialect is specified to carry. The position's distinctive content (MLIR over bespoke) has zero verified support; its verified support (F009) is shared property.
