'The facts worth preserving are known and language-level' contradicts caveats.md, which states the corpus is aliasing-heavy 'because of selection, not because relative importance has been established across arrays, layout, dispatch, numerics, concurrency, DSLs, dynamic runtimes, or ABI constraints,' and misleading-comparisons #1 bans exactly this promotion of the attested set to the enabler set. The 'shape/schedule' members of the claimed fact list (A002, A006) are provisional; the completeness claim that motivates an extensible dialect is unsupportable from this corpus.

'Plain LLVM IR must reconstruct from scalar soup' and 'standing hazards' language editorializes the pass-consumer matrix, which is self-authored scaffolding with every version cell TBD — caveats.md explicitly says implementation files pose questions and do not decide. Citing your own untested hazard hypotheses as 'the corpus's own accounting' of LLVM fragility is circular, and the same hypothesized hazards (dropped facts at lowering boundaries) apply to every inter-dialect lowering the MLIR-first pipeline adds.

The central move — 'an evidence-first process cannot prefer the architecture whose load-bearing claims are the unverified ones' — launders selection bias into an architectural conclusion. caveats.md states MLIR is simply uncovered and that the corpus's narrowness reflects selection, and it forbids design decisions from that narrowness ('No final language design decisions should be made from this plan'). Absence of verification is a property of the research run, not of MLIR. The concession admits this is 'largely an absence-of-evidence argument' but the core argument still presents evidentiary asymmetry as if it were evidence of superiority — the precise premature-decision failure the caveats file exists to prevent.

'Every optimization the corpus verifies is ultimately executed by an LLVM pass consuming LLVM-level facts' is false within the corpus. F009 verifies Swift-level ARC optimization executed by SIL passes explicitly staged before LLVM IR generation, and F007's escape-analysis optimizations run in HotSpot. Since this universal premise is what licenses 'any mid-level IR must re-materialize its facts anyway, so direct emission is the shortest sound path,' the argument's spine fails on the corpus's own confirmed cards.

F006 is cited backwards. SPARK's facts are 'true by construction' only because a verification toolchain enforces ownership transfer, anti-aliasing, and side-effect-freedom — the card describes a 'verification-oriented route.' Citing it as evidence the frontend can emit noalias 'without a mid-level verifier' inverts it: SPARK is evidence that substantial checking machinery is required between declared semantics and trusted facts. Without that machinery, F001's negative scope applies — restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness' — which the debate's soundness rule bans. The position quietly needs a checker layer; it has only argued about where not to put it.

Two citation overreaches: (1) F004 compares noalias return to C99 restrict only; 'stronger than any source-language precedent' generalizes beyond the card — Rust exclusivity and SPARK ownership are never compared. (2) F005 documents Fortran's aliasing MODEL and says nothing about pipeline architecture; using it as a 'production precedent' against a preserved high-level IR is unsupported, and its source is Flang documentation — a compiler whose actual architecture (MLIR/FIR) would, if it counted as evidence, count for the other side. The parenthetical concession does not neutralize its listing as supporting evidence.

The 'make the LLVM contract be the declared semantics' inversion (honestly flagged as uncarded) has an unexamined failure mode the corpus warns about: F003's negative scope says UB consequences belong to the IR contract, 'not necessarily desired source semantics.' Importing UB-bearing disjointness declarations into source semantics means every call site, generic instantiation, and function-pointer composition must be checked for aliasing or soundness is sacrificed — and checking it is the ownership/alias machinery the position claims to skip. Ergonomic sacrifice (verbosity) is permitted; trusted declarations are not. The rebuttal to F009 also over-narrows the card to reference counting when its verified scope names ownership — the fact class this advocate's own case centers via F001/F006.

The load-bearing architectural element — 'lower once, late, through a single audited boundary... narrow enough to test exhaustively' — has no card. F009 says nothing about how the SIL-to-LLVM boundary is audited, whether Swift emits rich alias metadata there, or that one boundary is more testable than a frontend's. Note the rival architecture is ALSO a single emission point (the frontend); the 'one boundary vs metadata scattered everywhere' contrast mischaracterizes direct-to-LLVM. What actually differs is whether a verified IR precedes emission — a real difference, but the containment-of-F003 claim as stated is design rhetoric wearing a card's clothing.

Survival symmetry is unaddressed: the moment the custom IR lowers, its emitted attributes/metadata are exactly as strippable by LLVM's mid-end as anyone else's — 'lowering strips metadata' and 'metadata dropped' (the matrix cells this advocate cites against the rival) apply identically post-boundary, since all three architectures feed the same LLVM pipeline toward the same consumers (ScopedNoAliasAA, LoopVectorize). The custom IR changes where emission-correctness is established, not whether facts survive to consumers. The only escape — doing enough optimization above LLVM that survival matters less — is never made explicit or quantified.

F008 is misused against its own warning label. Its design_generalization_confidence is 'low,' its negative scope says the workflow refuted two overbroad LLVM EH claims and instructs retaining exact wording 'before generalizing' — and the advocate generalizes it into a universal 'representation choice is itself an optimization fact' principle. Worse, the example undercuts the claim: funclet vs landingpad is dictated by target ABI (Windows EH), so a custom IR cannot 'pick the LLVM encoding late' out of that constraint. The card demonstrates inherited backend constraints, not deferral freedom.

'A purpose-built IR fits by construction' is a slogan set against the fit-gap TBDs, but the evidence is symmetric: nothing verifies that a small team can design, verify, and keep sound a bespoke IR either, and the corpus's ONE data point on custom-IR maintenance is F009's own negative scope — stale SIL docs and OSSA/non-OSSA phase confusion in a project with Apple-scale resources. The risks section also asserts uncarded facts ('duplicating LLVM passes as SIL does') and A005 is editorialized ('fragile hints' — the card says pragmas are hints with runtime checks/fallbacks, not that they are failure-prone).

All three treat the pass-consumer matrix's hazard cells as corpus evidence when caveats.md classifies implementation files as scaffolding that 'should constrain later debates by asking what can be represented, verified, preserved, and tested, not decide.' Every version cell reads 'LLVM version TBD' and every validation test is unrun. The debate's most-repeated 'facts' — lowering strips metadata, false noalias miscompiles — are hypotheses the project wrote for itself to test. They are plausible, but three advocates citing the same untested self-authored spreadsheet at each other is not evidence accumulation.

No advocate separates the two distinct hazards inside F003's negative scope — emission soundness (are the facts true when emitted?) and survival (do they reach consumers?) — even though the architectures differ only on the first and are identical on the second. This conflation lets Advocates 1 and 3 weaponize survival hazards against Advocate 2 that they inherit unchanged, and lets Advocate 2 claim 'shortest sound path' while the soundness of the path is the unsolved part.

unsupportedClaims: MLIR-first: 'progressive lowering... emitting LLVM's noalias/alias.scope/attribute encodings as the final, derived form of already-verified facts' — no card documents any MLIR-to-LLVM lowering of alias facts; the fit-gap alias row is TBD.
MLIR-first: 'the only route the corpus offers to make those UB-bearing promises sound' — the corpus demonstrates no route; F006 gestures at a second (source-rule) route.
MLIR-first: 'the facts worth preserving are known and language-level' — completeness of the fact list is unestablishable from a corpus that caveats.md calls aliasing-heavy by selection.

MLIR-first: that ownership/effects facts have a sound MLIR home with verifier obligations — fit-gap rows read 'missing primary sources' (conceded in risks, yet the position statement asserts it as the design).

Direct-to-LLVM: 'every optimization the corpus verifies is ultimately executed by an LLVM pass consuming LLVM-level facts' — refuted by F009 (SIL ARC passes) and F007 (HotSpot) within the corpus itself.
Direct-to-LLVM: 'LLVM's contract vocabulary is in places stronger than any source-language precedent' — F004 compares only to C99 restrict.
Direct-to-LLVM: 'direct emission removes one lossy, bug-prone translation layer' — the net-loss framing of intermediate layers is uncarded; the layer removed is where verification would live.

Direct-to-LLVM: 'the source rules discharge the F003 frontend obligations' without a mid-level verifier — F006 shows SPARK discharges obligations via a verification toolchain, i.e., evidence for a checking layer, not against one.
Direct-to-LLVM: F005 as a 'production precedent' of exploiting non-interference 'rather than requiring a preserved high-level optimizer IR' — the card is silent on pipeline architecture.

Custom IR: 'the UB-bearing metadata translation happens exactly once, at a boundary narrow enough to test exhaustively' — no card describes the SIL-to-LLVM boundary, its testability, or single-boundary superiority; the rival also has one emission point.
Custom IR: 'a purpose-built IR fits by construction' — asserted, with the corpus's only custom-IR maintenance data point (F009 negative scope: stale docs, phase distinctions) cutting against it.
Custom IR: 'a language IR lets you... pick the LLVM encoding late, per target' generalized from F008 — a low-generalization card whose funclet example is ABI-dictated regardless of mid-level IR.
Custom IR: 'potentially duplicating LLVM passes (as SIL does for some optimizations)' — not in F009 or any card.

All three: every characterization of 'lowering strips metadata' / 'false noalias causes miscompile' as established corpus findings — these are untested hypothesis cells in self-authored scaffolding (caveats.md), not verified evidence.
missingConsiderations: Division of optimization labor is the hidden variable nobody states: fact-preservation requirements depend entirely on how much transformation the language-level optimizer performs versus what is delegated to LLVM. If loop restructuring happens above (F009 pattern), metadata survival matters less; if LLVM's LoopVectorize does it, survival is everything. The three positions implicitly assume different splits and never surface the assumption, so they argue past each other about the same hazard cells.

Fact exceptions and retraction: F001, F005, and F006 negative scopes all document permitted-aliasing escape hatches (unsafe/UnsafeCell, POINTER/TARGET/coarrays, benign aliasing), and misleading-comparison #2 warns Rust's mapping breaks exactly at these edges. The hard soundness problem is representing where facts do NOT hold and suppressing emission there — the boundary of a fact, not the fact. No advocate discusses how their IR strategy represents escape hatches, interior mutability, or FFI-poisoned regions, though this is where real-world noalias miscompiles historically arise (a gap Advocate 2 partially names).

The source type system is the actual soundness guarantor in all three architectures — verifiers (MLIR or custom) can at most enforce derivation discipline; they cannot establish disjointness of runtime memory. Since the type-system layer is common to all three positions, the genuine architectural differentiator is much smaller than any advocate's rhetoric suggests, which should compress the perceived stakes of the choice.

F007 went unused by all sides despite being directly relevant calibration: it verifies that analysis-based fact recovery (HotSpot escape analysis) is conditional and implementation-dependent, 'not source-level guarantees.' This is the corpus's best evidence for the shared premise that declared/guaranteed facts beat re-derivation — a premise everyone assumes and no one grounds.

The corpus's two refuted LLVM EH claims (caveats.md orders they not be discarded) prove that documentation-derived claims about LLVM behavior can fail adversarial checking. This calibration should discount confident readings of LangRef semantics — especially Advocate 2's 'rich and stable fact language' framing and everyone's reliance on exact noalias/alias.scope semantics holding across the 'LLVM version TBD' cells.

Decision sequencing and reversibility: given ground rule 4 and the empty evidentiary middle, the actionable question is which commitments can be made now versus deferred behind the experiments all three gap-lists demand (fact-survival FileCheck tests, fit-gap closure, Mojo/Flang/Rust-noalias-history carding). The fact vocabulary and soundness-obligation ledger are architecture-invariant and could be designed first; no advocate proposes a staged decision or identifies which of their commitments are cheap to reverse.

Notably, the three advocates' evidence-gap sections converge on nearly the same experiment list — which is itself a finding: the corpus currently supports a shared requirements specification and test plan, not an architecture selection, and pretending otherwise violates the debate's own ground rule 4.

The N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, J001-J005 series named in the debate charter exist in none of the seven readable files; the debate is running on a card inventory that does not match its charter, and the decision record should state this explicitly so absent series are not later assumed to have been weighed.
result.sharpestTension: The corpus's two confirmed anchors point in opposite directions, and the debate lives entirely in the unverified gap between them. F009 — the only confirmed IR-strategy evidence — says facts should be kept above LLVM in a semantics-preserving IR long enough to optimize them; F003/F004 — the only confirmed encoding evidence — say the payoff contracts live below, inside LLVM IR, as UB-bearing promises that are sound only if frontend obligations are true and only useful if metadata survives lowering. Every position is a different theory of the translation layer that must connect those two levels, and that layer is precisely where the corpus is empty: the fit-gap table is all TBD, the pass-consumer matrix is untested scaffolding with every version cell TBD, the A-cards are single-source and unverified, and there is zero coverage of Mojo, Flang's FIR, Rust's noalias-toggling history, or any measured fact-survival data. Sharpened further: the architectures differ mainly on where emission soundness is established (source rules, dialect verifier, custom-IR verifier), yet all three are identical on the hazard everyone rhetorically emphasizes — post-emission survival through the same LLVM mid-end to the same consumers. Under the ground rules (soundness non-negotiable; do not settle what evidence cannot settle), this corpus can currently yield a fact vocabulary, a soundness-obligation ledger, and a decisive experiment list — but not an architecture, and any advocate claiming corpus support for their architecture is spending evidence the corpus does not contain.

result.question: How much array, layout, and loop structure should the language surface to the optimizer?

positionAssessments.position: Structured-arrays-first (shapes in types, affine-restricted indexing, first-class parallel/reduction loops, marked escape for irregular code)
positionAssessments.strengths: Best convergence between the adversarially verified findings and the precedent base after the critic's attacks: the confirmed payload is non-interference around writes (F001, F002), and every genuinely checked precedent in the corpus (Rust exclusivity per F001, SPARK ownership per F006, Futhark uniqueness/sizes per A008) achieved checkability by restricting the model — which is this position's move. Its central tractability intuition is directly carded (A003: affine restriction makes dependence analysis efficient and reliable), the target stack natively carries structured facts (A001-A004), and A005's documented consumer list (alias/dependence facts, reductions, trip counts, control-flow shape) is exactly what shapes-in-types plus declared loop kinds supply by construction. It also degrades honestly: unsupported claims were about ease and losslessness, not about the direction itself.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Its decisive differentiator — 'checkable rather than promised' — is uncarded for cross-iteration independence: the cited precedents (A010 DO CONCURRENT, F005 Fortran) are promise-based per the cards' own text, and no card attests any system statically checking loop-body independence; the critic's checkability bait-and-switch charge stands. F005 is causally misattributed (the card credits Fortran's aliasing contracts, not array-ness), F009 is non-discriminating, and 'lowering structure is easy / nearly lossless frontend' is asserted against the dossier's own open question on fact consumption. The escape-hatch/views boundary where every emitted shape/noalias fact can be invalidated (A001 negative scope, F003 UB) is waved at, not designed, and the workload share of the affine fragment is a complete unknown — if low, the design optimizes the wrong center.

positionAssessments.position: Algorithm/schedule separation (pure algorithm layer, distinct schedule layer for loop order/tiling/vectorization/layout, auto-derivable with override)
positionAssessments.strengths: The only position where optimization decisions are themselves first-class program structure rather than facts to trust or re-derive; aligns with the factoring MLIR Linalg independently adopted (A002) and the staged Vector lowering path (A004). Purity genuinely delivers F002-style non-interference for the interior of the dense fragment, the ISPC turn (baking execution strategy into algorithms is hardware-fragile, A007) is a real point against explicit-control rivals, and its scope honesty (primary surface for the dense/affine fragment only, with an escape hatch) was the most candid framing in the debate.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Thinnest evidence base of the three and the critic's sharpest hits land unanswered: the sole existence proof is one provisional tutorial card (A006) for a system caveats.md explicitly lists as lacking verified coverage; 'self-discharged obligations' fails at the host-buffer boundary, where user-supplied overlapping storage makes compiler-emitted noalias facts false (F003 UB) — its structural soundness is contractual exactly where it attacked the contracts rival; the storage-layout plank has zero cards (A006 lists only loop transformations); 'lowers deterministically' is contradicted by A004's own negative scope; the auto-scheduling half is conceded evidence-free, so as evidenced the position is mandatory expert hand-scheduling; and bufferization internalizes the write-interference problem with only A008 (itself flagged as needing further evidence) as mechanism.

positionAssessments.position: General memory plus checkable contracts (pointer/slice base model; disjointness/layout/alignment/contiguity as verified, runtime-checked, or explicitly-unsafe annotations lowering to LLVM legality machinery)
positionAssessments.strengths: The only position whose central fact family (disjointness) is backed end-to-end by adversarially verified cards from language rule (F001, F005, F006) to IR carrier (F003, F004); the only one with graceful degradation outside the dense fragment (conservative code, not rejection or escape hatch); and its substrate observation is correct and important — the structured stack itself bottoms out in a contract-bearing memory layer (A001 distinct_objects), so contracts are the load-bearing layer under every rival. Its verify-or-check-or-gate posture is the right soundness discipline for this project.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Its evidence lead is explicitly flagged by the corpus's own discipline notes as selection bias (caveats.md; misleading-comparisons entry 1), not established importance. Three of its four fact families (layout, alignment, contiguity) have no carded sound, consumed encoding — the 'best-attested facts' framing overstates by most of the position's surface area. A005's runtime checks are vectorizer-internal speculation, not a user-contract validation mechanism, so the claimed soundness template is uncarded. F006 partially backfires: the corpus's only static-checking precedent achieved checkability by restricting the memory model, making 'checkable contracts over unrestricted pointers' the one configuration no card attests. It concedes no path to loop-nest transformation parity (tiling/fusion/interchange evidence sits with A002/A003), and whether an explicitly-unsafe tier satisfies the soundness red line is asserted, not argued.

keyTradeoffs: Tractability vs coverage: affine/regular restriction demonstrably buys dependence-analysis reliability and loop-nest transformability (A003, A002), but forfeits or pessimizes irregular/sparse/pointer-heavy code whose workload share is entirely unmeasured; general memory retains coverage but has no carded route to loop-nest transformation parity.

Checkability vs generality: every checked precedent in the corpus (Rust, SPARK/F006, Futhark/A008) obtained checkability by restricting the memory or update model; checkable facts over an unrestricted model is the unattested configuration — so the more general the base model, the more the soundness rule forces either unpriced runtime checks or an unsafe tier.

Structural soundness vs boundary contracts: each design relocates rather than eliminates the trust boundary (structured core's escape hatch and views, pure layer's host-supplied buffers, contracts language's unsafe tier); which boundary is cheapest to police is the actual discriminator and the corpus is empty on it.

Guaranteed facts vs guaranteed transformations: A005 and A010 jointly show no design can promise optimization outcomes, only fact delivery — an optimizer-first language must define what it guarantees (facts surfaced, preserved, consumed) versus best-effort (transformations performed), and each position prices this differently.

Exclusive winner vs layered composition: MLIR's own stack (A001 contract substrate, A002/A003 structured ops, A004 staged vectors) instantiates all three positions simultaneously; the real decision variables are the default surface, where the checking burden falls, and the failure mode of a violated fact — treating the positions as mutually exclusive may be a framing error.
evidenceGaps: End-to-end fact consumption is unvalidated for every position's lowering path: no evidence that shape/affine/parallel facts or noalias metadata emitted by a frontend survive bufferization and lowering and actually change LLVM vectorizer/parallelizer decisions (dossier open question; F003 caveat; misleading-comparisons entry 5).

Failure-mode semantics for violated facts (static rejection vs runtime check vs trap vs unsafe promise vs conservative fallback vs IR UB) — the dossier poses it, no card answers it, and it is where the three positions genuinely differ.

All A-series cards (A001-A010) are unverified direct extraction from systems caveats.md lists as lacking verified coverage, against a measured base rate of 2 refuted and 2+ dropped claims from the same pipeline; nobody consulted notes/refuted-and-unverified.jsonl.

No card attests any system statically checking cross-iteration independence of loop bodies — the checkability claim central to structured-first is unattested; equally, no card attests a mechanism for validating user-written disjointness/contiguity contracts at call boundaries or its runtime/code-size cost.

Workload census absent: what fraction of intended programs fits the regular/affine (or pure/schedulable) fragment, and what the fallback costs for the remainder; sparse/irregular/graph workloads have zero corpus coverage.

Trust-boundary semantics uncovered for all designs: how externally supplied storage (FFI, callers, I/O buffers) acquires shape/disjointness/alignment facts, and views/slices aliasing over shaped arrays (A001 negative scope).

FP reduction ordering/reassociation policy: A005 makes math flags a legality input, so numeric semantics gate all three positions' parallel/vector stories; no card addresses determinism or reassociation contracts.
Degradation under dynamic/symbolic shapes is uncovered for all positions (runtime sizes weaken trip counts, bounds inference, and static contract discharge alike).
No quantified performance, compile-time, or code-size evidence for any position (caveats.md standing constraint); all comparative claims in the debate are architectural.
The N001-N006, D001-D010, C001-C006, and J001-J005 card series named as citable were absent from the assigned read set for every participant; the debate ran on F and A cards only.
result.leadingCandidate: Structured-arrays-first as the default surface, explicitly reframed as the top layer of a composition: a structured/affine-checked core over a contract-bearing general-memory substrate (the F003/A001 layer), with an optional schedule layer confined to the pure fragment — not structured-first as an exclusive design.
result.rationale: The verified corpus establishes exactly three things: non-interference around writes is the valuable payload (F001, F002), LLVM has carriers for it whose soundness is conditional on frontend obligations and metadata survival (F003, F004), and the only precedents that made such facts checked rather than promised did so by restricting the model (F006, and A008/Rust per F001). That pattern favors a restricted structured core as the default surface, and it is why structured-arrays-first leads despite the critic landing real hits on it: its direction is carded (A003) even where its decisive claims (checkable independence, lossless lowering) are not. The contracts position's apparent evidentiary lead is discounted by the corpus's own discipline notes as selection bias, and its distinctive configuration — checkable contracts over unrestricted pointers — is the one no card attests; schedule separation rests on a single provisional tutorial card, lost its soundness-by-construction claim at the host-buffer boundary, and has an entire plank (layout scheduling) and an entire half (auto-scheduling) with zero evidence. But the outcome cannot be design_candidate_later: the critic demonstrated that every position's core differentiating mechanism is unattested, that all three relocate rather than eliminate the trust boundary, and that the debate's true decision variables (default surface, checking-burden location, violated-fact failure mode) were argued by no one. These are mechanism-plausibility questions that more documentation research cannot close — caveats.md itself says documentation evidence cannot quantify what is missing — so the discriminating next step is a spike: prove facts emitted by a structured frontend survive lowering and change optimizer decisions, prove cross-iteration independence is checkable for the affine fragment, and price the runtime checks and boundary enforcement each design needs. Not research_needed alone, because the A-card verification pass, while necessary, cannot settle consumption or checkability; not evidence_conflict, because the cards are thin and biased rather than opposed; not no_decision, because a leading direction and a concrete discriminating experiment both exist.
followUps: Build the end-to-end fact-consumption spike (dossier open question 5): a minimal frontend emitting shaped/affine/parallel facts through Linalg/Affine/Vector and, separately, noalias metadata through the direct LLVM path; measure which facts survive bufferization/lowering and whether they measurably change loop/SLP vectorizer decisions on ~5 representative kernels.

Prototype the checkability claim that discriminates position 1 from position 3: implement static cross-iteration independence checking for affine-bodied parallel loops, and measure what fraction of representative kernels type-check statically vs require runtime checks vs must fall back to promises.

Run the adversarial verification pass on A001-A010 (priority: A002, A003, A006, A008), consulting notes/refuted-and-unverified.jsonl first and applying the measured 2-refuted/25-verified base rate as the discount for anything still unverified.

Convene a dedicated decision on failure-mode taxonomy (dossier open question 2): for each fact family (disjointness, shape, alignment, contiguity, independence), fix static-reject / runtime-check / trap / unsafe-promise / conservative-fallback semantics, and price the runtime-check variants inside the spike.

Design and adversarially review the trust boundary for the leading composition: how FFI/host/I-O buffers acquire facts on entry, views/slices semantics that keep aliasing explicit while preserving vectorization legality (A001 negative scope), and whether an explicitly-unsafe tier is compatible with the soundness red line.

Conduct a workload census of the language's intended program population: fraction fitting the regular/affine fragment, prevalence of dynamic/symbolic shapes, and sparse/irregular requirements — this directly resolves whether privileging the structured core optimizes the right center.

Define the FP reduction ordering/reassociation policy before fixing any parallel-loop or reduction-construct semantics, since A005 makes math flags a legality input gating all three positions equally.

Draft the composed architecture as the explicit null hypothesis for the next debate round: contract-bearing substrate + structured default surface + schedule layer for the pure fragment, and reframe the question as 'choose the default surface, checking-burden location, and failure modes' rather than an exclusive winner.
Bring the N/D/C/J card series into the read set and re-examine this topic if numerics, concurrency, or JIT cards bear on reduction semantics, layout, or dynamic shapes.

Specify the language's guarantee contract: which facts are guaranteed surfaced/preserved/consumed versus which transformations are best-effort (A005, A010), so performance-transparency promises are scoped to what any backend can honor.

challenges.challenge: The 'sharing proofs are an optimization gradient, not a soundness precondition' framing is incoherent with F003, which the position itself cites: emitted noalias facts are UB-if-false. The core argument's claim that 'a missing or wrong sharing annotation degrades performance' is false for wrong annotations — those are silent miscompiles, exactly the unbounded blast radius the position exists to prevent. Either every emitted non-sharing fact is checked by Rust-strength machinery (the fast path becomes Position 1 and 'simpler types' is forfeited, only half-conceded in concession 2) or the facts cannot be emitted and the entire performance story (F002 reclamation via F003) evaporates. The case never resolves which horn it takes, and its headline 'unconditional soundness' is conditional on resolving it.

Evidence-card misuse of C003, the position's stated cost function: the card restricts register-caching to 'volatile/atomic values', but the core argument broadens this to 'no caching possibly-shared values in registers across loop iterations'. Further, C003 attributes its restrictions to memory models generally — its sources include the C/C++-covering survey, and post-C++11 UB-race languages also forbid invented writes on possibly-shared memory — and its negative scope says 'rules differ per language/IR'. So the implied cost model ('bounded models pay the full C003 list; the UB column and type-enforced designs escape it') is not supported by the card. The true UB-vs-bounded delta on ordinary non-atomic memory is narrower (re-loading, some caching cases) than the headline 'these taxes paid pervasively' claims, which converges with C002's negative scope: the delta is unquantified, and the position's main advantage is priced against an inflated tax.

Internal contradiction: the structural invariant doing all the optimizer work — 'at every program point each datum has exactly one owning task', hence 'every core-language access lowers as NotAtomic' — is broken by the same argument's C006 immutable-publication carve-out. Shared immutable data is shared memory: multiple tasks alias the same storage, invented writes remain illegal (C003), and the carve-out needs freeze/escape/constructor discipline (C006 negative scope: 'escape analysis and constructor discipline are preconditions'; F004 scope note: 'source languages need lifetime/provenance/escape rules before emitting' such facts). Policing that carve-out plus residual-alias-free moves is precisely the borrow-checker-shaped machinery the position's rhetoric ('discharged by construction, rather than by a subtle borrow-checker boundary') claims to avoid — concession 4 quietly admits this, but the core argument's differentiation from Position 1 depends on denying it.

'Arbitrary-but-memory-safe wrong answers' is unsupported and likely false for an AOT systems language: no card establishes that bounded racy outcomes preserve memory safety. Racing on multiword values (fat pointers, slices, vtable/interface words) can tear and convert a 'bounded' race into out-of-bounds access unless the language constrains safety-critical values to single-word atomic layout — an uncarded, optimizer-relevant data-layout tax (Java's single-word references are exactly such a constraint) that the position neither states nor prices. Without it, 'bounded' collapses to 'UB with extra steps' at precisely the soundness boundary the ground rules protect. Evidence gap — no card covers race-vs-layout interaction — but the burden is on the position claiming 'unconditional soundness'.

Leg (c) of the core argument — 'the IR's own model sits closer to bounded than to C++-style UB, so a lowering path exists' — is an uncarded inference that C003's negative scope actively warns against ('LLVM IR semantics (undef racing loads) are not identical to source models'). Undef-producing racing loads are unusable as-is for a bounded source semantics, so faithful lowering of possibly-shared accesses likely requires stronger-than-NotAtomic orderings whose transformation prohibitions C005 documents and whose cost nothing quantifies. The position's own concession 3 admits this, which means leg (c) is negated by the case's own fine print while still standing in the headline argument.

F007 is used against a strawman: it is cited as 'direct evidence against the bounded-race competitor's claim that runtime analysis can recover what static non-sharing proofs give for free', but the actual bounded advocate's recovery mechanism is static type-level facts (C004/F003), not runtime analysis — F007 adjudicates nothing between the two positions as argued. The citation also omits that F007 carries the index's only 'low' design-generalization rating (HotSpot-specific, inlining/profile/tier-dependent) while stressing its 'workflow-confirmed' status, an asymmetric weighting of the same index row.

'Only a type system discharges that obligation mechanically' is contradicted by the corpus's own F006: SPARK demonstrates a workflow-confirmed verification-oriented route (anti-aliasing restrictions, ownership policy with transfer, contract proof) that also discharges non-interference obligations mechanically without being a Rust-style type system. Likewise 'type-enforced DRF is the only point in the space that gets both' ignores that Position 3's core makes the same both-claim and that hybrids (typed DRF + bounded unsafe fragment, listed in the position's own risks) are unexamined by any card. 'Only' is doing rhetorical work no evidence supports.

'Provably unshared', 'theorem about safe code': C004 is a provisional, Nomicon-sourced documentation claim, and caveats.md states the Nomicon is 'explanatory rather than a complete formal aliasing model' with future aliasing-model changes as live caveats. No card contains a formal DRF theorem for safe Rust (RustBelt/Stacked Borrows-class artifacts are absent — the position lists this as a gap, then keeps the theorem language anyway). Under F003/C005's UB-if-false contract, the difference between 'theorem' and 'design intent documented in an explainer' is exactly the difference between sound optimization and a miscompile engine, so the evidence-tier inflation is load-bearing, not stylistic.

The binary partition 'provably-unshared (NotAtomic, full freedom) vs explicitly-marked-shared (atomics, C003 tax)' hides the third and fourth states its own cited cards imply: Sync-shared immutable data (shared non-atomic memory where invented writes remain illegal) and UnsafeCell-style interior mutability inside 'safe' types (C004 negative scope: types must 'say otherwise', and caveats.md flags exactly this fragment as formally unsettled). 'The C003 taxes are confined to memory the programmer explicitly marked as shared' is therefore inaccurate even within the Rust model the position points to; C006 is additionally stretched into a claim the card does not make (that publication safety 'wants to be' a static non-escape fact provided 'uniformly' by borrowing — the card documents a narrow Java carve-out with preconditions, nothing more).

C003 misquote, same as Position 1: 'loses the right ... to cache values in registers across iterations' broadens the card's volatile/atomic-scoped restriction to all possibly-shared memory, and 'move code across fences' is presented as a tax on shared memory generally when fences exist only where synchronization exists. The structural invariant's payoff is priced against this inflated bill, and since C002's negative scope says the UB-vs-bounded delta is unquantified, the marginal optimizer benefit of structural non-sharing over Position 1's type-proven non-sharing — which the position itself names as 'the decisive unbenchmarked question' — has no corpus support at all.

'The classic objections to actor/channel style are substantially ergonomic' is uncarded and probably false: the standing objections include copy/queue/transfer throughput costs and the inefficiency or inexpressibility of shared-state algorithms (concurrent maps, work-stealing deques — listed in the position's own risks), which are performance objections squarely in-scope for an optimizer-first charter. The charter shields ergonomics only; the position uses the charter to dismiss a performance objection by relabeling it ergonomic, while conceding (concession 3) that no cost data exists for transfer vs synchronization. F006's scope limit ('may not map to a general-purpose performance language without usability costs') also covers expressiveness restrictions, not just comfort — a distinction the case never draws.

'Dissolves the field's hardest open tradeoff' is contradicted by the position's own risk list ('confined, not solved'): the UB-vs-bounded question (C002) reappears inside the systems module with no guidance, and — unstated anywhere in the case — inside the channel/scheduler runtime itself, which is permanently-resident shared-memory code that every core-language guarantee bootstraps on. 'Racy core programs are unrepresentable' is a claim about the surface language, conditional on the soundness of a transfer checker (miscompile-on-hole, per the position's own risk 3) and an unverified runtime kernel; 'DRF-SC holds trivially' (C001 citation) conceals that trusted base.

Data-parallel workloads are never addressed: fork-join mutation of disjoint slices of one array — the canonical optimizer-first workload — is inexpressible under single-owner move-on-send without deep copies (the position's own copy-fallback risk) or split-ownership machinery that is again borrow-checker-shaped. The F004 receive-disjointness analogy (self-flagged as extrapolation, correctly) does not cover it, and a moved message is not actually allocation-fresh: its address may survive in sender-side raw pointers or FFI, so emitting F004-strength disjointness facts is UB-if-any-residual-alias, not 'always-emittable metadata' as the F003 citation claims.

'A finite, concrete, implementable list' overreads C003: the card is an illustrative sample whose negative scope says rules differ per language/IR, and the position's own risk list (out-of-thin-air exclusion, mixed-size coherence, Java's memory-model revision history — all uncarded) shows the specification burden is open-ended. A semantics that is 'historically hard to specify without admitting out-of-thin-air values' cannot simultaneously be sold as an enumerable set of pass restrictions; the position concedes the specification risk and keeps the 'finite list' framing anyway.

The burden-shifting close ('the burden of proof sits on UB advocates to produce the missing cost data') is rhetorically asymmetric: C002's negative scope says the delta is unquantified in either direction, and the position's own soundness product is undelivered — detect-and-halt is permission not obligation (concession 6), racy outcomes absent a detector are unspecified, and the specifiability of the bounded semantics is itself an admitted gap. A position cannot claim the charter's soundness rule as its trump card while its own racy-outcome semantics is an IOU; on the current corpus, both sides' decisive claims are equally unevidenced.

All three cases argue from corpus density as if it were established importance: 'the central optimizer enabler' / 'best-confirmed theme' / 'strongest confirmed theme' (F001/F002) is precisely the inference misleading-comparisons.jsonl entry 1 warns against ('corpus is selection-biased toward aliasing... say: non-interference is well-attested in selected sources') and caveats.md repeats ('aliasing-heavy because of selection, not because relative importance has been established'). Each advocate concedes the selection bias in fine print while the headline argument treats non-interference-maximization as the decisive design axis. The corpus cannot rank the sharing model against layout, numerics, dispatch, or IR design as the highest-leverage choice — and per caveats.md's standing rules, implementation files and this evidence base 'should constrain later debates... not decide source semantics'.

unsupportedClaims: Position 1: 'no caching possibly-shared values in registers across loop iterations' attributed to C003 — the card scopes the register-caching restriction to 'volatile/atomic values'; Position 3 makes the same broadening ('to cache values in registers across iterations'). Position 2 quotes the card faithfully.

Position 1: 'Only a type system discharges that obligation mechanically' — contradicted by F006 (SPARK's workflow-confirmed verification-oriented ownership/anti-aliasing route), which Position 1 never cites.

Position 1: 'Type-enforced DRF is the only point in the space that gets both' — 'only' is unsupported; Position 3's core makes the same both-claim and hybrid designs (typed DRF + bounded unsafe fragment) are uncarded and unexamined.

Position 1: the implication that C003's restriction list is a bounded-model-only tax that the UB column escapes — C003 attributes the restrictions to memory models generally (sources span the C/C++-covering survey), and its negative scope says rules differ per language/IR; invented-write prohibitions bind the UB column too.

Position 1: C006 read as evidence that 'publication safety wants to be a static non-escape fact' provided 'uniformly' by a capability/borrowing type system — the card documents a narrow Java final-field carve-out with escape/constructor-discipline preconditions and states no such design inference.

Position 1: 'theorem about safe code' / 'provably unshared' — C004 is a provisional Nomicon-based documentation claim; caveats.md says the Nomicon is explanatory, not a formal aliasing model; no card contains a formal safe-fragment DRF theorem.

Position 2: 'the IR's own model sits closer to bounded than to C++-style UB' — uncarded inference; C003's negative scope explicitly warns that LLVM's undef racing loads are not identical to source-level bounded models.

Position 2: 'A missing or wrong sharing annotation degrades performance' — wrong annotations emitted as F003 noalias facts are UB/miscompiles, not performance degradation; the claim conflates missing with wrong and hides the checker-strength requirement.

Position 2: racy outcomes are 'arbitrary-but-memory-safe wrong answers' — no card establishes memory safety under bounded races; torn multiword values (fat pointers, slices) can break memory safety in AOT code absent uncarded single-word layout constraints.

Position 2: C003 as 'a finite, concrete, implementable list' of all costs — the card is an illustrative sample; the position's own admitted specification risks (out-of-thin-air, mixed-size coherence) are the open-ended remainder.

Position 3: 'the classic objections to actor/channel style are substantially ergonomic' — uncarded, and the strongest standing objections (transfer/copy throughput, shared-state algorithm inexpressibility) are performance objections in-scope for the charter.

Position 3: 'every core-language access lowers as NotAtomic' and 'DRF-SC holds trivially' — false as stated given the C006 immutable-sharing carve-out (shared memory reappears in the core), channel-boundary acquire/release lowering, and dependence on an unverified transfer checker and shared-memory runtime kernel.

Position 3: 'the frontend obligations that make LLVM noalias metadata sound are discharged by construction' — 'by construction' assumes a sound checker with no residual aliases through raw pointers/FFI; the position's own risks concede any hole is a silent miscompile, making this a conditional claim presented as structural.
missingConsiderations: F009 is cited by nobody despite being workflow-confirmed and directly on-point: a semantics-preserving language-owned IR (Swift SIL precedent) is the corpus's own mitigation for the lowering-fidelity risk all three positions list (F003 metadata surviving LLVM's pipeline). Whether sharing/non-interference facts are consumed by the language's own optimizer IR versus encoded as fragile LLVM metadata materially reprices every position's lowering risk — Position 1's 'partial optimizer payoff' risk, Position 2's Unordered-lowering cost, and Position 3's channel-boundary metadata all change under a SIL-like design.

Memory-safety-under-races versus data layout: bounded-outcome semantics preserves memory safety only if safety-critical values (references, slice/interface fat pointers, vtable words) cannot tear, which imposes single-word-atomicity layout constraints that themselves tax optimizer freedom (layout, scalarization, SROA-like transforms). No card covers this; it is decisive for Position 2's soundness headline and for any Position 1 hybrid that wants bounded semantics in the unsafe fragment.

The threat model behind the bounded column: C002 itself records that Java bounds races 'for security' (untrusted code in-process). Whether this optimizer-first language will ever run untrusted code in-process largely determines how much bounded-outcome value is worth paying for, yet no advocate engaged with the stated motive of the model they were arguing for or against.

Structured parallelism as a fourth design point: fork-join/scoped-thread/loop-parallel models make sharing regionally scoped and provable per-region (determinacy-race checking, scoped noalias facts), which is arguably the dominant paradigm for optimizer-first workloads (SIMD, GPU offload — GPU models are flagged open in the implementation file). The debate was framed entirely as unstructured threads vs channels; none of the three positions reasons about disjoint-slice parallel mutation of a single array, which Position 1 handles via split borrows, Position 3 handles poorly, and Position 2 leaves untyped.

Per-optimization delta accounting between the UB and bounded columns: since invented-write prohibitions bind both columns on possibly-shared memory (post-C++11), the true delta reduces to specific transforms (read duplication/re-loading, cross-iteration caching of ordinary fields, speculation). This accounting could be done analytically from C003/C005 sources before any benchmark exists, and would partially close the gap every position calls decisive; nobody attempted it.

The liveness failure class: message passing and detect-and-halt trade data races for deadlocks, unbounded queues, and production halts. No card covers this (gap), but no advocate even weighed the new failure class against the eliminated one — 'no shared mutable memory' eliminates race unsoundness while introducing protocol failures the optimizer-first charter has no stated position on.

The permanently-trusted runtime kernel is the common denominator: channels, schedulers, locks, and atomics must be implemented in unsafe shared-memory code under every position. Its size, its race semantics, and its verification story are the same unresolved question wearing three costumes (P1's unsafe fragment, P2's annotation soundness, P3's systems module), and no position specifies it.

Volatile/MMIO and the implementation file's own mapping checklist: the file explicitly directs mapping candidate facts to 'LLVM atomic orderings, fences, sync scopes, volatile, and MLIR async representations'. Device-memory access and signal-handler-visible memory sit outside all three DRF stories; only sync scopes got a passing mention (Position 1, as a GPU gap) and volatile/MLIR-async got none.

Specifiability of the residue semantics is a shared, not Position-2-specific, problem: out-of-thin-air exclusion and mixed-size coherence afflict Position 1's unsafe-fragment story and Position 3's systems module exactly as much as Position 2's bounded core, since all three must say what a race in their escape hatch means. Only Position 2 flagged it, and only against itself.

Verified for the record: the five provided files contain only F001-F009 and C001-C006; the N001-N006, A001-A010, D001-D010, J001-J005 series cited as available in the ground rules are absent. All advocates noted this, but the decision process must locate or formally strike those series before any case here is treated as complete — per the corpus's standing rule, decisions must cite exact cards, and cards that cannot be read cannot be cited.
result.sharpestTension: All three positions' performance stories converge on the identical mechanism — statically checked non-sharing facts lowered to F003 noalias / C005 NotAtomic, both of which are UB-if-false — so the debate's real disagreement is only about the semantics of the residue: the memory and code where non-sharing cannot be proven (Position 1's unsafe fragment, Position 2's possibly-shared remainder, Position 3's systems module plus the runtime kernel every position needs). The ground rule makes soundness inviolable exactly there, and the corpus is thinnest exactly there: no card quantifies the bounded-residue optimizer tax (C002 negative scope; listed 'Open' in the implementation file), no card measures what fraction of real optimizer-first workloads lives in the residue, and no card addresses whether any residue semantics is even soundly specifiable (out-of-thin-air exclusion, mixed-size coherence, torn multiword values breaking memory safety). Each position relocates C002's UB-vs-bounded question into its own escape hatch and presents the relocation as a resolution. The decisive variable of the whole debate — the size and per-access cost of the unproven-sharing residue — is therefore unmeasured on both axes, and no argument about the fast path, where the three positions substantively agree, can settle it. Until the corpus gains (a) empirical bounded-vs-UB cost data on representative kernels, (b) workload data on residue/unsafe-fragment fraction, and (c) at least one card on residue specifiability (plus the absent actor-language and N/A/D/J evidence), any choice among these positions is a bet, not a conclusion.

result.question: Should any optimizer-relevant facts be left to profile-driven or speculative runtime machinery instead of source contracts?

positionAssessments.position: Fully static AOT: every optimizer-relevant fact is a verified source contract; no guards, no deopt, no warmup
positionAssessments.strengths: Occupies all of the adversarially confirmed territory: F001/F002 (non-interference contracts enable register promotion, load/store elimination, reordering in shipping AOT languages), F003/F004 (LLVM carriers exist), F005/F006 (Fortran rule-assumed model; SPARK statically-verified route), F009 (semantics-preserving IR). It is the only position whose mechanism is proven in production AOT compilers, and its demand that contracts be verified (not asserted) correctly answers the F003 UB soundness condition. The qualitative costs of the rival runtime channel are documented, if provisionally (J002 invalidation machinery, J005 warmup/code-cache, J004/F007 conditionality).
positionAssessments.weaknesses: The critic's attacks largely land. The universal quantifier forfeits frequency facts (hotness, branch bias, receiver distributions) that have no known sound contract encoding — conceded in its own gaps — and forfeits J002's (provisional) class of profile-enabled optimizations by fiat, with unbounded unquantified cost. Several headline claims are overclaimed against the cards: 'guards paid by every execution forever' contradicts J002's invalidation-based design; 'zero runtime checks' is not what F006 says about SPARK; 'Fortran proves production viability' exceeds documentation-grade evidence per caveats.md; 'pipeline complete end-to-end' restates misleading-comparisons entry 5's flagged claim. The F007/J004 conditionality argument never shows a static contract guaranteeing allocation elimination — conditionality may be inherent to escape analysis. Its own strongest cards' negative scopes (F001 unsafe/restrict truthfulness, F005 POINTER/TARGET/coarrays) evidence that every studied static channel needed escape hatches, i.e., pure static coverage is incomplete. AOT's own costs (monomorphization code size, compile time, annotation/verification burden) are left off the ledger, exactly the dimensions caveats.md says are unquantified.

positionAssessments.position: Static correctness contracts + firewalled offline profile data for cost decisions only (guarded fallback, no deopt)
positionAssessments.strengths: Its core partition principle is the one discriminating argument actually derivable from confirmed cards: F003 makes noalias-family facts UB-on-violation, so a measurement of past runs can never soundly carry them — wrong contract = miscompile, wrong profile-steered cost decision = slowdown. This failure-mode-matched channel assignment survives the critic intact and fits the soundness-first charter. It fully captures the consensus territory (F001/F002/F004/F006), avoids the documented runtime-machinery costs (J002 deopt/invalidation, J005 warmup/code-cache), keeps optimization inputs inspectable build artifacts (consistent with F009's visibility lesson), and partially retains J002's conceded profile-enabled benefits via guarded fast paths with compiled statically-correct fallbacks.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: The PGO half rests on zero corpus cards — its own gap 1, confirmed by my reading: no offline-PGO evidence exists in any assigned file. The J003 citation ('concrete instance of the firewall working' via field ordering/hot-cold splitting) is fabricated; the card contains only V8 hidden-class content. 'Captures exactly that benefit' is contradicted by its own concession: non-local speculatable facts (field-never-written, map-never-transitions) need invalidation-style machinery the firewall forbids, so the capture is partial to an unmeasured degree. The firewall leaks by its own wording (guarded correctness speculation such as alias versioning is admitted, and that boundary is undefined precisely where it matters), and 'wrong profile ⇒ slower, never wrong' is an unverified implementation property — no card shows LLVM profile inputs are correctness-isolated. Profiles as build inputs also weaken strict source-determines-performance transparency and add reproducibility burden.

positionAssessments.position: Selective speculation runtime: contracts by default, guarded specialization with deoptimization at explicitly designated boundaries
positionAssessments.strengths: Correctly identifies, from confirmed cards' negative scopes (F001 unsafe/restrict, F005 POINTER/TARGET, F006 usability costs), that a residual set of true-but-statically-unprovable facts is normal, not exotic. Guard-plus-fallback is sound by construction where assert-and-eat-UB is not, honoring the non-negotiable soundness rule. It is the only position that can monetize non-local invalidation-style facts (per J002's provisional negative scope), and its source-visible speculation marks are a genuine attempt to reconcile speculation with performance transparency (F009 analogy). Its concessions are the most honest in the debate about the provisional status of its own evidence.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: The critic's false-trichotomy attack lands on the position's defining component: guarded fast path with a compiled statically-correct fallback (no deopt) is a fourth option, so 'only guard-plus-deopt is fast and sound' is false, and deoptimization — the thing distinguishing this position from position 2 — is supported by zero AOT evidence (own gap 1). All supporting cards are provisional J-cards from whole-program JITs for dynamic/managed, effectively single-mutator languages; concurrency (unverified corpus territory per caveats.md) undermines cheap guard-then-use. Guard-class mismatch: J001-J003 evidence cheap tag/shape checks, but the residual boundaries it targets need alias guards, unevidenced here — and that is where the F002 payoff lives. Deopt machinery is a language-wide tax even when rarely used (own concession 7), with F008 (funclet exit paths forbidding register allocation for live-across values, design-generalization confidence low) the only — and uncited — corpus analogue for how exit-path machinery pessimizes surrounding code. J002's one-directional de-specialization reintroduces input-history-dependent performance opacity, in direct tension with the transparency charter unless cliff diagnostics become mandatory language features.

keyTradeoffs: Failure-mode asymmetry per fact class: a false contract lowered to noalias-family IR is a miscompile (F003 UB), a stale profile-steered cost decision is a slowdown (if profile inputs are truly correctness-isolated — unverified), and a failed guard is a cliff or deopt churn (J002 one-directional degradation). The channel for each fact class should match its tolerable failure mode; only the first leg of this is confirmed evidence.

Optimization capture vs predictability: J002's provisional negative scope says deopt-supported speculation enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles; forfeiting them (position 1) is an unbounded unquantified sacrifice, while keeping them (position 3) buys history-dependent performance opacity and invalidation machinery — and no card measures either side's magnitude.

Contract-channel costs vs runtime-channel costs: SPARK-style verification burden and general-language usability (F006 negative scope), annotation density, monomorphization code size, and compile time are unquantified, exactly like the rival's warmup, code-cache, and guard costs (J005, J001 negative scope); the corpus explicitly refuses magnitude rankings (caveats.md, misleading-comparisons entries 1 and 3).

Per-site guard cost vs language-wide machinery tax: compiled-in guards (position 2) cost every fast-path execution but keep the pipeline simple; deopt support (position 3) can remove fast-path checks via invalidation but taxes the whole compiler with frame-reconstruction metadata and optimization barriers even when speculation is rare — F008 is the only (weak, low-confidence) corpus analogue for that pessimization, and the 2-vs-3 delta is priced by no card.

Transparency granularity: position 1 offers source-determines-machine-code; position 2 offers source-plus-inspectable-profile-artifact; position 3 offers source-visible speculation sites whose realized performance state is input-history-dependent. The corpus contains no card measuring the value of any of these predictability levels (startup, tail latency, real-time).
evidenceGaps: No offline/AOT PGO cards at all (LLVM instrumentation PGO, AutoFDO/sample PGO, BOLT/Propeller): magnitude of inlining/layout/devirt wins, staleness sensitivity, regression rates. This is the single most decisive gap — it adjudicates position 1 vs 2 and prices position 2's entire PGO half.

No AOT-plus-deopt evidence (LLVM deoptimization operand bundles/statepoints, GraalVM native image, .NET tiered compilation with dynamic PGO): needed to price position 3's defining mechanism and the position 2-vs-3 delta (compiled fallback vs deopt), which no current card touches.

Residual guard cost vs static knowledge is unquantified — explicitly named in J001's negative scope — including whether guards hoist/combine well under LLVM and what alias-guard (loop-versioning) checks cost where the F002-class payoff lives.

All J-cards (J001-J005) are provisional direct extractions, not adversarially verified; the pivot sentence of the whole debate (J002 negative scope: deopt enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles) needs verification before any decision leans on it.

The N/A/D/C card series referenced in the debate brief do not exist in the readable corpus; the D-series (dispatch) bears directly on speculative devirtualization, every position's flagship example, so all dispatch reasoning here is mechanism-plausible but corpus-unevidenced.

No evidence that LLVM profile inputs (branch weights, value profiles) are correctness-isolated — i.e., that no pass converts profile metadata into semantic assumptions; position 2's firewall is unenforceable-as-evidenced without this.

No quantification of the static channel's own costs: contract-authoring density, verification time at scale (F006 negative scope), monomorphization code-size growth, compile time — caveats.md lists these as exactly what documentation evidence does not show.

No measurement of the residual set size: how often contracts are genuinely impractical (FFI, plugins, deserialized shapes, open-world dispatch) in optimizer-first codebases; if near-empty, positions 2 and 3's machinery is unjustified, if large, position 1's forfeiture is severe.

Concurrency and memory models are unverified corpus territory (caveats.md): guard-then-use atomicity outside single-mutator runtimes, safepoint costs, and noalias-contract interaction with a concurrent memory model are undiscussed by all positions.

FFI/external-call boundaries: no card covers how contract facts survive extern calls, how guards could check foreign effects, or how any of the three designs keeps F003's soundness condition across them — the most common real boundary, unaddressed by everyone.

No card measures predictability's value directly (variance, tail latency, startup-sensitive or real-time deployments); position 1's headline pillar and position 2's transparency compromise are both unquantified.

The critic's proposed third channel — profile-checked source annotations (frequency hints validated against profiles at build time, error on contradiction) — is entirely unexplored; it could dissolve the frequency-facts-cannot-be-contracts argument and deserves a card of its own.
result.leadingCandidate: Static correctness contracts as the default channel, plus firewalled offline profile data restricted to cost decisions (position 2) — leading on the strength of its confirmed-evidence core (F003's UB-on-violation argument that correctness-grade facts can never be profile-sourced, and F001/F002/F006's confirmed contract channel), with its PGO half, firewall enforceability, and the guarded-fallback-vs-deopt boundary against position 3 explicitly unresolved pending evidence.
result.rationale: The adversarially confirmed cards (F001-F006, F009) settle only what all three positions already agree on: statically checked non-interference contracts, lowered to LLVM's UB-grade carriers only when the frontend obligation is verified, are the best-attested and default channel — and per misleading-comparisons entry 5 those carriers are conditional, not a solved pipeline. Every claim that discriminates among the positions lives where the corpus has thin or no evidence: the J-cards are provisional (ground rule 3), the debate's pivot sentence (J002's negative scope) is unverified, and there are zero cards on offline PGO, AOT-plus-deopt, guard-cost magnitude, dispatch (the D-series is unreadable), concurrency, or the size of the residual set of statically unprovable facts. The critic's verification-grade attacks confirmed this: position 1's absolutist headline claims overrun its cards (SPARK zero-runtime-checks, Fortran production viability, forever-tax, pipeline-complete), position 2's PGO half has no cards and one fabricated citation (J003), and position 3's defining mechanism — deoptimization — is supported by no AOT evidence and rests on a false trichotomy. Position 2 leads because its core partition principle is the only discriminating argument derivable from confirmed evidence: F003 makes correctness-grade facts UB-on-violation, so they cannot soundly come from measurements of past runs, while cost decisions fail only into slowdowns — a failure-mode-matched channel assignment that fits the soundness-first charter. But 'leading' cannot become 'clearly leading pending verification': the positions 2 and 3 nearly collapse into one another (both are contracts-plus-guarded-speculation; they differ on offline-vs-runtime profiles and compiled-fallback-vs-deopt) and no card prices that delta; the firewall's enforceability in LLVM is unverified; and caveats.md's standing rule forbids design decisions that outrun the cards. The evidence is too thin on precisely the questions that separate the candidates, so the correct outcome is research_needed with a targeted evidence phase, not a conditional design pick.
followUps: Adversarially verify J001-J005 (same process as the F-series), prioritizing J002's negative-scope sentence ('deoptimization enables optimizations static compilers cannot legally do without profiles'), since both non-static positions pivot on it.

Commission evidence cards on offline/AOT PGO: LLVM instrumentation PGO, AutoFDO/sample-based PGO, BOLT/Propeller — measured win magnitudes for inlining/layout/devirt, profile-staleness sensitivity, and regression rates. This is the highest-leverage gap.

Commission evidence cards on AOT-plus-deopt hybrids: LLVM deoptimization operand bundles and statepoints, GraalVM native image (deopt in AOT), .NET tiered compilation with dynamic PGO — to price position 3's mechanism and the compiled-fallback-vs-deopt delta against position 2.

Obtain quantified guard-cost evidence: guarded speculative devirtualization vs contract-proven direct calls, runtime alias-check loop versioning code-size/check costs, and how well guards hoist/combine in LLVM (closes the gap J001's negative scope names explicitly).
Produce or obtain the D-series (dispatch) cards and the N/A/C series before any devirtualization or speculation decision; check them against the J-card extrapolations all three advocates made.

Verify whether LLVM profile inputs (branch_weights, value-profile metadata) are correctness-isolated in the implementation — that no pass converts them into semantic assumptions; this is a hard precondition for position 2's firewall and should be tested, not assumed.

Gather evidence on contract-channel costs at scale: SPARK industrial proof-effort data, Rust annotation/unsafe-prevalence studies, monomorphization code-size and compile-time measurements — the static side of the ledger caveats.md says is currently unpriced.

Investigate the F008 analogue directly: measure how guard joins, compiled fallback paths, and deopt/statepoint metadata constrain register allocation and surrounding-code optimization in an LLVM AOT pipeline (nobody cited F008; it is the only confirmed card about exit-path pessimization).

Evaluate the critic's unexplored third channel — profile-checked source annotations (hotness/likelihood hints written in source, validated against profiles at build time with errors on contradiction) — as a possible sound frequency channel requiring no runtime machinery.

Measure the residual set: audit one or two representative optimizer-first codebases for boundaries where static contracts are genuinely impractical (FFI, plugin dispatch, deserialized data shapes, data-dependent trip counts) to size the territory positions 2 and 3 are fighting over.

Commission concurrency/memory-model evidence: guard-then-use soundness outside single-mutator runtimes, safepoint machinery costs, and interaction of noalias-grade contract facts with a concurrent memory model — flagged unverified in caveats.md and ignored by all three advocates.

Define the FFI/extern-call story as a design question with its own evidence needs: how contract facts, guards, and the firewall each classify and survive calls into unverified foreign code — the most common real boundary, currently undiscussed.

result.question: What dispatch and generics model should the performance core use?

positionAssessments.position: Advocate 1: Monomorphization-only core with an explicit, restricted, visibly-costed dyn construct
positionAssessments.strengths: Best direct card support of the three: D001 (monomorphization yields concrete-code runtime behavior) is the corpus's most direct generics card; D002's positive scope explicitly supports making dynamic dispatch explicit when used; D003 attests the restricted-dyn escape hatch is precedented. Its central asymmetry — source-level guarantee vs conditional recovery — is genuinely in the corpus: D007's negative scope (speculative devirt may be reverted, option-dependent), D008 (interposition blocks IPA), F007 and misleading-comparisons note 3 (managed runtimes recover performance conditionally, unlike source-level guarantees). Best fit to the charter (ergonomics sacrificeable, transparency prioritized). Concessions were honest and pre-empted several critic attacks. Notably, all three advocates converge on guaranteed specialization for representation-affecting (value-type/hot) paths — A1 is the common skeleton.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: The critic landed four real hits. (1) F009 is cited backwards: the corpus's only workflow-confirmed dispatch-adjacent card exemplifies Swift SIL, a shared-generics/witness-table IR that preserves generic semantics so specialization can be decided in the optimizer — it evidences a rival architecture at least as much as A1's frontend-monomorphization framing (though it is compatible with a semantic specialization guarantee implemented at IR level). (2) The load-bearing claim that erased/shared bodies 'cannot carry type-dependent alias facts' is uncarded inference, contestable (uniform pointer-shaped bodies can carry parameter-level noalias), and exactly the move misleading-comparisons note 5 polices. (3) Predictability double standard: A1's own D004 shows monomorphized code's optimization is codegen-unit/LTO-option-dependent, so the position silently needs a build/visibility model (i.e., part of A2). (4) Composition hole: per D003, dyn-compatible traits exclude generic dispatchable methods, so mono-only plus strict dyn offers no sanctioned path for heterogeneous generic APIs — A1's own risk list predicts hand-rolled void*/fn-pointer erasure that the optimizer and F003 machinery handle worse, with no mitigation offered. Minor overreaches: D008 'sidestep' claim, Julia 'only/manually' additions to D009. Core cost (code size/compile time) is flagged by D001/D009 negative scopes and quantified nowhere.

positionAssessments.position: Advocate 2: Closed-world whole-program compilation (sealed-by-default, mandatory LTO-style visibility, no interposition/reflection/dynamic loading in core)
positionAssessments.strengths: Correctly identifies that visibility and finality gate the documented devirtualization machinery (D004, D007), and D008 gives genuine, specific support for no-interposition module semantics (converting an unverified linking assumption into a language invariant). D005's negative scope really does support restricting reflection in a performance core. The position composes with either generics model and several of its elements (no-interposition modules, finality semantics feeding D007-class machinery) survive as valuable amendments regardless of the generics decision.
positionAssessments.weaknesses: The critic's strongest attack lands squarely: 'guaranteed / sound-by-construction / invariant' devirtualization is supported by no card — D007 documents only conditional, revertible, option-dependent mechanisms, and LLVM's actual WPD machinery is uncovered (A2's own gap list). Closed world makes devirtualization possible without guards; nothing in evidence makes it promised, and inliner heuristics remain heuristics in a closed world. The universal claim 'every documented obstacle is an open-world escape hatch' is refuted in-corpus (D004 codegen-unit boundaries are build artifacts; D009's Function-typed fields hide call targets inside closed programs — and no position handles closure/function-value dispatch). Structurally, this is a visibility/build model, not an answer to the dispatch-and-generics question — it borrows A1's monomorphization (D001) for the actual topic, so it is an amendment presented as a rival. Under the soundness rule, core/periphery fact attenuation across the FFI/dynamic-linking boundary (D008; the interop dossier's targets include dynamic linking, JVM/.NET, Python) is a precondition, not a risk, and it is undesigned. Ban-vs-restrict (reflection) and 'unverifiable' (interposition flag) exceed what the cards say.

positionAssessments.position: Advocate 3: Hybrid .NET-direction generics — specialize value-type instantiations, share one body across reference-type instantiations, static specialize/share boundary, devirtualization backstop
positionAssessments.strengths: The only position that takes the corpus's carded specialization costs seriously (D001 and D009 negative scopes: code size, compile time, cache pressure from over-specialization). D005 attests the hybrid is a shipped, load-bearing design, not a paper proposal — the only card documenting a bounded-code-growth generics strategy. The static, type-system-visible specialize/share boundary is a genuine transparency property versus JIT-heuristic specialization cliffs (D009, D010). Concessions were unusually candid (JIT-vs-AOT transfer, undecided reference-type representation).
positionAssessments.weaknesses: Three critic attacks land hard. (1) 'Pure code-size win with no representational compromise' is contradicted by its own risk #1, by D002's cost class on the shared path, and by uncarded .NET dictionary/type-handle lookup costs — what is lost is the compiler's knowledge of type-argument-dependent call targets. (2) The D007 backstop is mis-transferred (GCC C++ vtable devirtualization, not shared-generic/dictionary dispatch) and circular where it would work: recovery-by-specializing-hot-instantiations regenerates exactly the code growth the hybrid promises to bound. (3) It never prices the shared path against the corpus's strongest-attested evidence class — whether shared bodies can discharge F003 noalias/alias.scope obligations — a potential tax on the flagship optimization exactly where the design shares. Feasibility rests on two undecided priors: a uniform reference-type representation (which the performance core may not have) and an AOT instantiation-closure story (D005's precedent is a JIT with lazy instantiation; NativeAOT-style evidence is absent). 'Only design attested to deliver both' overstates D005, whose negative scope disclaims proof of JIT optimization quality.

keyTradeoffs: Guaranteed specialization (A1) vs bounded code growth (A3): the single trade every generics design must make; the corpus contains zero quantitative cards on runtime, code size, i-cache, or compile time (caveats.md), so this axis is empirically unarbitrable today — a decision now would be a charter-values commitment, not an evidence-driven one.

Guarantee-by-construction vs recovery-by-optimization: D001-style source guarantees vs D007/D008/F007-style conditional, revertible, option-dependent recovery. The corpus qualitatively favors the guarantee side for a transparency-first language, but never measures how much performance speculative/PGO devirtualization actually leaves on the table.

Meaning of 'performance transparency': predictable cost model vs guaranteed-optimization floor (e.g., may the compiler silently devirtualize an explicit dyn call it proves unique?). No card addresses this; A1 raises it as a gap while A2 and A3 silently assume opposite answers. This charter question is antecedent to the topic decision.

Closed-world visibility (devirtualization possible without guards, interposition banned per D008) vs build scalability, dynamic-loading capability, and FFI-boundary soundness (D004/D007 compile-time/memory costs; dossier interop targets require a periphery whose fact-attenuation is undesigned).

Escape-hatch expressiveness vs analyzability: strict dyn-compatibility (D003) keeps runtime representation coherent but excludes generic dispatchable methods, leaving heterogeneous generic workloads with no sanctioned path — risking user-rolled erasure the optimizer understands worse than a looser language construct would be.

Where the specialization decision lives: frontend monomorphization (A1) vs IR-level generics with optimizer-decided or annotation-guaranteed specialization (the F009/Swift-SIL fourth model no advocate proposed) — affects separate compilation, generic ABI, and code-size control, and is entirely unexamined.
evidenceGaps: No quantitative comparison anywhere in the read corpus of monomorphization vs shared-body vs dictionary-passing generics on runtime, code size, i-cache behavior, or compile time — the decisive measurement for the A1-vs-A3 axis (caveats.md: documentation evidence does not quantify magnitude, size, or compile-time cost).

LLVM whole-program devirtualization machinery (!type metadata, -fwhole-program-vtables, virtual-function elimination) has no card; for an LLVM-targeted language this is the most load-bearing implementation question behind A2's claims and A1/A3's backstops. D007 is GCC documentation only.
